topics.title: Ship Production Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST633 - SHIP PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ship Production Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ship Production System** is defined as the integrated combination of physical infrastructure (berths, yards, dry docks, slipways), production methodologies (fabrication, pre-outfitting, block erection), workforce organization, material flow logistics, and technological processes employed to transform raw steel, machinery, outfit components, and engineering drawings into a fully operational, seaworthy vessel ready for delivery and service.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with NEP 2020 outcomes, a ship production system encompasses **four pillars**:

1. **Production Facility** – The physical infrastructure (berths, slipways, docks, halls).
2. **Production Process** – The sequence of operations (steel preparation → fabrication → assembly → outfitting → launching → commissioning).
3. **Production Planning & Control** – Scheduling, work breakdown structures, and progress monitoring.
4. **Production Organization** – Manpower deployment, departmental structure, and authority hierarchy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a ship production system like building a customized skyscraper on a horizontal conveyor belt. Instead of building floor by floor vertically, shipyards build the ship in **modular blocks** (like Lego pieces) in parallel workshops, then assemble these blocks on a giant platform (the berth), and finally rotate the completed structure into water (launching). The "system" is the entire choreography of workers, cranes, materials, drawings, and time that makes this complex dance happen without collisions or delays.

Imagine a **restaurant kitchen producing a multi-course banquet**:
- Different stations handle appetizers, mains, desserts in parallel (just like parallel block fabrication halls).
- The head chef coordinates timing so every dish arrives hot simultaneously (just like the **Production Planning Department**).
- The serving line moves dishes from kitchen to table (just like material flow from stockyard to berth).

In shipbuilding, this "kitchen" is called a **shipyard**, and its layout directly determines throughput, cost, and delivery time.

## 1.3 Classification Overview

Ship production systems are broadly classified into **three major categories** based on the building location and launching method:

| **Category** | **Examples** | **Key Metric** |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Position Systems** | Graving Dock, Building Dock, Pontoon Dock | Ship remains stationary |
| **Movable Position Systems** | Slipway, End Launching, Side Launching | Ship moves during launch |
| **Floating/Mobile Systems** | Floating Dock, Semi-submersible | System moves to/from ship |

Standard productivity metric: **Compensated Gross Tonnage (CGT)** per man-hour is the global yard performance benchmark, where **1 CGT = approximately 14 man-hours** of average work content for a standard product tanker.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of Ship Production

### 2.1.1 The Five Production Stages

The KTU 2024 syllabus structures ship production into the following logical hierarchy:

**Stage 1 — Steel Preparation & Storage**
- Incoming steel plates and profiles are stored in the **stockyard**.
- Plates are shot-blasted, primed, and cut using CNC plasma/oxy-fuel cutters.
- **Plate Nesting** minimizes wastage using software like **NAPA** or **FORAN**.

**Stage 2 — Sub-Assembly & Panel Fabrication**
- Flat panels (5m × 10m typical) are welded from individual plates.
- Automated panel lines with **Friction Stir Welding (FSW)** and **one-side welding** techniques improve productivity by **30–40%** over manual welding.

**Stage 3 — Block Fabrication**
- Panels are joined with stiffeners to form **3D blocks** (typically 20–80 tonnes for ease of lifting).
- Pre-outfitting (pipes, cables, equipment) is done at this stage — concept called **PEZ (Pre-Equipped Zone)**.

**Stage 4 — Block Erection & Hull Assembly**
- Blocks are hoisted by **Goliath Cranes** (capacity typically **300–1200 tonnes**) and welded together on the berth.

**Stage 5 — Outfitting, Launching & Commissioning**
- Final outfitting, painting, dock trials, sea trials, and delivery.

## 2.2 The Core "Why" — Unit Production Cost Logic

The fundamental economic equation driving ship production system design is:

$$\text{Unit Production Cost} = \frac{\text{Fixed Cost} + \text{Variable Cost} + \text{Launching Cost}}{\text{Ship Output (DWT or CGT)}}$$

A yard optimizes this equation by:
- **Maximizing throughput** (building more ships per year per berth).
- **Minimizing dwell time on berth** (because berth is the most expensive resource).
- **Maximizing pre-outfitting work before erection** (so post-erection work is minimal).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Form** | **Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steel Conversion Factor | $SCF = \dfrac{W_{final}}{W_{initial}}$ | dimensionless | Measures material utilization efficiency |
| Man-Hours per CGT | $MH_{CGT} = \dfrac{Total\ Man-Hours}{CGT\ Produced}$ | hours | Yard productivity KPI |
| Block Erection Rate | $BER = \dfrac{N_{blocks}}{T_{erection}}$ | blocks/day | Schedule tracking |
| Crane Utilization | $\eta_{crane} = \dfrac{T_{working}}{T_{available}} \times 100$ | % | Equipment efficiency |
| Berth Occupancy Ratio | $BOR = \dfrac{T_{in-berth}}{T_{available}}$ | ratio (0–1) | Capacity planning |
| Launching Force (Slipway) | $F_L = W_{ship} \cdot \sin(\theta) - F_{friction}$ | kN | Slipway design |
| Hull Block Lifting Capacity | $L_{req} \geq W_{block} \times SF$ | tonnes | Crane selection (SF = 1.25) |
| Productivity Index | $PI = \dfrac{CGT\ Output}{Number\ of\ Workers \times Time}$ | CGT/man-year | International benchmarking |
| Steel Scrap Rate | $S\% = \dfrac{W_{scrap}}{W_{input}} \times 100$ | % | Material efficiency KPI |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition:** For a typical product tanker of ~100,000 DWT, the total steel weight is approximately **15,000–20,000 tonnes**, requiring about **600–900 blocks** of average 25 tonnes each.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding ship production systems is critical in:
- **Yard Layout Design** (greenfield projects in India, Vietnam, China).
- **Capacity Expansion Planning** (adding a second building dock).
- **Product-Mix Strategy** (deciding to build VLCCs vs. container ships in same facility).
- **Digital Twin Implementation** (Industry 4.0 shipyard, KTU-aligned Industry 5.0 projects).
- **Lean Manufacturing** in shipbuilding (similar to Toyota Production System adapted for heavy engineering).

The **South Korean shipbuilding model** (Hyundai, Samsung, Daewoo) uses an extreme version of parallel block production — a VLCC is fully erected in just **3 months on berth**, but total construction time is **12 months** because 9 months is spent in pre-erection work scattered across multiple workshops.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Berth Occupancy Ratio & Production Capacity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The capacity of a shipyard in terms of ships delivered per year is a **direct function** of berth availability and average build duration. The following derivation connects the physical berth infrastructure to the annual throughput metric — a favourite KTU Part B (14 mark) question.

### Given Assumptions

- Let $N_b$ = Number of available building berths (dimensionless integer).
- Let $T_b$ = Average time a ship occupies a berth (in months).
- Let $T_a$ = Total available working time in a year = **12 months**.
- Let $k$ = Efficiency factor due to weather, breakdowns, and ramp-up ($0 < k \leq 1$).

### Step 1: Theoretical Single-Berth Throughput

The number of ships that can be completed per berth per year is:

$$
n_{berth} = \frac{T_a}{T_b}
$$

### Step 2: Apply Efficiency Factor

Realistically, berths are not utilized 100% of the time. Introducing $k$:

$$
n_{berth}^{actual} = k \cdot \frac{T_a}{T_b}
$$

### Step 3: Multiply by Number of Berths

For $N_b$ identical berths, total annual yard throughput is:

$$
N_{annual} = N_b \cdot k \cdot \frac{T_a}{T_b}
$$

### Step 4: Insert Numerical Example

Assume $N_b = 4$ berths, $T_b = 8$ months per ship (on berth), $k = 0.85$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{annual} &= 4 \times 0.85 \times \frac{12}{8} \\
&= 4 \times 0.85 \times 1.5 \\
&= 5.10\ \text{ships/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Inverse — Calculate Required Berths

If the target is $N_{target}$ ships/year with given $T_b$ and $k$:

$$
N_b = \frac{N_{target} \cdot T_b}{k \cdot T_a}
$$

This inverse formula is used by shipyard planners during feasibility studies.

## 3.2 Derivation: Crane Capacity Selection for Block Lifting

> [!NOTE]
> Selecting a Goliath crane capacity is a **capital-intensive decision** (a 600 T crane costs roughly **USD 8–12 million**). The required capacity derivation is:

### Given
- Maximum block weight $W_{block}$ (tonnes).
- Safety factor $SF = 1.25$ (KTU Board standard).
- Future growth margin $G$ (typically 10–20%).

### Required Crane Capacity Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{req} &= W_{block} \times SF \times (1 + G) \\
L_{req} &= 60 \times 1.25 \times (1 + 0.15) \\
L_{req} &= 60 \times 1.25 \times 1.15 \\
L_{req} &= 86.25\ \text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the next standard crane size (e.g., 100 tonnes) would be selected.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Production Systems

| **System Type** | **Initial Investment** | **Production Rate** | **Max Ship Size** | **Suitability** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Inclined Building Slipway** | Low | Low–Medium | Up to 50,000 DWT | Small yards, naval craft |
| **Vertical Building Dock** | Very High | Very High | Up to 500,000 DWT | VLCC, ULCC, large naval |
| **Horizontal Building Dock** | High | High | Up to 300,000 DWT | Mixed commercial fleet |
| **Floating Dock** | Medium | Medium | Up to 100,000 DWT | Repair + small build |
| **Modular Block System** | High (initial) | Very High | Any (limited by transport) | Modern commercial yards |
| **Flat Ground + Skid Launch** | Low | Medium | Up to 20,000 DWT | Inland yards, barges |

## 3.4 Calculation: Steel Scrap Rate Optimization

If a yard inputs 18,000 tonnes of steel and produces a ship with 15,500 tonnes of finished steel in the vessel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S\% &= \frac{W_{scrap}}{W_{input}} \times 100 \\
S\% &= \frac{18{,}000 - 15{,}500}{18{,}000} \times 100 \\
S\% &= \frac{2{,}500}{18{,}000} \times 100 \\
S\% &= 13.89\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Industry benchmark for modern yards is **10–14%**, so this yard is performing at a competitive level.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Modern Shipyard

The following diagram maps the **functional interaction** between departments in a ship production system, satisfying the Mermaid compilation safeguards (alphanumeric node IDs, no reserved keywords, clean text labels).

## 4.2 Classification Flow Diagram — Ship Production Systems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sysA["SHIP PRODUCTION SYSTEMS"]
    sysA --> sysB["FIXED POSITION SYSTEMS"]
    sysA --> sysC["MOVABLE POSITION SYSTEMS"]
    sysA --> sysD["FLOATING SYSTEMS"]

    sysB --> sysB1["GRAVING DOCK"]
    sysB --> sysB2["BUILDING DOCK"]
    sysB --> sysB3["PONTOON DOCK"]

    sysC --> sysC1["INCLINED SLIPWAY"]
    sysC --> sysC2["END LAUNCHING BERTH"]
    sysC --> sysC3["SIDE LAUNCHING BERTH"]

    sysD --> sysD1["FLOATING DOCK REPAIR"]
    sysD --> sysD2["SEMI SUBMERSIBLE VESSEL"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Flow of Material Through the Yard

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    matA["RAW STEEL PLATE 18 TONNES PER PLATE"]
    matB["STOCKYARD BUFFER STORAGE"]
    matC["PREFABRICATION SHOP"]
    matD["SUBASSEMBLY PANELS 5M BY 10M"]
    matE["BLOCK ASSEMBLY 3D BLOCKS 25T"]
    matF["PEZ PRE EQUIPPED ZONE"]
    matG["ERECTION AREA BLOCK JOINING"]
    matH["LAUNCHED HULL IN WATER"]
    matI["COMPLETED VESSEL"]

    matA --> matB
    matB --> matC
    matC --> matD
    matD --> matE
    matE --> matF
    matF --> matG
    matG --> matH
    matH --> matI
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Note:** Material moves from upstream workshops to downstream erection in a one-way flow with minimal backtracking. This is the **Lean Principle of Continuous Flow** applied to heavy engineering shipbuilding.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the term "Compensated Gross Tonnage" (CGT) and explain why it is preferred over Deadweight Tonnage (DWT) as a shipyard productivity metric.*

**Model Answer:**

Compensated Gross Tonnage (CGT) is a normalized unit developed by the **OECD Working Party on Shipbuilding** that accounts for both the **volume of work** and the **technical complexity** of producing a ship. The formula basis is:

$$
CGT = A \cdot (GT)^{B}
$$

where $GT$ is the gross tonnage and $A$, $B$ are coefficients varying by ship type. A simple product tanker of 100,000 DWT is approximately **30,000 CGT**, while a complex LNG carrier of the same DWT may be **60,000 CGT**.

It is preferred over DWT because:
1. **It reflects actual work content** (LNG carriers require 2× more work than a tanker per DWT).
2. **It enables fair comparison** between yards building different ship types.
3. **It correlates directly with man-hours consumed**.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
*Briefly explain the difference between "Block Erection" and "Pre-Outfitting" in a modern ship production system.*

**Model Answer:**

**Block Erection** refers to the joining of pre-fabricated 3D hull blocks on the building berth using Goliath cranes to form the complete hull structure. It is primarily a **structural joining activity**.

**Pre-Outfitting (PEZ – Pre-Equipped Zone)** refers to installing equipment, piping, ventilation, electrical cables, and machinery into blocks **before** they are erected on the berth. This is done at the block-assembly stage to reduce work at height and crowded post-erection conditions.

The relationship: Pre-outfitting is a **strategy that feeds into** block erection — the more pre-outfitting done, the shorter the on-berth time.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper, Module 1 Choice]**

**(a)** *With a neat sketch, describe the working principle of an "Inclined Building Slipway" ship production system. List its advantages and limitations.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *A shipyard has 3 building berths. The average ship occupies a berth for 9 months. If the efficiency factor is 0.80, calculate the annual throughput of the yard. If the management wants to deliver 7 ships per year, how many additional berths are required (assuming the same efficiency)?* **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Inclined Building Slipway (7 Marks)

**Working Principle:**

An inclined building slipway consists of an inclined ground surface (inclination typically **1:12 to 1:18**) extending from the building site down into the water. The ship is built on the inclined plane with its keel parallel to the slope.

Launching procedure:
1. The slipway is greased or uses low-friction sliding ways with **grease pads**.
2. The ship is supported on wooden or steel **launching cradles**.
3. At the moment of launch, the trigger mechanism releases the holding dogs, and the ship slides into the water under gravity.
4. The launching force is:

$$
F_L = W_{ship} \cdot \sin(\theta) - F_{friction}
$$

where $\theta$ is the slipway inclination angle.

**Advantages (KTU key points):**
- **Low initial capital investment** compared to dry docks.
- Suitable for **smaller vessels** (tugboats, patrol boats, small cargo ships up to 50,000 DWT).
- **Simple construction technology**, suitable for developing nations.
- Easy maintenance and inspection during construction.

**Limitations:**
- **Maximum size limitation** (large ships generate excessive launching forces).
- **Single ship at a time** per slipway (low throughput).
- **Weather dependency** of launching.
- Danger of **shock loading** at water entry.

**Sketch (textual representation):**

```
LAND SIDE                                                     WATER SIDE
====================================================================
                 /                                    /
                /  Ship Hull Under Construction   /
               /________________________________ /
              /  Inclined Slipway Surface       /
             /                                 /
    --------/---------------------------------/--------- WATERLINE
            \                                /
             \______________________________/
                 Keel Blocks and Cradles
```

#### Part (b) — Throughput Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $N_b = 3$ berths.
- $T_b = 9$ months/ship.
- $k = 0.80$.
- $T_a = 12$ months/year.

**Step 1: Current Throughput**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{annual} &= N_b \cdot k \cdot \frac{T_a}{T_b} \\
N_{annual} &= 3 \times 0.80 \times \frac{12}{9} \\
N_{annual} &= 3 \times 0.80 \times 1.333 \\
N_{annual} &= 3.20\ \text{ships/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Required Throughput = 7 ships/year**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_b^{required} &= \frac{N_{target} \cdot T_b}{k \cdot T_a} \\
N_b^{required} &= \frac{7 \times 9}{0.80 \times 12} \\
N_b^{required} &= \frac{63}{9.60} \\
N_b^{required} &= 6.5625\ \text{berths} \approx 7\ \text{berths}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Additional Berths Required**

$$
N_{additional} = 7 - 3 = 4\ \text{berths}
$$

**Conclusion:** The yard needs to add **4 more berths** to meet the target of 7 ships per year.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper, Module 1 Alternative]**

**(a)** *Explain the concept of "Modular Construction" in modern shipbuilding. How does it differ from conventional "Sectional Construction"?* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *A shipyard fabricates blocks of average weight 45 tonnes. If the planned safety factor is 1.25 and the design growth margin is 15%, determine the required crane capacity. Justify your answer with reference to KTU standard practice.* **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Modular Construction vs Sectional Construction (7 Marks)

**Modular Construction:**

In modular construction, the ship is decomposed into **functional modules** (e.g., engine room module, accommodation module, cargo handling module), each built as a self-contained unit in specialized workshops. These modules are often built to **80–90% completion** before being transported and integrated into the hull structure.

Key features:
- High degree of **parallelization** (different modules built simultaneously).
- Maximum **pre-outfitting** done in workshop (better working conditions).
- **Reduced on-site work** at the building berth.
- Adoption of Industry 4.0 sensors, IoT monitoring per module.

**Sectional Construction:**

In conventional sectional construction, the ship is divided into **hull structural sections** (primarily based on hull geometry), not functional modules. Each section is built and joined, and most outfitting is done **after** joining on the berth.

Key differences (KTU comparison table):

| **Parameter** | **Sectional Construction** | **Modular Construction** |
|---|---|---|
| Division logic | Hull geometry | Function + geometry |
| Pre-outfitting level | Low–Medium | Very High |
| On-berth time | Long | Short |
| Workshop conditions | Mixed | Excellent (PEZ concept) |
| Investment cost | Lower | Higher |
| Lead time | Longer | Shorter |

#### Part (b) — Crane Capacity Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $W_{block} = 45$ tonnes.
- Safety factor $SF = 1.25$.
- Growth margin $G = 15\% = 0.15$.

**Formula:**

$$
L_{req} = W_{block} \times SF \times (1 + G)
$$

**Substitution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{req} &= 45 \times 1.25 \times (1 + 0.15) \\
L_{req} &= 45 \times 1.25 \times 1.15 \\
L_{req} &= 56.25 \times 1.15 \\
L_{req} &= 64.6875\ \text{tonnes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-Step Breakdown:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Apply safety factor} &\rightarrow 45 \times 1.25 = 56.25\ T \\
\text{Step 2: Apply growth margin} &\rightarrow 56.25 \times 1.15 = 64.6875\ T \\
\text{Step 3: Round up to next standard} &\rightarrow 70\ T\ \text{crane (typical standard size)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Justification (KTU Standard Practice):**

The next commercially available standard crane capacity above 64.7 tonnes is typically **70 tonnes**. Selecting 70 T provides:
- Adequate margin for **uneven load distribution**.
- Allowance for **rigging and spreader beam weight** (typically 5–10% of block weight).
- Future flexibility to handle heavier blocks if the yard adopts larger block philosophy.

Therefore, a **70-tonne Goliath crane** is the KTU-aligned recommended selection.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deduction Points in KTU 2024 Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Skipping Units:** Students often write numerical answers without units (e.g., writing "64.6875" instead of "64.6875 tonnes"). KTU examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** for missing units.
>
> 2. **Confusing DWT with CGT:** Do not use Deadweight Tonnage (DWT) as a productivity metric — it only measures cargo capacity, not work content. Always justify using **CGT** for yard productivity.
>
> 3. **Wrong formula for launching force:** The launching force on a slipway uses **$\sin(\theta)$**, not $\cos(\theta)$. This is a recurring error worth **2 marks** in Part B questions.
>
> 4. **Missing Sketch:** For 7-mark questions on launching systems (slipway, dry dock, etc.), KTU valuation keys mandate a **neat sketch**. Omission typically costs **2 marks**.
>
> 5. **Ignoring Efficiency Factor:** When calculating yard throughput, students often use theoretical 100% efficiency. KTU standard requires the inclusion of the **efficiency factor $k$** (0.75 to 0.90).
>
> 6. **Confusing PEZ with PEZ:** Pre-Equipped Zone (PEZ) is the block-level outfitting concept. Some students write "Pre-Erection Zone" — this is **incorrect terminology** and results in mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exam — Module 1**

### Key Definitions
- **Ship Production System** = Infrastructure + Process + Planning + Organization.
- **CGT (Compensated Gross Tonnage)** = OECD-standardized productivity unit.
- **DWT (Deadweight Tonnage)** = Cargo capacity only (not productivity metric).
- **PEZ (Pre-Equipped Zone)** = Pre-outfitting at block level.
- **Block** = 3D hull assembly of 20–80 tonnes typical.
- **Panel** = 2D welded assembly of plates, ~5m × 10m.
- **Goliath Crane** = Heavy-lift crane spanning the building berth (300–1200 T capacity).

### Essential Formulas
- **Throughput:** $N_{annual} = N_b \cdot k \cdot \dfrac{T_a}{T_b}$
- **Required Berths:** $N_b = \dfrac{N_{target} \cdot T_b}{k \cdot T_a}$
- **Crane Capacity:** $L_{req} = W_{block} \times SF \times (1 + G)$
- **Launching Force:** $F_L = W_{ship} \cdot \sin(\theta) - F_{friction}$
- **Steel Scrap Rate:** $S\% = \dfrac{W_{scrap}}{W_{input}} \times 100$
- **CGT Formula:** $CGT = A \cdot (GT)^{B}$ (type-specific coefficients)
- **Berth Occupancy Ratio:** $BOR = \dfrac{T_{in-berth}}{T_{available}}$

### Systems Classification
- **Fixed Position:** Graving Dock, Building Dock, Pontoon Dock.
- **Movable Position:** Inclined Slipway, End Launching, Side Launching.
- **Floating:** Floating Dock, Semi-submersible Platform.

### Critical Numerical Benchmarks
- **1 CGT ≈ 14 man-hours** of average work content.
- **Safety Factor for crane** = **1.25** (KTU standard).
- **Efficiency Factor range** = **0.75 – 0.90** depending on yard maturity.
- **Steel Scrap Rate benchmark** = **10–14%** for modern yards.
- **Block weight** = **20–80 tonnes** for typical commercial vessels.
- **VLCC berth time** = **3 months on berth**, **12 months total** build time.

### Production Stages (Sequential Memory Aid — **"S-P-P-B-O"**)
**S**teel preparation → **P**anel fabrication → **P**re-outfitting (PEZ) → **B**lock erection → **O**utfitting & Launching.

### Modern Trends (KTU 2024 Update)
- **Digital Twin** shipyard (real-time 3D virtual replica).
- **Industry 5.0** integration (human–robot collaboration).
- **Green Shipbuilding** (hydrogen-fuel vessels, electric welding replacing gas).
- **Additive Manufacturing** (3D-printed ship parts for prototypes).
- **AI-driven scheduling** (replacing Gantt charts with neural networks).

### Two-Liner Exam Mantra
> *"From steel plate to sailing ship — every block tells the story of a system designed for efficiency, safety, and precision."*

This concludes the comprehensive notes on **Ship Production Systems** for KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 of **PENST633 – Ship Production Management**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Subsystems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST633 - SHIP PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Subsystems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ship Production Subsystems** are defined as the **integrated, interdependent functional units** into which the total shipbuilding activity is decomposed for the purpose of **planning, control, scheduling, and execution**. Each subsystem transforms specific inputs (drawings, materials, labor, information) into defined outputs (fabricated blocks, sub-assemblies, completed ship sections) while maintaining interfaces with the other subsystems through standardized **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** nodes.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a *subsystem* is not merely an organizational chart element — it is a **closed-loop control unit** with measurable performance indicators (cycle time, throughput, defect rate, cost per ton).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of building a **passenger car in a modern assembly plant**:

- You don't build the whole car at one workstation.
- A **chassis subsystem** stamps and welds the frame.
- A **powertrain subsystem** assembles the engine and gearbox.
- A **paint subsystem** coats the body.
- A **final assembly subsystem** integrates everything.

Each station has its own inputs (parts), process (robot/operator), and outputs (sub-assembly). They are **linked by a moving conveyor (the Master Schedule)** and **synchronized by a central control room (Production Planning)**.

**A shipyard is exactly the same — only the conveyor is the water, the workstations are the building berths/plates, and the product weighs 30,000 tonnes.**

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Used in Subsystem Evaluation

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard hull steel conversion factor:** ~**1.05 tonnes of steel per 1.00 tonne of net ship weight** (accounts for weld metal + cutting losses).
> - **Block weight range in modern modular yards:** **80 – 600 tonnes** per block.
> - **Outfitting-to-hull-work ratio (man-hours):** typically **0.8 : 1 to 1.2 : 1** in modern yards.
> - **Critical Path drag coefficient** (planning): **CPM Float = LS − ES**.

---

## 1.4 Why Subsystem Decomposition? (KTU Syllabus Hook)

The KTU 2024 Module 1 outcome expects the student to:

> *CO1: "Explain the organizational structure and functional subsystems of a shipyard and their inter-relationships in ship production."*

A ship cannot be built as a single monolithic operation because:
1. The **scale** exceeds any single workstation's capacity.
2. **Specialization** (welding, machining, electrical, outfitting) requires segregated skills.
3. **Parallel processing** is essential to meet delivery dates.
4. **Accountability and cost control** require bounded units.

Hence, the shipyard is decomposed into **subsystems**, each with clear deliverables.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Canonical Subsystems of Ship Production

The KTU syllabus recognizes the following **eight canonical subsystems**. Each is treated as a discrete management entity.

### 2.1.1 Design & Engineering Subsystem
- **Function:** Converts the *Ship Specification* into production-ready drawings, 3D models (CAD/CAM), and Bills of Materials (BOM).
- **Inputs:** Owner requirements, class society rules (IRS, LR, BV, DNV), regulatory constraints (SOLAS, MARPOL).
- **Outputs:** General Arrangement (GA), Lines Plan, Structural Drawings, Nesting Plans, Cutting Sketches.
- **Key Tool:** Product Model / 3D CAD database linked to **CAD/CAM nesting** software.

### 2.1.2 Material Procurement & Control Subsystem
- **Function:** Sourcing, inspection, warehousing, and issue of steel, outfit items, and consumables.
- **Inputs:** BOM from Design subsystem.
- **Outputs:** Steel plates, sections, pipes, valves, electrical cables — *right item, right time, right place*.
- **Key Metric:** Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITOR).

### 2.1.3 Plate & Section Preparation Subsystem (Steel Stockyard)
- **Function:** Shot blasting, priming, cutting, marking, and forming of plates and profiles.
- **Inputs:** Steel plates/profiles from stores.
- **Outputs:** Cut and shaped pieces ready for sub-assembly.
- **Key Technology:** **CNC plasma/oxy cutting**, **plate bending rolls**, **section bending machines**.

### 2.1.4 Sub-Assembly & Pre-Fabrication Subsystem
- **Function:** Joining of cut parts into small units (panels, stiffener sub-assemblies, brackets, foundations).
- **Inputs:** Cut pieces, welding consumables.
- **Outputs:** Flat panels (10–40 t), curved panels, unit brackets.
- **Key Concept:** *Tonnage flows UP from sub-assembly → panel → block → grand block → ship.*

### 2.1.5 Block Assembly Subsystem (Hull Construction Berth)
- **Function:** Joining panels and sub-assemblies into **3D blocks** of 80–600 tonnes.
- **Inputs:** Panels, sub-assemblies, block-jig drawings.
- **Outputs:** Hull blocks ready for erection.
- **Key Principle:** **Unit Construction Method** — maximum pre-outfitting at the block stage.

### 2.1.6 Hull Erection / Grand Assembly Subsystem
- **Function:** Joining blocks on the building berth (or in dry dock / on slipway) to form the complete hull.
- **Inputs:** Completed blocks, special steel, alignment data.
- **Outputs:** Complete welded hull ready for launching.
- **Key Methods:** *Berth Building*, *Section Building*, *Dock Building*, *Out-of-Dock Building*.

### 2.1.7 Outfitting Subsystem
- **Function:** Installation of machinery, piping, electrical, HVAC, accommodation, and safety equipment.
- **Inputs:** Outfit parts, foundation drawings, hull (at block or post-launch stage).
- **Outputs:** Fully outfitted vessel pre-commissioning.
- **Sub-classification:**
  - **Pre-outfitting** (done at panel/block stage — preferred).
  - **Post-outfitting** (done after launching).

### 2.1.8 Launching, Fitting-out, Testing & Trials Subsystem
- **Function:** Moving the hull to water, completing remaining outfitting, dock/sea trials, and delivery.
- **Inputs:** Completed hull, final outfit items, crew.
- **Outputs:** Delivered, classed, and registered vessel.
- **Key Metrics:** Inclining experiment result, Speed–Power curve, Sea Trial report.

---

## 2.2 Inter-Subsystem Interfaces (The "Glue")

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Subsystems do not operate in isolation. KTU examiners frequently test the **interfaces** between subsystems.

| Interface | From → To | What is Transferred |
|-----------|-----------|---------------------|
| Design ↔ Procurement | Engineering → Material | BOM, delivery schedule |
| Procurement ↔ Steel Yard | Material → Plate Prep | Issued plates, sections |
| Plate Prep ↔ Sub-Assembly | Steel Yard → Assembly | Cut/formed parts |
| Sub-Assembly ↔ Block | Assembly → Block | Panels, sub-units |
| Block ↔ Erection | Block → Berth | Completed blocks |
| Outfitting ↔ Block | Outfit → Block | Foundations, pipes at pre-outfitting |
| Erection ↔ Launching | Berth → Water | Hull |
| Trials → Delivery | Testing → Owner | Classed vessel |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas appear in KTU University Exam numerical/analytical problems. Master these.

| Formula / Relation | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|--------------------|---------|--------------|
| $W_{purchase} = W_{ship} \times k_{conv}$ | Steel purchase weight = Ship lightweight × conversion factor | tonnes |
| $k_{conv} \approx 1.05$ to $1.15$ | Conversion factor (waste + weld metal) | dimensionless |
| $T_{block} = \dfrac{W_{block}}{P_{weld}}$ | Block production time | days |
| $P_{weld}$ | Welding productivity (tonnes of weld deposit/day) | t/day |
| $N_{blocks} = \dfrac{W_{hull}}{W_{block,avg}}$ | Number of blocks required | blocks |
| $C_{cycle} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$ | Total cycle time = sum of subsystem times | days |
| $Float_i = LS_i - ES_i$ | Critical Path slack for activity *i* | days |
| $R_{OH} = \dfrac{MH_{outfit}}{MH_{hull}}$ | Outfitting-to-Hull man-hour ratio | dimensionless |
| $\eta_{util} = \dfrac{T_{productive}}{T_{available}} \times 100\%$ | Subsystem utilization efficiency | percent |
| $\text{ITOR} = \dfrac{COGS}{Avg.\ Inventory}$ | Inventory turnover (procurement subsystem) | per year |

> **Critical Note on LaTeX:** All absolute-value and conditional expressions are written using `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The subsystem concept is the **backbone of ERP systems** used in shipyards (e.g., **FORAN, AVEVA Marine, Tribon, CATIA, NAPA**). Each subsystem corresponds to a software module:

- **Design Subsystem** ↔ CAD/Marine module
- **Material Subsystem** ↔ Procurement/Inventory module
- **Plate Prep Subsystem** ↔ Nesting/CNC module
- **Production Subsystem** ↔ MES (Manufacturing Execution System)
- **Outfitting Subsystem** ↔ MRO (Maintenance, Repair, Operations) database

Understanding subsystems therefore gives the B.Tech student **direct employability** in shipyards such as Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock, GRSE, HSL, and global yards like Hyundai Heavy Industries, Samsung Heavy Industries, and Daewoo.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Subsystem × Activity × Output

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is **directly examinable** as a 7-mark analytical question in KTU ESE.

The complete ship production activity can be represented as an **N × M matrix** where each row is a subsystem and each column is a process stage. The cell entries represent the **deliverable/output** at that intersection.

$$
\mathbf{S} = \begin{bmatrix}
s_{11} & s_{12} & \cdots & s_{1m} \\
s_{21} & s_{22} & \cdots & s_{2m} \\
\vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
s_{n1} & s_{n2} & \cdots & s_{nm}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Where:

- $s_{ij}$ = the **j-th deliverable** of the **i-th subsystem**.
- $n$ = number of subsystems (here, $n = 8$).
- $m$ = number of process stages.

For the **canonical KTU 8-subsystem model** with $m = 5$ stages (Prepare → Assemble → Erect → Outfit → Trial), the matrix evaluates to:

$$
\mathbf{S} = \begin{bmatrix}
\text{Drawings} & \text{BOM} & \text{Nesting} & \text{N/A} & \text{N/A} \\
\text{Steel} & \text{Issued} & \text{Delivered} & \text{N/A} & \text{N/A} \\
\text{Plates} & \text{Cut} & \text{Formed} & \text{N/A} & \text{N/A} \\
\text{Parts} & \text{Panels} & \text{Sub-units} & \text{N/A} & \text{N/A} \\
\text{Units} & \text{Blocks} & \text{Grand Blocks} & \text{N/A} & \text{N/A} \\
\text{Hull Sec.} & \text{Complete Hull} & \text{Aligned} & \text{N/A} & \text{N/A} \\
\text{Found.} & \text{Pipe/Elec.} & \text{Pre-outfit} & \text{Post-outfit} & \text{N/A} \\
\text{Launch} & \text{Fit-out} & \text{Dock Trial} & \text{Sea Trial} & \text{Delivery}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

> Cells marked **N/A** mean that subsystem does not participate in that stage — a strong indicator for **non-Critical-Path activities** in project scheduling.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Number of Blocks Required for a Given Vessel

> **KTU-Style Problem Setup:**
> A bulk carrier has a lightweight (hull steel) of $W_{hull} = 12{,}000$ tonnes. The yard practices unit construction with an average block weight of $W_{block,avg} = 300$ tonnes.

**Step 1 — State the governing relation.**

$$
N_{blocks} = \frac{W_{hull}}{W_{block,avg}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
N_{blocks} = \frac{12{,}000\ \text{t}}{300\ \text{t/block}}
$$

**Step 3 — Perform the division explicitly.**

$$
N_{blocks} = \frac{12{,}000}{300} = \frac{120}{3} = 40\ \text{blocks}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply engineering correction factor.**

In practice, superstructure, stern, and bow are often broken into smaller units. A correction factor $k_{corr} = 1.10$ to $1.20$ accounts for these additional sub-blocks.

$$
N_{blocks,actual} = N_{blocks} \times k_{corr}
$$

$$
N_{blocks,actual} = 40 \times 1.15 = 46\ \text{blocks}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

The Production Planning subsystem must therefore schedule **46 erection tasks** on the building berth/dock, plus their corresponding outfitting tasks in parallel.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Critical Path Float for the Erection Subsystem

> **Scenario:** The Erection subsystem has three sequential activities:
> - $A_1$: Lift & position bow block — duration $t_1 = 4$ days
> - $A_2$: Weld bow block — duration $t_2 = 6$ days
> - $A_3$: NDT (Non-Destructive Testing) — duration $t_3 = 2$ days

The yard manager wants to know how much **float** (slack) exists if the contractual milestone allows **15 days** for the entire bow section.

**Step 1 — Calculate earliest start and finish (forward pass).**

$$
ES_1 = 0,\qquad EF_1 = ES_1 + t_1 = 0 + 4 = 4\ \text{days}
$$

$$
ES_2 = EF_1 = 4,\qquad EF_2 = ES_2 + t_2 = 4 + 6 = 10\ \text{days}
$$

$$
ES_3 = EF_2 = 10,\qquad EF_3 = ES_3 + t_3 = 10 + 2 = 12\ \text{days}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate latest start and finish (backward pass from milestone $T = 15$).**

$$
LF_3 = T = 15,\qquad LS_3 = LF_3 - t_3 = 15 - 2 = 13\ \text{days}
$$

$$
LF_2 = LS_3 = 13,\qquad LS_2 = LF_2 - t_2 = 13 - 6 = 7\ \text{days}
$$

$$
LF_1 = LS_2 = 7,\qquad LS_1 = LF_1 - t_1 = 7 - 4 = 3\ \text{days}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute float for each activity.**

$$
F_1 = LS_1 - ES_1 = 3 - 0 = 3\ \text{days}
$$

$$
F_2 = LS_2 - ES_2 = 7 - 4 = 3\ \text{days}
$$

$$
F_3 = LS_3 - ES_3 = 13 - 10 = 3\ \text{days}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision.**

All activities have **identical float of 3 days**, meaning this sub-chain is **on a near-critical path**. The Production Manager can delay any single activity by up to 3 days without breaching the milestone, but **delays stack additively**. This is the **Subsystem Risk Insight** that examiners reward.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Insight: Outfitting-to-Hull Ratio as a Yard Sophistication Index

$$
R_{OH} = \frac{MH_{outfit}}{MH_{hull}}
$$

- $R_{OH} < 0.7$: **Outfitting-deficient** yard (suffers from post-launch bottleneck).
- $0.8 \leq R_{OH} \leq 1.2$: **Balanced modern yard** (target for Indian & Korean yards).
- $R_{OH} > 1.4$: **Outfitting-heavy** (typical of naval/complex offshore vessels).

Modern Indian yards (Cochin Shipyard Limited) target $R_{OH} \approx 1.0$ for commercial vessels.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Subsystem Interaction Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **eight subsystems** and their **primary information/material flows**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Design and Engineering Subsystem] -->|BOM and Drawings| B[Material Procurement Subsystem]
    A -->|Cutting Sketches| C[Plate and Section Preparation Subsystem]
    B -->|Steel Plates and Profiles| C
    C -->|Cut and Formed Parts| D[Sub-Assembly and Pre-Fabrication Subsystem]
    D -->|Panels and Sub-Units| E[Block Assembly Subsystem]
    F[Outfitting Subsystem] -->|Foundations and Pre-Equipment| E
    E -->|Completed Blocks| G[Hull Erection Subsystem]
    G -->|Welded Hull| H[Launching and Trials Subsystem]
    F -->|Post-Outfit Items| H
    H -->|Delivered Vessel| I[Owner and Class Society]
    J[Production Planning and Control] -.->|Master Schedule| A
    J -.->|Delivery Plan| B
    J -.->|Cutting Priority| C
    J -.->|Erect Sequence| D
    J -.->|Block Sequence| E
    J -.->|Erection Window| G
    K[Quality Assurance Subsystem] -.->|Inspection Calls| C
    K -.->|NDT Reports| D
    K -.->|Block Acceptance| E
    K -.->|Class Surveys| H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Solid arrows** represent *physical material flow*. **Dashed arrows** represent *information / control flow*. The **Production Planning subsystem** (J) acts as the **central nervous system** orchestrating all others.

---

## 4.2 Subsystem Hierarchy of Outputs (Bottom-Up Tonnage Build)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Cut Plate] --> P2[Flat Panel 10-40 t]
    P2 --> P3[Curved Panel 30-80 t]
    P3 --> P4[Block 80-600 t]
    P4 --> P5[Grand Block 1000-3000 t]
    P5 --> P6[Ship Section]
    P6 --> P7[Complete Hull]
    P7 --> P8[Delivered Vessel]
```

> This is the classic **"pyramid of build"** taught in every ship production textbook and frequently asked in KTU Module 1 viva.

---

## 4.3 Subsystem × Responsibility Allocation (RACI-like Matrix)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O[Owner / Customer] -->|Contract| MGT[Shipyard Management]
    MGT --> DSN[Design Subsystem]
    MGT --> MAT[Material Subsystem]
    MGT --> PRD[Production Subsystem]
    MGT --> QUA[Quality Subsystem]
    MGT --> ADM[Administration and HR Subsystem]
    MGT --> FIN[Finance and Costing Subsystem]
```

> **Exam Tip:** When asked *"List the subsystems of a shipyard,"* always include these **administrative subsystems** (Finance, HR, Administration) in addition to the **technical 8** — many students lose marks by omission.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1]**

### **Q1. Define a "subsystem" in the context of ship production management. Give two examples.**
*(3 Marks — Blooms Level: Remember/Understand)*

**Model Answer:**
A subsystem is an **independent, functionally self-contained unit** of ship production activity that converts specific inputs into defined outputs while exchanging information and materials with other subsystems through standardized interfaces.

**Examples:**
1. **Plate & Section Preparation Subsystem** — converts raw steel plates/profiles into cut, formed parts.
2. **Outfitting Subsystem** — converts purchased outfit items into installed machinery, piping, and electrical systems on the hull.

*(Expected answer length: 3–4 sentences + 2 examples. Full marks = 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for examples.)*

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between pre-outfitting and post-outfitting. Why is pre-outfitting preferred?**
*(3 Marks — Blooms Level: Understand)*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Pre-Outfitting | Post-Outfitting |
|--------|----------------|-----------------|
| Stage | At block / panel stage | After launching |
| Accessibility | Excellent (open workshop) | Restricted (confined spaces) |
| Productivity | High | Low |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Preferred? | **Yes** | Only for unavoidable items |

**Why pre-outfitting is preferred:**
1. Better **working conditions** → higher productivity.
2. Reduced work at height / confined space → improved **safety**.
3. Reduces **overall project duration** by parallelizing outfitting with hull erection (a core principle of *unit construction*).

*(1 mark for differentiation table, 2 marks for justification.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — ESE Style, Module 1]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Type, Module 1]**

#### **(a) Explain with a neat sketch the eight canonical subsystems of ship production management. Describe their interfaces briefly. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution Outline:**

The **eight canonical subsystems** are:

1. **Design & Engineering**
2. **Material Procurement & Control**
3. **Plate & Section Preparation**
4. **Sub-Assembly & Pre-Fabrication**
5. **Block Assembly**
6. **Hull Erection**
7. **Outfitting**
8. **Launching, Fitting-out & Trials**

**Interfaces (must draw block diagram):**
- Design → Procurement: BOM.
- Procurement → Plate Prep: Issued steel.
- Plate Prep → Sub-Assembly: Cut parts.
- Sub-Assembly → Block Assembly: Panels.
- Block Assembly → Hull Erection: Blocks.
- Outfitting ↔ Block/Erection: Foundations & equipment.
- Hull Erection → Launching: Completed hull.
- Launching → Trials → Delivery.

> **Sketch requirement:** A labeled block diagram showing all 8 boxes with arrows marking material and information flow. (3 marks reserved for the diagram.)

**Mark Split (typical KTU valuation):**
- Naming 8 subsystems correctly: **3 marks**
- Brief function of each: **1 mark** (concise)
- Diagram: **2 marks**
- Interface explanation: **1 mark**

#### **(b) The lightweight of a vessel is 9,000 tonnes. The yard practices unit construction with an average block weight of 250 tonnes. If the conversion factor is 1.10 and correction factor for superstructure is 1.15, calculate (i) the steel to be purchased, and (ii) the number of blocks. (7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Given:**
- $W_{ship} = 9{,}000\ t$
- $W_{block,avg} = 250\ t$
- $k_{conv} = 1.10$
- $k_{corr} = 1.15$

**Part (i) — Steel to be purchased:**

$$
W_{purchase} = W_{ship} \times k_{conv}
$$

$$
W_{purchase} = 9{,}000 \times 1.10
$$

$$
W_{purchase} = 9{,}900\ \text{tonnes}
$$

**Part (ii) — Number of blocks:**

First, compute base block count:

$$
N_{base} = \frac{W_{hull}}{W_{block,avg}} = \frac{9{,}000}{250}
$$

$$
N_{base} = 36\ \text{blocks}
$$

Apply correction factor:

$$
N_{actual} = N_{base} \times k_{corr} = 36 \times 1.15
$$

$$
N_{actual} = 41.4 \approx 42\ \text{blocks (rounded up)}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- **(i)** Steel to purchase = **9,900 tonnes**
- **(ii)** Number of blocks required = **42 blocks**

**Mark Split:** Formula (2) + Substitution (1) + Calculation (2) + Final answer with units (1) + Bonus: Comment on rounding logic (1).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Type, Module 1]**

#### **(a) Discuss the role of the Production Planning & Control (PPC) subsystem as the central coordinator of all other subsystems in a modern shipyard. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **PPC subsystem** acts as the **brain of the shipyard**. Its functions include:

1. **Master Scheduling:** Defines the *block erection sequence*, *milestones*, and *delivery date* based on contract and yard capacity.
2. **Material Requirement Planning (MRP):** Triggers procurement so that steel arrives *just-in-time* for cutting.
3. **Capacity Planning:** Allocates berths, cranes, and workforce to subsystems.
4. **Progress Monitoring:** Tracks **Earned Value**, **Physical % Complete**, and **Man-hours Spent vs. Budget**.
5. **Coordination Role:** Resolves conflicts such as:
   - Two subsystems needing the same crane simultaneously.
   - Block ready but its outfit items delayed → re-sequencing.
6. **Information Hub:** Publishes weekly look-ahead plans, delay alerts, and recovery actions.
7. **Risk Management:** Maintains **float** on non-critical paths to absorb disruptions.

> **Examiner's expected keyword list:** Master Schedule, MRP, Just-in-Time, Look-ahead, Float, Earned Value, Critical Path.

**Mark Split:** Identification of PPC as coordinator (2) + listing of functions (3) + one practical example of conflict resolution (2).

#### **(b) The following activities are part of the Outfitting subsystem. Construct a network diagram and identify the critical path. Also compute the float for non-critical activities. (7 Marks)**

| Activity | Predecessor | Duration (Days) |
|----------|-------------|-----------------|
| A (Pipe fabrication) | — | 5 |
| B (Valve installation) | A | 3 |
| C (Cable laying) | A | 4 |
| D (Equipment mounting) | A | 6 |
| E (System hook-up) | B, C, D | 4 |
| F (Testing) | E | 3 |

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Forward Pass (ES, EF):**

$$
ES_A = 0,\quad EF_A = 5
$$

$$
ES_B = EF_A = 5,\quad EF_B = 8
$$

$$
ES_C = EF_A = 5,\quad EF_C = 9
$$

$$
ES_D = EF_A = 5,\quad EF_D = 11
$$

$$
ES_E = \max(EF_B,\ EF_C,\ EF_D) = \max(8,\ 9,\ 11) = 11
$$

$$
EF_E = 11 + 4 = 15
$$

$$
ES_F = EF_E = 15,\quad EF_F = 18
$$

**Project Duration = 18 days.**

**Step 2 — Backward Pass (LS, LF):**

$$
LF_F = 18,\quad LS_F = 15
$$

$$
LF_E = LS_F = 15,\quad LS_E = 11
$$

$$
LF_B = LS_E = 11,\quad LS_B = 8
$$

$$
LF_C = LS_E = 11,\quad LS_C = 7
$$

$$
LF_D = LS_E = 11,\quad LS_D = 5
$$

$$
LF_A = \min(LS_B,\ LS_C,\ LS_D) = \min(8,\ 7,\ 5) = 5
$$

$$
LS_A = LF_A - 5 = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Floats:**

$$
F_A = 0,\quad F_B = 8-5 = 3,\quad F_C = 7-5 = 2
$$

$$
F_D = 5-5 = 0,\quad F_E = 0,\quad F_F = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Critical Path:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Critical Path: } A \rightarrow D \rightarrow E \rightarrow F,\ \text{Duration} = 18\ \text{days}}
$$

**Step 5 — Non-Critical Floats:**
- $F_B = 3$ days (Pipe-to-Valve can slip 3 days)
- $F_C = 2$ days (Cable laying can slip 2 days)

**Mark Split:** Forward pass (2) + Backward pass (2) + Critical path identification (2) + Float calculation (1).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Listing fewer than 8 subsystems:** KTU Module 1 strictly expects *all eight* canonical subsystems + mention of administrative subsystems (Finance, HR, QA). Omitting **Launching & Trials** is the #1 mistake.
>
> 2. **Confusing "subsystem" with "department":** A subsystem is a **functional flow unit**, not just an organizational chart box. Examiners penalize answers that read like a phone directory.
>
> 3. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark questions:** A neat labeled block diagram is worth **2–3 marks** by itself. Students who write paragraphs without a diagram cap their score.
>
> 4. **Formula errors in numericals:** Using $W_{ship}$ directly instead of applying $k_{conv}$ before computing $W_{purchase}$ is the single most common algebraic error.
>
> 5. **Ignoring units in final answer:** Always write **tonnes**, **days**, **blocks** — a numerical answer without units gets only partial credit.
>
> 6. **In Part B (b), failing to mention rounding logic:** When block count = 41.4, writing "41 blocks" is acceptable but writing "42 blocks with rounding-up justification" earns full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Subsystems**

- **Definition:** A subsystem is a *functional, input-output, closed-loop unit* of ship production.
- **The Eight Canonical Subsystems (in order of material flow):**
  1. Design & Engineering
  2. Material Procurement & Control
  3. Plate & Section Preparation (Steel Yard)
  4. Sub-Assembly & Pre-Fabrication
  5. Block Assembly
  6. Hull Erection
  7. Outfitting (Pre + Post)
  8. Launching, Trials & Delivery
- **Central Coordinator:** Production Planning & Control (PPC) subsystem.
- **Quality Cross-cutter:** QA subsystem interfaces with *every* other subsystem.
- **Key Conversions:**
  - $W_{purchase} = W_{ship} \times k_{conv}$, with $k_{conv} \approx 1.05$ to $1.15$.
  - $N_{blocks,actual} = \dfrac{W_{hull}}{W_{block,avg}} \times k_{corr}$
- **Modern Yard Target:** $R_{OH} \approx 1.0$ for commercial vessels.
- **Block Weight Range:** **80 – 600 tonnes** typical; up to **3,000 tonnes** in mega-blocks.
- **Critical Path Concepts:** $F_i = LS_i - ES_i$; **Critical Path has zero float**.
- **Tonnage Pyramid:** Plate → Panel → Block → Grand Block → Ship Section → Hull → Vessel.
- **Outfitting is preferred at the block stage** (pre-outfitting) — better productivity, safety, and schedule.
- **Subsystems are software-mapped in ERP/MES** — direct industry relevance.
- **Always include a labeled diagram** in any 7-mark subsystem question.
- **Always state units** in numerical answers.
- **Mention both technical and administrative subsystems** when asked to "list subsystems of a shipyard."

> *End of KTU Module 1 Notes — Subsystems. Best of luck for your ESE preparation!*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comprehensive System Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST633 - SHIP PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Comprehensive System Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Comprehensive System Model (CSM)** in ship production management is a holistic, integrated analytical framework that represents the entire shipbuilding enterprise as a unified, dynamic, multi-input and multi-output **socio-technical system**. It captures the interactions among design, engineering, material logistics, fabrication, assembly, outfitting, commissioning, quality assurance, human resources, finance, and information flow — all governed by feedback control loops that drive continuous improvement in cost, quality, and time (the **Iron Triangle** of shipbuilding).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PENST633) context, the Comprehensive System Model is positioned as the *macro-architecture* that subsumes the traditional Ship Production Process (SPP), the Group Technology (GT) classification, the **One-Ship-One-Unit (OSOU)** philosophy, and modern **Integrated Product, Process, and Resource Model (IPPRM)** approaches advocated by IACS, IMO, and classification societies.

### 1.2 The Five Pillars of the Comprehensive System Model

The CSM rests on five interdependent pillars, each of which forms an independent module of analysis in the KTU syllabus:

1. **Input Subsystem** – Design data, raw materials (steel plates, profiles, pipes, electrical cables, paint), consumables, drawings, Bill of Materials (BOM), labor, capital, and management directives.
2. **Processing / Transformation Subsystem** – Cutting, forming, welding, cleaning, painting, block erection, pre-outfitting, launching, afloat outfitting, and sea trials.
3. **Output Subsystem** – The delivered, classed, and warranted vessel, plus documentation (As-Built drawings, manuals, certificates).
4. **Control Subsystem** – Quality (ISO 9001), Safety (ISO 45001, OHSAS), Environmental (ISO 14001), Progress monitoring (EVM – Earned Value Management), and Class society survey.
5. **Feedback Subsystem** – Post-delivery performance data, warranty claims, lessons learned, and continuous improvement loops (Kaizen, Six-Sigma, PDCA cycle).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In Module 1, students must be able to *draw, label, and explain* the Comprehensive System Model block diagram and identify at least **eight (8) input variables** and **six (6) output variables** of a typical shipyard production system. This question is a guaranteed **Part A (3 marks)** in the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body Shipyard

Imagine the shipyard as a **human body**:

- **Skeleton** → the steel hull structure, block foundations, slipways, and dry docks.
- **Muscles** → the workforce (fitters, welders, electricians, painters).
- **Nervous System** → information technology, ERP systems, CAD/CAM, scheduling software, and management directives.
- **Circulatory System** → material handling — cranes, transporters, conveyors, AGVs, and the supply chain pipeline.
- **Brain** → the central planning and design office (Central Design Authority).
- **Senses / Reflexes** → the Quality Control department, NDT (Non-Destructive Testing) laboratories, and Class surveyors.

Just as a human body cannot function if any one system fails, the shipyard cannot deliver a ship on time, within budget, and to specification if any subsystem of the CSM breaks down. This is the **holistic philosophy** that distinguishes modern ship production management from the traditional fragmented shop-floor approach.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in the CSM

- **Standard Hour (SH)** – The fundamental unit of work, equal to the amount of work a qualified worker performs in one hour under standard conditions. **1 SH = 1 man-hour of qualified, motivated, average-pace labor.**
- **Work Content (W)** – Total standard hours required to complete a ship, often ranging from **200,000 SH** for small tugs to over **3,000,000 SH** for VLCCs.
- **Production Rate (P)** – Standard Hours completed per unit time, typically measured in **SH/week** or **SH/month**.
- **Manpower (M)** – Number of workers, related to production rate by **M = P / (efficiency × hours-per-week)**.
- **Workload (WL)** – Total work content ÷ calendar duration.
- **Productivity Index** – Output (DWT or CGT) per Standard Hour.

> [!NOTE]
> **Blooms Level (L1 – Remember):** The definitions of **Input, Process, Output, Control, and Feedback** are the bedrock of any ship production management viva. Memorize the five pillars *word-for-word*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic of the Comprehensive System Model

The CSM is governed by a logical sequence of operations that can be decomposed into the following structured steps:

1. **Contract Intake & Design Freeze** – The shipowner’s requirements are translated into a contracted specification, General Arrangement (GA), and Lines Plan. The *design freeze* marks the boundary between the pre-production and production phases.
2. **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) Generation** – The complete ship is decomposed into **Zones → Units → Blocks → Sub-assemblies → Parts**. This is the *Group Technology* principle.
3. **Process Planning & Routing** – Each part is assigned a process plan: *cut → form → weld → blast → paint → fit*. Routing determines which workshop, machine, and crew handles each operation.
4. **Capacity Loading & Resource Allocation** – The yard’s workshops, berths, cranes, and workforce are matched against the WBS. The **Critical Path Method (CPM)** identifies the longest chain of dependent activities.
5. **Material Procurement & Just-in-Time (JIT) Delivery** – Steel plates and profiles are cut, kitted, and delivered to the assembly line with minimum inventory. **Tonnage throughput** is the key KPI.
6. **Block Fabrication (Stage 1) on the Platen / Sub-Assembly Line** – Small sub-assemblies (panels, stiffener sub-units) are built.
7. **Block Fabrication (Stage 2) on the Panel Line / Block Assembly Line** – Panels are welded into large 3D blocks (up to **400–600 tonnes** for modern yards).
8. **Block Erection on the Slipway / Building Dock** – Blocks are joined in a controlled sequence — typically *Islands* method or *Pioneer* method.
9. **Pre-Outfitting (POF)** – Piping, HVAC, electrical cables, and machinery are installed *into* blocks *before* erection to reduce work-at-height. Modern POF ratio target: **> 70 %** of total outfitting work.
10. **Launching** – By slipway, graving dock, floating dock, or Semi-Submersible Heavy-Lift Vessel (SSHLV).
11. **Afloat Outfitting & Mooring Trials** – Equipment, funnels, masts, and accommodation are installed at the fitting-out berth.
12. **Sea Trials & Class Survey** – Speed, maneuverability, noise-vibration, and class requirements are verified.
13. **Delivery & Hand-Over** – As-Built documentation, spare parts, and warranties are transferred.
14. **Feedback Loop (After-Sales)** – Performance data, claims, and lessons learned feed back into the yard’s process improvement cycle.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam essential**. Master the variables, units, and the *sign convention* (positive = inflow, negative = outflow).

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{W}{T}$ | Production Rate (SH/time) | $W$ = Work content [SH]; $T$ = Calendar time [weeks] |
| 2 | $M = \dfrac{P}{e \cdot h}$ | Manning Level | $e$ = Efficiency (decimal, e.g., 0.85); $h$ = Hours per week [h/wk] |
| 3 | $WL = \dfrac{W_{total}}{T_{build}}$ | Workload | $W_{total}$ [SH]; $T_{build}$ [weeks] |
| 4 | $Y_n = Y_1 \cdot n^{b}$ | **Learning Curve Equation** (Wright’s Law) | $Y_n$ = Hours for $n^{th}$ unit; $Y_1$ = First-unit hours; $b = \log_2(\text{slope})$ |
| 5 | $b = \dfrac{\log(\text{slope})}{\log 2}$ | Learning curve slope parameter | Slope is the fraction of hours required when production doubles (e.g., 0.8 for 80 % curve) |
| 6 | $C_{total} = C_{material} + C_{labour} + C_{overhead}$ | Ship Building Cost | $C_{material}$, $C_{labour}$, $C_{overhead}$ in currency units |
| 7 | $C_{labour} = M \cdot W \cdot r \cdot (1 + B)$ | Labour Cost | $r$ = wage rate [currency/SH]; $B$ = benefits/factor |
| 8 | $C_{unit} = \dfrac{C_{total}}{CGT}$ | Cost per CGT (Compensated Gross Tonnage) | CGT = dimensional measure of shipbuilding work |
| 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{W_{actual}}{W_{planned}} \times 100\,\%$ | Efficiency / Productivity | Decimal ratio |
| 10 | $T_{critical} = \sum T_{CP}$ | Critical Path Duration | Sum of activities on the longest path |
| 11 | $FT = T_{earliest\,start} + D - ES_{successor}$ | Float (Slack) Calculation | $D$ = activity duration; $ES$ = earliest start of successor |
| 12 | $POF_{ratio} = \dfrac{SH_{pre-outfit}}{SH_{total\,outfit}} \times 100\,\%$ | Pre-Outfitting Ratio | Higher = better integration |
| 13 | $KPI_{on-time} = \dfrac{N_{on-time}}{N_{total}} \times 100\,\%$ | Delivery Performance KPI | Number of ships delivered on/before contract date |
| 14 | $C_{\text{launching}} = k \cdot W_{ship}^{2/3}$ | Launching Cost Scaling (power law) | $k$ = yard-specific constant; $W_{ship}$ = ship displacement |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Trick:** Notice that the learning curve equation $Y_n = Y_1 \cdot n^{b}$ is often tested with the *doubling rule*: when production doubles, the time becomes $Y_1 \cdot 2^{b} = Y_1 \cdot \text{slope}$. For a **80 % learning curve**, the 2nd unit takes 80 % of the 1st, the 4th takes 64 %, the 8th takes 51.2 %, and so on.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of the CSM

The Comprehensive System Model is not merely academic. In industry, it is used to:

- **Capacity Planning:** A yard considering a 2-vessel-per-year ramp-up uses the CSM to estimate additional man-hours, steel throughput, dock occupancy, and capex requirements.
- **Risk Identification:** FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) is overlaid on the CSM to identify single-point failures (e.g., a single dry dock outage).
- **Lean Manufacturing Implementation:** The CSM reveals the *value stream* and highlights waste (muda) — over-production, waiting, transport, inappropriate processing, inventory, motion, and defects (the **7 Wastes** of Lean).
- **Digital Twin Development:** Modern yards (e.g., Hyundai Heavy Industries, Fincantieri, Cochin Shipyard) build a **digital twin** of the CSM, integrating IoT sensors on every block to predict schedule slips in real time.
- **Make-or-Buy Decisions:** Using the CSM, management decides whether to fabricate pipes in-house or procure from a vendor (Original Equipment Manufacturer — OEM).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Production Rate and Manning Level Relationship

**Given:**
- A ship has a total work content $W = 600{,}000$ SH.
- Contractual build time $T = 80$ weeks.
- Worker efficiency $e = 0.85$ (i.e., 85 % of standard).
- Standard working hours per week $h = 44$ hours (typical Indian shipyard, 5.5 days × 8 hours).

**Find:** Required manning level $M$.

**Step 1: Calculate the production rate required.**

$$P = \frac{W}{T}$$

$$P = \frac{600{,}000 \text{ SH}}{80 \text{ weeks}}$$

$$P = 7{,}500 \text{ SH/week}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the effective man-hours per worker per week.**

A worker paid for 44 hours per week, working at 85 % efficiency, produces:

$$H_{eff} = e \cdot h = 0.85 \times 44$$

$$H_{eff} = 37.4 \text{ SH/week per worker}$$

**Step 3: Solve for manning level $M$.**

$$M = \frac{P}{H_{eff}}$$

$$M = \frac{7{,}500 \text{ SH/week}}{37.4 \text{ SH/week per worker}}$$

$$M \approx 200.5 \text{ workers}$$

**Step 4: Practical rounding & interpretation.**

A real shipyard would plan for a **direct workforce of 200 workers** on the hull line, supplemented by **40 indirect workers** (supervisors, QC, material handlers — typically 15–20 % indirect ratio).

$$M_{total} = M_{direct} \times (1 + f_{indirect})$$

$$M_{total} = 200 \times 1.20 = 240 \text{ workers}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Question Pattern:** Any 14-mark Part B question in Module 1 will require you to compute (i) production rate, (ii) manning level, AND (iii) project cost using the labour cost formula $C_{labour} = M \cdot W \cdot r \cdot (1+B)$. Always show all three steps.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Learning Curve Application in Series Production

**Problem Statement (KTU Model Question):**
A shipyard is building a series of 8 identical offshore patrol vessels (OPVs). The first vessel required **250,000 SH**. The yard has achieved an **80 % learning curve** ($b = \log_2 0.8 \approx -0.3219$). Calculate the standard hours required for the 4th, 6th, and 8th vessels.

**Step 1: Recall the Wright Learning Curve equation.**

$$Y_n = Y_1 \cdot n^{b}$$

where $Y_n$ is the cumulative or unit hours for the $n^{th}$ ship (we will use *unit hours* per ship, the standard convention).

**Step 2: Compute the exponent $b$.**

$$b = \frac{\log(0.8)}{\log(2)} = \log_2(0.8) = -0.32193$$

**Step 3: Compute $Y_4$.**

$$Y_4 = 250{,}000 \times 4^{-0.32193}$$

$$\log_{10}(4) = 0.60206$$

$$4^{-0.32193} = 10^{-0.32193 \times 0.60206} = 10^{-0.19389}$$

$$10^{-0.19389} = 0.6400$$

$$Y_4 = 250{,}000 \times 0.6400 = 160{,}000 \text{ SH}$$

**Step 4: Compute $Y_6$.**

$$Y_6 = 250{,}000 \times 6^{-0.32193}$$

$$\log_{10}(6) = 0.77815$$

$$6^{-0.32193} = 10^{-0.32193 \times 0.77815} = 10^{-0.25050}$$

$$10^{-0.25050} = 0.5623$$

$$Y_6 = 250{,}000 \times 0.5623 = 140{,}575 \text{ SH}$$

**Step 5: Compute $Y_8$.**

$$Y_8 = 250{,}000 \times 8^{-0.32193}$$

$$\log_{10}(8) = 0.90309$$

$$8^{-0.32193} = 10^{-0.32193 \times 0.90309} = 10^{-0.29077}$$

$$10^{-0.29077} = 0.5120$$

$$Y_8 = 250{,}000 \times 0.5120 = 128{,}000 \text{ SH}$$

**Step 6: Cumulative hours for the series of 8 vessels.**

$$W_{cum} = \sum_{n=1}^{8} Y_n$$

$$W_{cum} = 250{,}000 + 200{,}000 + 160{,}000 + 128{,}000 + 128{,}000 \times 0.80 + \ldots$$

A more elegant way: use the **summation formula** for an 80 % learning curve series of 8 units:

$$W_{cum} \approx 5.387 \times Y_1 = 5.387 \times 250{,}000 = 1{,}346{,}750 \text{ SH}$$

(For an 80 % curve, the multiplier for $n=8$ is approximately **5.387**.)

> [!TIP]
> **Short-cut for KTU Exams:** Memorize the *cumulative multiplier* for an 80 % learning curve:
> - $n=2$: 1.800
> - $n=4$: 3.240
> - $n=8$: 5.387
> - $n=16$: 8.244
> These are direct multipliers of $Y_1$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical Path Calculation for Block Erection

**Scenario:** A ship has 5 major blocks to be erected. The block erection sequence with dependencies is:

| Activity | Predecessor | Duration (days) |
|----------|-------------|-----------------|
| A (Engine Room) | — | 12 |
| B (Cargo Hold 1) | A | 18 |
| C (Cargo Hold 2) | A | 16 |
| D (Bow) | B | 14 |
| E (Stern) | C | 15 |

**Step 1: Draw the network.**

A → B → D
A → C → E

**Step 2: Calculate Earliest Start (ES) and Earliest Finish (EF) for each node.**

$$ES_A = 0, \quad EF_A = 0 + 12 = 12$$

$$ES_B = EF_A = 12, \quad EF_B = 12 + 18 = 30$$

$$ES_C = EF_A = 12, \quad EF_C = 12 + 16 = 28$$

$$ES_D = EF_B = 30, \quad EF_D = 30 + 14 = 44$$

$$ES_E = EF_C = 28, \quad EF_E = 28 + 15 = 43$$

**Step 3: Calculate Latest Start (LS) and Latest Finish (LF) by backward pass.**

Project completion = max(EF_D, EF_E) = **44 days**

$$LF_D = 44, \quad LS_D = 44 - 14 = 30$$

$$LF_E = 44, \quad LS_E = 44 - 15 = 29$$

$$LF_B = LS_D = 30, \quad LS_B = 30 - 18 = 12$$

$$LF_C = LS_E = 29, \quad LS_C = 29 - 16 = 13$$

$$LF_A = \min(LS_B, LS_C) = \min(12, 13) = 12, \quad LS_A = 12 - 12 = 0$$

**Step 4: Identify the Critical Path.**

Critical Path = **A → B → D** (Total = 44 days). Note that activity C has **Total Float** = $LS_C - ES_C = 13 - 12 = 1$ day; activity E has Float = $LS_E - ES_E = 29 - 28 = 1$ day. Any delay on A, B, or D will delay the entire project.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cost Estimation Using the CSM Framework

**Given:**
- Work content $W = 600{,}000$ SH
- Manning $M = 200$ workers
- Wage rate $r = \text{INR } 350/\text{SH}$ (Indian yard, 2024 rates)
- Benefits factor $B = 0.40$ (PF, ESI, bonus, gratuity)
- Material cost $C_{mat} = \text{INR } 80$ crores
- Overhead $C_{oh} = 25$ % of $(C_{mat} + C_{lab})$

**Step 1: Labour cost.**

$$C_{lab} = M \cdot W \cdot r \cdot (1 + B)$$

Wait — note that $M \cdot W$ over-counts. The correct formula applies the wage rate to *all work hours*, not $M \cdot W$. The correct form is:

$$C_{lab} = W \cdot r \cdot (1 + B)$$

$$C_{lab} = 600{,}000 \times 350 \times 1.40 = \text{INR } 294{,}000{,}000 = \text{INR } 29.4 \text{ crores}$$

**Step 2: Overhead.**

$$C_{oh} = 0.25 \times (C_{mat} + C_{lab}) = 0.25 \times (80 + 29.4) = 0.25 \times 109.4 = 27.35 \text{ crores}$$

**Step 3: Total.**

$$C_{total} = C_{mat} + C_{lab} + C_{oh} = 80 + 29.4 + 27.35 = \text{INR } 136.75 \text{ crores}$$

**Step 4: Cost per CGT.**

If the vessel’s CGT = 8,000:

$$C_{unit} = \frac{136.75 \times 10^7}{8{,}000} = \text{INR } 170{,}938 \text{ per CGT}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **International Benchmark (KTU Exam Favourite):** Global average newbuild cost in 2024 ranges from **USD 1,800 – 2,800 per CGT** for standard commercial vessels. Indian yards typically quote **USD 1,500 – 1,900 per CGT**, giving a competitive edge.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics
