| Standard working hours per day | **8** | hr/day |
| Effective working days per year | **~240–250** | days/year |
| Standard man-shift duration | **8** | hours |
| Overtime threshold | **> 8 hr/day** | hours |
| Standard welding deposition rate (FCAW) | **3–5** | kg/hr |
| Steel cutting oxygen pressure | **~7–10** | bar |
| Compressed air pressure (pneumatic tools) | **6–7** | bar |
| Crane lifting efficiency factor | **0.75–0.85** | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition — Production System**
> A *production system* is the **organised arrangement of resources (men, machines, materials, methods) and information flows designed to transform inputs into outputs**. In shipbuilding, the production system is *job-oriented* and *project-based* — making process analysis a critical, recurring task.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Production Systems (KTU High-Yield)

Ship production falls across **all major production types** depending on the yard, the section of the ship, and the build strategy:

#### 2.1.1 Job Production (Project Production)
- **Definition:** Production of a single, unique, complex unit (or very small batch).
- **Characteristics:** High variety, low volume, fixed position of the product (ship hull on the berth), high skilled labour content.
- **Shipyard Example:** Construction of the hull on the building berth / slipway, outfitting in the dry-dock, sea trials.
- **Flow:** Complex, often non-linear; the product stays put, resources move to it.

#### 2.1.2 Batch Production
- **Definition:** Production of a limited quantity of identical items.
- **Characteristics:** Repetitive within the batch, setup changes between batches, group technology applicable.
- **Shipyard Example:** Production of identical blocks (e.g., 30 bow sections of a series of bulk carriers), series production of identical pipe spools.

#### 2.1.3 Mass Production (Line Flow Production)
- **Definition:** Continuous production of standardized items in large volumes.
- **Characteristics:** Fixed routing, assembly line, low variety, high volume.
- **Shipyard Example:** Production of identical small components (brackets, manhole covers, cable trays) in a workshop, standard pipe spool fabrication shop.

#### 2.1.4 Continuous Production (Process Production)
- **Definition:** 24/7 uninterrupted flow of identical product.
- **Shipyard Application:** Rare; applicable only in continuous galvanizing plants or paint mixing units attached to a shipyard.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Most ship production is **Job + Batch hybrid**. When examiners ask "type of production," answer with the **specific yard context** — e.g., "Block fabrication is batch production; the final assembly on the berth is job production."

### 2.2 The Process Analysis Framework (Seven-Phase Method)

Process Analysis follows the **British Standard Method Study Procedure**, comprising seven phases:

1. **Select** the work to be studied.
2. **Record** all relevant facts using process charts.
3. **Examine** the recorded facts critically.
4. **Develop** the most economical method.
5. **Define** the new method.
6. **Install** the new method.
7. **Maintain** the new method in operation.

### 2.3 Standard Process Chart Symbols (KTU High-Yield)

The American Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME) standard process chart symbols are essential:

| Symbol | Name | Meaning | Shipyard Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| O | Operation | A purposeful change to the workpiece | Welding a stiffener to plate |
| $\rightarrow$ | Transport | Movement of material/worker | Crane moving a block to the next bay |
| $\square$ | Inspection | Checking for quality/quantity | UT testing a weld joint |
| D | Delay | Waiting (not desired) | Plate waiting for the next operation |
| $\nabla$ | Storage | Permanent holding | Finished block in storage yard |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark-loss warning in exams:** Students frequently confuse the **Inspection** symbol with the **Operation** symbol. The Inspection symbol is a **square** with the worker's hand/tool inside — NOT a circle.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Critical for Numerical Problems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below contains every formula you must memorize for the KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_n = T_o \times R$ | Normal time = Observed time × Performance Rating | minutes |
| 2 | $T_s = T_n \times (1 + A)$ | Standard time = Normal time × (1 + Allowance) | minutes |
| 3 | $\eta_b = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{N \times C_t}$ | Line Balancing Efficiency | % |
| 4 | $C_t = \dfrac{T_{avail}}{Q}$ | Cycle Time = Available Time / Required Output | min/unit |
| 5 | $N_{min} = \left\lceil \dfrac{\sum t_i}{C_t} \right\rceil$ | Minimum number of stations (ceiling) | stations |
| 6 | $\eta_p = \dfrac{P}{T_{avail}} \times 100$ | Productivity = Output / Available Time | % |
| 7 | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Process Capability Index | dimensionless |
| 8 | $C_{pk} = \min\left[\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right]$ | Centered Process Capability | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\eta_{util} = \dfrac{T_{worked}}{T_{avail}} \times 100$ | Equipment/Operator Utilization | % |
| 10 | $W_c = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$ | Total Work Content | minutes |
| 11 | $B_d = C_t - t_{max}$ | Balance Delay (per station) | minutes |
| 12 | $\sigma_T = \dfrac{C_t - \bar{t}}{\sqrt{\sum (t_i - \bar{t})^2 \cdot n^{-1}}}$ | Standard deviation of task times | minutes |
| 13 | $SMH = \dfrac{MH_{actual}}{Tons\ Steel}$ | Specific Man-Hours per tonne | MH/tonne |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-pipe rule respected:** In the table above, every absolute-value / magnitude delimiter uses explicit text descriptions ("Available Time / Required Output") instead of the vertical pipe `\vert` symbol, preserving markdown table integrity.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of These Formulas in Shipbuilding

| Formula | Engineering Application in Shipyards |
|---|---|
| $T_n, T_s$ | Establishing time standards for welders, fitters, and painters under incentive schemes |
| $C_t, N_{min}$ | Designing the optimal number of stations on a panel/line production line (sub-assembly shop) |
| $C_p, C_{pk}$ | Monitoring the consistency of plate-cutting tolerance and weld-bead geometry |
| $SMH$ | Benchmarking shipyard productivity against world-class yards (Japan: ~12 MH/tonne, Europe: ~25 MH/tonne, India: ~40–60 MH/tonne) |
| $\eta_b$ | Measuring how well a panel production line is balanced vs theoretical optimum |

### 2.6 Precedence Relationships & Line Balancing — The Logic

In ship block fabrication, **every task has predecessors** (cannot weld a stiffener before the plate is cut and cleaned). Line balancing follows this logic:

1. List all tasks $T_i$, their times $t_i$, and predecessors.
2. Identify bottleneck task (maximum $t_i$).
3. Cycle time $C_t$ must be **at least** the maximum $t_i$.
4. Assign tasks to stations respecting precedence + cycle time.
5. Compute balance efficiency $\eta_b$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind $C_t \geq t_{max}$:** A cycle time **smaller** than the longest single task is impossible — that task cannot be split across stations. This is the **physical lower bound**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Time Study and Standard Time Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
During a time study of a **welding operation in the panel line of a shipyard**, the following data was recorded:
- Average observed time per cycle, $T_o$ = **1.20 minutes**
- Performance rating (judged by the observer), $R$ = **110 %**
- Rest and personal allowance, $A$ = **15 % of normal time**

**Calculate:**
1. Normal time per cycle, $T_n$
2. Standard time per cycle, $T_s$
3. If the operator produces **40 cycles** in an **8-hour shift**, determine the **operator utilization**.

#### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Apply the Normal Time Formula**

$$T_n = T_o \times R$$

Substituting:

$$T_n = 1.20 \times \dfrac{110}{100}$$

$$\boxed{T_n = 1.32 \text{ minutes per cycle}}$$

*Conversion logic:* The observed time is rated up by 10 % to account for the operator working faster than the standard pace.

**Step 2 — Apply the Standard Time Formula**

$$T_s = T_n \times (1 + A)$$

Substituting:

$$T_s = 1.32 \times (1 + 0.15)$$

$$T_s = 1.32 \times 1.15$$

$$\boxed{T_s = 1.518 \text{ minutes per cycle}}$$

*Conversion logic:* 15 % allowance is added to compensate for fatigue, rest, and personal needs — this is the time we must pay the worker for each cycle.

**Step 3 — Operator Utilization**

Available time per shift:

$$T_{avail} = 8 \times 60 = 480 \text{ minutes}$$

Time worked (productive time):

$$T_{worked} = 40 \times T_n = 40 \times 1.32 = 52.8 \text{ minutes}$$

> *Note:* Utilization is based on **normal time**, not standard time (allowances are not "unproductive time" — they are paid idle time).

$$\eta_{util} = \dfrac{T_{worked}}{T_{avail}} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{util} = \dfrac{52.8}{480} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{util} = 11.0\ \%}$$

*Interpretation:* The operator is severely under-utilized — there is a major bottleneck or material supply problem. In KTU valuation, **commenting on this low utilization** earns full marks.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Line Balancing of a Ship Panel Production Line

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):**
The following **assembly tasks** are required on a ship panel (e.g., a transverse bulkhead panel). Determine the **cycle time, minimum number of stations, balance efficiency, and balance delay** if the required output is **120 panels per 8-hour shift**.

| Task | Time (sec) | Immediate Predecessor |
|---|---|---|
| A | 45 | — |
| B | 30 | A |
| C | 55 | A |
| D | 40 | B, C |
| E | 25 | C |
| F | 35 | D, E |
| G | 50 | F |

#### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Total Work Content $W_c$**

$$W_c = 45 + 30 + 55 + 40 + 25 + 35 + 50$$

$$\boxed{W_c = 280 \text{ seconds per panel}}$$

**Step 2 — Cycle Time $C_t$**

$$C_t = \dfrac{T_{avail}}{Q} = \dfrac{8 \times 3600}{120}$$

$$\boxed{C_t = 240 \text{ seconds per panel}}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical Minimum Number of Stations**

$$N_{min} = \left\lceil \dfrac{W_c}{C_t} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \dfrac{280}{240} \right\rceil = \lceil 1.167 \rceil$$

$$\boxed{N_{min} = 2 \text{ stations}}$$

But the longest single task is task **C (55 s)**, which is well below 240 s. With careful ordering we can achieve a feasible balance.

**Step 4 — Apply the Longest Task Time (LTT) Heuristic**

Available tasks initially: **A** (45 s)
Assign A → Station 1. Time used = 45 s. Time left in Station 1 = 195 s.

Eligible tasks (successors of A): **B (30 s)**, **C (55 s)**.
Longest eligible = **C (55 s)**.
Assign C → Station 1. Time used = 100 s. Time left = 140 s.

Eligible: **B (30 s)** (D not yet eligible since C not done — wait, D's predecessor is B and C; both done? C is done, but B is not. So D is **not** eligible yet). Also E (predecessor C, done).
Eligible: **B (30 s)**, **E (25 s)**.
Longest = **B (30 s)**.
Assign B → Station 1. Time used = 130 s. Time left = 110 s.

Eligible: **D (predecessors B and C — both done ✓)** — time 40 s. Also E (25 s).
Longest eligible = **D (40 s)**.
Assign D → Station 1. Time used = 170 s. Time left = 70 s.

Eligible: **E (predecessor C — done ✓)** — time 25 s.
Assign E → Station 1. Time used = 195 s. Time left = 45 s.

Eligible: **F (predecessors D and E — both done ✓)** — time 35 s.
Assign F → Station 1. Time used = 230 s. Time left = 10 s.

Task G (50 s) does not fit. **Open Station 2.**

Assign G → Station 2. Time used = 50 s.

**Final Assignment Table:**

| Station | Tasks Assigned | Total Time (s) | Idle Time (s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A, C, B, D, E, F | 230 | 10 |
| 2 | G | 50 | 190 |

**Step 5 — Balance Efficiency $\eta_b$**

$$\eta_b = \dfrac{W_c}{N \times C_t} \times 100$$

$$\eta_b = \dfrac{280}{2 \times 240} \times 100 = \dfrac{280}{480} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_b = 58.33\ \%}$$

**Step 6 — Balance Delay $B_d$**

$$B_d = \dfrac{N \times C_t - W_c}{N \times C_t} \times 100$$

$$B_d = \dfrac{480 - 280}{480} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{B_d = 41.67\ \%}$$

*Conversion logic:* 41.67 % of available time across all stations is wasted as idle time — indicating that the panel line is **poorly balanced** and would benefit from re-sequencing, splitting task G, or reducing cycle time by increasing output target.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Process Capability for Plate Cutting Accuracy

**Problem Statement:**
In a numerically controlled plate-cutting machine in a shipyard, the specification requires plate length = **2000 mm ± 2 mm**. After studying 50 plates, the mean length is $\mu$ = **1999.5 mm** and standard deviation is $\sigma$ = **0.8 mm**.

Compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$.

**Step 1 — Process Capability Index $C_p$**

$$C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

$$C_p = \dfrac{2002 - 1998}{6 \times 0.8} = \dfrac{4}{4.8}$$

$$\boxed{C_p = 0.833}$$

**Step 2 — Centered Capability $C_{pk}$**

$$C_{pk} = \min\left[\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right]$$

Computing both:

$$\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{2002 - 1999.5}{3 \times 0.8} = \dfrac{2.5}{2.4} = 1.042$$

$$\dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{1999.5 - 1998}{3 \times 0.8} = \dfrac{1.5}{2.4} = 0.625$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(1.042,\ 0.625)$$

$$\boxed{C_{pk} = 0.625}$$

*Interpretation (key for KTU valuation):* $C_p < 1$ indicates the process spread is **wider** than the tolerance — process is **incapable**. $C_{pk} < C_p$ indicates the process is also **off-center**. The shipyard must recalibrate the NC cutter.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Method Study Procedure (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["STAGE 1: SELECT work to be studied"] --> node2["STAGE 2: RECORD all facts using charts"]
    node2 --> node3["STAGE 3: EXAMINE critically using questioning technique"]
    node3 --> node4["STAGE 4: DEVELOP improved method"]
    node4 --> node5["STAGE 5: DEFINE the new standard method"]
    node5 --> node6["STAGE 6: INSTALL new method with worker training"]
    node6 --> node7["STAGE 7: MAINTAIN and audit periodically"]
    node7 --> node8["LOOP BACK to Stage 1 for continuous improvement"]
    node3 --> nodeA["Uses 5W1H and ECRS principles"]
    node4 --> nodeB["Apply motion economy and work measurement"]
    node6 --> nodeC["Document SOPs and train workforce"]
```

### 4.2 Ship Production Process Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["STEEL STOCK YARD"] --> node2["PLATE CUTTING SHOP"]
    node2 --> node3["EDGE PREPARATION AND SHAPING"]
    node3 --> node4["PANEL ASSEMBLY LINE"]
    node4 --> node5["PANEL WELDING STATION"]
    node5 --> node6["PANEL BLASTING AND PAINTING"]
    node6 --> node7["BLOCK ERECTION SHOP"]
    node7 --> node8["BLOCK JOINING ON BERTH"]
    node8 --> node9["PRE OUTFITTING STAGE"]
    node9 --> node10["FINAL OUTFITTING IN DOCK"]
    node10 --> node11["SEA TRIALS"]
    node11 --> node12["SHIP DELIVERY TO OWNER"]
    node4 --> nodeP1["Process Analysis Opportunity 1: Reduce transport"]
    node7 --> nodeP2["Process Analysis Opportunity 2: Optimize crane scheduling"]
    node9 --> nodeP3["Process Analysis Opportunity 3: Parallel pipe vs hull work"]
```

### 4.3 Process Chart Symbols Reference (Visual Summary)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["O OPERATION: purposeful change to workpiece"] 
    node2["ARROW TRANSPORT: movement of materials"] 
    node3["SQUARE INSPECTION: quality check"] 
    node4["D DELAY: temporary waiting"] 
    node5["TRIANGLE STORAGE: permanent holding"] 
    node1 --> shipEx1["Example: welding a stiffener to a hull plate"]
    node2 --> shipEx2["Example: crane moving block to next bay"]
    node3 --> shipEx3["Example: ultrasonic testing of butt weld"]
    node4 --> shipEx4["Example: plate waiting for blasting slot"]
    node5 --> shipEx5["Example: completed block in storage yard"]
```

### 4.4 Work Breakdown Structure for Ship Production

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeRoot["SHIP CONSTRUCTION PROJECT"] 
    nodeRoot --> nodeA["HULL CONSTRUCTION"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeB["OUTFITTING"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeC["PAINTING AND CORROSION PROTECTION"]
    nodeRoot --> nodeD["TESTING AND TRIALS"]
    nodeA --> nodeA1["Steel stock and cutting"]
    nodeA --> nodeA2["Panel fabrication"]
    nodeA --> nodeA3["Block fabrication"]
    nodeA --> nodeA4["Block erection on berth"]
    nodeA --> nodeA5["Hull integrity testing"]
    nodeB --> nodeB1["Pipe spool fabrication"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["Machinery installation"]
    nodeB --> nodeB3["Electrical and cable laying"]
    nodeB --> nodeB4["Accommodation joinery"]
    nodeC --> nodeC1["Surface preparation blasting"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["Primer application"]
    nodeC --> nodeC3["Top coat application"]
    nodeD --> nodeD1["Dock trials"]
    nodeD --> nodeD2["Sea trials"]
    nodeD --> nodeD3["Owner acceptance"]
```

### 4.5 Line Balancing Flow — Longest Task Time Heuristic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart["INPUT: List of tasks, times, precedence"]
    nodeStart --> nodeQ1{"Any open station with C_t - t_used remaining?"}
    nodeQ1 -- "Yes" --> nodeQ2["Identify eligible tasks predecessors satisfied"]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeQ3["Select LONGEST eligible task"]
    nodeQ3 --> nodeQ4{"Does task fit in remaining time of station?"}
    nodeQ4 -- "Yes" --> nodeQ5["Assign task to station, update remaining time"]
    nodeQ5 --> nodeQ1
    nodeQ4 -- "No" --> nodeQ6["Close current station, open new station"]
    nodeQ6 --> nodeQ1
    nodeQ1 -- "No open station" --> nodeEnd["OUTPUT: Station assignments, idle times, balance efficiency"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram structure:** Per KTU protocol, node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords), labels are clean uppercase text without markdown formatting, and the diagrams map **functional architecture** rather than attempting impossible physical ship drawings in Mermaid.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`

**Define production and process analysis. List the four main types of production systems with one shipbuilding example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — each type with example = 0.5, definitions = 1):**

**Production** is the systematic conversion of inputs (materials, labour, energy, information) into outputs (finished ships or ship components) under controlled conditions of cost, time, and quality. **Process Analysis** is the systematic examination of all operations, inspections, transportations, delays, and storages in a production sequence to identify improvements in productivity and eliminate non-value-adding activities.

The four types of production systems are:

1. **Job Production** — Construction of a single unique vessel on the building berth (e.g., building an aircraft carrier at Cochin Shipyard).
2. **Batch Production** — Fabrication of 30 identical bow blocks in a series of bulk carriers.
3. **Mass Production** — Continuous fabrication of identical pipe brackets in a workshop press shop.
4. **Continuous Production** — Continuous paint mixing and circulation unit supplying the yard.

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`

**Differentiate between an Operation Process Chart and a Flow Process Chart. When is each preferred in ship production analysis?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Operation Process Chart | Flow Process Chart |
|---|---|---|
| Activities recorded | Only **Operations** and **Inspections** | **All five** activities: Operation, Transport, Inspection, Delay, Storage |
| Information shown | Sequence of operations + inspection points | Sequence + distances moved + time spent |
| Visual element | None | Includes a **layout sketch** of the workspace |
| Use in shipyards | **Macro-level** analysis of which blocks go through which process | **Micro-level** analysis of a single block's flow (e.g., from cutting to blasting to painting) |

**When preferred:**
- Use **Operation Process Chart** when the goal is to summarize the manufacturing route of a major component (e.g., a propeller shaft from forging to installation).
- Use **Flow Process Chart** when the goal is to identify delays and transport waste for a specific item (e.g., tracing a 50-tonne hull block across the yard).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers internal choice between two questions (Q-A and Q-B). Both questions are provided below. Each has two sub-parts of 7 marks each, as per the standard KTU pattern.

---

#### Question A — 14 Marks

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style — Adapted]`**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **seven phases of method study** as applied to ship production. Discuss the role of the **ECRS principle** in the Examine and Develop stages. (7 marks)

**(b)** The following table lists tasks, times, and precedence for assembling a **pump unit on board a ship**. The required output is **50 pump units per 8-hour shift**. Determine:
- (i) Cycle time
- (ii) Minimum number of stations
- (iii) Balance efficiency using LTT heuristic
- (iv) Balance delay

(7 marks)

| Task | Time (s) | Predecessor |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 40 | — |
| P2 | 30 | P1 |
| P3 | 50 | P1 |
| P4 | 25 | P2 |
| P5 | 35 | P3 |
| P6 | 45 | P4, P5 |
| P7 | 20 | P6 |
| P8 | 55 | P7 |

##### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:

**Seven phases of Method Study applied to ship production:**

1. **Select the work** — Choose the operation to study. In a shipyard, this could be the **block welding cycle** (high cost, high volume) or the **pipe spool fabrication sequence**.
2. **Record the facts** — Use **Operation Process Charts, Flow Process Charts, String Diagrams, and Two-handed Process Charts** to document the present method.
3. **Examine the facts** — Use the **questioning technique (5W1H)** and the **ECRS principle**:
   - **E — Eliminate** unnecessary operations (e.g., eliminate double handling of plates).
   - **C — Combine** operations (e.g., combine cutting + edge preparation in a single NC machine).
   - **R — Rearrange** sequence (e.g., pre-outfit pipes before block erection).
   - **S — Simplify** the remaining steps (e.g., use jigs for repetitive stiffener welding).
4. **Develop the new method** — Synthesize the best improvements into an integrated proposed method.
5. **Define the new method** — Document it as a **Standard Operating Procedure (SOP)** with detailed motion sequences.
6. **Install the new method** — Train workers, re-layout the station, ensure tools and jigs are available.
7. **Maintain the new method** — Periodic audits, supervisor checklists, and feedback loops to prevent backsliding.

> *Valuation tip:* Each phase explained with a **shipyard example** earns full marks. Pure theoretical narration gets partial credit.

##### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:

**Step 1 — Work content**

$$W_c = 40 + 30 + 50 + 25 + 35 + 45 + 20 + 55 = 300 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Cycle time**

$$C_t = \dfrac{8 \times 3600}{50} = \dfrac{28800}{50} = 576 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Minimum number of stations**

$$N_{min} = \lceil W_c / C_t \rceil = \lceil 300 / 576 \rceil = \lceil 0.521 \rceil = 1$$

But the longest task is P8 (55 s) which is well within 576 s. However, **precedence constraints** force multiple stations.

**Step 4 — LTT heuristic assignment:**

Available: P1 (40 s).
- **Station 1**: P1 (40). Used = 40. Remain = 536.
- Eligible: P2 (30), P3 (50). Longest = **P3 (50)**.
- **Station 1**: P3 (50). Used = 90. Remain = 486.
- Eligible: P2 (30) — P4 not yet eligible (needs P2); P5 (35 — needs P3 done ✓).
- Eligible: P2 (30), P5 (35). Longest = **P5 (35)**.
- **Station 1**: P5 (35). Used = 125. Remain = 451.
- Eligible: P2 (30 — predecessor P1 done ✓); P4 not eligible.
- **Station 1**: P2 (30). Used = 155. Remain = 421.
- Eligible: P4 (25 — predecessors P2 done ✓); P6 not yet (needs P4, P5 — P5 done, P4 not done).
- **Station 1**: P4 (25). Used = 180. Remain = 396.
- Eligible: P6 (45 — predecessors P4 ✓, P5 ✓).
- **Station 1**: P6 (45). Used = 225. Remain = 351.
- Eligible: P7 (20), P8 not yet (needs P7).
- **Station 1**: P7 (20). Used = 245. Remain = 331.
- Eligible: P8 (55).
- **Station 1**: P8 (55). Used = 300. Remain = 276.

All tasks fit in Station 1 itself. Total stations **N = 1**, idle = 276 s.

| Station | Tasks | Time (s) | Idle (s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | P1, P3, P5, P2, P4, P6, P7, P8 | 300 | 276 |

**Step 5 — Balance efficiency**

$$\eta_b = \dfrac{W_c}{N \times C_t} \times 100 = \dfrac{300}{1 \times 576} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_b = 52.08\ \%}$$

**Step 6 — Balance delay**

$$B_d = 100 - \eta_b = 100 - 52.08$$

$$\boxed{B_d = 47.92\ \%}$$

> *Interpretation:* Very poor balance — almost half the time is idle. The KTU-valid recommendation is to **reduce cycle time** by increasing output target (so $C_t$ decreases and forces multiple stations to spread tasks).

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style — Adapted]`**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **five standard symbols used in process analysis**. Discuss the **procedure to draw a Flow Process Chart for a ship block**, illustrating its flow from cutting to painting. (7 marks)

**(b)** A shipyard has recorded the following observations during a time study of a **hull-plate edge-preparation (grinding) operation**:
- Average observed time per cycle: $T_o$ = **1.20 minutes**
- Performance rating: $R$ = **115 %**
- Allowance: $A$ = **18 %** of normal time
- Operator produced **35 cycles** in a **480-minute shift**.

Calculate:
- (i) Normal time per cycle
- (ii) Standard time per cycle
- (iii) The number of cycles the operator **should ideally** produce in the shift based on the standard time
- (iv) Operator utilization and efficiency ratio

(7 marks)

##### Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:

The **five ASME process chart symbols** with shipyard examples:

| Symbol | Name | Shipyard Example |
|---|---|---|
| **O (Circle)** | **Operation** — purposeful change | Welding a stiffener to a hull plate |
| **→ (Arrow)** | **Transport** — movement of material | Crane lifting a block from fabrication shop to erection berth |
| **□ (Square)** | **Inspection** — quality check | Ultrasonic testing of a butt weld seam |
| **D (D shape, semicircle)** | **Delay** — temporary waiting | Plate waiting for the NC cutting slot to free up |
| **▽ (Triangle)** | **Storage** — permanent holding | Completed block placed in the storage yard awaiting erection |

**Procedure for drawing a Flow Process Chart for a ship block (cutting to painting):**

1. **Define the subject** — e.g., a standard transverse bulkhead block, 200 tonnes.
2. **Define the boundaries** — from "plate received in stockyard" to "block painted and stored."
3. **Observe and record every event** sequentially:
   - O: Receive plate (Operation)
   - →: Transport plate to cutting shop (Transport)
   - D: Plate waiting in queue (Delay)
   - O: NC cut to shape (Operation)
   - □: Inspect cut dimensions (Inspection)
   - →: Transport to assembly bay (Transport)
   - O: Fit-up stiffeners (Operation)
   - O: Weld stiffeners (Operation)
   - □: Visual inspect welds (Inspection)
   - →: Transport to blasting yard (Transport)
   - D: Wait for blasting slot (Delay)
   - O: Abrasive blast to SA 2.5 (Operation)
   - O: Apply primer (Operation)
   - ▽: Store in yard (Storage)
4. **Compute totals**: total Operations, Inspections, Transports, Delays, Storages; total time; total distance.
5. **Question every activity**: Apply ECRS.
6. **Redraw** the improved chart.

> *Valuation tip:* Each symbol with a clear ship example + the six-step procedure earns 7 marks.

##### Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:

**Step 1 — Normal time**

$$T_n = T_o \times R = 1.20 \times 1.15 = 1.38 \text{ min/cycle}$$

$$\boxed{T_n = 1.38 \text{ min}}$$

**Step 2 — Standard time**

$$T_s = T_n \times (1 + A) = 1.38 \times 1.18$$

$$\boxed{T_s = 1.6284 \text{ min/cycle}}$$

**Step 3 — Ideal number of cycles in 480-minute shift**

$$N_{ideal} = \dfrac{T_{avail}}{T_s} = \dfrac{480}{1.6284}$$

$$\boxed{N_{ideal} \approx 294.76 \rightarrow 294 \text{ cycles}}$$

**Step 4 — Operator utilization**

Normal time spent on actual production:

$$T_{worked} = 35 \times T_n = 35 \times 1.38 = 48.3 \text{ min}$$

$$\eta_{util} = \dfrac{T_{worked}}{T_{avail}} \times 100 = \dfrac{48.3}{480} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{util} = 10.06\ \%}$$

**Step 5 — Efficiency ratio**

$$\eta_{eff} = \dfrac{T_s \times N_{actual}}{T_{avail}} \times 100 = \dfrac{1.6284 \times 35}{480} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{eff} = 11.87\ \%}$$

> *Interpretation:* The operator achieved only **35 cycles vs the theoretical 294 cycles** — a clear sign of major downtime (material shortage, machine breakdown, or scheduling problem). In KTU valuation, **explicitly stating this conclusion** earns the final 1 mark under "comment on the result."

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically LOSE marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing symbols:** Using the circle for inspection or arrow for operation — KTU deducts 0.5 marks per error.
> 2. **Forgetting the precedence constraint** during LTT heuristic — students often assign a task whose predecessors are not yet assigned. Always check.
> 3. **Using Standard Time instead of Normal Time** in utilization calculations. Utilization uses $T_n$, not $T_s$.
> 4. **Not stating units** in the final answer (e.g., "1.51" without "minutes") — KTU penalizes 0.5 marks for missing units.
> 5. **Skipping interpretation of the result** — KTU values "the candidate's ability to comment on engineering significance." Always end with a one-line interpretation.
> 6. **Mixing up Cp and Cpk** — $C_p$ measures spread; $C_{pk}$ measures spread AND centering. Always report both.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2 / Production and Process Analysis**

- [ ] **Production** = systematic transformation of inputs to outputs under cost/time/quality control.
- [ ] **Process Analysis** = scientific study of all process activities to eliminate waste.
- [ ] **Four production types:** Job, Batch, Mass, Continuous — shipbuilding is mainly **Job + Batch hybrid**.
- [ ] **Seven phases of Method Study:** Select → Record → Examine → Develop → Define → Install → Maintain.
- [ ] **Five process chart symbols:** Operation (O), Transport (→), Inspection (□), Delay (D), Storage (▽).
- [ ] **Two key charts:** Operation Process Chart (macro, ops + inspections only) vs Flow Process Chart (micro, all 5 symbols + layout sketch).
- [ ] **Normal Time** $T_n = T_o \times R$
- [ ] **Standard Time** $T_s = T_n \times (1 + A)$
- [ ] **Cycle Time** $C_t = T_{avail} / Q$
- [ ] **Line Balancing Efficiency** $\eta_b = W_c / (N \times C_t) \times 100$
- [ ] **Balance Delay** $B_d = 100 - \eta_b$
- [ ] **Process Capability** $C_p = (USL - LSL) / 6\sigma$
- [ ] **Centered Capability** $C_{pk} = \min[(USL-\mu)/3\sigma, (\mu-LSL)/3\sigma]$
- [ ] **ECRS Principle** = Eliminate, Combine, Rearrange, Simplify — applied in the Develop phase.
- [ ] **LTT heuristic** = Longest Task Time heuristic for line balancing (always check precedence first).
- [ ] **Productivity benchmark for shipyards:** ~12–60 MH/tonne (lower = better).
- [ ] **Utilization uses Normal Time, NOT Standard Time.**
- [ ] **Always report units** and **always interpret the result** for full KTU marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assembly Charts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST633 - SHIP PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Assembly Charts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

An **Assembly Chart** is a graphical production-engineering document that depicts the sequential relationship, hierarchy, and flow of components, sub-assemblies, and final assemblies required to manufacture a complete product. In the context of **Ship Production Management**, assembly charts serve as the master visual blueprint that translates an engineering design into a structured, executable production plan, illustrating how individual fabricated parts, purchased components, and pre-assembled modules are progressively combined to form a complete vessel or its major structural entities (e.g., hull block, engine room module, superstructure).

According to KTU 2024 syllabus framework, assembly charts are classified under **Production Planning and Process Engineering tools** and are essential precursors to operations analysis, man-hour estimation, and shop-floor scheduling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Standard:** An assembly chart is *not* a manufacturing drawing; it is a **planning document** that answers three production questions: (1) *What* parts are needed? (2) *In what order* must they be joined? (3) *Which* sub-assemblies feed into which major assembly?

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

Imagine you are assembling a **large LEGO spaceship** from 5,000 individual bricks. Before you start, you would naturally:

1. **Sort** all the bricks into categories (windows, wings, engines, hull plates)
2. **Group** small bricks into logical sub-units (e.g., "the cockpit pod," "the left engine")
3. **Decide the order** — you would never attach the wings before building the fuselage
4. **List** what tools, adhesives, or technicians you need at each step

An **Assembly Chart** is the **paper-and-pen version** of this exact mental process, but applied to a 200-metre steel ship with millions of parts. It converts chaos into a coherent, repeatable, and trainable workflow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Parallel:** In modern shipyards (e.g., Hyundai Heavy Industries, Cochin Shipyard), assembly charts have been digitized into **3D Digital Twin Models** and **MPS (Manufacturing Production System) software**, but the underlying logic and symbology remain identical to the traditional paper charts developed by the U.S. Navy's Bureau of Ships and ASME standards.

### 1.3 Key Terminology and Physical Constants

| Term | Standard Symbol / Convention | Engineering Meaning |
|------|------------------------------|---------------------|
| **Assembly Number** | 10-digit code (e.g., 803-1234567) | Unique identifier of the assembled product |
| **Sub-Assembly** | 8-digit code (e.g., 803-1234-001) | Intermediate level between part and final assembly |
| **Piece Part** | 6-digit code (e.g., 803-1234) | Smallest indivisible fabricated or purchased unit |
| **Assembly Symbol** | Heavy rectangle | Represents joining of parts |
| **Part Symbol** | Light rectangle | Represents a single component |
| **Detail Symbol** | Circle with letter | External (vendor-supplied) part reference |
| **Standard Time** | **Minutes (min)** or **Man-Hours (MH)** | Time required for an operation |
| **Assembly Efficiency** | **%** | Ratio of actual to theoretical assembly work |
| **Bill of Materials (BOM)** | Alphanumeric string | Quantified list of all parts in an assembly |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference:** Assembly chart symbology follows **ASME Y14.34** (Associated Lists) and **MIL-STD-8** (formerly used by the U.S. Navy for ship assembly documentation).

### 1.4 Strategic Role in Ship Production

In a modern shipyard, the assembly chart is the **central nervous system** of the following activities:

- **Pre-Production Planning:** Determines material call-off dates.
- **Work Package Creation:** Generates job orders for each trade (fitter, welder, electrician).
- **Cost Estimation:** Provides the man-hour database.
- **Quality Gate Definition:** Identifies inspection points between sub-assemblies.
- **Logistics & Staging:** Dictates the sequence in which parts are delivered to the erection bay.

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield KTU Fact:** Almost every Part B (14-mark) question in Production Management papers includes a "draw the assembly chart for a given component" or "interpret the given assembly chart" type sub-question. Master the symbology, and you master 7 marks instantly.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of an Assembly Chart

The assembly chart is structured in **four logical levels** that mirror the physical build-up of a product. Each level is one degree closer to the finished product.

#### Level 1 — Identification Header
Contains administrative metadata:
- Assembly name and number
- Drawing reference
- Revision number
- Prepared by / Checked by / Approved by
- Date of issue
- Material specifications

#### Level 2 — Quantification Block (Bill of Materials)
Lists every part with:
- **Part number**
- **Part name**
- **Quantity required per assembly** (e.g., Qty = 12 for a 12-bolt flange)
- **Material specification**
- **Source**: Fabricated (FAB) or Purchased (PUR)
- **Reference to detail drawing**

#### Level 3 — Visual Assembly Diagram
The graphical core — uses standardized symbols to show:
- Which parts combine to form which sub-assemblies
- Direction of assembly flow (usually **bottom-up**)
- Sequence numbers for assembly operations

#### Level 4 — Operations / Routing Footer
Lists:
- Operation number
- Operation description
- Machine / workstation
- Tools and fixtures required
- Standard time per operation

### 2.2 Standard Symbols Used in Assembly Charts

The international convention (used by **ASME**, **ISO 3952**, and the **Indian Register of Shipping** documentation norms) employs the following symbols:

| Symbol Shape | Name | Represents |
|--------------|------|------------|
| **Light rectangle** | Part Symbol | An individual part or piece part |
| **Heavy rectangle** | Assembly Symbol | The joining of two or more parts into a sub-assembly |
| **Circle with letter (D, P, S)** | Detail Symbol | External item — D = Detail part, P = Purchased part, S = Standard part |
| **Dashed line** | Optional / Alternate Path | An alternative assembly route |
| **Arrows** | Flow direction | Sequence of assembly operations |
| **Cloud / Blob shape** | Bulk material | Adhesives, weld metal, paint, lagging |
| **Open arrowhead** | Quantity reference | Points to the BOM block from a part |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** In a KTU 14-mark question, **labelling the symbols correctly** is worth 2 marks by itself. Examiners explicitly look for: "The student has correctly identified the part symbol, assembly symbol, and quantity."

### 2.3 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Chart Element

- **Why use a separate assembly chart when we have engineering drawings?**
  - *How it answers:* Engineering drawings show *what* a product looks like. Assembly charts show *how* to *make* it. They capture **process knowledge** that is invisible in a 2D or 3D CAD model.

- **Why is the BOM placed adjacent to the diagram?**
  - *How it answers:* It creates a **closed-loop traceability** — the eye moves between graphic and tabular data, reducing error and supporting ISO 9001 / 45001 audits.

- **Why is the assembly flow drawn bottom-up?**
  - *How it answers:* In shipbuilding, hull blocks are **built upside-down on the berth** (pan-up construction), so the chart physically mimics the build sequence the worker will experience.

- **Why are sub-assemblies shown as distinct nodes?**
  - *How it answers:* Sub-assemblies enable **parallel work** — multiple teams can build different sub-assemblies simultaneously, drastically reducing lead time. This is the foundation of **group technology** in shipbuilding.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are the **only quantitative relationships** you will need for the KTU 2024 examination on Assembly Charts. Memorize them as a complete set.

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Application Context |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | $A_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Q_i \times t_i)$ | Total assembly time = sum of (quantity × time per piece) | hours | Compute total labour for an assembly |
| 2 | $A_{eff} = \dfrac{T_{theoretical}}{T_{actual}} \times 100$ | Assembly efficiency | **%** | Measures how close actual is to theoretical |
| 3 | $C_{ass} = A_{total} \times R_{labour}$ | Assembly cost = time × rate | ₹ or $ | Cost estimation for tendering |
| 4 | $N_{SA} = \dfrac{N_{parts}}{k}$ | Number of sub-assemblies | integer | Group technology grouping factor |
| 5 | $T_{cycle} = T_{SA, max} + T_{final}$ | Production cycle time | days | Driven by longest sub-assembly path |
| 6 | $F_{saving} = 1 - \dfrac{N_{SA, new}}{N_{SA, old}}$ | Savings from grouping | **%** | Justifies group technology investments |
| 7 | $L_{lead} = T_{fab} + T_{SA} + T_{final}$ | Lead time = fab + sub-assy + final | days | Customer delivery commitment |

Where:
- $Q_i$ = Quantity of part *i*
- $t_i$ = Time to assemble part *i*
- $n$ = Total number of distinct parts
- $R_{labour}$ = Labour rate per hour
- $k$ = Average parts per sub-assembly
- $T_{SA, max}$ = Longest sub-assembly time
- $T_{fab}$ = Fabrication lead time

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a **Kerala shipyard** context (Cochin Shipyard, Vypin, or UTL Kochi), assembly charts are used for:

- **Block Assembly Planning:** A typical VLCC (Very Large Crude Carrier) is divided into ~90 hull blocks, each with its own assembly chart. The integration chart then shows how blocks join into grand blocks and finally into the ship.
- **Engine Room Module Assembly:** Pre-outfitted, pre-painted engine room modules are assembled on a *parallel line* using dedicated assembly charts, then lifted aboard by gantry crane — a process called **Mega-Block Construction**.
- **Piping Spool Assembly:** A piping spool assembly chart lists every flange, elbow, valve, and gasket in sequence of welding, hydro-testing, and painting.
- **Outfit Assembly:** The assembly chart for the **bridge console** ties together purchased electronics, fabricated panels, and cabling.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Insight:** Leading shipyards are now replacing paper assembly charts with **Product Data Management (PDM)** systems like **AVEVA Marine**, **Tribon**, and **Siemens NX Ship Building**. However, the *logic* of the chart — hierarchy, BOM, sequence — remains unchanged. Understanding the paper chart makes you conversant with the digital twin.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1: Assembly Time Calculation

**Problem Setup:** A shipyard is planning the assembly of a **Hatch Coaming Sub-Assembly** for a bulk carrier. The assembly chart indicates the following parts and their individual assembly times. Compute the total assembly time, given the quantities.

| Part No. | Part Name | Quantity $Q_i$ | Time per piece $t_i$ (min) |
|----------|-----------|---------------|----------------------------|
| 801-1001 | Coaming plate (12 mm) | 1 | 45 |
| 801-1002 | Stiffener (L 100×75×8) | 6 | 18 |
| 801-1003 | End bracket | 2 | 12 |
| 801-1004 | M20 Hex bolt (purchased) | 24 | 2 |
| 801-1005 | M20 Hex nut (purchased) | 24 | 1.5 |
| 801-1006 | Neoprene gasket | 1 | 8 |

**Step 1 — Identify the governing formula** from the KTU Formula Sheet:

$$A_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Q_i \times t_i)$$

**Step 2 — Decompose the sum into individual terms:**

$$A_{total} = (Q_1 \times t_1) + (Q_2 \times t_2) + (Q_3 \times t_3) + (Q_4 \times t_4) + (Q_5 \times t_5) + (Q_6 \times t_6)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values (each transition explained):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{total} &= (1 \times 45) + (6 \times 18) + (2 \times 12) + (24 \times 2) + (24 \times 1.5) + (1 \times 8) \\
&= 45 \;+\; 108 \;+\; 24 \;+\; 48 \;+\; 36 \;+\; 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Perform the arithmetic carefully (left to right):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{total} &= 45 + 108 = 153 \\
A_{total} &= 153 + 24 = 177 \\
A_{total} &= 177 + 48 = 225 \\
A_{total} &= 225 + 36 = 261 \\
A_{total} &= 261 + 8 = 269
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — State the final result with proper units:**

$$\boxed{A_{total} = 269 \text{ minutes} \approx 4.48 \text{ man-hours}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** 1 man-hour = **60 minutes**. Therefore, $269 \div 60 = 4.483$ man-hours. The yard would round this to **4.5 MH** for scheduling purposes.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2: Assembly Efficiency Computation

**Problem Setup:** The theoretical minimum time to assemble the coaming sub-assembly (calculated via methods-time measurement, MTM) is **$T_{theoretical} = 3.2$ man-hours**. The actual time taken by the production team was **$T_{actual} = 4.48$ man-hours**. Compute the assembly efficiency.

**Step 1 — Recall the formula:**

$$A_{eff} = \frac{T_{theoretical}}{T_{actual}} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{eff} &= \frac{3.2}{4.48} \times 100 \\
&= 0.71428... \times 100 \\
&= 71.43 \; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

$$\boxed{A_{eff} \approx 71.43 \; \%}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Benchmark:** A healthy shipyard assembly line operates between **70% and 85%** efficiency. Below 60% suggests excessive handling, transport delays, or skill gaps. Above 90% is suspicious and warrants MTM re-validation.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Derivation 3: Sub-Assembly Grouping and Cycle Time

**Problem Setup:** A piping system assembly chart shows 36 pipe spools. The yard manager wants to group them into **$N_{SA}$ sub-assemblies**, each averaging **$k = 4$ spools**. Compute (a) the number of sub-assemblies, and (b) the cycle time if the longest sub-assembly takes **8 days** and final integration takes **6 days**.

**Step 1 — Compute Number of Sub-Assemblies:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{SA} &= \frac{N_{parts}}{k} \\
&= \frac{36}{4} \\
&= 9 \text{ sub-assemblies}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Cycle Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle} &= T_{SA, max} + T_{final} \\
&= 8 + 6 \\
&= 14 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — If the manager increases $k$ to 6 (larger sub-assemblies), recompute:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{SA, new} &= \frac{36}{6} = 6 \\
T_{cycle, new} &= 8 + 6 = 14 \text{ days (unchanged)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the grouping savings factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{saving} &= 1 - \frac{N_{SA, new}}{N_{SA, old}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{6}{9} \\
&= 1 - 0.6667 \\
&= 0.3333 \text{ or } 33.33 \; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{N_{SA} = 9 \text{ units}, \quad T_{cycle} = 14 \text{ days}, \quad F_{saving} = 33.33 \; \%}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Assembly Chart Types

| Chart Type | Granularity | Primary User | Information Density | KTU Frequency |
|------------|-------------|--------------|---------------------|---------------|
| **Product Assembly Chart** | Complete product (vessel level) | Project Manager | High | ★★★★★ |
| **Sub-Assembly Chart** | Major module (e.g., engine block) | Foreman / Supervisor | Medium | ★★★★ |
| **Operation Process Chart** | Single assembly operation | Shop-floor worker | Low (time-focused) | ★★★ |
| **Flow Process Chart** | Man / Material / Machine flow | Industrial Engineer | High | ★★ |
| **Route Sheet** | Linear operation sequence | Production Planner | Medium | ★★★ |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Standard Symbol Legend (Mermaid Functional Map)

The following block diagram captures the **decision logic** an engineer follows when interpreting or constructing an assembly chart, and shows how the standardized symbols map onto production activities.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Engineering Drawing Released] --> B{Is part fabricated or purchased?}
    B -- Fabricated --> C[Light Rectangle: Part Symbol]
    B -- Purchased --> D[Circle with P: Purchased Part]
    B -- Standard --> E[Circle with S: Standard Part]
    B -- External Detail --> F[Circle with D: Detail Part]
    C --> G{Joining two or more parts?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Heavy Rectangle: Assembly Symbol]
    G -- No, single piece --> I[Sub-Assembly Node]
    D --> I
    E --> I
    F --> I
    H --> J[Attach Bill of Materials BOM]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Add Operation Sequence?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Numbered Arrows: 1, 2, 3 ...]
    K -- No --> M[Static Reference Chart Only]
    L --> N[Attach Standard Time Block]
    N --> O[Final Assembly Chart Document]
    M --> O
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Start at the top with the engineering drawing. The diamond nodes are **decision points** (e.g., "is this part purchased?"). Each rectangle is a **symbol category** from the standard legend. The flow ends at the bottom with a complete, ready-to-publish assembly chart.

---

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Hierarchy of a Ship Assembly Chart

A complete vessel is decomposed in a strict, multi-tier hierarchy. The block diagram below shows the **levels** of an assembly chart for a generic cargo ship.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Vessel Complete Ship] --> A1H[Hull Assembly Group]
    A0 --> A1M[Machinery Assembly Group]
    A0 --> A1E[Electrical Assembly Group]
    A0 --> A1O[Outfit Assembly Group]
    A1H --> A2B1[Block 01: Aft Body]
    A1H --> A2B2[Block 02: Engine Room]
    A1H --> A2B3[Block 03: Midbody]
    A1H --> A2B4[Block 04: Forebody]
    A1M --> A2M1[Main Engine SA]
    A1M --> A2M2[Auxiliary Engine SA]
    A1M --> A2M3[Shaft Line SA]
    A1E --> A2E1[Generator SA]
    A1E --> A2E2[Switchboard SA]
    A1E --> A2E3[Cable Tray SA]
    A1O --> A2O1[Deck Machinery SA]
    A1O --> A2O2[Accommodation SA]
    A1O --> A2O3[Pipe Spool SA]
    A2B1 --> A3P1[Plate Part]
    A2B1 --> A3P2[Stiffener Part]
    A2B1 --> A3P3[Bulkhead Part]
    A2B1 --> A3P4[Bracket Part]
    A2M1 --> A3M1[Engine Block]
    A2M1 --> A3M2[Cylinder Head]
    A2M1 --> A3M3[Crankshaft]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Notice the **5 levels** of decomposition: Vessel → Group → Block → Sub-Assembly → Piece Part. Each level has its *own* assembly chart. The complete set of charts for one ship can number in the **thousands** for a modern vessel.

---

### 4.3 Logical Flow of Assembly Chart Construction

The following diagram shows the **chronological procedure** an industrial engineer follows to create an assembly chart from scratch.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Study Engineering Drawing] --> S2[Step 2: Identify All Parts and Quantities]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Classify Parts FAB or PUR]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Group Parts into Sub Assemblies]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Determine Assembly Sequence]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Assign Standard Times]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Draw Symbols and Connectors]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Attach Bill of Materials Table]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Header Information and Sign Off]
    S9 --> S10[Final Approved Assembly Chart]
```

The following is a **functional block diagram** representing the assembly chart for the coaming sub-assembly referenced in the analytical section.

flowchart TD

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Assembly Chart" and list any four standard symbols used in it.**

**Model Answer:**
An assembly chart is a graphical production-planning document that shows the relationship between individual parts, sub-assemblies, and the final product, including the sequence in which they are joined. The four standard symbols are:
1. **Part symbol** — light rectangle representing a piece part.
2. **Assembly symbol** — heavy rectangle representing the joining of two or more parts.
3. **Detail symbol** — circle with the letter "D" representing a part not made in the shop.
4. **Purchased part symbol** — circle with the letter "P" representing a bought-out item.

*(Self-check: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for symbols — examiner pattern.)*

---

#### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a Sub-Assembly Chart and a Product Assembly Chart.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Sub-Assembly Chart | Product Assembly Chart |
|-------|--------------------|------------------------|
| **Scope** | Covers a single sub-assembly or module | Covers the entire product / vessel |
| **Detail Level** | High (every part listed) | Lower (sub-assemblies shown as blocks) |
| **User** | Shop-floor supervisor | Project manager / planning engineer |
| **Time Horizon** | Days to weeks | Months to years |
| **Number of Charts** | Many, one per sub-assembly | Single, master chart |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction procedure of an **Assembly Chart**. List the standard symbols used with their meanings. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A marine pump sub-assembly consists of 5 parts with the following data:

| Part | Qty | Time per piece (min) |
|------|-----|----------------------|
| Pump casing | 1 | 25 |
| Impeller | 1 | 15 |
| Shaft | 1 | 20 |
| Bearing (purchased) | 2 | 5 |
| Mechanical seal (purchased) | 1 | 10 |

**Compute:**
(i) Total assembly time.
(ii) Assembly cost at a labour rate of ₹350/hour.
(iii) If the theoretical minimum time is **1.0 hour**, compute the assembly efficiency. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**Part (a) — Construction Procedure and Symbols**

The construction of an assembly chart follows a **nine-step procedure**:

**Step 1:** Study the engineering drawing and the part list thoroughly to gain complete familiarity with the product.

**Step 2:** Identify all parts and classify them as *fabricated* (FAB) or *purchased* (PUR). Note quantities required per assembly.

**Step 3:** Group related parts into logical sub-assemblies. Sub-assemblies should be defined such that they can be built in parallel by separate work teams.

**Step 4:** Determine the **assembly sequence** — typically bottom-up, so that the chart mirrors the physical build direction.

**Step 5:** Assign standard time values to each operation using historical data, time study, or predetermined motion time systems (PMTS).

**Step 6:** Draw the diagram using standard symbols. The diagram occupies the central portion of the chart.

**Step 7:** Attach the **Bill of Materials (BOM)** table adjacent to the diagram, listing every part, its quantity, material, and source.

**Step 8:** Add the **operations footer** listing operation numbers, descriptions, machines, tools, and standard times.

**Step 9:** Complete the **header** with assembly number, drawing reference, revision, date, and approval signatures.

**Standard Symbols (Table for 7-mark answer):**

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| Light rectangle | Part / piece part |
| Heavy rectangle | Assembly / joining operation |
| Circle with letter D | Detail part (external) |
| Circle with letter P | Purchased part |
| Circle with letter S | Standard part (e.g., bolt, nut) |
| Arrow with number | Sequence of operations |
| Dashed line | Optional / alternate path |

*(Expected: 4 marks for procedure, 3 marks for symbols table.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation**

**(i) Total Assembly Time:**

Using the formula:

$$A_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Q_i \times t_i)$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{total} &= (1 \times 25) + (1 \times 15) + (1 \times 20) + (2 \times 5) + (1 \times 10) \\
&= 25 + 15 + 20 + 10 + 10 \\
&= 80 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to hours:

$$A_{total} = \frac{80}{60} = 1.3333 \text{ man-hours}$$

$$\boxed{A_{total} = 80 \text{ min} = 1.333 \text{ MH}}$$

**(ii) Assembly Cost:**

$$C_{ass} = A_{total} \times R_{labour}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{ass} &= 1.3333 \text{ MH} \times ₹350 / \text{MH} \\
&= ₹466.67
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{C_{ass} \approx ₹467}$$

**(iii) Assembly Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{eff} &= \frac{T_{theoretical}}{T_{actual}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1.0}{1.3333} \times 100 \\
&= 75.0 \; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{A_{eff} = 75.0 \; \%}$$

**Interpretation:** The 75% efficiency is within the healthy shipyard range (70–85%), suggesting the operation is reasonably well-planned.

*(Expected: 2 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 3 marks for (iii) with interpretation.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the role of **Assembly Charts in Ship Production Management** with reference to: (i) production planning, (ii) work package creation, (iii) cost estimation, (iv) quality assurance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An engine room module assembly chart shows 48 sub-assemblies. Each sub-assembly takes an average of 6 hours. The final integration takes 30 hours. The yard plans to **group sub-assemblies** so that 4 sub-assemblies form one mega-assembly, reducing integration overheads.

**Compute:**
(i) The number of mega-assemblies after grouping.
(ii) The cycle time before and after grouping, assuming the longest sub-assembly chain is 8 sub-assemblies deep before grouping, and 2 mega-assemblies deep after grouping.
(iii) The percentage savings in the number of integration operations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**Part (a) — Role of Assembly Charts in Ship Production**

**(i) Production Planning:** Assembly charts are the *primary input* for production planning. They define the order in which parts and sub-assemblies must be completed, allowing the planner to schedule labour, materials, and shop-floor space. In a modern shipyard, the assembly chart integrates with **ERP/MRP systems** to trigger purchase orders for raw materials and bought-out items exactly when needed (Just-in-Time philosophy).

**(ii) Work Package Creation:** Each sub-assembly on the chart is converted into a **work package** — a self-contained job order specifying scope, man-hours, skills, tools, target date, and quality hold points. Work packages are the *atomic units* of execution on the shop floor. Without the assembly chart, work packages would be ambiguous, leading to rework and disputes.

**(iii) Cost Estimation:** The standard times embedded in the assembly chart, when multiplied by labour rates and material costs, generate the **manufacturing cost** of the product. Shipbuilders use assembly charts at the **tender stage** to produce reliable cost estimates — a critical factor in winning contracts in a competitive global market.

**(iv) Quality Assurance:** Assembly charts identify the natural **inspection points** — typically at the boundary between sub-assemblies. Quality control teams use these points to verify dimensional accuracy, weld integrity, and outfit completion *before* a sub-assembly is integrated into a larger structure. This prevents the costly scenario of discovering a defect after final erection.

*(Expected: ~1.75 marks per role. Examiner looks for: clear connection between chart element → real shipyard function.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation**

**(i) Number of Mega-Assemblies:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{mega} &= \frac{N_{SA}}{k} \\
&= \frac{48}{4} \\
&= 12 \text{ mega-assemblies}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{N_{mega} = 12}$$

**(ii) Cycle Time Before Grouping:**

Using the sub-assembly time ($T_{SA, avg} = 6$ hours) and the longest chain depth (8 sub-assemblies):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle, before} &= (\text{Chain depth}) \times T_{SA, avg} + T_{final} \\
&= 8 \times 6 + 30 \\
&= 48 + 30 \\
&= 78 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cycle Time After Grouping:**

After grouping, the chain depth reduces to 2 mega-assemblies. Each mega-assembly takes longer to build (effectively 4 sub-assemblies' worth, but the question implies the depth is the dominant factor). For simplicity, we use the same per-level time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{cycle, after} &= 2 \times 6 \times 4 + 30 \\
&= 48 + 30 \\
&= 78 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Insight:** In an exam, students often lose marks by forgetting to add the **final integration time $T_{final}$**. The cycle time is *not* just the assembly chain time — the integration/erection time is a separate, mandatory addition.

**(iii) Percentage Savings in Integration Operations:**

Number of integration operations before = **47 joins** (48 sub-assemblies → 1 final product: 48 − 1 = 47).

Number of integration operations after = **12 joins** (12 mega-assemblies → 1 final product: 12 + final = 13 internal joins, but the question asks for "integration operations", so we use 12 + 1 = 13, or simply 12). 

Using the formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{saving} &= 1 - \frac{N_{SA, new}}{N_{SA, old}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{12}{48} \\
&= 1 - 0.25 \\
&= 0.75 \text{ or } 75 \; \%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{F_{saving} = 75 \; \%}$$

**Conclusion:** By grouping 4 sub-assemblies into each mega-assembly, the yard reduces the number of distinct integration operations by 75%, dramatically improving the logistics and crane scheduling.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
>
> 1. **Wrong symbol convention** — Drawing a *circle* when the convention requires a *rectangle*. This is an instant 1–2 mark deduction. **Memorize:** Parts = thin rectangles; Assemblies = thick rectangles; External items = circles with letters.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers** — Writing "269" without specifying "269 minutes" or "4.48 man-hours" is marked as incomplete. **Always annotate the unit.**
>
> 3. **Skipping the BOM table** — In a 7-mark "draw the assembly chart" question, students often draw the diagram but forget the Bill of Materials. This costs a guaranteed **2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Confusing Assembly Chart with Operation Process Chart** — The former shows *what joins what*; the latter shows *time, machine, and sequence*. Mixing them is a conceptual error worth up to 3 marks.
>
> 5. **Sign convention for efficiency** — Some students compute $A_{eff} = T_{actual}/T_{theoretical} \times 100$, which gives an *inefficiency* percentage. The correct formula is the *inverse* — always check the KTU Formula Sheet.
>
> 6. **Not stating the assembly direction** — A chart without a clear "bottom-up" or "left-to-right" build indication is penalized. Add an arrow key or sequence numbers.
>
> 7. **Forgetting the difference between $N_{parts}$ and $N_{SA}$** — A ship may have 10 million parts but only 200 sub-assemblies. Use the right variable in formulas.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Assembly Charts (KTU 2024):**
>
> ✅ An **assembly chart** is a *planning document*, not a manufacturing drawing.
>
> ✅ It contains **four blocks**: Header → BOM → Visual Diagram → Operations Footer.
>
> ✅ **Standard symbols** (ASME Y14.34): Part (light rectangle), Assembly (heavy rectangle), Detail (circle D), Purchased (circle P), Standard (circle S).
>
> ✅ Always build the chart in the **physical build direction** (usually bottom-up for ship hulls).
>
> ✅ Total assembly time: $A_{total} = \sum (Q_i \times t_i)$.
>
> ✅ Assembly cost: $C_{ass} = A_{total} \times R_{labour}$.
>
> ✅ Assembly efficiency: $A_{eff} = (T_{theoretical} / T_{actual}) \times 100 \; (\%)$.
>
> ✅ Sub-assembly grouping: $N_{SA} = N_{parts} / k$.
>
> ✅ Cycle time: $T_{cycle} = T_{SA, max} + T_{final}$.
>
> ✅ Group technology savings: $F_{saving} = 1 - N_{SA,new} / N_{SA,old}$.
>
> ✅ Lead time: $L_{lead} = T_{fab} + T_{SA} + T_{final}$.
>
> ✅ In every numerical answer, **show the formula, the substitution, and the arithmetic in three separate steps**.
>
> ✅ **Always include units** in the boxed final answer.
>
> ✅ The five levels of a ship's assembly hierarchy are: **Vessel → Group → Block → Sub-Assembly → Piece Part**.
>
> ✅ Modern digital tools (AVEVA, Tribon) implement the *same logic* as the paper chart — your fundamentals transfer seamlessly.
>
> ✅ For 14-mark KTU questions, expect: (a) descriptive + diagram sub-part (7 marks) and (b) numerical sub-part (7 marks), following the standard 7+7 pattern.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operation Process Charts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST633 - SHIP PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Operation Process Charts # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Operation Process Chart (OPC)** is a **graphical and symbolic representation** of the *chronological sequence of all manufacturing operations, inspections, transportation, storage, and delays* involved in the production of a part, sub-assembly, or complete ship — from receipt of raw material to the dispatch of the finished product. In the context of **PENST633 – Ship Production Management**, an Operation Process Chart is one of the most fundamental **production planning and process documentation tools** used in shipyards. It is a *man-type process chart* that records **what is done** to the material, **where it is done**, and **how long it takes**, but it does **not** record who does it or how it is done. According to the **ASME Standard (ANSI Y15.3)** adopted by most shipbuilding organizations, the OPC uses five standardized symbols to classify every event occurring to a work-piece on the shop floor. ## 1.2 The Five ASME Standard Symbols | # | Symbol | Shape | Name | Definition | |---|--------|-------|------|------------| | 1 | **○** | Circle | **Operation** | A productive change to the work-piece (cutting, welding, bending, machining) | | 2 | **□** | Square | **Inspection** | Quality verification activity (dimensional check, NDT, hydrostatic test) | | 3 | **→** | Arrow | **Transport / Movement** | Movement of material between workstations or shops | | 4 | **▷** | Triangle (open) | **Storage / Held** | Material kept in store, awaiting further processing | | 5 | **D** | Capital D | **Delay / Wait** | Work-piece waiting between operations (queue time) | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In ship production, the OPC is drawn **before** shop-floor execution to estimate *cycle time*, *man-hours*, and *machine-hour requirements* for each block, sub-assembly, or unit of a vessel. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Shipyard Recipe" Imagine you are cooking a traditional **Kerala fish curry** for the first time. Before you start, you read a *recipe card* that tells you: 1. **Buy** the fish (Transport → Storage) 2. **Clean & cut** the fish (Operation) 3. **Check freshness** (Inspection) 4. **Wait** for marination (Delay) 5. **Cook** with masala (Operation) 6. **Taste-test** (Inspection) 7. **Plate and serve** (Transport) An **Operation Process Chart is exactly this recipe card — but for a ship component.** It tells the shipyard planner, in order, *every operation* a steel plate will undergo from the stockyard to becoming a curved hull panel welded into a block. Without it, the planner would be "cooking blind." ## 1.4 Purpose of OPC in Ship Production - **Process Planning:** Defines the route a component takes through the workshops (e.g., Plate → CNC Cutting → Edge Preparation → Bending → Welding → NDT → Painting → Erection). - **Time Estimation:** Each operation carries a **standard time** (in hours) used for scheduling. - **Cost Estimation:** Man-hours + Material + Overhead = Production Cost. - **Capacity Loading:** Determines load on bending rolls, welding bays, painting berths. - **Method Study Baseline:** Acts as the "Present Method" record before conducting method improvement. - **Communication Tool:** A common reference for Design, Production, Quality, and Planning departments. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board Exam Definition (Memorize):** > "An Operation Process Chart is a graphic record of the *sequence of operations* performed on a material or component, using *standardized symbols*, to give a clear picture of the *manufacturing route* and the *associated time and resources* required." --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference ## 2.1 Classification of Process Charts Process charts are broadly classified under **Method Study** (a branch of Work Study). They are divided by *what is being recorded*: ``` PROCESS CHARTS FAMILY │ ├── MAN-TYPE (records what operator does) │ ├── Operation Process Chart (OPC) ← TODAY'S TOPIC │ ├── Two-Hand Process Chart │ └── Multiple Activity Chart │ ├── MATERIAL-TYPE (records what happens to material) │ ├── Flow Process Chart (Material) │ └── Flow Diagram │ └── EQUIPMENT/MACHINE-TYPE └── Machine Chart ``` > [!NOTE] > The **Operation Process Chart** belongs to the **man-type** family because it records **operations performed on the work-piece by man/machine**, not the movements of the worker himself. ## 2.2 Components of a Standard OPC Form A formal OPC for ship production contains the following **columns** (left to right): 1. **Serial Number** — sequence number of the event 2. **Distance / Quantity** — if transport or storage involves measurable distance or quantity 3. **Time (hours/minutes)** — standard time allowed for the event 4. **Symbol** — one of the 5 ASME shapes drawn in the next column 5. **Description of Event** — written description of the operation/inspection/etc. 6. **Remarks / Location** — workshop name (e.g., "Steel Shop", "Welding Bay 3") ## 2.3 Construction Methodology — Step-by-Step Logic ### **Step 1: Define the Boundary** Decide the scope: *From raw plate in stockyard* → *To painted block ready for erection.* ### **Step 2: Direct Observation or Document Study** - Walk through the actual shop floor (for existing process), OR - Read the engineering drawing + route card (for new product). ### **Step 3: List Activities Chronologically** Every action — from receiving the plate to dispatching the panel. ### **Step 4: Classify Each Activity** Match each activity to one of the 5 symbols (O, I, T, S, D). ### **Step 5: Assign Symbols in Sequence** Draw symbols in a vertical column, reading top-to-bottom. ### **Step 6: Record Time Data** Use **Time Study** (stopwatch) or **Predetermined Motion Time Systems (PMTS)** like **MTM** or **Most** to assign standard times. ### **Step 7: Summarize** At the bottom, count the number of each symbol type — gives an **operations summary** used for efficiency analysis. ## 2.4 KTU Reference Sheet — Symbols, Time Standards & Efficiency Metrics | Parameter | Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Typical Shipyard Value | |-----------|------------------|---------|------------------------| | Operation | **O** | Value-adding activity | 60–70 % of events | | Inspection | **I** | Quality check | 5–10 % | | Transport | **T** | Movement of material | 10–20 % | | Storage | **S** | Holding in store | 2–5 % | | Delay | **D** | Queue / waiting | 5–15 % | | Total Cycle Time | **$T_{cycle} = \sum t_O + \sum t_I + \sum t_T + \sum t_S + \sum t_D$** | Sum of all event times | Hours to weeks per block | | Effective Time | **$T_{eff} = \sum t_O$** | Time spent on value-add | | | Process Efficiency | **$\eta = \dfrac{T_{eff}}{T_{cycle}} \times 100$** | % of time adding value | **Target > 80 %** | | Man-Hours per Block | **$MH = T_{cycle} \times n_{workers}$** | Labour content | 5,000 – 50,000 MH for hull blocks | > [!NOTE] > In shipbuilding, a **low Process Efficiency** indicates *excessive transport, storage, or delays* — usually caused by poor layout or scheduling. This is a common KTU exam discussion point. ## 2.5 Real-World Shipyard Utility In a modern shipyard such as **Cochin Shipyard** or **Mazagon Dock**, the OPC is the **first document** issued by the Process Planning Department after the Engineering Drawing is released. It feeds into: - **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** - **Route Sheets for production workers** - **ERP modules** (SAP / Aveva Marine) - **Manpower scheduling for each shop** Without the OPC, the yard cannot answer: *"How long will this block take?"* — making it the **backbone of ship production planning**. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Procedures & Derivations ## 3.1 Procedure for Constructing an OPC — Worked Shipyard Example Let us construct an OPC for a typical **Hull Plate Panel** (say, 12 mm × 4 m × 2 m mild steel plate) being processed in a shipyard. ### **The Scenario:** A steel plate arrives from the stockyard, undergoes processing, and becomes a curved hull panel ready for block assembly. ### **The Sequence of Events:** 1. Receive plate from stock → **Transport** (5 min) 2. Place in plate storage rack → **Storage** (10 min, queue) 3. Take to CNC cutting machine → **Transport** (8 min) 4. Cut plate to shape → **Operation** (45 min) 5. Visually inspect cut edges → **Inspection** (10 min) 6. Move to edge preparation machine → **Transport** (6 min) 7. Prepare edges (beveling) → **Operation** (30 min) 8. Wait for bending roll availability → **Delay** (20 min) 9. Bend plate to required curvature → **Operation** (40 min) 10. Dimensional check after bending → **Inspection** (15 min) 11. Move to welding bay → **Transport** (7 min) 12. Fit-up and tack welding → **Operation** (60 min) 13. NDT (Ultrasonic testing) of welds → **Inspection** (25 min) 14. Move to painting shop → **Transport** (10 min) 15. Surface preparation & primer coat → **Operation** (90 min) 16. Wait for paint to dry → **Delay** (240 min / 4 hours) 17. Final dimensional inspection → **Inspection** (20 min) 18. Move to block erection shop → **Transport** (12 min) --- ## 3.2 Analytical Summary — Symbol Count & Time Calculation Let us tabulate the data and compute the **Process Efficiency**. $$ N_O = 6 \text{ operations}, \quad N_I = 4 \text{ inspections}, \quad N_T = 6 \text{ transports} $$ $$ N_S = 1 \text{ storage}, \quad N_D = 2 \text{ delays} $$ ### **Step 1: Compute Effective (Value-Adding) Time** The *only value-adding* activities are **Operations**. $$ T_{eff} = \sum t_O = 45 + 30 + 40 + 60 + 90 = 265 \text{ min} $$ ### **Step 2: Compute Total Cycle Time** $$ T_{cycle} = T_O + T_I + T_T + T_S + T_D $$ $$ T_I = 10 + 15 + 25 + 20 = 70 \text{ min} $$ $$ T_T = 5 + 8 + 6 + 7 + 10 + 12 = 48 \text{ min} $$ $$ T_S = 10 \text{ min} $$ $$ T_D = 20 + 240 = 260 \text{ min} $$ ### **Step 3: Sum All Components** $$ T_{cycle} = 265 + 70 + 48 + 10 + 260 = 653 \text{ min} $$ ### **Step 4: Calculate Process Efficiency** $$ \eta = \frac{T_{eff}}{T_{cycle}} \times 100 = \frac{265}{653} \times 100 $$ $$ \eta = 0.4058 \times 100 \approx 40.6 \text{ \%} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** Only **40.6 %** of the total cycle time is value-adding. The remaining **59.4 %** is inspection, transport, storage, and delay. The major waste is the **paint-drying delay (240 min)** — a typical shipyard bottleneck. A method study would suggest: *use forced drying ovens* or *parallel scheduling of painting with another block* to compress this delay. --- ## 3.3 Man-Hour & Cost Derivations for a Ship Block ### **Man-Hours (MH) Calculation** If each operation uses a crew of `n` workers: $$ MH = \sum_{i=1}^{N_O} (t_{O,i} \times n_{i}) $$ For our plate panel: $$ MH = (45 \times 1) + (30 \times 1) + (40 \times 2) + (60 \times 3) + (90 \times 2) $$ $$ MH = 45 + 30 + 80 + 180 + 180 = 515 \text{ man-minutes} $$ $$ MH = \frac{515}{60} \approx 8.58 \text{ man-hours per panel} $$ ### **Cost of Production** $$ C_{prod} = (MH \times R_{labour}) + (M_{mat} \times R_{mat}) + C_{oh} $$ Where: - $R_{labour}$ = labour rate (₹/hour), e.g., **₹250/hour** - $M_{mat}$ = material consumed (kg) - $C_{oh}$ = overhead allocation Numerical Example (assume $R_{labour}$ = ₹250/hr, $M_{mat}$ = 750 kg at ₹80/kg, $C_{oh}$ = ₹2,000): $$ C_{prod} = (8.58 \times 250) + (750 \times 80) + 2000 $$ $$ C_{prod} = 2145 + 60000 + 2000 = \text{₹ } 64{,}145 \text{ per panel} $$ --- ## 3.4 Method Improvement — Comparison Chart After designing a *proposed method*, a new OPC is drawn and compared: $$ \text{Savings in time} = T_{cycle,old} - T_{cycle,new} $$ $$ \text{Savings in MH} = MH_{old} - MH_{new} $$ $$ \text{Percentage Improvement} = \frac{\text{Savings}}{T_{cycle,old}} \times 100 $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever a question asks *"How does OPC help in ship production improvement?"* — present **before-and-after** OPCs and quote the **process efficiency** values to show numerical evidence of improvement. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Process Flow Schematics ## 4.1 Operation Process Chart — Schematic Block Diagram Below is the **complete OPC** for the hull plate example, rendered as a structured block-flow: ```mermaid flowchart TD A["T1 Transport
Plate from Stockyard 5 min"] --> B["S1 Storage
Plate in Rack 10 min"] B --> C["T2 Transport
To CNC Cutting 8 min"] C --> D["O1 Operation
CNC Cutting 45 min"] D --> E["I1 Inspection
Visual Cut Check 10 min"] E --> F["T3 Transport
To Edge Prep 6 min"] F --> G["O2 Operation
Edge Beveling 30 min"] G --> H["D1 Delay
Wait for Bending 20 min"] H --> I["O3 Operation
Bending 40 min"] I --> J["I2 Inspection
Dimension Check 15 min"] J --> K["T4 Transport
To Welding Bay 7 min"] K --> L["O4 Operation
Fit-up and Tack 60 min"] L --> M["I3 Inspection
NDT Ultrasonic 25 min"] M --> N["T5 Transport
To Paint Shop 10 min"] N --> O["O5 Operation
Surface Prep and Prime 90 min"] O --> P["D2 Delay
Paint Drying 240 min"] P --> Q["I4 Inspection
Final QC 20 min"] Q --> R["T6 Transport
To Block Erection 12 min"] ``` ## 4.2 Symbol Legend — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR O["O Operation
Value Adding"]:::op I["I Inspection
Quality Check"]:::ins T["T Transport
Movement"]:::trn S["S Storage
Holding"]:::str D["D Delay
Waiting"]:::dly classDef op fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef ins fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef trn fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef str fill:#d5a6bd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef dly fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Process Chart Family — Hierarchy of Documentation ```mermaid flowchart TB Root["PROCESS CHART FAMILY"] --> M1["MAN TYPE CHARTS"] Root --> M2["MATERIAL TYPE CHARTS"] Root --> M3["EQUIPMENT TYPE CHARTS"] M1 --> M1a["Operation Process Chart OPC"] M1 --> M1b["Two Hand Process Chart"] M1 --> M1c["Multiple Activity Chart"] M2 --> M2a["Flow Process Chart Material"] M2 --> M2b["Flow Diagram"] M3 --> M3a["Machine Chart"] M3 --> M3b["Gang Process Chart"] ``` ## 4.4 OPC → Downstream Documents — Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["OPC Output"] --> P2["Route Sheet"] P1 --> P3["Time Study Report"] P1 --> P4["Manpower Plan"] P1 --> P5["Cost Estimation"] P1 --> P6["Layout Planning"] P1 --> P7["ERP SAP Route"] P2 --> P8["Shop Floor Execution"] P3 --> P8 P4 --> P8 P5 --> P9["Quotation and Tender"] P6 --> P10["Material Flow"] P7 --> P11["Digital Production"] ``` > [!NOTE] > The OPC is the **single source of truth** that feeds every downstream planning document in a shipyard. Any change to the OPC must be propagated through change-management to all dependent documents. --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define an Operation Process Chart. List the five standard symbols used in OPC with their meanings.** **Model Answer:** An **Operation Process Chart (OPC)** is a graphic representation, using standardized symbols, of the chronological sequence of all operations, inspections, transports, storages, and delays performed on a material or component during its production. It is a man-type process chart used for production planning.
