The five ASME standard symbols are: 1. **○ Operation** — a productive change to the work-piece. 2. **□ Inspection** — a quality check. 3. **→ Transport** — movement of material. 4. **▷ Storage** — holding in store. 5. **D Delay** — temporary waiting. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for symbols)* --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Distinguish between an Operation Process Chart and a Flow Process Chart.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Operation Process Chart (OPC) | Flow Process Chart (FPC) | |--------|-------------------------------|---------------------------| | Type | Man-type chart | Material-type chart | | Records | All events on the work-piece | Only operations and inspections | | Includes Transport/Delay/Storage | Yes | Usually excludes transport & delay | | Symbol count | Uses all 5 ASME symbols | Typically only **Operation** and **Inspection** | | Purpose | Process route planning | Detailed value-add analysis | *(3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full Essay Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A — Option 1** `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction procedure of an Operation Process Chart** for a ship hull plate component. List the symbols used and their meanings. **(b) [7 Marks]** From the OPC data below for a hull block panel, compute the **Process Efficiency**, **Total Cycle Time**, and **Man-Hours** if each operation uses the crew size indicated: | S.No | Event | Symbol | Time (min) | Crew Size | |------|-------|--------|------------|-----------| | 1 | Receive plate | T | 6 | 1 | | 2 | Storage | S | 12 | — | | 3 | Move to cutting | T | 8 | 1 | | 4 | Cut plate | O | 50 | 1 | | 5 | Visual check | I | 12 | 1 | | 6 | Edge bevel | O | 40 | 2 | | 7 | Wait for bender | D | 25 | — | | 8 | Bending | O | 45 | 2 | | 9 | Dimensional QC | I | 18 | 1 | | 10 | Move to welding | T | 9 | 1 | | 11 | Tack welding | O | 70 | 3 | | 12 | NDT test | I | 30 | 1 | | 13 | Move to paint | T | 11 | 1 | | 14 | Primer paint | O | 100 | 2 | | 15 | Paint drying | D | 300 | — | | 16 | Final QC | I | 22 | 1 | | 17 | Move to block shop | T | 14 | 1 | --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A (a):** The construction procedure for an OPC is as follows: 1. **Define the objective** — Identify the component (e.g., hull plate panel) and its boundaries. 2. **Decide data source** — Direct observation on shop floor OR engineering drawing study. 3. **List events chronologically** — Record each event from receiving raw plate to final dispatch. 4. **Classify each event** — Match to one of the 5 ASME symbols (O, I, T, S, D). 5. **Plot symbols vertically** — Draw in sequence using standard shapes. 6. **Record time data** — Use stopwatch time study or PMTS data. 7. **Add descriptions and locations** — Write brief description and workshop name beside each symbol. 8. **Summarize** — Tally counts and compute process efficiency. **Sketch** (description in words): The OPC has a vertical line in the centre, symbols drawn one below the other in chronological order. To the left, a vertical bar chart shows time. To the right, a description column lists each activity. Below the chart, a summary table gives counts. --- #### **Model Solution for Q.A (b):** **Step 1: Classify and sum time by symbol** $$ T_O = 50 + 40 + 45 + 70 + 100 = 305 \text{ min} $$ $$ T_I = 12 + 18 + 30 + 22 = 82 \text{ min} $$ $$ T_T = 6 + 8 + 9 + 11 + 14 = 48 \text{ min} $$ $$ T_S = 12 \text{ min} $$ $$ T_D = 25 + 300 = 325 \text{ min} $$ **Step 2: Total Cycle Time** $$ T_{cycle} = 305 + 82 + 48 + 12 + 325 = 772 \text{ min} $$ $$ T_{cycle} = \frac{772}{60} \approx 12.87 \text{ hours} $$ **Step 3: Process Efficiency** $$ \eta = \frac{T_O}{T_{cycle}} \times 100 = \frac{305}{772} \times 100 \approx 39.5 \text{ \%} $$ **Step 4: Man-Hours Calculation** $$ MH = (50 \times 1) + (40 \times 2) + (45 \times 2) + (70 \times 3) + (100 \times 2) $$ $$ MH = 50 + 80 + 90 + 210 + 200 = 630 \text{ man-minutes} $$ $$ MH = \frac{630}{60} = 10.5 \text{ man-hours per panel} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > - Students often forget that **Storage and Delay times are non-zero** — include them in $T_{cycle}$ but **exclude from MH calculation**. > - Process Efficiency is computed using **sum of operation times**, NOT the **number of operations**. > - Crew size multiplies **only operation time**, not all symbols. --- ### **Question B — Option 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **role and importance of Operation Process Charts in ship production management**. How does the OPC integrate with ERP systems in modern shipyards? **(b) [7 Marks]** A shipyard is experiencing delays in hull block fabrication. Using the concept of OPC, propose a **method improvement plan** with quantitative justification. Assume present cycle time = 800 min, and propose three improvements that reduce it to 600 min. --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B (a):** The **Operation Process Chart** plays the following roles in ship production: 1. **Process Documentation:** It creates a permanent record of every operation, inspection, and movement — essential for ISO 9001 and classification society audits (DNV, ABS, IRS). 2. **Time Standardization:** Provides baseline times for each operation, used for quotation, scheduling, and bonus payments. 3. **Production Planning:** Helps planners sequence work across multiple workshops (Steel Shop → Welding Bay → Paint Shop). 4. **Cost Control:** Enables accurate cost estimation by linking times to labour and overhead rates. 5. **Method Study:** Serves as the "Present Method" chart, from which a "Proposed Method" chart is derived after improvement. 6. **Communication:** Common reference for design, production, QA, and procurement teams. **Integration with ERP (e.g., SAP S/4HANA or Aveva Marine):** - OPC events are converted into **routing records** in the ERP. - Each symbol corresponds to a **work center** (machine/workstation) in the system. - Time data becomes **standard values** for capacity planning. - Real-time shop-floor execution (MES) feeds back actual times for variance analysis. --- #### **Model Solution for Q.B (b):** **Present State:** $T_{cycle,old} = 800 \text{ min}$, $\eta_{old} = \frac{400}{800} = 50 \text{ \%}$ **Proposed Improvements:** | # | Improvement | Time Saved | Justification | |---|-------------|-----------|---------------| | 1 | Replace air-drying with **oven-drying** | 150 min | Reduces paint-drying delay | | 2 | **Combine Transport 3 & 4** by relocating welding bay next to bender | 20 min | Layout improvement | | 3 | Use **automated NDT** instead of manual | 30 min | Reduces inspection time | **New Cycle Time:** $$ T_{cycle,new} = 800 - (150 + 20 + 30) = 600 \text{ min} $$ **Percentage Improvement:** $$ \text{Improvement} = \frac{200}{800} \times 100 = 25 \text{ \%} $$ **New Efficiency** (assuming operation time stays at 400 min): $$ \eta_{new} = \frac{400}{600} \times 100 \approx 66.7 \text{ \%} $$ **Conclusion:** A 25 % reduction in cycle time and a 16.7 % absolute rise in process efficiency make the proposed method significantly superior. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:** > > - **Definition:** OPC = graphic record of all events on a work-piece using 5 ASME symbols. > - **Type:** Man-type process chart (records operations, not worker movements). > - **Five Symbols:** **O** Operation, **I** Inspection, **T** Transport, **S** Storage, **D** Delay. > - **Value-Adding:** Only **Operations** add value to the product. > - **Efficiency Formula:** $\eta = (T_O / T_{cycle}) \times 100$ — target is **> 80 %**. > - **Man-Hours:** Sum of (Operation time × crew size) for each operation. > - **Applications in Shipbuilding:** Plate processing, block fabrication, pipe spool fabrication, outfitting assembly, painting, and module erection. > - **Tools derived from OPC:** Route Sheets, Gantt Charts, ERP routings, method study comparisons. > - **Limitation:** OPC does NOT show *worker movements* — for that, use **Two-Hand Process Chart**. > - **Limitation:** OPC does NOT show *spatial flow* — for that, use **Flow Diagram**. > - **Common Exam Mistake:** Confusing OPC (man-type) with Flow Process Chart (material-type). > - **Rule of thumb:** In shipyards, **delays account for 30–50 %** of cycle time — biggest target for improvement. ---

topics.title: Flow Process Charts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST633 - SHIP PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flow Process Charts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Flow Process Charts

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flow Process Chart (FPC):** A graphic and symbolic representation of all the activities (operations, inspections, transports, delays, and storages) that occur during the manufacture of a product, or the sequence of operations carried out by a man, a machine, or a material, from the beginning of the process to the end.

According to **KTU PENST633 (2024 Scheme)** and the standard industrial-engineering text by **ILO (Introduction to Work Study)**, the Flow Process Chart is the *mother tool* of process analysis. It records **what happens, where it happens, who does it, and how long it takes** in a single two-dimensional sheet.

There are three classical types recognised by the syllabus:

1. **Man-type Flow Process Chart** – records *what a worker does* (his motions, walking, waiting).
2. **Material-type Flow Process Chart** – records *what happens to the material* (the part being made, from raw plate to installed component).
3. **Machine/Equipment-type Flow Process Chart** – records *how a machine/equipment is utilised* (CNC, welding set, crane) over a period.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** The question "Distinguish between the three types of flow process charts" carries a 7-mark weightage almost every cycle. Memorise the *subject* of recording in each case.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "GPS of the Workshop"

Imagine you order food from a **cloud kitchen**. The restaurant gives you a live tracking screen that shows your parcel:

*It leaves the kitchen → moves on a bike → stops at a traffic light (delay) → arrives at your home (operation: handover) → you inspect it (inspection) → it is in your fridge (storage).*

A **Flow Process Chart** does exactly this for a steel plate being welded into a ship’s bulkhead, or for a welder doing his shift, or for a welding machine cutting steel. It is the **GPS of the workshop** — telling you the exact route, the stoppages, and the wasted kilometres (delays & reworks) so you can re-route the journey efficiently.

### 1.3 The Five Standard ANSI / ASME Symbols

Every Flow Process Chart uses the universally accepted **five basic process symbols**. These were codified by the **American Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME)** and are adopted by **KTU 2024 Scheme**.

| Symbol | Activity | Definition (KTU Verbatim) |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **○** | **Operation** | A purposeful change in the physical or chemical characteristics of an object, or the planning/calculation step. |
| **□** | **Inspection** | An activity of checking quality, quantity, or attributes of an object. |
| **→** | **Transport** | Movement of an object from one location to another (excluding movement *during* an operation). |
| **D** | **Delay** | An event (often a wait) when immediate action cannot be taken; the object is held up. |
| **▽** | **Storage** | An event when an object is kept and protected against unauthorised removal (a permanent hold). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic to remember the five symbols:** **O-I-T-D-S** → **"O**ver **I**nspection **T**akes **D**elay **S**torage" or simply think of it as **Operations – Inspection – Transport – Delay – Storage** in the natural life-cycle of a part.

### 1.4 What the Chart Looks Like — The Standard KTU Table Layout

A typical KTU board-exam answer will reproduce the chart as a ruled table with the following columns:

| Sl. No. | Symbol | Activity Description | Time (min) | Distance (m) | Remarks |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | ○ | Cut plate from stock | 8 | – | CNC plasma |
| 2 | → | Move plate to bending bay | – | 25 m | Forklift |
| 3 | ○ | Bend plate to angle | 6 | – | Press brake |
| 4 | □ | Inspect bend angle | 1 | – | With template |
| 5 | D | Wait for next shift | 30 | – | Resource hold |
| … | … | … | … | … | … |

The student then **counts symbols** at the bottom to give a summary.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind the Flow Process Chart

The fundamental purpose is to convert a chaotic, verbal account of work into a **concise, analysable, visual map**. Once the map is in hand, management can answer four questions:

1. **What is being done?** (Activity description)
2. **Where is it being done?** (Location)
3. **When is it being done?** (Time, sequence)
4. **How is it being done?** (Method, operator, machine)

By counting and classifying the activities, the engineer discovers the **non-proportionate dominance of non-value-adding work** (transports and delays) in shipbuilding — historically **70–80 %** of total throughput time.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Constructing a FPC

Following the ILO/KTU prescribed methodology:

* **Step 1 – Define the scope:** Decide *what* is being charted (a single plate? a complete block assembly? one welder? one welding transformer?).
* **Step 2 – Define the boundaries:** Specify the start point (e.g., plate leaves the stockyard) and end point (e.g., welded panel enters the paint bay).
* **Step 3 – Direct observation / method study:** Walk the route; record every event as it occurs. Use a **stop-watch** for time and a **measuring tape / pedometer** for distance.
* **Step 4 – Symbolise each event:** Place the appropriate symbol (○, □, →, D, ▽) in column 2.
* **Step 5 – Write the activity description:** Be terse, begin with a verb (e.g., *“Inspect weld toe for porosity”*).
* **Step 6 – Tabulate time and distance:** Enter the measured value; mark with an em-dash (–) if not applicable.
* **Step 7 – Summarise:** At the bottom, **sum the symbols and the times**.
* **Step 8 – Analyse (Questioning Technique):** Apply the **5-W 1-H** and **ECRS principles** (Eliminate, Combine, Rearrange, Simplify) to propose improvements.

### 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are the **only quantitative relationships** you need for any numerical KTU question on Flow Process Charts. Memorise the units carefully.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Activity Ratio (AR)} &= \frac{\text{Number of Operations}}{\text{Total Number of Activities}} \times 100\;[\%] \\[6pt]
\text{Operation Ratio (OR)} &= \frac{\text{Operation Time}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}} \times 100\;[\%] \\[6pt]
\text{Value-Added Ratio} &= \frac{\text{Value-Added Time}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}} \times 100\;[\%] \\[6pt]
\text{Non-Value-Added Ratio} &= \frac{\text{Delay Time} + \text{Transport Time}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}} \times 100\;[\%] \\[6pt]
\text{Inspection Ratio} &= \frac{\text{Number of Inspections}}{\text{Total Activities}} \times 100\;[\%] \\[6pt]
\text{Average Transport Distance} &= \frac{\sum \text{Transport Distances (m)}}{\text{Number of Transports}} \;[\text{m/transport}]
\end{aligned}
$$

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Engineering Interpretation in Shipbuilding |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Operation time | $T_O$ | seconds / minutes | Adds value to the plate/block |
| Inspection time | $T_I$ | seconds / minutes | Adds to QA cost, not intrinsic value |
| Transport time | $T_T$ | seconds / minutes | Pure waste — minimise |
| Delay time | $T_D$ | seconds / minutes | Pure waste — biggest shipyard killer |
| Storage time | $T_S$ | minutes / hours | Acceptable only for **process buffers** |
| Total cycle time | $T_C = T_O + T_I + T_T + T_D + T_S$ | seconds | **Lead time** for one unit |
| Transport distance | $d_T$ | metres | Use **pedometer / laser range-finder** |
| Throughput | $\eta = \frac{1}{T_C}$ | units / hour | Higher $\eta$ → leaner yard |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder:** In KTU valuation, the examiner checks the **units** of every numerical answer. Writing *“Activity Ratio = 0.74”* without the *“× 100 → 74 %”* is the **most common mark-deduction cause**.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Ship Production

* **Block-Erection Sequence Optimisation:** Before erecting a 200-tonne superblock, the FPC reveals the transport-to-delay ratio. If delays exceed **30 %**, the yard adds a **sub-block pre-assembly bay** to compress lead time.
* **Welding Transformer Utilisation:** A machine-type FPC on a Lincoln-Power-Wave shows that the transformer is **in use only 22 %** of the shift — the FPC becomes the basis for **fleet reduction** and **rental savings**.
* **Welder Productivity Studies (Man-type FPC):** Used by industrial engineers to redesign the workstation in the **panel-line shop**, reducing welder walking distance from **800 m/shift to 250 m/shift** — a typical Kaizen result.
* **Lean-4.0 / Digital-Twin Baseline:** Modern shipyards (Daewoo, Hyundai, Cochin Shipyard) digitise the FPC into a **Value-Stream Mapping (VSM)** software; the symbols become real-time RFID triggers on each plate.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Standard Process Efficiency Equation

Let us start from the *definition* of the total cycle time and then re-arrange the ILO work-study identity.

**Step 1 — Definition of Cycle Time.**

The time a single unit (a plate, a sub-assembly, a ship) spends inside the system boundary is the sum of the five activity times:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_C &= T_O + T_I + T_T + T_D + T_S
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the Process Efficiency $\eta_p$.**

Process efficiency is the *fraction* of cycle time that actually adds intrinsic value to the part. In shipbuilding, **only operations (O)** and **necessary inspections (I)** add value; the rest is waste per the **Lean Manufacturing doctrine**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_p &= \frac{T_O + T_I}{T_C} = \frac{T_O + T_I}{T_O + T_I + T_T + T_D + T_S}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Express in Percentage.**

Multiply by 100 to express in percent (KTU 2024 Scheme convention):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_p\;[\%] &= \frac{T_O + T_I}{T_C} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Waste Fraction (the complement).**

By the **partition-of-unity property** of ratios:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Waste fraction} &= 1 - \eta_p = \frac{T_T + T_D + T_S}{T_C}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the derivation matters:** KTU question stems often give you *only* $T_O$, $T_I$ and *the delay and transport totals*. You must recognise that **storage** is *implicitly zero* unless stated, and substitute cleanly. Skipping this step is the number-one reason for 0.5 mark deductions in Section-A of the answer booklet.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — A Shipyard Plate-Process

**Question (modelled on KTU Dec 2023 ESE pattern):**
A 12 mm thick mild-steel plate is moved through a fabrication bay. The following activities are observed:

* 4 Operations (cut, roll, weld, drill) of 6, 4, 18, 3 min respectively.
* 2 Inspections of 1.5 and 2 min.
* 3 Transports over distances of 12 m, 18 m, 9 m, with a forklift speed of **1.5 m/s**.
* 1 Delay of 28 min (waiting for a welder).
* 1 Storage of 240 min (overnight hold).

Compute **(a)** total cycle time, **(b)** process efficiency, **(c)** activity ratio, and **(d)** average transport distance.

**Solution:**

**(a) Total Cycle Time.**

Summing operation and inspection times first:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum T_O &= 6 + 4 + 18 + 3 = 31\;\text{min} \\[4pt]
\sum T_I &= 1.5 + 2 = 3.5\;\text{min} \\[4pt]
T_T &= \frac{12 + 18 + 9}{1.5} = \frac{39}{1.5} = 26\;\text{s} = \frac{26}{60}\;\text{min} \approx 0.433\;\text{min} \\[4pt]
T_D &= 28\;\text{min} \\[4pt]
T_S &= 240\;\text{min} \\[4pt]
T_C &= 31 + 3.5 + 0.433 + 28 + 240 = 302.93\;\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Process Efficiency.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_p &= \frac{31 + 3.5}{302.93} \times 100 = \frac{34.5}{302.93} \times 100 \approx 11.39\;\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Only **≈ 11.4 %** of the plate’s residence time adds real value — a classic *“shipyard signature”* of high inventory, high WIP, low lean maturity.

**(c) Activity Ratio.**

Total activities = 4 O + 2 I + 3 T + 1 D + 1 S = **11 activities**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AR} &= \frac{4}{11} \times 100 \approx 36.36\;\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(d) Average Transport Distance.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{d}_T &= \frac{12 + 18 + 9}{3} = \frac{39}{3} = 13\;\text{m/transport}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Symbol vs Decision

| Question on the FPC | Use This KTU Verdict |
| :--- | :--- |
| “Is this activity an *Operation* or an *Inspection*?” | If the *character* of the object changes → ○. If the *quality* is verified → □. |
| “Is this a *Delay* or a *Storage*?” | If the part can be taken out anytime by authorised person → **D**. If locked / protected / controlled → **▽**. |
| “Is a 5-m plate move an *Operation* or a *Transport*?” | If the move is *part of* an operation (e.g., rolling, flipping) → ○. If it is *between* workplaces → **→**. |
| “How to count activity ratio?” | Count all five symbols in the summary cell, then divide operations by total. |
| “What unit for time in FPC?” | **Always** convert to a *single unit* (KTU prefers minutes for FPC, seconds for detailed *flow diagrams*). |
| “What about man vs material chart?” | Same symbols, **different subject**: *who* does it (man) vs *what happens to* the part (material). |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner’s trap:** A welder *holding* the electrode while the parent plate is being set is **NOT a delay**; it is an *operation* (clamping/positioning). Conversely, a part lying on a rack between two shifts is **NOT a transport**; it is a **delay (D)** unless officially booked in stores.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of FPC Construction

The diagram below maps the **engineering logic** of how a Flow Process Chart is built, starting from a real shipyard operation and ending in a management decision. (Rendered in Mermaid for clarity — no physical drawing is attempted.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Study Scope: e.g. one plate] --> B[Fix Start and End Boundary]
    B --> C[Direct Observation at Site]
    C --> D{Activity Type?}
    D -->|Object Character Changes| E[Operation: O]
    D -->|Quality Check Only| F[Inspection: I]
    D -->|Position Changes| G[Transport: Tr]
    D -->|Idle Hold| H[Delay: D]
    D -->|Locked Hold| I[Storage: S]
    E --> J[Tabulate in FPC Sheet]
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Measure Time using Stop Watch]
    J --> L[Measure Distance using Tape]
    K --> M[Compute Activity Ratio AR]
    K --> N[Compute Process Efficiency eta]
    L --> O[Compute Avg Transport Distance]
    M --> P{Analyse: Waste > 30 percent?}
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P -->|Yes| Q[Apply ECRS: Eliminate Combine Rearrange Simplify]
    P -->|No| R[Document as Best Method]
    Q --> S[Redesign Shipyard Layout]
    R --> T[Publish Standard Operating Procedure]
    S --> T
```

### 4.2 Logical Decision Tree — Choosing the FPC Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need to Record a Process] --> Q1{What is the SUBJECT of study?}
    Q1 -->|The Worker / Welder / Fitter| Man[Man Type FPC]
    Q1 -->|The Material / Plate / Block| Mat[Material Type FPC]
    Q1 -->|The Equipment / Crane / Machine| Mach[Machine Type FPC]
    Man --> X1[Captures Human Effort, Fatigue, Walking]
    Mat --> X2[Captures Value Add, WIP, Bottlenecks]
    Mach --> X3[Captures Utilisation, Idle Time, Downtime]
```

### 4.3 Hierarchical Placement of the FPC in Work-Study

```mermaid
graph LR
    WS[Work Study ILO Framework] --> MT[Method Study]
    WS --> WM[Work Measurement]
    MT --> PS[Process Charts]
    MT --> FC[Flow Diagrams]
    MT --> LA[Layout Analysis]
    MT --> MO[Motion Study]
    PS --> OPM[Operation Process Chart]
    PS --> FPC[Flow Process Chart: Man Material Machine]
    PS --> THP[Two Hand Process Chart]
    PS --> SOP[String Diagram]
    FPC -.->|Baseline Data| VSM[Value Stream Map for Lean 4.0]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid for the Diagram:** Notice that the **Flow Process Chart sits inside the Process Charts family**, which in turn sits inside the **Method Study** wing of the parent **Work Study** discipline. This is the exact hierarchy KTU expects in a 7-mark “scope and family” answer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** Define a *Flow Process Chart*. Mention the five symbols used in it with one-line significance.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *Flow Process Chart* is a graphic and symbolic representation of all activities (operation, inspection, transport, delay, storage) that occur in a process, recorded in their correct sequence, with the time and distance for each.
The five symbols are:

* **○ Operation** – purposeful change in object.
* **□ Inspection** – checking quality / quantity.
* **→ Transport** – movement between locations.
* **D Delay** – temporary wait, object is idle.
* **▽ Storage** – protected hold, object is locked.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner’s Pitfall:** Students often write *“inspection = inspection”* and lose **1 mark** for failing to specify that it is a *quality / quantity check*. Always write the *purpose* of the symbol.

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** Distinguish briefly between *Operation Process Chart* and *Flow Process Chart*.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Operation Process Chart** shows only the *main operations* and *inspections* in their sequence, omitting transport, delay, and storage. It is a *macro* tool used for initial method planning. The **Flow Process Chart** records *all five* activities (O, I, T, D, S) with their time and distance, giving a *micro* view of the entire process and a complete waste analysis. Thus, every Flow Process Chart is more detailed than an Operation Process Chart.

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Set B]`**

**(a) Construct a Material-type Flow Process Chart for the fabrication of a 2-m × 1-m steel panel from raw plate to a finished welded sub-assembly, given the following observed sequence:** (raw plate 6 mm, cut on plasma → move to edge-prep bay → grind edges → inspect edge profile → move to fit-up bay → tack weld stiffeners → inspect tack welds → move to welding bay → full weld stiffeners → inspect final weld → NDT inspection → move to paint bay → hold for paint → paint → dry/cure 480 min → store in dispatch yard).

**(b) Compute the (i) Activity Ratio, (ii) Operation Ratio, (iii) Process Efficiency, and (iv) suggest two improvements using the ECRS principles.**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Flow Process Chart Table (7 marks):**

| Sl. No. | Symbol | Activity Description | Time (min) | Distance (m) |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | ○ | Plasma-cut 6 mm plate | 5 | – |
| 2 | → | Move plate to edge-prep bay | 1 | 30 |
| 3 | ○ | Grind edges to bevel | 8 | – |
| 4 | □ | Inspect edge bevel angle | 1.5 | – |
| 5 | → | Move to fit-up bay | 1 | 25 |
| 6 | ○ | Tack-weld stiffeners | 6 | – |
| 7 | □ | Inspect tack-weld quality | 1 | – |
| 8 | → | Move to welding bay | 1.2 | 35 |
| 9 | ○ | Full-weld stiffeners (FCAW) | 22 | – |
| 10 | □ | Visual inspect final weld | 2 | – |
| 11 | □ | NDT inspection (MPI) | 6 | – |
| 12 | → | Move to paint bay | 1 | 20 |
| 13 | D | Wait for paint slot | 45 | – |
| 14 | ○ | Apply primer + top coat | 14 | – |
| 15 | D | Cure in oven | 480 | – |
| 16 | ▽ | Store in dispatch yard | – | – |

**Summary of symbols:** O = 5, I = 4, T = 4, D = 2, S = 1, **Total activities = 16**.

**(b) Quantitative Analysis (7 marks):**

**(i) Activity Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AR} &= \frac{5}{16} \times 100 = 31.25\;\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Operation Ratio:**

Total time = 5+8+6+22+14 = **55 min (Operations)**; Total cycle time = 5+1+8+1.5+1+6+1+1.2+22+2+6+1+45+14+480 = **593.7 min**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OR} &= \frac{55}{593.7} \times 100 \approx 9.26\;\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Process Efficiency:**

Value-added time (Operations + Inspections) = 55 + 13.5 = 68.5 min.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_p &= \frac{68.5}{593.7} \times 100 \approx 11.54\;\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Improvements using ECRS (any two valid suggestions carry full marks):**

1. **Eliminate the cure wait (480 min)** by introducing a *forced-air-drying tunnel* — this is the single largest waste in the process.
2. **Combine transports** by re-laying out the bay so that *cutting → edge-prep → fit-up → welding* form a **U-shaped line**, reducing transport distance from **110 m to 35 m**.
3. **Rearrange** the paint bay *next* to the welding bay to remove the 45-min wait for the paint slot.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner’s Warning (Part B, Q.A):** Marks are frequently *lost* because students forget to **convert the cure time** to minutes (already in minutes here) and **sum the totals correctly** at the bottom. Always **show the summary row of totals** in the FPC table — the examiner verifies it before reading the calculations.

---

#### **Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Set A]`**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch (block diagram), explain the *five basic symbols* of a Flow Process Chart as per ASME standards. List the **three types** of flow process charts and state **one engineering situation** in a shipyard where each is most appropriate.

**(b) For a Man-type Flow Process Chart of a fitter working on engine-room pipe supports, the following data are observed:**
* 6 Operations (3, 2, 4, 5, 1.5, 2 min)
* 3 Inspections (1, 1.5, 1 min)
* 4 Transports (1, 0.8, 0.6, 0.5 min)
* 2 Delays (8, 12 min)

Compute the **Activity Ratio**, **Process Efficiency** and the **Idle (Delay) Fraction**. Comment on the result and suggest a **technological improvement** to reduce the delays.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theory Part (7 marks):**

The **five ASME symbols** (already listed in Section 1.3) form the backbone. The *three types* are:

1. **Man-type FPC** — best used to study the **fatigue, walking and waiting** of a **welder on the panel line** of a ship.
2. **Material-type FPC** — best used to track the **flow of a curved shell plate** from the steel stockyard to its welded position on the hull.
3. **Machine/Equipment-type FPC** — best used to record the **utilisation of a 600-T gantry crane** in the erection bay.

**Sketch credit:** A clean labelled block diagram showing the five symbols with their names earns 3 of the 7 marks.

**(b) Quantitative Part (7 marks):**

Totalling the categories:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Operations} &= 3+2+4+5+1.5+2 = 17.5\;\text{min} \\[3pt]
\text{Inspections} &= 1+1.5+1 = 3.5\;\text{min} \\[3pt]
\text{Transports} &= 1+0.8+0.6+0.5 = 2.9\;\text{min} \\[3pt]
\text{Delays} &= 8+12 = 20\;\text{min} \\[3pt]
\text{Total activities} &= 6+3+4+2 = 15 \\[3pt]
T_C &= 17.5 + 3.5 + 2.9 + 20 = 43.9\;\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Activity Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AR} &= \frac{6}{15} \times 100 = 40\;\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Process Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_p &= \frac{17.5 + 3.5}{43.9} \times 100 = \frac{21}{43.9} \times 100 \approx 47.84\;\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Idle (Delay) Fraction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Delay \%} &= \frac{20}{43.9} \times 100 \approx 45.56\;\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** The fitter is *idle for nearly half his shift* — a sign of poor kit-bring, slow transport of pipes, or waiting for an upstream fitting to be released. **Technological improvement:** Introduce a *mechanised pipe-handling trolley on a monorail loop* to remove the need for the fitter to walk to the pipe-rack; this directly attacks the **20 min of delay** and the four transport steps.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Part B, Q.B):** Students *add the totals in the wrong column* (e.g., put transports in the *Time* column without converting seconds to minutes). Always **carry units** in the calculation. Also, the comment carries **2 of the 7 marks** — never skip it, even if the numerical answer is correct.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* Flow Process Chart = *the visual map of a process* using the **five ASME symbols** (O, I, T, D, S).
* Three types: **Man, Material, Machine** — same symbols, different subject.
* Construction sequence: **scope → boundaries → observe → symbolise → tabulate → summarise → analyse (ECRS)**.
* Key formulas: **AR, OR, $\eta_p$ (process efficiency), Delay fraction, Average transport distance**.
* Process efficiency in shipyards is **typically 10 – 25 %**; the rest is transport, delay, and storage — a Lean-improvement opportunity.
* The **cure / paint / overnight hold** is *always* the largest single waste term in a shipyard FPC.
* Always **convert time to a single unit** and **show the summary row** in the FPC table.
* Memorise the **KTU pyramid**: Work Study → Method Study → Process Charts → Flow Process Chart.
* **Mnemonic for symbols:** *“Operations Inspects Trucks during Delays and Storage”* → **O I T D S**.
* In KTU valuation: **units (1 mark), summary table (1 mark), and the qualitative comment (2 marks)** are the *hidden marks* most students miss.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 30-Second Exam Strategy:** For any 14-mark FPC question, structure your answer as **(1) brief theory + sketch (3 marks) → (2) FPC table with summary row (5 marks) → (3) numerical analysis with formulas (4 marks) → (4) ECRS-based improvement (2 marks)**. This order maps perfectly to the KTU 2024 Scheme answer-key weightage pattern.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST633 - SHIP PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Selection** is the strategic engineering decision-making activity in ship production management that involves identifying, evaluating, and choosing the most appropriate manufacturing processes, equipment, tooling, workforce skills, and material handling systems required to convert raw materials (steel plates, profiles, pipes) into finished ship components and assemblies, while satisfying design specifications, quality standards, cost targets, delivery schedules, and production volume requirements.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **Ship Production Management (PENST633)**, Process Selection is positioned as a **foundational pillar** of production planning that directly influences:
- **Capital Investment (CapEx)** — yard infrastructure, CNC machines, robotic welders
- **Operational Expenditure (OpEx)** — labour, consumables, energy
- **Throughput & Cycle Time** — tonnes of steel fabricated per day
- **Product Quality** — dimensional accuracy, weld integrity (Class society compliance)
- **Workplace Safety & Ergonomics**

The discipline sits at the intersection of **Industrial Engineering, Manufacturing Technology, and Naval Architecture**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Master Chef's Kitchen Decision**

Imagine you run a coastal restaurant in Kerala that must serve **10 customers a day** versus **10,000 customers a day**.

| Scenario | Customer Volume | Your Decision (Analogy) | Shipyard Equivalent |
|----------|----------------|-------------------------|---------------------|
| Small eatery | 10/day | Hand-chop vegetables on a wooden board with a single knife | Manual flame cutting, stick welding |
| Mid-scale canteen | 500/day | Use a mixer grinder + semi-skilled cooks | Semi-automatic SAW, panel line |
| Industrial kitchen | 10,000/day | CNC vegetable processors, conveyor ovens, robotic arms | Robotic welding, CNC plasma/laser, panel/block production line |

You would **never** put a CNC robotic arm in a small tea stall, nor would you hand-cut vegetables for 10,000 customers. **Process Selection is exactly this decision** — matching the **production volume, complexity, quality, and cost** demands to the **right combination of processes and equipment**.

In shipbuilding:
- A **naval vessel hull** (low volume, high customization) → Job-shop processes
- A **bulk carrier series** (medium-high volume, repeatability) → Batch production
- A **standardised barge or container ship series** (high volume) → Mass/continuous production lines

---

## 1.3 Why Process Selection Matters in Shipbuilding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Strategic Significance**
> 1. Ships are **one-of-a-kind or low-volume** capital goods — but built from thousands of standardized sub-assemblies.
> 2. Steel constitutes **60–70%** of shipbuilding cost — process selection directly governs steel fabrication economics.
> 3. **Welding** is the dominant joining process — **40–50%** of shipyard man-hours.
> 4. Poor process selection leads to **rework, distortion, weld defects, schedule overruns, and class survey rejections**.

---

## 1.4 Scope of Process Selection in PENST633

The following **core decision areas** fall under Process Selection:

```
Process Selection
├── Primary Forming Processes (Rolling, Bending, Pressing)
├── Cutting Processes (Oxy-fuel, Plasma, Laser, Waterjet)
├── Joining Processes (SMAW, GMAW, SAW, FCAW, GTAW, FSW)
├── Surface Treatment (Blasting, Painting, Galvanizing)
├── Assembly Strategy (Erection method: piece-small, block, modular)
├── Material Handling (Cranes, transporters, self-propelled modules)
└── Inspection & NDT (UT, RT, MPI, DPI)
```

---

## 1.5 The KTU 2024 Scheme Course Outcome Linkage

| CO Code | Course Outcome | Process Selection Linkage |
|---------|---------------|---------------------------|
| CO2 | Plan and control ship production activities | Selecting processes is the **first planning step** |
| CO3 | Apply modern production tools in shipbuilding | Selection incorporates **Industry 4.0 readiness** |
| CO4 | Optimize resources in yard operations | Selection directly determines **resource utilization** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Process Selection Framework (Systematic Approach)

> [!NOTE]
> Process selection in ship production follows a structured **6-step framework** aligned with KTU Module 2 expectations:

### Step 1 — Define Product Requirements
- **Geometric complexity**: Plate thickness, curvature, tolerances
- **Material specification**: Grade of steel (e.g., AH36, DH36, EH36)
- **Quality standards**: IACS Class rules, ISO 9001, AWS D1.1
- **Production volume**: Number of sister ships in series
- **Delivery deadline**: Keel-laying to delivery timeline

### Step 2 — Identify Candidate Processes
Generate a long-list of all technically feasible processes. For example, for **edge preparation of 20 mm thick hull plate**:
- Oxy-fuel (flame) cutting
- Plasma cutting
- Laser cutting
- Waterjet cutting
- Machining (milling)
- Shearing

### Step 3 — Apply Screening Criteria (Eliminate infeasible options)
- **Process capability index** ($C_p$ and $C_{pk}$) must exceed design tolerance
- **Material compatibility** with the process
- **Available thickness range** of equipment
- **Existing yard infrastructure** constraints

### Step 4 — Economic Evaluation (Compare survivors)
- Fixed cost (machine investment, tooling)
- Variable cost (consumables, labour per metre)
- Break-even analysis
- Payback period

### Step 5 — Non-Economic Evaluation
- Flexibility for future products
- Skill availability in regional workforce
- Safety and ergonomics
- Environmental compliance (emissions, noise)

### Step 6 — Final Selection & Documentation
The chosen process is documented in the **Process Route Sheet (PRS)** and forms part of the **Process Plan**.

---

## 2.2 Factors Affecting Process Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Big Nine Factors in Ship Production**

| # | Factor | Engineering Consideration |
|---|--------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Production Volume & Rate** | Higher volume → higher mechanization/automation |
| 2 | **Product Design (Geometry)** | Curved hull plates favour cold/hot forming |
| 3 | **Material Properties** | High-tensile steel needs controlled-heat-input welding |
| 4 | **Quality Requirements** | Critical joints → automate (SAW/GMAW robotic) |
| 5 | **Available Capital** | Yard budget constrains technology tier |
| 6 | **Labour Skill Availability** | Kerala shipyards — semi-skilled labour dominant |
| 7 | **Lead Time** | Tight schedules → prefabrication & block construction |
| 8 | **Flexibility Required** | One-off navy vessels → CNC flexible systems |
| 9 | **Ergonomics & Safety** | Confined spaces → mechanized/robotic solutions |

---

## 2.3 Classification of Production Systems (Volume-Variety Matrix)

> [!NOTE]
> **T.P. Wright's Product-Process Matrix** is the foundational classification:

| Production Type | Volume | Variety | Process Layout | Shipbuilding Example |
|-----------------|--------|---------|----------------|----------------------|
| **Job Shop (Project)** | Very Low | Very High | Functional (by process) | Naval warship, research vessel |
| **Batch** | Low–Medium | High | Cellular / Group | Offshore patrol vessels (3–6 units) |
| **Mass** | High | Low | Line / Assembly line | Standard bulk carriers (10+ series) |
| **Continuous** | Very High | Very Low | Flow / Continuous | Sub-assembly panels, pipe spools |

---

## 2.4 Process Capability Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Capability Index** measures whether a process can consistently produce output within design tolerances.

The standard indices are:

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$

$$C_{pk} = \min\left[\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right]$$

where:
- **USL** = Upper Specification Limit
- **LSL** = Lower Specification Limit
- $\sigma$ = standard deviation of the process
- $\mu$ = process mean

**Interpretation Rule:**
- $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ → Process is **capable**
- $1.00 \leq C_{pk} < 1.33$ → Process is **marginally capable** (monitor)
- $C_{pk} < 1.00$ → Process is **NOT capable** — do NOT select

---

## 2.5 Break-Even Analysis (The Core Economic Tool)

> [!NOTE]
> Used to compare two alternative processes on a **purely cost basis**.

Let:
- $F_1, F_2$ = Fixed costs of Process 1 and Process 2
- $V_1, V_2$ = Variable costs per unit of Process 1 and Process 2
- $Q$ = Annual production quantity (units or metres)

**Total Cost Equations:**

$$TC_1 = F_1 + V_1 \cdot Q$$

$$TC_2 = F_2 + V_2 \cdot Q$$

**Break-Even Point (where $TC_1 = TC_2$):**

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{F_2 - F_1}{V_1 - V_2}$$

**Decision Rule:**
- If expected production $Q > Q_{BE}$ → Choose Process with **higher fixed cost** (more automated)
- If expected production $Q < Q_{BE}$ → Choose Process with **lower fixed cost** (manual/semi-auto)

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas — Memorize These**

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Application in Ship Production |
|---|------------------|----------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | **Break-Even Quantity** | $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F_2 - F_1}{V_1 - V_2}$ | Choosing between manual vs. robotic welding |
| 2 | **Total Cost** | $TC = F + V \cdot Q$ | Annual process cost comparison |
| 3 | **Process Capability** | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Weld bead geometry acceptance |
| 4 | **Adjusted Capability** | $C_{pk} = \min\left[\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right]$ | Centred process check |
| 5 | **Production Rate** | $R_p = \dfrac{Q}{T}$ | Tonnes of steel/day capacity planning |
| 6 | **Machine Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{\text{Loading Time}}{\text{Available Time}}$ | CNC plate processor uptime |
| 7 | **Payback Period** | $T_{pb} = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Savings}}$ | ROI of new process technology |
| 8 | **Unit Cost (learning curve)** | $Y_x = Y_1 \cdot x^{b}$ | Cost reduction with experience |
| 9 | **Learning Curve Exponent** | $b = \dfrac{\log(0.8)}{\log(2)} \approx -0.322$ | 80% learning curve standard |
| 10 | **Welding Cost per Metre** | $C_w = C_{elec} + C_{gas} + C_{wire} + C_{labour}$ | Process selection for welding |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** Always state **units** explicitly in break-even problems (e.g., "metres of weld per year" or "tonnes of steel per year"). Marks are deducted when units are missing.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Ship Production Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Engineering Applications:**
>
> 1. **Welding Process Selection** — Selecting GMAW vs. SAW vs. FCAW based on position (1G, 2G, 3G, 4G), thickness, and required deposition rate.
> 2. **Cutting Line Selection** — Plasma vs. Laser for plate profiling — laser wins for thin plates (<12 mm) and complex contours; plasma dominates for thicker plates.
> 3. **Erection Strategy** — Choosing piece-small vs. block vs. modular erection based on crane capacity and yard throughput targets.
> 4. **Outfit Prefabrication** — Pipe spools and cable ladders — selecting workshop vs. on-board fabrication.
> 5. **Digital Twin & Simulation** — Modern yards use discrete-event simulation to evaluate process alternatives before capital commitment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Break-Even Analysis for Welding Process Selection

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Problem Context:** A Cochin shipyard must select between **Manual Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** and **Semi-Automatic Flux-Cored Arc Welding (FCAW)** for butt-welding 12 mm thick mild steel hull plates. The following data is given.

| Parameter | SMAW (Process 1) | FCAW (Process 2) |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| Fixed cost (electrode oven, basic equipment, training) | ₹80,000/year | ₹4,50,000/year |
| Variable cost (labour + consumables per metre of weld) | ₹350/m | ₹180/m |

**Annual production** is estimated at $Q$ metres of butt welds per year.

---

### Derivation Step 1: Define the Total Cost Function

The total cost of any production process has **two components**:

$$TC = \text{Fixed Cost} + \text{Variable Cost} \times \text{Quantity}$$

This is the standard **linear cost-volume-profit model** used in industrial engineering. For Process 1 (SMAW):

$$TC_1 = F_1 + V_1 \cdot Q = 80{,}000 + 350 \cdot Q$$

For Process 2 (FCAW):

$$TC_2 = F_2 + V_2 \cdot Q = 4{,}50{,}000 + 180 \cdot Q$$

---

### Derivation Step 2: Equate the Two Total Costs

At the **break-even point**, the total costs of both processes are equal — neither process is economically superior:

$$TC_1 = TC_2$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$80{,}000 + 350 \cdot Q = 4{,}50{,}000 + 180 \cdot Q$$

---

### Derivation Step 3: Isolate the Variable Q

Move all $Q$ terms to one side and constants to the other:

$$350 \cdot Q - 180 \cdot Q = 4{,}50{,}000 - 80{,}000$$

$$170 \cdot Q = 3{,}70{,}000$$

---

### Derivation Step 4: Solve for Q (Break-Even Quantity)

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{3{,}70{,}000}{170}$$

$$Q_{BE} = 2{,}176.47 \text{ metres/year}$$

Rounding up for engineering safety margin:

$$Q_{BE} \approx 2{,}177 \text{ metres/year}$$

---

### Derivation Step 5: Decision Rule Application

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Logic:**
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \text{If } Q_{expected} &> Q_{BE} \Rightarrow \text{Select FCAW (higher fixed, lower variable)} \\
> \text{If } Q_{expected} &< Q_{BE} \Rightarrow \text{Select SMAW (lower fixed, higher variable)} \\
> \text{If } Q_{expected} &= Q_{BE} \Rightarrow \text{Indifferent (cost-equal)}
> \end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:**
- The FCAW equipment requires ₹4,50,000 in fixed investment but saves ₹170 per metre in consumables and labour.
- After welding **2,177 metres per year**, the savings from FCAW's lower variable cost fully recover the higher fixed investment.
- **Above this volume, FCAW is cheaper; below it, SMAW is cheaper.**

---

### Derivation Step 6: Cost Verification at Break-Even

Substitute $Q_{BE} = 2177$ into both cost equations to verify:

$$TC_1 = 80{,}000 + 350 \times 2{,}177 = 80{,}000 + 7{,}61{,}950 = 8{,}41{,}950$$

$$TC_2 = 4{,}50{,}000 + 180 \times 2{,}177 = 4{,}50{,}000 + 3{,}91{,}860 = 8{,}41{,}860$$

A small rounding difference (~₹90) confirms the correctness of $Q_{BE}$.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Selection Criteria Scoring Method

> [!NOTE]
> When multiple criteria are involved (cost, quality, flexibility, safety), use a **Weighted Scoring Model**.

**Step A — Identify Selection Criteria** with weights (must total 100):

| Criterion | Weight $w_i$ |
|-----------|-------------|
| Cost-effectiveness | 30 |
| Weld Quality | 25 |
| Flexibility | 15 |
| Labour Skill Requirement | 10 |
| Safety | 10 |
| Maintenance | 10 |
| **Total** | **100** |

**Step B — Score Each Alternative** (scale 1–10):

| Criterion | Weight | SMAW Score | FCAW Score | Robotic GMAW Score |
|-----------|--------|-----------|------------|---------------------|
| Cost-effectiveness | 30 | 6 | 8 | 7 |
| Weld Quality | 25 | 5 | 7 | 10 |
| Flexibility | 15 | 9 | 7 | 6 |
| Labour Skill | 10 | 8 | 6 | 4 |
| Safety | 10 | 4 | 6 | 9 |
| Maintenance | 10 | 9 | 6 | 5 |

**Step C — Compute Weighted Scores:**

For SMAW:
$$S_{SMAW} = (30 \times 6) + (25 \times 5) + (15 \times 9) + (10 \times 8) + (10 \times 4) + (10 \times 9)$$

$$S_{SMAW} = 180 + 125 + 135 + 80 + 40 + 90 = 650$$

For FCAW:
$$S_{FCAW} = (30 \times 8) + (25 \times 7) + (15 \times 7) + (10 \times 6) + (10 \times 6) + (10 \times 6)$$

$$S_{FCAW} = 240 + 175 + 105 + 60 + 60 + 60 = 700$$

For Robotic GMAW:
$$S_{Robot} = (30 \times 7) + (25 \times 10) + (15 \times 6) + (10 \times 4) + (10 \times 9) + (10 \times 5)$$

$$S_{Robot} = 210 + 250 + 90 + 40 + 90 + 50 = 730$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision:** Robotic GMAW has the highest weighted score (**730**) and is the recommended process, **provided the yard can afford the higher fixed cost and has the technical infrastructure**.

---

## 3.3 Process Capability Worked Example

**Problem:** A CNC plate cutting machine must cut 20 mm thick hull plate to a tolerance of ±0.5 mm. From a sample of 50 plates, the measured thickness deviation has $\mu = 0.05$ mm and $\sigma = 0.18$ mm.

**Determine if the process is capable.**

**Step 1:** Set specification limits based on tolerance:
$$\text{USL} = +0.5 \text{ mm}, \quad \text{LSL} = -0.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $C_p$:

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} = \frac{0.5 - (-0.5)}{6 \times 0.18} = \frac{1.0}{1.08} = 0.926$$

**Step 3:** Calculate $C_{pk}$:

$$C_{pk, \text{upper}} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{0.5 - 0.05}{3 \times 0.18} = \frac{0.45}{0.54} = 0.833$$

$$C_{pk, \text{lower}} = \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \frac{0.05 - (-0.5)}{3 \times 0.18} = \frac{0.55}{0.54} = 1.019$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(0.833, \, 1.019) = 0.833$$

**Step 4: Conclusion:**

> [!WARNING]
> Since $C_{pk} = 0.833 < 1.00$, **the process is NOT capable** for this tolerance specification. The yard must either tighten process control, recalibrate the CNC machine, or select a higher-precision process (e.g., laser cutting for thin plates, or fine plasma).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Selection Decision Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Product Design Available"] --> B["Identify Production Volume and Lead Time"]
    B --> C{"Is Volume Greater Than 100 Units Per Year"}
    C -- Yes --> D["Consider Mass Production Processes"]
    C -- No --> E{"Is Volume Between 10 and 100 Units Per Year"}
    E -- Yes --> F["Consider Batch Production Processes"]
    E -- No --> G["Select Job Shop or Project Production"]
    D --> H["Evaluate Process Candidates"]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I["Apply Screening Criteria"]
    I --> I1["Material Compatibility"]
    I --> I2["Process Capability Cpk Greater Than 1.33"]
    I --> I3["Available Equipment in Yard"]
    I1 --> J["Survivors Enter Economic Evaluation"]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    J --> K["Compute Break Even Quantity QBE"]
    K --> L{"Is Expected Q Greater Than QBE"}
    L -- Yes --> M["Choose Process with Higher Fixed Cost"]
    L -- No --> N["Choose Process with Lower Fixed Cost"]
    M --> O["Weighted Scoring for Non Economic Factors"]
    N --> O
    O --> P["Final Process Selected and Documented in Process Route Sheet"]
    P --> Q["End"]
```

---

## 4.2 Process Selection Hierarchy (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L1["Strategic Level"]
        S1["Business Strategy and Market Demand"]
        S2["Product Family Definition"]
    end
    subgraph L2["Tactical Level"]
        T1["Production Volume Forecast"]
        T2["Process Selection Decision"]
    end
    subgraph L3["Operational Level"]
        O1["Process Route Sheet"]
        O2["Work Instructions and SOPs"]
    end
    subgraph L4["Control Level"]
        C1["Process Capability Monitoring"]
        C2["Quality Inspection and NDT"]
    end
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
    O2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 -. Feedback Loop .-> T2
```

---

## 4.3 Product-Process Matrix Visual (Shipbuilding Context)

```mermaid
quadrantChart
    title Product Process Matrix for Ship Production
    x-axis Low Volume --> High Volume
    y-axis Low Variety --> High Variety
    quadrant-1 Mass Production Standardised Barges
    quadrant-2 Continuous Production Pipe Spool Lines
    quadrant-3 Batch Production Offshore Patrol Vessels
    quadrant-4 Job Shop Production Naval Warships
    Naval Warships: [0.15, 0.85]
    Offshore Patrol Vessels: [0.40, 0.65]
    Standardised Barges: [0.75, 0.20]
    Pipe Spool Lines: [0.90, 0.15]
    LNG Carrier Series: [0.70, 0.40]
    Bulk Carrier Series: [0.80, 0.30]
```

---

## 4.4 Welding Process Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W["Welding Process Selection"] --> W1{"Plate Thickness in mm"}
    W1 -- "Less than 3 mm" --> W2["GTAW or Pulsed GMAW"]
    W1 -- "3 to 12 mm" --> W3["GMAW or FCAW"]
    W1 -- "Greater than 12 mm" --> W4{"Position"}
    W4 -- "Flat 1G" --> W5["SAW or Electrogas"]
    W4 -- "Vertical or Overhead" --> W6["FCAW or SMAW"]
    W5 --> W7["Class Society Approval Check"]
    W6 --> W7
    W2 --> W7
    W3 --> W7
    W7 -- Approved --> W8["Final Welding Procedure Specification WPS"]
    W7 -- Not Approved --> W9["Re Evaluate Alternative Process"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Define Process Selection. List any four factors that influence process selection in ship production.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Process Selection is the engineering activity of identifying and choosing the most appropriate manufacturing processes, equipment, and tooling to produce a ship component or assembly, balancing cost, quality, volume, and flexibility requirements.

**Four Factors:**
1. **Production Volume and Rate** — Determines the level of automation feasible.
2. **Material Properties** — Steel grade and thickness govern cutting/welding process choices.
3. **Quality Requirements** — Class society rules dictate weld process control level.
4. **Available Capital and Labour Skill** — Yard budget and workforce competency.

*(Any four well-explained factors: ½ mark each = 2 marks; definition = 1 mark)*

---

> **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Explain the significance of process capability index ($C_{pk}$) in ship production with reference to its acceptance value.**

**Model Answer:**

**Significance:**
- $C_{pk}$ measures whether a process can consistently produce output within design tolerances, accounting for **process centring**.
- In ship production, $C_{pk}$ is critical for **weld bead geometry, plate cutting tolerance, and pipe bending accuracy** — all of which affect class survey approval.

**Acceptance Values:**

$$C_{pk} \geq 1.33 \rightarrow \text{Process is capable (preferred for shipbuilding)}$$

$$1.00 \leq C_{pk} < 1.33 \rightarrow \text{Marginally capable, requires monitoring}$$

$$C_{pk} < 1.00 \rightarrow \text{Not capable, process must be rejected or improved}$$

**Application:** A $C_{pk} < 1.00$ in hull plate cutting would mean frequent rework, schedule delays, and non-conformance to IACS UR S21 tolerances — unacceptable for commercial shipbuilding.

*(Definition + formula reference = 1.5 marks; interpretation in shipbuilding context = 1.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Module 2 follows the **internal choice pattern**. Attempt EITHER **Question A** OR **Question B**.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 2 Choice 1]`

A shipyard is evaluating two welding processes for fabricating 600 tonne of stiffened panel sub-assemblies annually:
- **Process P1 — Manual SMAW:** Fixed cost = ₹2,00,000/year; Variable cost = ₹420/tonne
- **Process P2 — Semi-automatic SAW:** Fixed cost = ₹12,00,000/year; Variable cost = ₹150/tonne

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Determine the break-even quantity and recommend the appropriate process for an expected annual production of 600 tonnes. Show the break-even chart qualitatively.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Write Total Cost Equations:**

$$TC_1 = 2{,}00{,}000 + 420 \cdot Q$$

$$TC_2 = 12{,}00{,}000 + 150 \cdot Q$$

**Step 2: Equate $TC_1 = TC_2$:**

$$2{,}00{,}000 + 420 Q = 12{,}00{,}000 + 150 Q$$

$$270 Q = 10{,}00{,}000$$

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{270} \approx 3{,}704 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

**Step 3: Apply Decision Rule:**
- Expected production $Q = 600$ tonnes/year
- $Q = 600 < Q_{BE} = 3{,}704$
- Therefore, **select Process P1 (Manual SMAW)**.

**Step 4: Verify by Total Cost Comparison at $Q = 600$:**

$$TC_1 = 2{,}00{,}000 + 420 \times 600 = 2{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}52{,}000 = 4{,}52{,}000$$

$$TC_2 = 12{,}00{,}000 + 150 \times 600 = 12{,}00{,}000 + 90{,}000 = 12{,}90{,}000$$

Savings with SMAW = ₹12,90,000 − ₹4,52,000 = **₹8,38,000 per year** — confirms SMAW choice.

**Step 5: Break-Even Chart (Qualitative Description):**
- X-axis: Annual production Q (tonnes)
- Y-axis: Total cost (₹)
- SMAW line: lower slope, lower intercept
- SAW line: higher slope, higher intercept
- Intersection at $Q \approx 3{,}704$ tonnes — beyond this volume, SAW is cheaper.

*(Equation setup = 1 mark; Solving $Q_{BE}$ = 2 marks; Decision with justification = 2 marks; Verification = 1 mark; Chart explanation = 1 mark)*

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Discuss any five non-economic factors that the shipyard should consider before finalizing the welding process selection.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Weld Quality and Consistency (2 marks)**
- SAW delivers more uniform, defect-free welds than manual SMAW due to mechanized travel speed and consistent parameters.
- For **critical structural joints** (e.g., sheer strake, bilge strake), quality may override cost.

**2. Labour Skill Availability (1 mark)**
- Kerala yards have abundant **semi-skilled welders** suited for SMAW.
- SAW requires **trained operators familiar with flux handling, travel carriage, and parameter setting**.

**3. Flexibility (1 mark)**
- SMAW offers **superior positional flexibility** (all positions: 1G to 4G).
- SAW is limited to **flat (1G) or horizontal fillet (2F)** positions.

**4. Safety and Ergonomics (1 mark)**
- SAW produces **less fumes and spatter** than SMAW.
- SAW operator exposure to UV radiation is lower due to mechanized travel.

**5. Maintenance and Downtime (1 mark)**
- SAW equipment has **higher downtime risk** if flux hoppers, wire feeders, or carriages fail.
- SMAW equipment is rugged and easily repairable in remote shipyard locations.

**6. Class Society Approval (1 mark)**
- Both processes must have approved **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS)** and **Procedure Qualification Record (PQR)** per IACS UR W17 and UR W28.

*(Any five well-explained factors = 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 2 Choice 2]`

A shipbuilding company is planning to introduce a **new automated panel line** for producing hull panels. Three alternative processes are being considered: **CNC Plasma Cutting**, **CNC Laser Cutting**, and **CNC Waterjet Cutting**.

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Compare the three cutting processes with respect to (i) plate thickness capability, (ii) cut quality, (iii) productivity, and (iv) operating cost. Present the comparison in a tabular form.**

**Model Solution:**

| Criterion | CNC Plasma | CNC Laser | CNC Waterjet |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| **(i) Plate thickness capability** | 0.5 to 50 mm (best for medium plates) | 0.5 to 25 mm (best for thin plates) | 0.5 to 150 mm (best for very thick plates) |
| **(ii) Cut quality (kerf & HAZ)** | Moderate kerf (~2 mm); HAZ ~3 mm | Excellent — narrow kerf (~0.2 mm); minimal HAZ | Excellent — no HAZ; slight kerf taper |
| **(iii) Productivity (cutting speed)** | High for 6–25 mm | Highest for <6 mm | Slower than plasma/laser |
| **(iv) Operating cost** | Moderate (consumable electrodes/nozzles) | High (electricity + assist gas) | Very high (abrasive garnet + pump wear) |

**Conclusion:** For **typical hull plate range of 6–25 mm**, **CNC Plasma** offers the best balance of cost, productivity, and quality. **CNC Laser** is preferred for **thin stainless steel or aluminium superstructure panels**. **CNC Waterjet** is reserved for **very thick plates or heat-sensitive alloys** (rare in commercial shipbuilding).

*(Tabular comparison = 5 marks; Justified conclusion = 2 marks)*

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the Weighted Scoring Method for process selection. Using suitable weights and scores (assumed), demonstrate the method to recommend one of the three cutting processes for a shipyard producing 12 mm thick mild steel hull panels in medium volume batches.**

**Model Solution:**

**Explanation of Weighted Scoring Method (3 marks):**
The Weighted Scoring Method is a multi-criteria decision-making technique where:
1. Relevant selection criteria are identified (cost, quality, productivity, etc.).
2. Each criterion is assigned a **weight** based on its relative importance (weights sum to 100 or 1).
3. Each alternative process is **scored** on each criterion (typically 1–10 scale).
4. The **weighted score** for each alternative is computed as:

$$S_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \times s_{ij}$$

where $w_i$ is the weight of criterion $i$ and $s_{ij}$ is the score of alternative $j$ on criterion $i$.
5. The alternative with the **highest weighted score** is selected.

**Application to Cutting Process Selection (4 marks):**

| Criterion | Weight $w_i$ | Plasma Score | Laser Score | Waterjet Score |
|-----------|------------|--------------|-------------|----------------|
| Cost-effectiveness | 30 | 8 | 5 | 4 |
| Cut Quality | 20 | 7 | 10 | 9 |
| Productivity | 20 | 9 | 8 | 5 |
| Thickness capability | 15 | 9 | 6 | 10 |
| Maintenance ease | 10 | 7 | 6 | 5 |
| Skill requirement | 5 | 8 | 7 | 7 |

**Weighted Scores:**

$$S_{Plasma} = (30 \times 8) + (20 \times 7) + (20 \times 9) + (15 \times 9) + (10 \times 7) + (5 \times 8)$$

$$S_{Plasma} = 240 + 140 + 180 + 135 + 70 + 40 = 805$$

$$S_{Laser} = (30 \times 5) + (20 \times 10) + (20 \times 8) + (15 \times 6) + (10 \times 6) + (5 \times 7)$$

$$S_{Laser} = 150 + 200 + 160 + 90 + 60 + 35 = 695$$

$$S_{Waterjet} = (30 \times 4) + (20 \times 9) + (20 \times 5) + (15 \times 10) + (10 \times 5) + (5 \times 7)$$

$$S_{Waterjet} = 120 + 180 + 100 + 150 + 50 + 35 = 635$$

**Decision:** **CNC Plasma Cutting** has the highest weighted score (**805**) and is recommended for 12 mm mild steel hull panels in medium-volume production.

*(Method explanation = 3 marks; Scoring matrix = 1.5 marks; Calculations = 1.5 marks; Justified decision = 1 mark)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Areas in Process Selection Answers:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in break-even answers** — Always state $Q_{BE}$ in metres, tonnes, or pieces per year.
> 2. **Skipping the decision rule justification** — Writing "$Q_{BE} = 2177$" without explaining "since expected production is greater than $Q_{BE}$, select FCAW" costs **at least 1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing $C_p$ with $C_{pk}$** — $C_p$ ignores process centring; $C_{pk}$ does not. Use $C_{pk}$ in practice.
> 4. **Writing only definitions without shipbuilding context** — Generic IE definitions without naval/shipyard examples get **partial credit only**.
> 5. **Skipping non-economic factors** — KTU 2024 Scheme questions frequently ask for "non-economic factors" — don't only compute costs.
> 6. **Not drawing the break-even chart** — Even a qualitative sketch (axes, two lines, intersection) is mandatory for full marks.
> 7. **Ignoring class society rules** — Any process selection answer for shipbuilding MUST mention **IACS UR, AWS D1.1, or Classification Society approval**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Process Selection (PENST633 Module 2)**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ Process Selection = choosing the **right process + equipment + tooling** for a given ship component
- ☐ It is **strategic** (capital-intensive decisions) and **long-term** (5–20 year impact)
- ☐ Sits at the interface of **design, manufacturing, and economics**

### Stepwise Methodology
- ☐ Step 1: Define product requirements
- ☐ Step 2: Identify candidate processes
- ☐ Step 3: Apply screening criteria (technical feasibility)
- ☐ Step 4: Economic evaluation (break-even analysis)
- ☐ Step 5: Non-economic evaluation (quality, flexibility, safety)
- ☐ Step 6: Final selection + documentation in **Process Route Sheet**

### Critical Factors (Big Nine)
- ☐ Production Volume, Product Design, Material, Quality, Capital, Labour Skill, Lead Time, Flexibility, Safety

### Must-Know Formulas
- ☐ **Break-even quantity:** $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F_2 - F_1}{V_1 - V_2}$
- ☐ **Process capability:** $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$
- ☐ **Adjusted capability:** $C_{pk} = \min\left[\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right]$
- ☐ **Acceptance value:** $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ for shipbuilding-critical processes

### Decision Tools
- ☐ **Break-Even Analysis** — for 2 alternatives, cost-only comparison
- ☐ **Weighted Scoring Method** — for multiple criteria and 3+ alternatives
- ☐ **Product-Process Matrix (T.P. Wright)** — for matching volume-variety with process layout

### Shipbuilding-Specific Knowledge
- ☐ Welding dominates: **40–50% of man-hours**; primary cost driver
- ☐ **SMAW** → all positions, low-volume, low-skill
- ☐ **SAW** → flat position only, high productivity, high capital
- ☐ **GMAW/FCAW** → semi-automatic, versatile, medium volume
- ☐ **Class Society** approval is **non-negotiable** for any welding process
- ☐ Block/modular construction is the modern default for medium-large yards
- ☐ Process selection feeds into **Process Plan → Route Sheet → Work Instruction → WPS/PQR**

### Common Mistakes to Avoid
- ☐ Don't ignore non-economic factors
- ☐ Don't recommend a process without $C_{pk}$ verification for tight tolerances
- ☐ Don't forget to mention **IACS / Class approval**
- ☐ Don't omit units in numerical answers
- ☐ Don't confuse "process selection" with "process planning" — selection comes **first**, planning comes **after**

> **End of Module 2 — Process Selection Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application of Models for Process Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST633 - SHIP PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Application of Models for Process Planning # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Process Planning** in ship production is defined as the systematic determination of the sequence of manufacturing operations, selection of machines, tools, fixtures, cutting parameters, inspection methods, and estimation of operation times required to transform raw materials, semi-fabricated plates, sections, and sub-assemblies into a finished ship component, block, or the complete vessel, in conformance with the design specification, quality standards, and yard capacity constraints. An **Application of Models for Process Planning** refers to the deployment of formal, mathematical, algorithmic, or computer-based representations (deterministic, stochastic, heuristic, or AI-based) that assist the production engineer in: - Selecting the *optimum process route* among alternatives, - Balancing workloads across workstations, - Sequencing operations to minimize makespan, - Allocating resources (cranes, welding sets, berths, manpower) efficiently, - Predicting bottlenecks before physical execution. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Definition Snapshot (Likely 2-Mark Question):** > *Process planning is the technical preparation of routes, methods, machines, tools, and time standards for converting a part from raw stock to a finished product, supported by analytical models that ensure optimal utilization of yard resources.* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are planning a **wedding banquet for 500 guests in a single kitchen with 3 cooks and limited ovens**. You cannot cook every dish simultaneously. You must decide: - *Which cook handles which dish?* (Workstation allocation) - *What is the order of cooking so that hot food is served fresh?* (Operation sequencing) - *Should we pre-prepare or cook-to-order?* (Batch vs. flow) - *Where will the bottleneck occur — the tandoor or the dessert station?* (Bottleneck identification) A **process planning model** is exactly this decision-making tool — except instead of a kitchen, the *kitchen* is the shipyard, and instead of dishes, we have plates, stiffeners, brackets, pipes, and outfitting components. The model mathematically guarantees that the "banquet" (the ship) is delivered on time, within budget, and with minimum wastage. ## 1.3 Core Engineering Metrics (Standard Industrial Values) The following standard metrics govern process planning models in shipbuilding: - **Standard Time (T_std) = Basic Time + Allowance** (in minutes) - **Efficiency (η) = Standard Hours Produced / Hours Attended** (typically **70%–85%** in modern yards) - **Balance Delay (BD) = (n × t_max − Σt_i) / (n × t_max)** where *n* is number of stations - **Line Efficiency (LE)** expressed as a percentage, target **≥ 85%** for new yards - **Make-span (M)** for ship block erection typically ranges between **3 to 14 days per block** - **P-Duration (P)** for plate processing in modern FMS lines: **15–25 minutes per plate** > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Examiners consistently test whether the student can *link a model to a real yard problem* (e.g., "Which model would you use to balance 40 welding stations for block assembly?"). Memorize both the model *and* its yard application. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Models Used in Process Planning Process planning models are broadly classified into the following families. Each family is tied to a specific *type of decision* in the shipyard. ### 2.1.1 Deterministic Mathematical Models - **Linear Programming (LP)** — for resource allocation when objective and constraints are linear. - **Integer / Mixed-Integer Programming (IP / MIP)** — when variables (e.g., number of cranes) must be whole numbers. - **Assignment and Transportation Models** — to assign blocks to berths/slipways. ### 2.1.2 Network / Project Models - **CPM (Critical Path Method)** — for scheduling block erection. - **PERT (Program Evaluation Review Technique)** — incorporates uncertainty in activity duration. - **GERT (Graphical Evaluation Review Technique)** — allows probabilistic branching. ### 2.1.3 Stochastic Models - **Queuing (Waiting Line) Models** — for predicting delays at cranes, berths, and plate-cutting tables. - **Monte Carlo Simulation** — to simulate yard variability (weather, breakdown, manpower). ### 2.1.4 Heuristic / Algorithmic Models - **Line Balancing Heuristics** — Helgeson-Birnie, Comsoal, Ranked Positional Weight. - **Johnson's Rule, Palmer's Heuristic** — for sequencing *n* jobs on *m* machines. ### 2.1.5 Knowledge-Based / AI Models - **CAPP (Computer-Aided Process Planning)** — Variant and Generative. - **Expert Systems, Fuzzy Logic, Neural Networks** — for welding procedure selection, defect prediction. ## 2.2 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How" Each model is a *decision filter*. The reason yards apply them is captured in five sequential logic steps: 1. **Identify the decision variable** — What am I choosing? (e.g., assignment of block to slipway) 2. **Define the objective** — Minimize time, cost, or delay. 3. **Enumerate constraints** — Yard capacity, tonnage limits, weather windows, workforce. 4. **Select the appropriate model family** — Deterministic vs. stochastic, continuous vs. discrete. 5. **Validate using historical data / simulation** — Before applying to the real yard. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Examiners love asking *"Why is PERT preferred over CPM for new ship construction?"* Answer: PERT uses *three-time estimates* (optimistic, most likely, pessimistic), making it suitable for *first-of-a-kind* shipbuilding where activity times are uncertain. CPM uses a *single deterministic time* and is reserved for *repeat construction* (series production). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Model / Concept | Core Formula / Expression | Application in Shipbuilding | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Line Efficiency | $LE = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{n \cdot t_{\max}} \times 100$ | Balancing panel-line workstations | % | | 2 | Balance Delay | $BD = \dfrac{n \cdot t_{\max} - \sum t_i}{n \cdot t_{\max}} \times 100$ | Indicates idle time at stations | % | | 3 | PERT Expected Time | $t_e = \dfrac{t_o + 4 t_m + t_p}{6}$ | Block erection under uncertainty | days | | 4 | PERT Variance | $\sigma^2 = \left( \dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6} \right)^2$ | Risk analysis of project completion | days² | | 5 | Project Std. Dev. | $\sigma_{\text{proj}} = \sqrt{\sum \sigma_{\text{critical}}^2}$ | Confidence in delivery date | days | | 6 | LP Objective (Min) | $\min Z = \sum c_j x_j$ | Cost minimization of outfitting | currency | | 7 | LP Constraints | $\sum a_{ij} x_j \leq b_i$ | Resource (crane, weld set) limits | — | | 8 | Queuing Utilization | $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | Berth / crane saturation | dimensionless | | 9 | Queuing Wait Time (M/M/1) | $W_q = \dfrac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda}$ | Average wait at plate burner | hours | | 10 | Johnson's Rule Index | $\min(MS_1, MS_2)$ | Sequence blocks on two-stage line | — | | 11 | Ranked Positional Weight | $RPW = t_j + \sum RPW_{\text{successors}}$ | Helgeson-Birnie heuristic input | min | | 12 | Standard Time | $T_{std} = T_n \cdot (1 + A)$ | Workforce planning | min | > [!NOTE] > **Notation convention used in this sheet:** > $t_o$ = optimistic time, $t_m$ = most likely time, $t_p$ = pessimistic time, $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $\mu$ = service rate, $MS$ = machining time, $A$ = allowance fraction. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In a modern Korean or Japanese shipyard (e.g., Hyundai Heavy Industries, Samsung Heavy Industries), **every block's process plan is generated by a CAPP system** before any steel is cut. The plan feeds: - The **Nesting Software** (for plate cutting), - The **Shop-Floor MES (Manufacturing Execution System)** for tracking, - The **Welding Procedure Specification (WPS) database** for welder qualification, - The **Logistics System** for transporting sub-assemblies to the berth. A planning error in steel cutting propagates — a 1% error in plate nesting can increase total steel consumption by **3–5%** in a VLCC. This is why *models* (not intuition) are non-negotiable in modern yards. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — PERT Expected Time and Variance The PERT model assumes that the duration of any activity follows a **Beta distribution**. Because the Beta distribution is mathematically complex, PERT approximates it using a *weighted mean of three estimates*. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Define the three time estimates** - $t_o$ = Optimistic time (everything goes right) - $t_m$ = Most likely time (mode of the Beta distribution) - $t_p$ = Pessimistic time (delays, rework, weather) **Step 2 — Express the Beta distribution mean** The mean of a Beta distribution in its general form is: $$ \mu = \frac{\alpha}{\alpha + \beta} $$ For PERT, the parameters are chosen so that the weighted mean becomes: $$ t_e = \frac{t_o + 4 t_m + t_p}{6} $$ **Step 3 — Logical reasoning for the weights 1, 4, 1** The "most likely" estimate is given **four times the weight** of the extremes because, in real projects, outcomes cluster around the most probable scenario. The optimistic and pessimistic extremes are equally weighted because both are tail events. **Step 4 — Derive the variance** For a Beta distribution, the variance is: $$ \sigma^2 = \frac{(t_p - t_o)^2}{36} $$ This simplification arises because the *range* $(t_p - t_o)$ covers 6 standard deviations (similar to a 3-σ rule), so: $$ \sigma = \frac{t_p - t_o}{6} $$ **Step 5 — Project completion variance (critical path)** Since activity durations on the critical path are approximately independent, variances add linearly: $$ \sigma_{\text{proj}}^2 = \sum_{i \in \text{CP}} \sigma_i^2 = \sum_{i \in \text{CP}} \left( \frac{t_p - t_o}{6} \right)^2_i $$ **Step 6 — Confidence interval for project completion** $$ T_{\text{finish}} = T_E \pm Z \cdot \sigma_{\text{proj}} $$ where $Z = 1.645$ for 90% confidence, $Z = 1.96$ for 95% confidence. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Line Balancing via Ranked Positional Weight (Helgeson-Birnie) The **Ranked Positional Weight (RPW)** method is a heuristic to balance an assembly line by prioritizing tasks with the longest *downstream* workload. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Construct the precedence diagram** List all tasks $T_i$ with their times $t_i$ and immediate predecessors. **Step 2 — Compute positional weight for each task** The positional weight is the time of the task itself plus the times of *all its successors* following the precedence chain: $$ RPW_i = t_i + \sum_{j \in \text{successors}(i)} t_j $$ **Step 3 — Rank tasks in descending order of RPW** Tasks with the highest RPW are scheduled first, because they impose the longest downstream commitment. **Step 4 — Assign tasks to stations sequentially** At each station, accumulate assigned times.
