# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
>
> **Q1.** What is a Transportation Model? Mention any two of its industrial applications in ship production management.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A Transportation Model is a special class of Linear Programming Problem used to determine the optimal shipping schedule of a homogeneous commodity from multiple sources to multiple destinations at minimum total cost, subject to supply and demand constraints. **(2 marks)**
>
> In ship production management, two key applications are: **(1 mark)**
> 1. **Block Movement Scheduling** — Optimal routing of pre-fabricated hull blocks from fabrication shops to the assembly berth.
> 2. **Material Distribution** — Distribution of steel plates, pipes, and outfitting components from multiple stockyards to multiple shipyards at minimum freight cost.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
>
> **Q2.** State the optimality condition for a transportation problem using the MODI method. What is degeneracy, and how is it resolved?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **MODI Optimality Condition (minimization):** A transportation solution is optimal if, for every unoccupied cell $(i,j)$, the opportunity cost $\Delta_{ij} = c_{ij} - (u_i + v_j) \geq 0$. **(1.5 marks)**
>
> **Degeneracy:** A BFS of an $m \times n$ transportation problem is said to be **degenerate** if the number of occupied cells is **less than** $m + n - 1$. **(1 mark)**
>
> **Resolution:** Insert a very small quantity $\epsilon$ (epsilon, approaching zero) in one of the **independent** empty cells so that the total occupied cells become $m + n - 1$. The choice of cell must not form a closed loop with other occupied cells. **(0.5 mark)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — VAM + MODI Full Problem

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
>
> **Q3(a)** [7 Marks] A shipbuilding company has four steel stockyards $S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4$ with capacities **50, 60, 50, 40 tonnes** respectively. These supply four shipyards $D_1, D_2, D_3, D_4$ with demands **40, 50, 60, 50 tonnes** respectively. The unit freight cost (₹/tonne) matrix is:
>
> | | $D_1$ | $D_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | $S_1$ | 3 | 1 | 7 | 4 |
> | $S_2$ | 2 | 6 | 5 | 9 |
> | $S_3$ | 8 | 7 | 3 | 5 |
> | $S_4$ | 4 | 2 | 6 | 6 |
>
> Find the Initial Basic Feasible Solution using **Vogel's Approximation Method**.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

Check balance: $\sum a_i = 50 + 60 + 50 + 40 = 200$; $\sum b_j = 40 + 50 + 60 + 50 = 200$ ✓ **Balanced**

Number of cells needed: $m + n - 1 = 4 + 4 - 1 = 7$

**Iteration 1:**

Row penalties: $S_1: 3-1=2$, $S_2: 5-2=3$, $S_3: 5-3=2$, $S_4: 4-2=2$
Column penalties: $D_1: 3-2=1$, $D_2: 2-1=1$, $D_3: 5-3=2$, $D_4: 5-4=1$

Max penalty = 3 (row $S_2$). Lowest cost in $S_2$ is $D_1$ (cost 2). Allocate $\min(60, 40) = 40$. $D_1$ satisfied, strike column.

**Iteration 2:** Recompute penalties on remaining 4×3 matrix.
- Row penalties: $S_1: 4-1=3$, $S_2: 6-5=1$, $S_3: 5-3=2$, $S_4: 4-2=2$
- Column penalties: $D_2: 2-1=1$, $D_3: 5-3=2$, $D_4: 5-4=1$

Max penalty = 3 (row $S_1$). Lowest cost in $S_1$: $D_2$ (cost 1). Allocate $\min(50, 50) = 50$. Both $S_1$ and $D_2$ satisfied.

**Iteration 3:** Reduced to 3×2.
- Row penalties: $S_2: 9-5=4$, $S_3: 5-3=2$, $S_4: 6-4=2$
- Column penalties: $D_3: 5-3=2$, $D_4: 5-4=1$

Max penalty = 4 (row $S_2$). Lowest cost in $S_2$: $D_3$ (cost 5). Allocate $\min(20, 60) = 20$. $S_2$ exhausted.

**Iteration 4:** 2×2 matrix.
- $S_3 \to D_3$: $\min(50, 40) = 40$. $D_3$ satisfied.
- $S_3 \to D_4$: $\min(10, 50) = 10$. $S_3$ exhausted.
- $S_4 \to D_4$: $\min(40, 40) = 40$. All satisfied.

**Final IBFS Table:**

| Allocation | $x_{ij}$ | $c_{ij}$ | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S_1 \to D_2$ | 50 | 1 | 50 |
| $S_2 \to D_1$ | 40 | 2 | 80 |
| $S_2 \to D_3$ | 20 | 5 | 100 |
| $S_3 \to D_3$ | 40 | 3 | 120 |
| $S_3 \to D_4$ | 10 | 5 | 50 |
| $S_4 \to D_4$ | 40 | 6 | 240 |

**Total IBFS Cost = ₹640** (7 occupied cells, non-degenerate ✓)

> **Q3(b)** [7 Marks] Test the above solution for optimality using the **MODI method**. If not optimal, find the optimal solution.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

Set $u_1 = 0$. Solve for all $u_i, v_j$:

- $(S_1, D_2)$: $0 + v_2 = 1 \Rightarrow v_2 = 1$
- $(S_2, D_1)$: $u_2 + v_1 = 2$
- $(S_2, D_3)$: $u_2 + v_3 = 5 \Rightarrow u_2 = 5 - v_3$
- $(S_3, D_3)$: $u_3 + v_3 = 3$
- $(S_3, D_4)$: $u_3 + v_4 = 5 \Rightarrow v_4 = 5 - u_3$
- $(S_4, D_4)$: $u_4 + v_4 = 6 \Rightarrow u_4 = 1$
- $v_4 = 5 - u_3 \Rightarrow u_3 = 5 - v_4$ and $u_4 = 1 \Rightarrow v_4 = 5$, so $u_3 = 0$, $v_3 = 3$, $u_2 = 2$, $v_1 = 0$.

Compute $\Delta_{ij}$ for all empty cells:
- $\Delta_{11} = 3 - (0+0) = 3$ ✓
- $\Delta_{13} = 7 - (0+3) = 4$ ✓
- $\Delta_{14} = 4 - (0+5) = -1$ ❌
- $\Delta_{21} = 0$ (occupied)
- $\Delta_{22} = 6 - (2+1) = 3$ ✓
- $\Delta_{24} = 9 - (2+5) = 2$ ✓
- $\Delta_{31} = 8 - (0+0) = 8$ ✓
- $\Delta_{32} = 7 - (0+1) = 6$ ✓
- $\Delta_{41} = 4 - (1+0) = 3$ ✓
- $\Delta_{42} = 2 - (1+1) = 0$ ✓
- $\Delta_{43} = 6 - (1+3) = 2$ ✓

Most negative: $\Delta_{14} = -1$ at $(S_1, D_4)$. Form closed loop:

$(S_1, D_4)^{+} \to (S_3, D_4)^{-} \to (S_3, D_3)^{+} \to (S_1, D_3)^{-}$

Negative corner values: 10 (at $S_3, D_4$), and... since $(S_1, D_3)$ is not in our IBFS, we need a valid closed loop with existing occupied cells.

**Alternative loop:** $(S_1, D_4)^{+} \to (S_1, D_2)^{-} \to (S_2, D_2)^{+}$ ... but $(S_2, D_2)$ is not occupied. Correct loop using only occupied cells:

$(S_1, D_4)^{+} \to (S_1, D_2)^{-} \to (S_2, D_1)^{+}/\text{not on path}$...

**Valid loop:** $(S_1, D_4)^{+} \to (S_4, D_4)^{-} \to (S_4, D_4)$ is at corner. Use:
$(S_1, D_4)^{+} \to (S_4, D_4)^{-} \to (S_3, D_4)^{+} \to (S_3, D_3)^{-} \to (S_2, D_3)^{+}$

Negative corners: 40 (at $S_4, D_4$) and 40 (at $S_3, D_3$). Reallocation = $\min(40, 40) = 40$.

**Updated Allocations:**

| Allocation | $x_{ij}$ | $c_{ij}$ | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S_1 \to D_2$ | 50 | 1 | 50 |
| $S_1 \to D_4$ | 40 | 4 | 160 |
| $S_2 \to D_1$ | 40 | 2 | 80 |
| $S_2 \to D_3$ | 60 | 5 | 300 |
| $S_3 \to D_4$ | 50 | 5 | 250 |
| $S_4 \to D_4$ | 0 | 6 | 0 |

**New Total Cost = ₹840**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Critical):**
> 1. **Do not skip re-verification** — after reallocation, you must re-solve $u, v$ and re-check ALL $\Delta_{ij}$. The new solution must satisfy $\Delta_{ij} \geq 0$ for all empty cells to be declared optimal. **(−2 marks)**
> 2. **Closed loop must be valid** — students often form loops that include unoccupied cells other than the entering cell. The loop touches only **occupied cells** (plus the entering cell) at its corners. **(−1 mark per wrong loop)**
> 3. **Sign alternation is mandatory** — the corners must alternate $+, -, +, -$ starting from the entering cell. **(−1 mark)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative: Unbalanced + Prohibited Route

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024]**
>
> **Q4(a)** [7 Marks] A shipyard has three fabrication shops with monthly outputs of **100, 80, 50 tonnes** of steel sections. These supply three assembly lines with requirements of **90, 70, 60 tonnes**. The cost matrix (₹/tonne) is:
>
> | | $L_1$ | $L_2$ | $L_3$ |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | $F_1$ | 5 | 3 | 2 |
> | $F_2$ | 7 | 5 | 4 |
> | $F_3$ | 4 | 6 | 8 |
>
> Find the IBFS using the **Least Cost Method** and check for degeneracy.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

Check balance: $\sum a_i = 230$; $\sum b_j = 220$. **Unbalanced** (excess supply = 10).
Add a **dummy destination** $D$ with demand 10 and zero cost. Now $m + n - 1 = 3 + 4 - 1 = 6$ cells.

**LCM Iterations:**

1. Lowest cost in matrix = 2 at $(F_1, L_3)$. Allocate $\min(100, 60) = 60$. $L_3$ satisfied.
2. Next lowest = 3 at $(F_1, L_2)$. Allocate $\min(40, 70) = 40$. $F_1$ exhausted.
3. Next lowest = 4 at $(F_2, L_3)$ — but $L_3$ is done. Skip to next: 4 at $(F_3, L_1)$. Allocate $\min(50, 90) = 50$. $F_3$ exhausted.
4. Lowest available: 5 at $(F_2, L_1)$. Allocate $\min(80, 40) = 40$. $L_1$ satisfied.
5. Remaining: $F_2$ to $L_2$: $\min(40, 30) = 30$. $F_2$ done; $L_2$ needs 30 more.
6. Allocate remaining 30 to dummy destination from $L_2$? — Actually, $L_2$ still has 30 demand; route $F_2$ is exhausted. Need allocation from... $L_2$ remains with 30 demand and no source. This indicates a **mismatch** — recheck.

**Re-iteration:** After step 4, $L_1$ satisfied, but we still have $F_2$ with 40 and $L_2$ with 30. Allocate $F_2 \to L_2 = 30$, leaving $F_2$ with 10; allocate $F_2 \to D = 10$ (dummy).

**Final IBFS:**

| Route | $x_{ij}$ | $c_{ij}$ | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| $F_1 \to L_3$ | 60 | 2 | 120 |
| $F_1 \to L_2$ | 40 | 3 | 120 |
| $F_3 \to L_1$ | 50 | 4 | 200 |
| $F_2 \to L_1$ | 40 | 5 | 200 |
| $F_2 \to L_2$ | 30 | 5 | 150 |
| $F_2 \to D$ | 10 | 0 | 0 |

**Total Cost = ₹790** (6 occupied cells = $m + n - 1$ ✓ non-degenerate)

> **Q4(b)** [7 Marks] If route $F_3 \to L_1$ is prohibited due to a damaged access road, modify the problem and recompute the IBFS using **VAM**. Comment on the change in cost.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

With prohibited route, set $c_{31} = M$ (very large penalty). Rebalance: still $\sum a_i = 230, \sum b_j = 220$, so dummy demand = 10. New cost matrix:

| | $L_1$ | $L_2$ | $L_3$ | $D$ | Supply |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $F_1$ | 5 | 3 | 2 | 0 | 100 |
| $F_2$ | 7 | 5 | 4 | 0 | 80 |
| $F_3$ | M | 6 | 8 | 0 | 50 |
| Demand | 90 | 70 | 60 | 10 | |

**VAM Iteration 1:**
Row penalties: $F_1: 2-0=2$, $F_2: 4-0=4$, $F_3: 6-0=6$
Col penalties: $L_1: 7-5=2$, $L_2: 5-3=2$, $L_3: 4-2=2$, $D: 0-0=0$

Max penalty = 6 (row $F_3$). Lowest cost in $F_3$: $D$ (cost 0). Allocate $\min(50, 10) = 10$. $D$ satisfied.

**Iteration 2:** $F_1$ penalty = 2, $F_2$ = 3, $F_3$ = 2; $L_1$ = 2, $L_2$ = 2, $L_3$ = 2.
Max = 3 (row $F_2$). Lowest cost: $D$ is closed; next is $L_3$ (4). Allocate $\min(80, 60) = 60$. $L_3$ satisfied.

**Iteration 3:** Row penalties: $F_1: 3-2=1$, $F_2: 5-4=1$, $F_3: 8-6=2$. Col: $L_1: 5-5=0$, $L_2: 3-3=0$.
Max = 2 (row $F_3$). Lowest: $L_2$ (6). Allocate $\min(40, 70) = 40$. $F_3$ exhausted.

**Iteration 4:** $F_1$ (60) and $F_2$ (20) remain. $L_1$ needs 90, $L_2$ needs 30.
- $F_1 \to L_2$: $\min(60, 30) = 30$. $L_2$ satisfied.
- $F_1 \to L_1$: $\min(30, 90) = 30$.
- $F_2 \to L_1$: $\min(20, 60) = 20$.
- $F_2 \to L_1$: $\min(0, 40) = 0$ — but need 40 more. **Degeneracy arises** (only 6 allocations, but we need 6). Let's recount: 10, 60, 40, 30, 30, 20 = 6 cells ✓.

Wait — recheck the chain: allocations are $(F_3,D)=10, (F_2,L_3)=60, (F_3,L_2)=40, (F_1,L_2)=30, (F_1,L_1)=30, (F_2,L_1)=20$. Total = 6 allocations, $m + n - 1 = 3 + 4 - 1 = 6$ ✓.

**Cost:** $10(0) + 60(4) + 40(6) + 30(3) + 30(5) + 20(7) = 0 + 240 + 240 + 90 + 150 + 140 = \mathbf{₹860}$

**Cost increase** due to prohibited route = $860 - 790 = \mathbf{₹70}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** When a route is prohibited, students forget to **increase the cost to a very large $M$**. Forgetting this step leads to selecting the prohibited route, which loses **1.5 marks** in the KTU valuation key.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Transportation Models (Module 2)**

- ✅ Transportation Model is a **special LPP** with $m + n - 1$ basic variables (not $m \times n$).
- ✅ Always **check balance** first: $\sum a_i = \sum b_j$. If unequal, add a **dummy row/column** with **zero cost**.
- ✅ Three IBFS methods: **NWCR (fastest, worst) → LCM (moderate) → VAM (slowest, best)**. Use VAM in exams.
- ✅ VAM **penalty** = difference between the two smallest costs in a row/column.
- ✅ In VAM ties, break by choosing the row/column with the **lowest minimum cost**.
- ✅ Number of allocations must equal **$m + n - 1$**; if less, problem is **degenerate** → insert $\epsilon$ in an independent cell.
- ✅ **MODI optimality** (minimization): $\Delta_{ij} = c_{ij} - (u_i + v_j) \geq 0$ for all empty cells.
- ✅ Set $u_1 = 0$ as anchor, solve for all $u_i, v_j$ from occupied cells using $u_i + v_j = c_{ij}$.
- ✅ If any $\Delta_{ij} < 0$, the **most negative** cell enters the basis.
- ✅ **Closed loop** must use only horizontal/vertical moves and touch only occupied cells (plus the entering cell) at corners. Alternate signs $+, -, +, -$.
- ✅ **Reallocation** value = minimum of all quantities at **negative corners** of the loop.
- ✅ For **prohibited routes**, set cost $c_{ij} = M$ (or a very large number) to make selection economically unviable.
- ✅ **Maximal profit problems** convert to minimization by using opportunity loss or by flipping signs of profits.
- ✅ **Stepping Stone method** is an alternative to MODI for optimality testing; the logic of loop construction is identical.
- ✅ In ship production context, TP models **block transportation, material logistics, and outfitting distribution**.
- ✅ **Total IBFS cost** = $\sum (\text{allocation} \times \text{unit cost})$ across all occupied cells.
- ✅ If $\Delta_{ij} = 0$ for an empty cell, the solution has **alternate optima** (multiple optimal solutions exist).
- ✅ **Iteration continues** until all $\Delta_{ij} \geq 0$ (for minimization) — declare **OPTIMAL** and state the minimum cost.
- ✅ Final answer must include: **optimal allocations table + minimum total cost** (and sometimes the percentage saving vs. naive allocation).
- ✅ **Common KTU exam traps:** Forgetting to balance the problem, miscalculating penalties, forming invalid closed loops, and failing to re-verify after reallocation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Operation Research
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST633 - SHIP PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Operation Research ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Operations Research (OR)** is a scientific, analytical, and mathematical approach to decision-making that involves the formulation of complex real-world problems into mathematical models, application of quantitative methods to derive optimal solutions, and systematic evaluation of alternatives under constraints of limited resources. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Essence):** > Operations Research is defined as the application of scientific methods, mathematical techniques, and systematic reasoning to complex problems arising in the direction and management of large systems of men, machines, materials, and money in industry, business, government, and defense. In the context of **Ship Production Management**, OR is the backbone of resource allocation, production scheduling, block erection sequencing, and steel stockyard optimization. ### 1.2 Etymology and Origin - The term "**Operations Research**" was first used during **World War II (1939–1945)** by military planners of the British and American armed forces to allocate scarce war resources (anti-submarine operations, convoy routing, bombing strategy). - Post-war, the discipline was adopted by industry to improve productivity, efficiency, and profitability. - Pioneers include **George Dantzig** (Simplex Method, 1947), **Leonid Kantorovich**, and **John von Neumann** (Game Theory). - The professional society governing OR is **INFORMS (Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences)**. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Shipyard Analogy — "The Block Erection Dilemma":** > Imagine a shipyard superintendent who has **50 fabrication blocks** to weld, **6 gantry cranes** available, **3 launching ways**, and a contractual **delivery deadline of 18 months**. The superintendent must decide: > - Which blocks to fabricate first? > - How to allocate welders (a limited workforce) across different zones? > - Whether to outsource plate cutting or do it in-house? > > Without OR, decisions are based on **intuition, experience, and trial-and-error** (sub-optimal). > With OR, the superintendent **mathematically models** the shipyard as a system of equations/inequalities, defines the **objective** (minimize time or cost), identifies **constraints** (manpower, cranes, dock space), and **computes** the optimal block sequence, workforce schedule, and resource allocation. ### 1.4 Defining Features of Operations Research | Feature | Description | |---|---| | **Systems Approach** | Considers the problem as a whole system, not in isolation | | **Inter-disciplinary Team** | Team includes mathematicians, engineers, economists, statisticians | | **Scientific Method** | Uses hypothesis, data collection, model building, testing | | **Mathematical Models** | Translates real problems into equations/inequalities | | **Optimization** | Seeks best (optimal) solution under given constraints | | **Use of Computers** | Heavy reliance on digital computation for large-scale problems | > [!TIP] > **Key Insight for KTU Students:** OR is **NOT** a single technique — it is a **methodology (a way of thinking)** that uses a family of techniques such as **Linear Programming (LP)**, **Dynamic Programming (DP)**, **Queuing Theory**, **Game Theory**, **Simulation**, **CPM/PERT**, and **Inventory Models**. ### 1.5 Why OR Matters in Ship Production Management A modern shipyard involves: - **Millions of components** (plates, stiffeners, pipes, outfit items) - **Multidisciplinary workforce** (welders, fitters, electricians, painters) - **Tight delivery deadlines** (often with penalty clauses) - **Capital-intensive equipment** (gantry cranes, dry docks, CNC cutters) OR provides the **quantitative tools** to plan, schedule, monitor, and control this complex ecosystem efficiently. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Phases / Steps of Operations Research (OR Methodology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is a HIGH-YIELD topic for KTU 2024 ESE. Students must memorize the 6–7 phases in correct order.** The classical methodology of OR consists of the following sequential phases: #### **Phase 1: Formulation of the Problem** - Define the **decision variables** (what we control — e.g., number of welders assigned to Zone A). - Define the **objective function** (what we want to optimize — e.g., minimize cost, maximize output). - Define the **constraints** (limitations on resources — e.g., only 200 welders available). - Gather data and identify the scope of the problem. #### **Phase 2: Construction of the Mathematical Model** - Translate the problem into a mathematical representation: - Equations (e.g., material balance) - Inequalities (e.g., resource limits) - Logical conditions (e.g., integer or binary) - A general OR model has the form: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Optimize } & Z = f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) \\ \text{Subject to } & g_i(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) \;\{\le, =, \ge\}\; b_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m \\ \text{and } & x_j \;\ge\; 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, n \end{aligned} $$ Where: - $Z$ = objective function - $x_j$ = decision variables - $f(\cdot)$ = objective expression - $g_i(\cdot)$ = constraint functions - $b_i$ = resource availabilities #### **Phase 3: Derivation of a Solution** - Solve the model using appropriate mathematical technique. - For Linear Programming: **Simplex Method**, **Graphical Method**, or solver software. - For Discrete problems: **Integer Programming**, **Branch and Bound**. - For Complex Systems: **Simulation**, **Heuristics**, **Metaheuristics (GA, PSO)**. #### **Phase 4: Testing the Model and Solution (Validation)** - Check the model against **historical/past data**. - Ensure the solution is **realistic and implementable** in the shipyard context. - Conduct **sensitivity analysis** — what if a parameter changes? #### **Phase 5: Establishing Controls (Implementation)** - Translate the mathematical solution into **operational rules**. - Define key performance indicators (KPIs) such as **Schedule Adherence Index (SAI)**, **On-Time Delivery (OTD %)**. #### **Phase 6: Implementation of the Solution** - Execute the optimized plan in the actual shipyard. - Monitor and review continuously; refine the model if conditions change. ### 2.2 Characteristics / Features of OR Models - **Assumptions-based:** Every model rests on simplifying assumptions. - **Trade-off analysis:** Helps quantify cost vs. time vs. quality trade-offs. - **What-if capability:** Enables scenario planning. - **Descriptive vs. Prescriptive:** Some models describe reality; others prescribe actions. ### 2.3 Classification of OR Models | Type | Description | Example in Ship Production | |---|---|---| | **Deterministic** | All parameters known with certainty | Block erection sequence with fixed durations | | **Probabilistic / Stochastic** | Parameters are random variables | Welding defect rate, weather delay modeling | | **Static** | Decisions at a single point in time | Steel stockyard layout | | **Dynamic** | Decisions over multiple time periods | Multi-stage shipbuilding over 18 months | | **Linear** | Objective and constraints are linear | LP-based workforce allocation | | **Non-linear** | Non-linear relationships exist | Hull form optimization for resistance | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!TIP] > **Use this as your rapid-revision table before the exam. Master the standard LP form, it's a guaranteed 14-mark question in KTU 2024.** | Concept | Mathematical Form | Application in Ship Production | |---|---|---| | **General OR Model** | $Z = f(\mathbf{x})$ s.t. $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le b_i$ | Universal template | | **LP Canonical Form** | Maximize $Z = \sum c_j x_j$ s.t. $\sum a_{ij} x_j \le b_i$, $x_j \ge 0$ | Plate cutting, weld allocation | | **Total Cost Function** | $C_T = C_{\text{setup}} + C_{\text{holding}} + C_{\text{shortage}}$ | Inventory / EOQ model | | **Cycle Time (CPM)** | $T_c = \text{Longest path in project network}$ | Block erection planning | | **Float / Slack** | $S_i = LS_i - ES_i$ | Schedule cushioning | | **Cost Slope (Crashing)** | $\text{Slope} = \dfrac{\text{Crash Cost} - \text{Normal Cost}}{\text{Normal Time} - \text{Crash Time}}$ | Project compression | | **Utilization (ρ)** | $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | Dock/bay utilization | ### 2.5 Real-World Utility of OR in Engineering - **Shipbuilding:** Block erection sequencing, steel stockyard layout, panel line scheduling, dry-dock allocation. - **Aerospace:** Aircraft routing, fleet maintenance scheduling. - **Civil Engineering:** Resource leveling, project crashing, traffic flow optimization. - **Defense:** Logistics, sonar search patterns, convoy routing (historical origin). - **Manufacturing:** Production planning, inventory control, quality engineering. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough — The Six Phases Applied to a Shipbuilding Problem > [!NOTE] > **Illustrative Problem:** A shipyard must decide the number of standard panels ($x_1$) and curved panels ($x_2$) to produce per week. Each standard panel gives a profit of **₹8,000** and each curved panel **₹12,000**. The plate-cutting CNC machine has **40 hours/week**, and standard panels need **2 hours** while curved panels need **3 hours**. The painting bay has **30 hours/week**, with standard panels requiring **1 hour** and curved panels **2 hours**. Formulate the OR model. --- #### **Step 1 — Identify Decision Variables** Let: - $x_1$ = number of standard panels produced per week (units/week) - $x_2$ = number of curved panels produced per week (units/week) These are the variables the shipyard manager **controls**. #### **Step 2 — Define the Objective Function** The manager wants to **maximize profit** $Z$ (in ₹/week): $$ Z = 8000\,x_1 + 12000\,x_2 $$ This is the linear function to be **optimized**. #### **Step 3 — Formulate the Constraints** **Constraint 1: CNC machine time** The CNC machine has only **40 hours/week**. Standard panels consume 2 hours each; curved panels consume 3 hours each. Therefore, total hours used cannot exceed 40: $$ 2x_1 + 3x_2 \;\le\; 40 \quad \text{(CNC hours)} $$ **Constraint 2: Painting bay time** The painting bay has only **30 hours/week**. Standard panels consume 1 hour; curved panels consume 2 hours: $$ 1x_1 + 2x_2 \;\le\; 30 \quad \text{(Painting hours)} $$ **Constraint 3: Non-negativity** The shipyard cannot produce a negative number of panels: $$ x_1 \;\ge\; 0, \qquad x_2 \;\ge\; 0 $$ #### **Step 4 — Complete Mathematical Model (LP Standard Form)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Maximize } & Z = 8000\,x_1 + 12000\,x_2 \\ \text{Subject to } & 2x_1 + 3x_2 \;\le\; 40 \\ & 1x_1 + 2x_2 \;\le\; 30 \\ & x_1, x_2 \;\ge\; 0 \end{aligned} $$ #### **Step 5 — Derive the Optimal Solution (Graphical Method for Illustration)** We find the **corner points** of the feasible region by solving the constraint equations as equalities. **Intersection of Constraint 1 with $x_2$-axis:** Set $x_1 = 0$: $$ 3x_2 = 40 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; x_2 = \dfrac{40}{3} \approx 13.33 $$ So point $A = (0, 13.33)$. **Intersection of Constraint 2 with $x_1$-axis:** Set $x_2 = 0$: $$ x_1 = 30 $$ So point $B = (30, 0)$. **Intersection of the two constraints (solve simultaneously):** $$ \begin{aligned} 2x_1 + 3x_2 &= 40 \\ 1x_1 + 2x_2 &= 30 \end{aligned} $$ Multiply the second equation by 2: $$ \begin{aligned} 2x_1 + 3x_2 &= 40 \\ 2x_1 + 4x_2 &= 60 \end{aligned} $$ Subtract the first from the second: $$ x_2 = 20 - 13.33 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; x_2 = 20 $$ Substitute back: $$ x_1 + 2(20) = 30 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; x_1 + 40 = 30 $$ Wait — this gives a **negative value**, which is infeasible. The feasible region corner point is at the intersection where: $$ \begin{aligned} 2x_1 + 3x_2 &= 40 \\ x_1 + 2x_2 &= 30 \end{aligned} $$ Solving: Multiply the second equation by 2: $$ \begin{aligned} 2x_1 + 3x_2 &= 40 \\ 2x_1 + 4x_2 &= 60 \end{aligned} $$ Subtract the first from the second: $$ x_2 = 20 $$ Substitute $x_2 = 20$ into $x_1 + 2(20) = 30$: $$ x_1 = 30 - 40 = -10 $$ This is **infeasible** ($x_1 < 0$). Hence the actual binding corner is at $x_2 = 20$, $x_1 = 0$ (origin axis intercept). Let us re-evaluate: For **Constraint 2**: $x_1 + 2x_2 \le 30$. When $x_1 = 0$, $x_2 = 15$. So the actual intercept on the $x_2$-axis for the painting bay is $15$, not $20$. Re-examining **Constraint 1**: $2x_1 + 3x_2 \le 40$. At $x_1 = 0$, $x_2 = 40/3 \approx 13.33$. This is the **more restrictive** upper bound on $x_2$ alone. So the feasible region corner points are: - $O = (0, 0)$ — origin - $C = (30, 0)$ — only standard panels, painting bay fully used - $D = (0, 13.33)$ — only curved panels, CNC fully used - $E = $ intersection of both constraints (binding) Re-solve intersection of $2x_1 + 3x_2 = 40$ and $x_1 + 2x_2 = 30$: From the second equation: $x_1 = 30 - 2x_2$. Substitute into the first: $$ 2(30 - 2x_2) + 3x_2 = 40 $$ $$ 60 - 4x_2 + 3x_2 = 40 $$ $$ 60 - x_2 = 40 $$ $$ x_2 = 20 $$ Substitute back: $$ x_1 = 30 - 2(20) = 30 - 40 = -10 $$ So intersection occurs at $(-10, 20)$, which lies **outside the feasible region** (negative $x_1$). This means the two constraints do not intersect in the first quadrant within the feasible region. The binding constraints are: - The CNC machine constraint (more restrictive) is active at $D = (0, 13.33)$. - The painting bay constraint allows up to $x_1 = 30$ but CNC restricts to $x_1 = 20$ (from $2(20) + 0 = 40$). So the **actual corner points** of the feasible region are: - $O = (0, 0)$ - $F = (20, 0)$ — only standard panels, CNC fully used (also satisfies painting bay: $20 + 0 = 20 \le 30$ ✓) - $D = (0, 13.33)$ — only curved panels, CNC fully used (also satisfies painting bay: $0 + 2(13.33) = 26.66 \le 30$ ✓) Compute $Z$ at each corner: - At $O$: $Z = 8000(0) + 12000(0) = 0$ - At $F = (20, 0)$: $Z = 8000(20) + 12000(0) = 1,60,000$ - At $D = (0, 13.33)$: $Z = 8000(0) + 12000(13.33) = 1,60,000$ Both non-trivial corners yield the **same optimal value of ₹1,60,000**, indicating **alternate optimal solutions** exist along the segment $F$ to $D$. #### **Step 6 — Validate and Implement** The model is validated by checking historical production data. Implementation involves issuing production orders: produce any combination of $x_1$ and $x_2$ on the line segment $FD$ that totals ₹1,60,000 of profit. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — OR Techniques vs. Ship Production Problems | Shipyard Problem | OR Technique | Difficulty Level (KTU) | |---|---|---| | Block erection sequencing | CPM / PERT | High | | Stockyard plate allocation | Linear Programming | High | | Workforce shift scheduling | Integer Programming | Medium | | Dry-dock turnaround time | Queuing Theory | Medium | | Welding defect rate analysis | Statistical Quality Control | Medium | | Steel procurement decisions | Inventory Models (EOQ) | Medium | | Crane scheduling | Assignment Problem | Low–Medium | | Route optimization for material movement | Transportation Problem | Medium | | Competitive bidding for new orders | Game Theory | Low | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** The above matrix is a **must-know mapping** for ESE answers. Linking an OR technique correctly to its shipyard application often earns the student a **'concept clarity' comment** in the answer script, which boosts marks. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Flow Diagram — OR Methodology in Ship Production ```mermaid flowchart TD A["PHASE 1: Problem Formulation

[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** *Define Operations Research. List any four characteristics of OR.* **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Operations Research is the scientific method of providing a quantitative basis for decision-making through the formulation of a mathematical model representing the real-world problem and finding the optimal solution subject to specified constraints. **Four Characteristics of OR:** 1. **Systems Approach:** OR views the problem as a part of a larger system and considers all interdependencies. 2. **Inter-disciplinary:** It uses a team of experts from mathematics, statistics, engineering, economics, and management. 3. **Mathematical Modeling:** Real problems are converted into mathematical equations/inequalities. 4. **Optimization:** OR seeks the **best (optimal)** solution — minimum cost, maximum profit, or minimum time. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write a *single-line* definition and forget to list the characteristics. In KTU 2024, the 3-mark question expects **1 mark for definition** and **2 marks for the list**. Skipping characteristics costs at least 1.5 marks. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** *Briefly explain the different phases of the Operations Research methodology.* **Model Answer:** The six phases of OR methodology are: 1. **Formulation of the Problem** — identifying decision variables, objective, and constraints. 2. **Construction of the Mathematical Model** — translating the problem into equations/inequalities. 3. **Derivation of a Solution** — applying techniques like Simplex, CPM, or Queuing. 4. **Testing the Model** — validating with historical data; performing sensitivity analysis. 5. **Establishing Controls** — defining KPIs and monitoring mechanisms. 6. **Implementation** — putting the optimized solution into actual operation with continuous feedback. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Writing the phases in **wrong order** is a common error. KTU examiners award **zero partial marks** for out-of-sequence phases. Memorize the order: **Formulation → Model → Solution → Testing → Controls → Implementation**. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B has an internal choice between two questions. Both alternatives are provided below with complete model solutions.** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the methodology of Operations Research with the help of a neat block diagram. How is it different from conventional research methods? **(b) [7 Marks)** A shipyard produces two types of ship sections: **Type A (small hull blocks)** and **Type B (large hull blocks)**. The profit per Type A block is **₹15,000** and per Type B block is **₹20,000**. The available fabrication hours per week are **120**, with Type A requiring 4 hours and Type B requiring 5 hours. The welding station has **90 hours/week** available, with Type A needing 3 hours and Type B needing 4 hours. Formulate this as a Linear Programming Problem. Find the optimal number of blocks to produce per week to maximize profit using the graphical method. --- #### **Model Solution to Q-A (a):** **1. Methodology of Operations Research:** The OR methodology consists of six sequential phases: **Phase 1 — Formulation of the Problem:** The decision-maker identifies the real-world problem, defines the **decision variables** (controllable factors), states the **objective** (goal to optimize), and lists the **constraints** (limitations). **Phase 2 — Construction of the Mathematical Model:** The problem is translated into a mathematical model comprising an objective function and a set of constraints. For example: $$ \text{Maximize } Z = c_1x_1 + c_2x_2 $$ subject to linear/non-linear constraints. **Phase 3 — Derivation of the Solution:** Mathematical techniques (Simplex Method, Graphical Method, Branch and Bound, etc.) are applied to find the optimal values of the decision variables. **Phase 4 — Testing the Model and Solution:** The model is validated by feeding historical/real data. Sensitivity analysis checks how robust the solution is to parameter changes. **Phase 5 — Establishing Controls:** Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) are defined to monitor implementation. Examples: **Schedule Performance Index (SPI)**, **Cost Performance Index (CPI)**. **Phase 6 — Implementation:** The optimized solution is put into practice. Continuous monitoring ensures that deviations are corrected. **2. Difference between OR and Conventional Research:** | Aspect | Conventional Research | Operations Research | |---|---|---| | **Purpose** | Discovery of new knowledge | Optimal decision-making | | **Approach** | Trial-and-error, empirical | Scientific, mathematical | | **Subject** | Natural/physical phenomena | Operational/business systems | | **Team** | Individual researchers | Inter-disciplinary team | | **Output** | Theories, principles | Optimal decisions/policies | (7 marks distributed: 5 for methodology phases with diagram; 2 for the comparison table) --- #### **Model Solution to Q-A (b):** **Step 1 — Decision Variables:** Let: - $x_1$ = number of Type A (small hull blocks) produced per week - $x_2$ = number of Type B (large hull blocks) produced per week **Step 2 — Objective Function:** Maximize weekly profit $Z$ (in ₹): $$ Z = 15000\,x_1 + 20000\,x_2 $$ **Step 3 — Constraints:** Fabrication hours constraint: $$ 4x_1 + 5x_2 \;\le\; 120 $$ Welding station hours constraint: $$ 3x_1 + 4x_2 \;\le\; 90 $$ Non-negativity: $$ x_1 \;\ge\; 0, \quad x_2 \;\ge\; 0 $$ **Step 4 — Complete LP Formulation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Maximize } & Z = 15000x_1 + 20000x_2 \\ \text{Subject to } & 4x_1 + 5x_2 \le 120 \\ & 3x_1 + 4x_2 \le 90 \\ & x_1, x_2 \ge 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Graphical Method — Find Corner Points:** - **Origin:** $O = (0, 0)$, $Z = 0$ - **On $x_1$-axis:** Set $x_2 = 0$ in the more restrictive constraint. From $3x_1 \le 90$, $x_1 = 30$. From $4x_1 \le 120$, $x_1 = 30$. Both give the same: $A = (30, 0)$. - **On $x_2$-axis:** Set $x_1 = 0$. From $5x_2 \le 120$, $x_2 = 24$. From $4x_2 \le 90$, $x_2 = 22.5$. The more restrictive is $B = (0, 22.5)$. - **Intersection of two constraints:** Solve simultaneously: $$ \begin{aligned} 4x_1 + 5x_2 &= 120 \\ 3x_1 + 4x_2 &= 90 \end{aligned} $$ Multiply the second equation by 4 and the first by 3: $$ \begin{aligned} 12x_1 + 15x_2 &= 360 \\ 12x_1 + 16x_2 &= 360 \end{aligned} $$ Subtract the first from the second: $$ x_2 = 0 $$ Substitute back: $4x_1 = 120 \Rightarrow x_1 = 30$. So the intersection point coincides with $A = (30, 0)$, meaning the two constraints intersect on the $x_1$-axis. The feasible region is the quadrilateral with corners $O, A, C, B$ where $C$ is the intersection with the painting bay... Wait — let us re-check by solving more carefully: From the second equation: $x_1 = (90 - 4x_2)/3$. Substitute into the first: $$ 4 \cdot \dfrac{90 - 4x_2}{3} + 5x_2 = 120 $$ $$ \dfrac{360 - 16x_2}{3} + 5x_2 = 120 $$ Multiply through by 3: $$ 360 - 16x_2 + 15x_2 = 360 $$ $$ -x_2 = 0 $$ $$ x_2 = 0, \quad x_1 = 30 $$ The two constraints intersect at $(30, 0)$, so the feasible region corners are: - $O = (0, 0)$ - $A = (30, 0)$ - $B = (0, 22.5)$ **Step 6 — Evaluate $Z$ at Each Corner:** $$ \begin{aligned} Z(O) &= 15000(0) + 20000(0) = 0 \\ Z(A) &= 15000(30) + 20000(0) = 4,50,000 \\ Z(B) &= 15000(0) + 20000(22.5) = 4,50,000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Optimal Solution:** The maximum profit is **₹4,50,000 per week**, achieved at the corner points $A = (30, 0)$ and $B = (0, 22.5)$. This indicates **alternate optima** — the shipyard may produce any convex combination of these two solutions along the boundary segment $AB$. (7 marks distributed: 2 for formulation, 2 for graphical method setup, 2 for solving and corner evaluation, 1 for the optimal conclusion) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the applications of Operations Research in ship production management. Give at least six specific examples. **(b) [7 Marks]** A shipyard has to decide between two OR models for forecasting steel stockyard requirements. Model X has a fixed cost of **₹50,000** and a variable cost of **₹800 per ton** of steel handled. Model Y has a fixed cost of **₹30,000** and a variable cost of **₹1,200 per ton**. - (i) Determine the **break-even quantity** of steel at which both models yield the same cost. - (ii) If the expected annual steel requirement is **80 tons**, which model should be chosen and why? - (iii) Comment on the role of **break-even analysis** in OR-based decision making. --- #### **Model Solution to Q-B (a):** **Applications of OR in Ship Production Management:** 1. **Block Erection Sequencing (CPM/PERT):** Planning the critical path for fabricating and erecting hull blocks ensures minimum project duration. 2. **Linear Programming for Steel Allocation:** Deciding how much steel plate of each grade to cut from available stock to minimize wastage and meet design requirements. 3. **Inventory Control (EOQ Models):** Optimizing the Economic Order Quantity for consumables (welding rods, paints, electrodes) to minimize total inventory cost. 4. **Queuing Theory for Dry-Dock Operations:** Modeling ship arrivals at the dry dock to minimize waiting time and maximize dock utilization. 5. **Assignment Problem for Crane Allocation:** Assigning gantry cranes to shipbuilding berths to minimize idle time and material handling cost. 6. **Simulation for Production Line Balancing:** Simulating panel fabrication lines to identify bottlenecks and balance workstation loads. 7. **Game Theory for Competitive Bidding:** Modeling strategic bidding behavior for new shipbuilding contracts. 8. **Dynamic Programming for Welding Sequence Optimization:** Breaking the welding problem into stages to minimize heat distortion and rework. (7 marks — 1 mark each for any 6 applications with brief explanation + 1 mark for overall presentation) --- #### **Model Solution to Q-B (b):** **Total Cost of Model X:** $$ C_X = 50000 + 800Q $$ **Total Cost of Model Y:** $$ C_Y = 30000 + 1200Q $$ **Part (i) — Break-Even Quantity:** At break-even, $C_X = C_Y$: $$ \begin{aligned} 50000 + 800Q &= 30000 + 1200Q \\ 50000 - 30000 &= 1200Q - 800Q \\ 20000 &= 400Q \\ Q &= \dfrac{20000}{400} = 50 \text{ tons} \end{aligned} $$ **Break-even quantity = 50 tons per year.** **Part (ii) — Decision for Q = 80 tons:** Compute cost for Model X at $Q = 80$: $$ C_X = 50000 + 800(80) = 50000 + 64000 = 1,14,000 $$ Compute cost for Model Y at $Q = 80$: $$ C_Y = 30000 + 1200(80) = 30000 + 96000 = 1,26,000 $$ Since $C_X < C_Y$ at $Q = 80$ tons, **Model X should be chosen** because it is more economical (₹12,000 cheaper per year). The rule is: choose **Model X** when $Q > 50$ tons, choose **Model Y** when $Q < 50$ tons. **Part (iii) — Role of Break-Even Analysis in OR:** Break-even analysis helps decision-makers identify the **threshold** at which two alternatives become equally attractive. It is a fundamental tool in: - **Make-or-buy decisions** in ship production - **Equipment selection** (in-house vs. outsourcing) - **Cost-volume-profit planning** - **Sensitivity analysis** — shows how robust the decision is to demand fluctuations (7 marks distributed: 2 for break-even calculation, 2 for comparison at $Q=80$, 1 for the decision logic, 2 for the role of break-even analysis in OR) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting non-negativity constraints** in LP formulation — leads to deduction of **at least 1 mark**. > 2. **Not specifying units** in the objective function (e.g., "Maximize Z" without saying Z is profit in ₹/week) — strict examiners deduct 0.5 mark. > 3. **In Graphical Method:** Not plotting the **feasible region** clearly or **not labeling corner points** with coordinates — loses up to 2 marks. > 4. **In Break-Even Analysis:** Students often write the cost equation but forget to equate $C_X = C_Y$ — an automatic zero. > 5. **In OR applications:** Writing vague answers like "OR is used in planning" without specifying **which technique** (CPM, LP, etc.) — loses content marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Use this checklist for a 5-minute pre-exam revision. Each bullet is a potential 1–3 mark item in the KTU 2024 paper.** - **Definition:** OR = application of scientific/mathematical methods to operational decision-making under constraints. - **Origin:** World War II military operations (1939–45). Pioneers: George Dantzig (Simplex), John von Neumann (Game Theory). - **Six Phases (in order):** Formulation → Mathematical Model → Solution → Testing & Validation → Controls → Implementation. - **Three Pillars of OR:** Systems Approach + Mathematical Modeling + Optimization. - **General Model Form:** $$ Z = f(\mathbf{x}) \;\text{ s.t. }\; g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le b_i, \; x_j \ge 0 $$ - **Standard LP Form:** Maximize/Minimize linear objective subject to linear constraints and non-negativity. - **Classification of OR Models:** Deterministic vs. Probabilistic; Static vs. Dynamic; Linear vs. Non-linear. - **Key OR Techniques (Name + Use):** - **LP** → Resource allocation - **CPM/PERT** → Project scheduling in shipbuilding - **Queuing Theory** → Dock utilization - **EOQ** → Inventory of consumables - **Game Theory** → Competitive bidding - **Simulation** → Bottleneck analysis - **Break-Even Point Formula:** $Q^* = \dfrac{F_1 - F_2}{V_2 - V_1}$ where $F$ = fixed cost, $V$ = variable cost per unit. - **OR in Shipbuilding:** Block erection sequencing, steel stockyard optimization, crane scheduling, workforce allocation, dry-dock management, welding planning, paint inventory. - **Inter-disciplinary nature** of OR team: Mathematics + Statistics + Engineering + Economics + Management. - **Sensitivity Analysis** is a must-do post-solution step — checks how the optimal solution changes with parameter variations. - **KTU 2024 Marking Pattern:** Part A (3 marks) = definition + characteristics; Part B (14 marks) = formulation + graphical/simplex solution + interpretation.

topics.title: Operation Planning and Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST633 - SHIP PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Operation Planning and Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Operation Planning and Control (OPC)** in ship production is the **systematic, integrated function** of determining the optimal sequence, timing, and allocation of resources (manpower, materials, machines, dock space, and information) required to transform a ship design into a delivered vessel, while continuously monitoring progress against the plan and initiating corrective action when deviations occur.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** curriculum for *PENST633 – Ship Production Management*, OPC encompasses the entire **post-contract shipbuilding cycle**, from keel laying to delivery, including hull construction, block erection, outfitting, painting, testing, and commissioning.

Operation Planning and Control rests on **four pillars**, universally accepted in modern shipyards (Korean, Japanese, and European standards):

1. **Planning** – What must be done? (Work Breakdown Structure, Master Schedule)
2. **Scheduling** – When must it be done? (Network diagrams, Gantt charts, line-of-balance)
3. **Executing** – Who does it and with what? (Resource loading, dispatching)
4. **Controlling** – Is it on track? (Progress measurement, Earned Value Analysis, variance analysis)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 3):** Operation Planning and Control deals with *day-to-day production decisions on the berth, in the panel shop, and in outfitting halls*. It is the bridge between the *strategic master plan* (Module 2) and the *shop-floor execution* (Module 4).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Shipyard as a Symphony Orchestra" Analogy

Imagine you are a **maestro conducting a 100-piece orchestra** to perform a two-hour symphony:

- The **musicians** are your **tradesmen** (fitters, welders, electricians, painters).
- The **instruments** are your **workshops, cranes, blocks, and welding machines**.
- The **sheet music** is your **production plan / network diagram**.
- The **concert hall timeline** is your **delivery date**.
- The **conductor's baton** is your **Production Control Department**.

If you begin playing without a score, chaos results — players enter late, cues are missed, and the audience (the **shipowner**) leaves unsatisfied. The maestro, however, plans every beat, signals entry points, monitors tempo, and corrects errors instantly.

**Operation Planning and Control is exactly this — it is the "maestro function" of a shipyard.** Without it, even a magnificent ship design remains a drawing on paper; with it, steel, paint, and cables coalesce into a seaworthy vessel delivered on time.

---

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics Used in OPC

The following metrics are universally applied in ship production control. They must be memorized in **bold** for KTU examinations:

| Metric | Standard Value / Unit | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Schedule Performance Index (SPI)** | Ratio (target = **1.0**) | Measures time efficiency |
| **Cost Performance Index (CPI)** | Ratio (target = **1.0**) | Measures cost efficiency |
| **Critical Path Length** | Days | Defines minimum project duration |
| **Block Erection Rate** | blocks / month | Measures throughput |
| **Man-hours per Compensated Gross Tonnage (CGT)** | MH/CGT | Industry productivity benchmark |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant to Remember:** A modern Korean shipyard achieves approximately **15–25 man-hours per CGT**, while older European yards operate at **40–80 MH/CGT**. This metric is the **gold standard** for comparing shipyard productivity in KTU case-study questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of OPC

Operation Planning and Control in shipbuilding is decomposed into the following **logical, sequential hierarchy**:

- **Level 1 — Contract Planning**
  - Establishes the master delivery date.
  - Develops the **Master Production Schedule (MPS)**.
  - Identifies major milestones (Keel Laying → Launching → Outfitting → Sea Trials → Delivery).

- **Level 2 — Zone / Stage Planning**
  - Ship is divided into **zones** (forward, midship, aft) and **stages** (pre-outfitting, block outfitting, dock outfitting, quay outfitting).
  - Each zone is planned independently using a **Zone Outfitting Plan**.

- **Level 3 — Weekly / Daily Control**
  - Issues **work orders** to shop foremen.
  - Tracks progress using **daily progress reports (DPRs)**.
  - Reallocates resources in case of bottlenecks.

- **Level 4 — Feedback and Corrective Action**
  - Compares *actual progress* with *planned progress*.
  - Computes variances and triggers recovery plans (overtime, subcontracting, resequencing).

### 2.2 Tools of Operation Planning and Control

The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates study of the following four tools:

1. **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)**
2. **Gantt / Bar Charts**
3. **Network Analysis (CPM and PERT)**
4. **Line-of-Balance (LOB) Technique**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why are these four tools chosen by KTU?**
> Because they form the **complete toolkit** used by Indian shipyards (Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock, GRSE) and global yards (Hyundai Heavy Industries, Daewoo, Fincantieri). Mastering them is essential for placements in the Indian shipbuilding industry.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations are **exam-critical**. Each appears routinely in KTU Part B (14-mark) questions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{CPM Float Calculations:} \quad & \\
\text{Total Float (TF)} &= \text{LS} - \text{ES} \;=\; \text{LF} - \text{EF} \\
\text{Free Float (FF)} &= \text{ES}_{\text{successor}} - \text{EF}_{\text{current}} \\
\text{Independent Float (IF)} &= \text{ES}_{\text{successor}} - \text{LF}_{\text{current}} \\
\text{where ES} &= \text{Earliest Start, EF} = \text{Earliest Finish} \\
\text{LS} &= \text{Latest Start, LF} = \text{Latest Finish}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Earned Value Management (EVM):} \quad & \\
\text{EV} &= \text{PV} \times \text{Percent Complete} \\
\text{CV} &= \text{EV} - \text{AC} \quad (\text{Cost Variance}) \\
\text{SV} &= \text{EV} - \text{PV} \quad (\text{Schedule Variance}) \\
\text{CPI} &= \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}} \quad (\text{target} = 1.0) \\
\text{SPI} &= \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{PV}} \quad (\text{target} = 1.0) \\
\text{EAC} &= \frac{\text{BAC}}{\text{CPI}} \quad (\text{Estimate at Completion})
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Productivity Metrics:} \quad & \\
\text{MH/CGT} &= \frac{\text{Total Man-Hours Expended}}{\text{Compensated Gross Tonnage}} \\
\text{Slip} &= \text{Planned Finish} - \text{Actual Finish} \\
\text{Critical Path Duration (CPD)} &= \sum_{i \in \text{CP}} D_i
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions for KTU:**
> - If **CPI < 1**, the project is **over budget** (unfavourable).
> - If **SPI < 1**, the project is **behind schedule** (unfavourable).
> - If **TF = 0**, the activity lies on the **Critical Path** and any delay delays the entire ship delivery.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where These Concepts Are Used in Real Shipyards

- **Gantt charts** are printed daily in the Production Control room of every major yard.
- **CPM networks** are used to plan the **1,500–4,000 activities** of a single vessel.
- **EVM** is reported monthly to shipowners in naval and commercial contracts.
- **Line-of-Balance** is essential for **series production** (e.g., Indian Navy frigate programmes, bulk carriers).

---

### 2.5 Critical Path Method — Forward Pass & Backward Pass Logic

The Critical Path Method (CPM) is the **backbone of OPC** in shipbuilding. It identifies the longest sequence of dependent activities and determines the **minimum project duration**.

**Forward Pass Rules:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ES}_j &= \max_{i \in \text{pred}(j)} \left( \text{EF}_i \right) \\
\text{EF}_j &= \text{ES}_j + D_j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Backward Pass Rules:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LF}_i &= \min_{j \in \text{succ}(i)} \left( \text{LS}_j \right) \\
\text{LS}_i &= \text{LF}_i - D_i
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Intuition (the "Why"):**
> - The *forward pass* answers: *"How soon can we finish?"* It bubbles the earliest finish times from the start node to the end node.
> - The *backward pass* answers: *"How late can we start without delaying the ship?"* It pulls the latest start times backward from the project deadline.
> - Activities where **ES = LS** (or **EF = LF**) lie on the **Critical Path** — they have **zero float** and demand uninterrupted monitoring.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

### 3.1 Worked Example — CPM for a 7-Activity Ship Hull Block

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Pattern:** This is the *exact style* of 14-mark Part B question. Practice it until you can solve in under 25 minutes.

**Problem Statement:**
A shipbuilding project consists of **7 activities (A to G)** for the hull block. The table below shows the logical sequence and durations (in weeks):

| Activity | Duration (weeks) | Predecessor |
|---|---|---|
| A — Steel cutting | 3 | — |
| B — Plate bending | 4 | A |
| C — Sub-assembly welding | 5 | B |
| D — Panel fabrication | 6 | B |
| E — Block assembly | 7 | C, D |
| F — Surface treatment & painting | 4 | E |
| G — Inspection & despatch to berth | 2 | F |

**Required:**
1. Construct the network and identify the **Critical Path**.
2. Calculate the **Total Float, Free Float, and Independent Float** for every activity.
3. Compute the **project duration**.

---

#### Step 1 — Identify Network Logic

The logical dependency diagram is:

- A → B → {C, D} → E → F → G
- (C and D are parallel branches merging into E)

#### Step 2 — Forward Pass (Compute ES and EF)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ES}_A &= 0, \quad \text{EF}_A = 0 + 3 = 3 \\
\text{ES}_B &= \text{EF}_A = 3, \quad \text{EF}_B = 3 + 4 = 7 \\
\text{ES}_C &= \text{EF}_B = 7, \quad \text{EF}_C = 7 + 5 = 12 \\
\text{ES}_D &= \text{EF}_B = 7, \quad \text{EF}_D = 7 + 6 = 13 \\
\text{ES}_E &= \max(\text{EF}_C, \text{EF}_D) = \max(12, 13) = 13 \\
\text{EF}_E &= 13 + 7 = 20 \\
\text{ES}_F &= \text{EF}_E = 20, \quad \text{EF}_F = 20 + 4 = 24 \\
\text{ES}_G &= \text{EF}_F = 24, \quad \text{EF}_G = 24 + 2 = 26
\end{aligned}
$$

**Project Duration = EF_G = 26 weeks.**

#### Step 3 — Backward Pass (Compute LS and LF)

We begin from the last activity G (LF_G = 26 because it is the project end).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LF}_G &= 26, \quad \text{LS}_G = 26 - 2 = 24 \\
\text{LF}_F &= \text{LS}_G = 24, \quad \text{LS}_F = 24 - 4 = 20 \\
\text{LF}_E &= \text{LS}_F = 20, \quad \text{LS}_E = 20 - 7 = 13 \\
\text{LF}_C &= \text{LS}_E = 13, \quad \text{LS}_C = 13 - 5 = 8 \\
\text{LF}_D &= \text{LS}_E = 13, \quad \text{LS}_D = 13 - 6 = 7 \\
\text{LF}_B &= \min(\text{LS}_C, \text{LS}_D) = \min(8, 7) = 7 \\
\text{LS}_B &= 7 - 4 = 3 \\
\text{LF}_A &= \text{LS}_B = 3, \quad \text{LS}_A = 3 - 3 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Compute Total Float, Free Float, and Independent Float

Recall:
- **Total Float** $\text{TF}_i = \text{LS}_i - \text{ES}_i$
- **Free Float** $\text{FF}_i = \text{ES}_{succ} - \text{EF}_i$
- **Independent Float** $\text{IF}_i = \text{ES}_{succ} - \text{LF}_i$

For activities with **multiple successors**, Free Float uses the *minimum* ES of successors.

| Activity | D | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | FF | IF | Critical? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 3 | 0 | 3 | 0 | 3 | 0 | 0 | 0 | ✅ YES |
| B | 4 | 3 | 7 | 3 | 7 | 0 | 0 | 0 | ✅ YES |
| C | 5 | 7 | 12 | 8 | 13 | 1 | 1 | 0 | ❌ No |
| D | 6 | 7 | 13 | 7 | 13 | 0 | 0 | 0 | ✅ YES |
| E | 7 | 13 | 20 | 13 | 20 | 0 | 0 | 0 | ✅ YES |
| F | 4 | 20 | 24 | 20 | 24 | 0 | 0 | 0 | ✅ YES |
| G | 2 | 24 | 26 | 24 | 26 | 0 | 0 | 0 | ✅ YES |

**Critical Path = A → B → D → E → F → G**
**Project Duration = 26 weeks**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation:** Activity C has TF = 1 week. This means steel sub-assembly welding can be delayed by up to 1 week without delaying the ship. However, this float is **fragile** and any slippage must trigger rescheduling.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Earned Value Management (EVM)

**Problem Statement:**
A shipyard reports the following at the *end of month 8* of a 12-month shipbuilding contract:

- **Budget at Completion (BAC)** = ₹ 240 Crore
- **Planned Value (PV)** at month 8 = ₹ 160 Crore (planned progress)
- **Actual Cost (AC)** incurred = ₹ 180 Crore
- **Actual percent complete** = 55 %

**Required:** Compute EV, CV, SV, CPI, SPI, and EAC.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EV} &= \text{PV} \times \text{Percent Complete} = 160 \times 0.55 = \text{₹ 88 Crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — let us apply the correct definition. EV is computed directly from the budget proportional to work done:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EV} &= \text{BAC} \times \text{Percent Complete} = 240 \times 0.55 = \text{₹ 132 Crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CV} &= \text{EV} - \text{AC} = 132 - 180 = -\text{₹ 48 Crore (over budget)} \\
\text{SV} &= \text{EV} - \text{PV} = 132 - 160 = -\text{₹ 28 Crore (behind schedule)} \\
\text{CPI} &= \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}} = \frac{132}{180} = 0.73 \quad (\text{< 1, over budget}) \\
\text{SPI} &= \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{PV}} = \frac{132}{160} = 0.825 \quad (\text{< 1, behind schedule}) \\
\text{EAC} &= \frac{\text{BAC}}{\text{CPI}} = \frac{240}{0.73} \approx \text{₹ 328.77 Crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many KTU students compute EV as *PV × % complete*. This is **incorrect**. The correct formula is **EV = BAC × % complete**, because EV must reflect the *budgeted value of work actually performed*, independent of how much was planned.

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- CPI = 0.73 ⇒ for every ₹1 spent, only ₹0.73 of work is earned. The yard is **burning 27 % extra cash**.
- SPI = 0.825 ⇒ the yard is **17.5 % behind schedule**.
- Without corrective action, the final cost will balloon to **₹ 328.77 Crore** versus the contracted ₹240 Crore — a **₹ 88.77 Crore loss**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Situation | SPI | CPI | Yard Manager's Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ahead of schedule, under budget | > 1 | > 1 | Accelerate to capture early-delivery bonus |
| Ahead of schedule, over budget | > 1 | < 1 | Maintain tempo, audit costs |
| Behind schedule, under budget | < 1 | > 1 | Add resources (overtime, subcontract) |
| Behind schedule, over budget | < 1 | < 1 | **Crisis mode** — resequence, re-baseline, escalate to owner |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Operation Planning and Control — Master Process Flow

The following Mermaid block illustrates the **integrated OPC loop** as practised in modern shipyards:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Contract Signed]) --> planNode[Master Production Schedule]
    planNode --> wbsNode[Work Breakdown Structure]
    wbsNode --> netNode[Network Construction CPM and PERT]
    netNode --> ganttNode[Gantt Chart Generation]
    ganttNode --> dispNode[Work Order Dispatch to Workshops]
    dispNode --> execNode[Shop Floor Execution]
    execNode --> measNode[Daily Progress Measurement]
    measNode --> evmNode[Earned Value Analysis]
    evmNode --> decideNode{Variance within Tolerance}
    decideNode -- YES --> continueNode[Continue Execution]
    continueNode --> execNode
    decideNode -- NO --> recoverNode[Recovery Plan: Overtime or Reschedule]
    recoverNode --> netNode
    measNode --> finalNode([Ship Delivery])
    finalNode --> stopNode([Project Closed])
```

> **How to read this diagram:**
> - The **left-to-right flow** shows the *planning sequence*.
> - The **feedback loop** from `decideNode` back to `netNode` is the *controlling function*.
> - Every modern shipyard (e.g., Hyundai Heavy Industries Ulsan) runs this exact loop digitally through an **ERP system** such as SAP-PM or AVEVA Marine.

---

### 4.2 Critical Path Network for Hull Block Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1([Start]) --> actA[A: Steel Cutting\n3 weeks]
    actA --> actB[B: Plate Bending\n4 weeks]
    actB --> actC[C: Sub-Assembly\n5 weeks]
    actB --> actD[D: Panel Fabrication\n6 weeks]
    actC --> actE[E: Block Assembly\n7 weeks]
    actD --> actE
    actE --> actF[F: Painting\n4 weeks]
    actF --> actG[G: Inspection\n2 weeks]
    actG --> node2([Delivery])

    style actA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style actB fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style actD fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style actE fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style actF fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style actG fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style actC fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
```

> **Interpretation:** The **gold-highlighted path** (A → B → D → E → F → G) is the **Critical Path** of duration **26 weeks**. Activity C (silver) has a total float of 1 week. The ship cannot be delivered in less than 26 weeks.

---

### 4.3 Earned Value Management Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    calcNode[Compute EV, CPI, SPI] --> check1{CPI greater than 1}
    check1 -- YES --> budgetGood[Under Budget Zone]
    check1 -- NO --> budgetBad[Over Budget Zone]
    check2{SPI greater than 1}
    check2 -- YES --> schedGood[Ahead of Schedule Zone]
    check2 -- NO --> schedBad[Behind Schedule Zone]

    budgetGood --> actionA[Maintain Tempo and Audit Costs]
    budgetBad --> actionB[Cost Recovery: Renegotiate Vendors]
    schedGood --> actionC[Accelerate for Early Delivery Bonus]
    schedBad --> actionD[Schedule Recovery: Overtime or Resequence]

    style actionA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style actionC fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style actionB fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style actionD fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
```

> **Reading the Matrix:** *Green* cells are favourable; *Pink* cells demand immediate management intervention.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define Operation Planning and Control. List its four functional pillars.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Operation Planning and Control (OPC) is the integrated function of planning, scheduling, executing, and controlling all ship production activities from contract to delivery to ensure the vessel is built **on time, within budget, and to specification**.

**Four Functional Pillars:**
1. **Planning** — What work is required (WBS, MPS).
2. **Scheduling** — When each task occurs (CPM, Gantt).
3. **Executing** — Who does the work and with what resources.
4. **Controlling** — Comparing actual vs planned and taking corrective action.

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**What is the Critical Path in a shipbuilding network? Why does it require special monitoring?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Critical Path is the **longest path** of dependent activities in a project network, which determines the **minimum project duration**. Activities on this path have **zero total float**.

**Special monitoring is required because:**
- Any delay in a critical activity **directly delays ship delivery**.
- Critical activities cannot tolerate slippage — they must be **resourced first** (manpower, cranes, dock space).
- They form the **focus of weekly progress review meetings** in the Production Control department.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

A ship block consists of **9 activities (A through I)** with the following logical sequence and durations:

| Activity | Duration (days) | Predecessor(s) |
|---|---|---|
| A | 5 | — |
| B | 6 | A |
| C | 4 | A |
| D | 7 | B |
| E | 3 | C |
| F | 8 | D, E |
| G | 5 | F |
| H | 4 | G |
| I | 6 | H |

**(a)** Construct the network, perform the forward and backward pass, and identify the **Critical Path** and project duration. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **Total Float and Free Float** for every activity and comment on the activities with non-zero float. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) Solution — Forward and Backward Pass:**

Forward Pass:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ES}_A &= 0, \quad \text{EF}_A = 5 \\
\text{ES}_B &= 5, \quad \text{EF}_B = 5 + 6 = 11 \\
\text{ES}_C &= 5, \quad \text{EF}_C = 5 + 4 = 9 \\
\text{ES}_D &= 11, \quad \text{EF}_D = 11 + 7 = 18 \\
\text{ES}_E &= 9, \quad \text{EF}_E = 9 + 3 = 12 \\
\text{ES}_F &= \max(18, 12) = 18, \quad \text{EF}_F = 18 + 8 = 26 \\
\text{ES}_G &= 26, \quad \text{EF}_G = 26 + 5 = 31 \\
\text{ES}_H &= 31, \quad \text{EF}_H = 31 + 4 = 35 \\
\text{ES}_I &= 35, \quad \text{EF}_I = 35 + 6 = 41
\end{aligned}
$$

Backward Pass:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LF}_I &= 41, \quad \text{LS}_I = 35 \\
\text{LF}_H &= 35, \quad \text{LS}_H = 31 \\
\text{LF}_G &= 31, \quad \text{LS}_G = 26 \\
\text{LF}_F &= 26, \quad \text{LS}_F = 18 \\
\text{LF}_E &= \text{LS}_F = 18, \quad \text{LS}_E = 18 - 3 = 15 \\
\text{LF}_D &= \text{LS}_F = 18, \quad \text{LS}_D = 18 - 7 = 11 \\
\text{LF}_C &= \text{LS}_E = 15, \quad \text{LS}_C = 15 - 4 = 11 \\
\text{LF}_B &= \text{LS}_D = 11, \quad \text{LS}_B = 11 - 6 = 5 \\
\text{LF}_A &= \min(\text{LS}_B, \text{LS}_C) = \min(5, 11) = 5 \\
\text{LS}_A &= 5 - 5 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Critical Path = A → B → D → F → G → H → I**
**Project Duration = 41 days**

**Part (b) Solution — Float Table:**

| Activity | D | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | FF | Critical? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 5 | 0 | 5 | 0 | 5 | 0 | 0 | ✅ |
| B | 6 | 5 | 11 | 5 | 11 | 0 | 0 | ✅ |
| C | 4 | 5 | 9 | 11 | 15 | 6 | 9 | ❌ |
| D | 7 | 11 | 18 | 11 | 18 | 0 | 0 | ✅ |
| E | 3 | 9 | 12 | 15 | 18 | 6 | 6 | ❌ |
| F | 8 | 18 | 26 | 18 | 26 | 0 | 0 | ✅ |
| G | 5 | 26 | 31 | 26 | 31 | 0 | 0 | ✅ |
| H | 4 | 31 | 35 | 31 | 35 | 0 | 0 | ✅ |
| I | 6 | 35 | 41 | 35 | 41 | 0 | 0 | ✅ |

**Comment:** Activities C and E are non-critical. They have a Total Float of **6 days each**. This means activity C (parallel sub-workstream) can be delayed up to 6 days without delaying the ship. However, the Free Float of C is 9 days because it does not feed the critical path immediately. The shipyard manager should allocate **resources preferentially to the critical path** to protect the 41-day deadline.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the **Earned Value Management (EVM) system** with its key metrics. How is it applied in shipbuilding for progress control? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A naval vessel project has the following data at the end of month 10 of a 24-month contract:

- **BAC** = ₹ 600 Crore
- **PV** = ₹ 250 Crore (planned to date)
- **AC** = ₹ 290 Crore (actually spent)
- **Actual % complete** = 45 %

Calculate **EV, CV, SV, CPI, SPI, and EAC**, and interpret the results. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — EVM Explanation:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Earned Value Management (EVM)** is an integrated project control technique that measures **scope, schedule, and cost performance simultaneously**. It was developed by the US Department of Defense in the 1960s and is today the *gold standard* for shipbuilding progress reporting under MoD contracts.

**Key Metrics:**

- **Planned Value (PV)** — Budgeted cost of work *scheduled* to date.
- **Earned Value (EV)** — Budgeted cost of work *actually performed*.
- **Actual Cost (AC)** — Money *actually spent* to date.

**Derived Metrics:**

$$
\text{CV} = \text{EV} - \text{AC} \qquad \text{SV} = \text{EV} - \text{PV}
$$

$$
\text{CPI} = \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{AC}} \qquad \text{SPI} = \frac{\text{EV}}{\text{PV}}
$$

**Application in Shipbuilding:**
1. Monthly progress reports from each zone (forward, midship, aft) feed the EVM model.
2. The Production Control department computes CV, SV, CPI, SPI and publishes a dashboard.
3. The Project Manager uses CPI to forecast final cost (EAC) and SPI to forecast final delivery date.
4. If both indices fall below 1, the project is flagged as **"at-risk"** and a recovery plan is initiated.
5. EVM provides **early warning** — typically problems are detected 3 to 6 months before they become crises.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EV} &= \text{BAC} \times \text{\% Complete} = 600 \times 0.45 = \text{₹ 270 Crore} \\
\text{CV} &= \text{EV} - \text{AC} = 270 - 290 = -\text{₹ 20 Crore (over budget)} \\
\text{SV} &= \text{EV} - \text{PV} = 270 - 250 = +\text{₹ 20 Crore (ahead of schedule)} \\
\text{CPI} &= \frac{270}{290} = 0.93 \quad (\text{over budget, slightly}) \\
\text{SPI} &= \frac{270}{250} = 1.08 \quad (\text{ahead of schedule}) \\
\text{EAC} &= \frac{600}{0.93} \approx \text{₹ 645.16 Crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- **SPI = 1.08** → the project is approximately **8 % ahead of schedule**. Good news — early delivery may be possible.
- **CPI = 0.93** → the project is **7 % over budget**. Bad news — cost discipline is slipping.
- **EAC = ₹ 645.16 Crore** → the final cost is forecast to be **₹ 45.16 Crore above the contract value**.
- **Recommended Action:** Since the yard is *ahead of schedule but over budget*, the manager should **maintain the schedule pace** (to capture any early-delivery bonus) while **auditing cost overruns** in procurement (steel, engines, electronics). Renegotiating vendor contracts is the immediate priority.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in OPC Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to identify the Critical Path explicitly** — KTU examiners award 2 marks purely for stating "Critical Path = A → B → D → F → G" with the correct duration. Many students compute floats but never label the path.
> 2. **Confusing Total Float with Free Float** — TF considers *project* deadline; FF considers *successor's* earliest start. Many students write TF = FF for all activities, losing 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Wrong EVM formula for EV** — Using *EV = PV × % complete* instead of *EV = BAC × % complete* is a **3-mark penalty** in 14-mark questions.
> 4. **Skipping units in answers** — Always write "₹ Crore" or "weeks". Examiners deduct marks for missing units.
> 5. **Not commenting on managerial interpretation** — A 14-mark question on EVM demands both *calculation* (5 marks) and *interpretation* (2 marks). Students who only compute lose 2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **OPC = Planning + Scheduling + Execution + Control** — these four pillars are the heart of Module 3.
- **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** decomposes the ship into *zones → blocks → sub-blocks → units → parts*.
- **CPM identifies the Critical Path** — the sequence of activities with zero total float that determines the **minimum project duration**.
- **Forward pass** computes ES and EF; **Backward pass** computes LS and LF.
- **Total Float = LS − ES**; **Free Float = ES of successor − EF of current**; **Independent Float = ES of successor − LF of current**.
- **Earned Value Management** integrates **scope, schedule, and cost** into a single dashboard using EV, PV, AC, CV, SV, CPI, SPI.
- **CPI < 1 ⇒ over budget**; **SPI < 1 ⇒ behind schedule**; **CPI > 1, SPI > 1 ⇒ healthy project**.
- **EAC = BAC / CPI** is the standard forecast for final cost.
- **Line-of-Balance (LOB)** is used for series production (multiple identical ships); it balances the workflow across zones.
- **Gantt charts** are visual tools showing activities as horizontal bars against time.
- **Productivity benchmark:** modern yards achieve **15–25 MH/CGT**; older yards operate at **40–80 MH/CGT**.
- **Indian shipyards** (Cochin Shipyard, MDL, GRSE) follow the same OPC principles as Korean and European yards, often using SAP-ERP.
- **Pre-outfitting** is preferred over post-launch outfitting because it reduces the **critical path of the dry-dock phase** by 20–30 %.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production Planning
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST633 - SHIP PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Production Planning

# Production Planning in Ship Production Management

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Production Planning** is the systematic process of defining, organizing, scheduling, and controlling all manufacturing activities, resources (manpower, material, machines, dock space, and time), and sub-operations required to transform raw materials (steel plates, sections, pipes) into a finished ship, ensuring delivery within the contracted time, cost, and quality envelope.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, production planning for shipyards is treated as an **integrative management function** that bridges **contract design**, **engineering preparation**, **material procurement**, **fabrication**, **assembly (hull and outfitting)**, **launching**, **fitting-out**, and **sea trials** into a single coordinated time-bound workflow.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are organizing a **large Indian wedding banquet for 500 guests**:
- You must book the hall (dock/berth),
- Order raw materials (vegetables, groceries) — in the right quantity and at the right time,
- Schedule cooks, servers, decorators, and musicians (workforce),
- Sequence events — mehendi → wedding → reception,
- Track delays — if the cook is late, decoration must wait.

**A shipyard is just a much larger, more technical version of this.** The "wedding hall" is the building dock; the "guests" are the steel blocks; the "cooks" are welders, fitters, and machinists. **Production planning is the master checklist that keeps the entire shipyard orchestra in sync.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Highlight:** In the 2024 Scheme, production planning is explicitly linked to **Productivity Engineering** outcomes. The examiner expects answers framed around *time-reduction*, *cost-reduction*, and *quality-improvement* levers.

### Core Production Planning Metrics (KTU Board Emphasis)

The following quantitative and qualitative metrics are **must-know** for any KTU 2024 answer:

- **Lead Time (LT):** Total time from order receipt to delivery.
- **Takt Time:** Available production time ÷ Customer demand.
- **Cycle Time (CT):** Time required to complete one unit/operation.
- **Work Content (WC):** Total manual + machine time per task.
- **Learning Curve Rate (b):** Typically **80%** or **90%** in shipbuilding.
- **Capacity Utilization (CU):** Actual output ÷ Maximum possible output, expressed as a percentage.
- **Critical Path Length:** Longest duration path in a project network.
- **Standard Shipyard KPIs:** Steel throughput (tonnes/month), man-hours per Compensated Gross Tonnage (CGT), block erection rate (blocks/week).

> [!TIP]
> Always quote at least **one numerical metric** in a 14-mark answer — examiners reward quantitative reasoning under the KTU 2024 OBE rubric.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of Production Planning

Production planning in a modern shipyard follows a **multi-tier hierarchy** that the KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly maps to decision horizons:

| Planning Level | Time Horizon | Decision Type | Shipyard Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Strategic / Long-Range** | 3 – 5 years | Yard expansion, new berths, capital investment | Adding a new Panamax-size building dock |
| **Tactical / Medium-Range** | 6 – 18 months | Aggregate production plan, workforce, dock occupancy | Allocating berths to 6 ships in a year |
| **Operational / Short-Range** | 1 day – 3 months | Detailed shop-floor scheduling, daily dispatch | Sequencing block welds on Plate Shop Line 2 |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Module 3 focuses predominantly on the **Operational and Tactical** tiers — the level where ship production managers actually "live."

## 2.2 Core Steps in Production Planning (KTU High-Yield Flow)

1. **Demand Forecasting** — Predict order inflow (new ship contracts).
2. **Capacity Planning** — Match yard throughput to forecast demand.
3. **Aggregate Planning** — Decide output rates, inventory, subcontracting.
4. **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** — Quantify *which* ship class, *how many*, and *when*.
5. **Material Requirements Planning (MRP)** — Explode the Bill of Materials (BOM) into purchase orders for steel, pipes, outfitting.
6. **Routing & Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** — Break ship into blocks → panels → sub-assemblies.
7. **Scheduling** — Assign dates using Gantt charts, CPM/PERT, Line-of-Balance (LOB).
8. **Dispatching & Execution** — Release work orders to shops.
9. **Expediting & Control** — Monitor progress, take corrective action.

## 2.3 The Line-of-Balance (LOB) Technique — *KTU Favourite*

The **Line-of-Balance** is a planning technique used in **repetitive ship production** (e.g., a series of 5 bulk carriers). It plots the **cumulative number of units completed** versus **time**, producing straight "objectives lines" and "production lines" that must remain parallel for on-time delivery.

If the actual production line **falls below** the objective line, the planner must take **expediting action** (overtime, additional shifts, sub-contracting).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` (not `\|`) when writing absolute values in tables to avoid breaking markdown syntax.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **EOQ (Economic Order Quantity)** | $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D S}{H}}$ | $D$ = annual demand (tonnes), $S$ = ordering cost (₹/order), $H$ = holding cost (₹/tonne/yr) | Steel plate procurement |
| 2 | **Learning Curve (Unit Cost)** | $Y_{x} = Y_{1} \, x^{b}$ | $Y_{x}$ = cost of $x$-th unit, $Y_{1}$ = first-unit cost, $b = \dfrac{\log(\text{learning rate})}{\log 2}$ | Man-hour estimation for repeated blocks |
| 3 | **Learning Slope (b)** | $b = \dfrac{\log r}{\log 2}$ | $r$ = learning rate (e.g., 0.80 for 80%) | Block fabrication learning |
| 4 | **Takt Time** | $T_{takt} = \dfrac{T_{available}}{D}$ | $T_{available}$ = available time, $D$ = demand | Dock occupancy planning |
| 5 | **Capacity Utilization** | $CU = \dfrac{\text{Actual Output}}{\text{Max Capacity}} \times 100\%$ | Percent (%) | Yard performance KPI |
| 6 | **Critical Path (CPM)** | $T_{proj} = \max \sum t_{i}$ | $t_{i}$ = activity durations | Project duration |
| 7 | **PERT Expected Time** | $t_{e} = \dfrac{a + 4m + b}{6}$ | $a$ = optimistic, $m$ = most likely, $b$ = pessimistic | Activity duration estimate |
| 8 | **PERT Variance** | $\sigma^{2} = \left(\dfrac{b - a}{6}\right)^{2}$ | Same as above | Risk / probability analysis |
| 9 | **Line-of-Balance Lead** | $\text{Lead} = \dfrac{\text{Cumulative completion}}{\text{Cumulative target}}$ | Unitless ratio (target = 1) | Schedule monitoring |
| 10 | **Standard Man-Hours per CGT** | $MH/CGT = f(\text{ship complexity})$ | Hours | Industry productivity benchmark |
| 11 | **Float (Slack)** | $F_{i} = LS_{i} - ES_{i}$ | Days / hours | Scheduling flexibility |
| 12 | **Crash Cost Slope** | $\text{Slope} = \dfrac{C_{c} - C_{n}}{T_{n} - T_{c}}$ | ₹/day | Project compression cost |

## 2.5 Why Production Planning Matters (Engineering Utility)

> [!NOTE]
> In shipbuilding, **a one-day delay in the critical path can cascade into a 30-day delivery slip** because of dependency chains (steel → panel → block → erection → launching → fitting-out → trials). Production planning converts this **inherent uncertainty** into a **controllable risk profile**.

Real-world applications:

- **Berth scheduling** at shipyards (e.g., Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock) — avoids dock-bottlenecks.
