- **Block erection sequencing** — minimizes crane movements and tack-welding time.
- **Outfitting zoning** — maximizes parallel work pre- and post-launch.
- **Sub-contractor coordination** — aligns vendor deliveries with erection readiness.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Content Mandate:** Every algebraic step, every conversion, and every logic transition is shown explicitly. No shortcuts, no placeholders.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Learning Curve Calculation

### Problem Statement (Model KTU 14-mark sub-part)
A shipyard takes **1,00,000 man-hours** to build the **first bulk carrier** in a series of **8 identical vessels**. Assume an **85% learning curve** applies.
Calculate: (i) the slope $b$, (ii) man-hours for the 4th vessel, and (iii) cumulative man-hours for all 8 vessels.

### Step-by-Step Solution

#### Step 1 — Compute the learning slope $b$

$$b = \frac{\log r}{\log 2} = \frac{\log(0.85)}{\log 2} = \frac{-0.07058}{0.30103} = -0.2345$$

> *Reasoning:* The learning curve formula $Y_{x} = Y_{1} \cdot x^{b}$ expresses cumulative learning as a power law. For $r = 85\%$, every doubling of cumulative output reduces the per-unit man-hours to 85% of the previous value, giving a negative exponent.

#### Step 2 — Compute man-hours for the 4th vessel

$$Y_{4} = Y_{1} \cdot 4^{b} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 4^{-0.2345}$$

Compute $4^{-0.2345}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(4^{-0.2345}) &= -0.2345 \times \log_{10}(4) \\
&= -0.2345 \times 0.60206 \\
&= -0.14118 \\
\Rightarrow 4^{-0.2345} &= 10^{-0.14118} = 0.7207
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$Y_{4} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7207 = 72{,}070 \text{ man-hours}$$

#### Step 3 — Cumulative man-hours for all 8 vessels

For each unit $x$ from 1 to 8:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_1 &= 1{,}00{,}000 \\
Y_2 &= 1{,}00{,}000 \times 2^{-0.2345} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8500 = 85{,}000 \\
Y_3 &= 1{,}00{,}000 \times 3^{-0.2345} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.8041 = 80{,}410 \\
Y_4 &= 72{,}070 \\
Y_5 &= 1{,}00{,}000 \times 5^{-0.2345} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7222 = 72{,}220 \\
Y_6 &= 1{,}00{,}000 \times 6^{-0.2345} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.7003 = 70{,}030 \\
Y_7 &= 1{,}00{,}000 \times 7^{-0.2345} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6830 = 68{,}300 \\
Y_8 &= 1{,}00{,}000 \times 8^{-0.2345} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.6685 = 66{,}850
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Reasoning:* The factor $x^{-0.2345}$ is computed by taking the antilog of $(-0.2345 \times \log_{10} x)$ for each $x$. Values are obtained from standard log tables or a calculator; the trend shows asymptotic flattening of the learning curve.

**Cumulative Total:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{x=1}^{8} Y_x &= 1{,}00{,}000 + 85{,}000 + 80{,}410 + 72{,}070 \\
&\quad + 72{,}220 + 70{,}030 + 68{,}300 + 66{,}850 \\
&= 6{,}14{,}880 \text{ man-hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** Notice the cumulative saving is **$8 \times 1{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}14{,}880 = 1{,}85{,}120$ man-hours (~23%)** just from learning — a major reason shipbuilders prefer **series production**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — EOQ for Steel Plate Procurement

### Problem Statement
A shipyard consumes **D = 5,000 tonnes of steel plates per year**. The ordering cost is **S = ₹25,000 per order**, and the holding cost is **H = ₹400 per tonne per year**. Find the EOQ and the number of orders per year.

### Step-by-Step Solution

#### Step 1 — Apply the EOQ formula

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5{,}000 \times 25{,}000}{400}}
$$

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{25{,}00{,}00{,}000}{400}} = \sqrt{62{,}50{,}000} = 2500 \text{ tonnes}
$$

#### Step 2 — Number of orders per year

$$N = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{5000}{2500} = 2 \text{ orders per year}$$

#### Step 3 — Time between orders (Order Cycle)

$$T_{cycle} = \frac{365}{N} = \frac{365}{2} \approx 182.5 \text{ days}$$

#### Step 4 — Verify total cost (optional KTU 2024 add-on)

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC &= \frac{D}{Q} S + \frac{Q}{2} H \\
&= \frac{5000}{2500}(25{,}000) + \frac{2500}{2}(400) \\
&= 50{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 = 5{,}50{,}000 \text{ ₹/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Reasoning:* The first term is the annual ordering cost (₹50,000); the second is the average inventory holding cost (half of 2500 tonnes × ₹400 = ₹5,00,000). At EOQ, ordering cost equals holding cost — a useful sanity check.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — PERT Three-Point Estimate

### Problem Statement
An activity has optimistic time $a = 8$ days, most likely $m = 10$ days, and pessimistic $b = 18$ days. Find expected time and variance.

### Step-by-Step Solution

$$
t_{e} = \frac{a + 4m + b}{6} = \frac{8 + 40 + 18}{6} = \frac{66}{6} = 11 \text{ days}
$$

$$
\sigma^{2} = \left(\frac{b - a}{6}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{18 - 8}{6}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{10}{6}\right)^{2} = 2.78 \text{ days}^{2}
$$

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{2.78} \approx 1.667 \text{ days}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The 4-to-1 weighting on $m$ in the PERT formula reflects empirical evidence that project managers are **optimistic** and underestimate the most-likely value.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Planning Techniques

| Criterion | Gantt Chart | CPM | PERT | LOB |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Best for** | Visual task sequencing | Deterministic projects | Uncertain / R&D projects | Repetitive / series production |
| **Time estimates** | Single value | Single value | Three estimates $(a,m,b)$ | Production rate based |
| **Critical path** | Not directly | Yes | Yes (with probability) | Implied via slope |
| **Shipyard use** | Block erection schedule | Ship-new-building | First-of-kind engineering | Series production of sister ships |
| **Output** | Bar chart | Network + critical path | Expected time + probability | Slope of cumulative output |
| **Strength** | Simple to read | Highlights bottlenecks | Handles uncertainty | Optimizes flow balance |
| **Weakness** | No dependencies | Static | Data hungry | Needs repetitive product |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguard:** All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`). No reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style` are used as standalone node names. Labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text.

## 4.1 Production Planning Flow in a Shipyard

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[CONTRACT AWARDED] --> A2[ENGINEERING PREPARATION]
    A2 --> A3[WORK BREAKDOWN STRUCTURE]
    A3 --> A4[MASTER PRODUCTION SCHEDULE]
    A4 --> A5[MATERIAL REQUIREMENTS PLANNING]
    A5 --> A6[CAPACITY AND DOCK PLANNING]
    A6 --> A7[DETAILED SCHEDULING GANTT CPM PERT]
    A7 --> A8[DISPATCHING TO SHOPS]
    A8 --> A9[PROGRESS MONITORING LOB]
    A9 --> A10[CORRECTIVE ACTION EXPEDITING]
    A10 --> A11[SHIP LAUNCH AND TRIALS]
    A11 --> A12[SHIP DELIVERY]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The flow is **cyclic** — feedback from A10 (Corrective Action) loops back into A7 (Detailed Scheduling) whenever slippage is detected. This is the **Plan → Do → Check → Act (PDCA)** cycle in its shipyard form.

---

## 4.2 Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) for Ship Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[SHIP LEVEL 0] --> B2[HULL LEVEL 1]
    B1 --> B3[OUTFITTING LEVEL 1]
    B1 --> B4[PAINTING AND PRESERVATION LEVEL 1]
    B1 --> B5[COMMISSIONING AND TRIALS LEVEL 1]
    B2 --> B6[BLOCK LEVEL 2]
    B2 --> B7[PANEL LEVEL 2]
    B2 --> B8[GRAND BLOCK LEVEL 2]
    B6 --> B9[STIFFENER PANEL LEVEL 3]
    B6 --> B10[PLATE PANEL LEVEL 3]
    B6 --> B11[UNIT PANEL LEVEL 3]
    B3 --> B12[MACHINERY OUTfitting LEVEL 2]
    B3 --> B13[ELECTRICAL OUTfitting LEVEL 2]
    B3 --> B14[ACCOMMODATION OUTfitting LEVEL 2]
    B4 --> B15[SHOP PRIMING LEVEL 2]
    B4 --> B16[FINAL PAINTING LEVEL 2]
    B5 --> B17[MOORING TRIALS LEVEL 2]
    B5 --> B18[SEA TRIALS LEVEL 2]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always draw the WBS in **3 or 4 levels** for a 14-mark question. A well-decomposed WBS earns **easy 4–5 marks** even before any further content is added.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Production Planning Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[DESIGN OFFICE] --> C2[PRODUCTION PLANNING DEPARTMENT]
    C3[COMMERCIAL AND SALES] --> C2
    C4[MATERIAL CONTROL] --> C2
    C5[SHOP SUPERVISORS] --> C2
    C2 --> C6[GENERATION OF MPS]
    C2 --> C7[MRP AND BOM EXPLOSION]
    C2 --> C8[SCHEDULES GANTTS]
    C6 --> C9[PRODUCTION SHOPS]
    C7 --> C9
    C8 --> C9
    C9 --> C10[FEEDBACK ON PROGRESS]
    C10 --> C2
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** showing how the central planning department acts as a hub. The **feedback loop** from shops to planning is what makes modern shipyards reactive rather than static.

---

## 4.4 Line-of-Balance (LOB) Conceptual Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[UNITS COMPLETED AXIS Y] --> D2[TIME AXIS X]
    D3[OBJECTIVE LINE STRAIGHT] --> D2
    D4[ACTUAL PRODUCTION CURVE] --> D2
    D5[SCHEDULED DELIVERY TARGET] --> D2
    D4 --> D6[CHECK SLOPE]
    D6 --> D7[SCHEDULE IS ON TRACK]
    D4 --> D8[ACTUAL BELOW OBJECTIVE]
    D8 --> D9[EXPEDITE OVERTIME SUBCONTRACT]
    D4 --> D10[ACTUAL ABOVE OBJECTIVE]
    D10 --> D11[REALLOCATE RESOURCES]
```

> [!TIP]
> **For the KTU Exam:** If asked to "explain LOB," draw a **simple graph with Y = cumulative units completed, X = time, and two lines** — objective (straight) and actual (curved). State the rule: **actual must not fall below objective.**

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions are modeled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern** — Bloom's-tagged, CO-mapped, and validated against typical board valuation trends.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define production planning. List any four objectives of production planning in a shipyard.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

> **Definition (1 mark):** Production planning is the systematic determination of what is to be produced, when, in what quantity, using which resources, and through what sequence of operations to deliver a ship on time, within cost, and to specification.
>
> **Four objectives (2 marks, 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Minimize lead time** from contract award to delivery.
> 2. **Maximize dock and workforce utilization** to improve productivity.
> 3. **Ensure timely material availability** (steel, outfitting items) at each workstation.
> 4. **Balance workload** across shops, trades, and shifts to avoid bottlenecks.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between CPM and PERT in shipbuilding project planning.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

| Aspect | CPM | PERT |
|---|---|---|
| **Time estimates** | Deterministic (single value) | Probabilistic (three estimates $a$, $m$, $b$) |
| **Use case** | Routine, repetitive shipbuilding (series tonnage) | First-of-class, R&D-heavy projects |
| **Output** | Critical path, float | Expected time + variance / probability |
| **Network** | Activity-on-Arrow or Precedence | Typically Activity-on-Arrow |
| **Critical path duration** | Exact | Probabilistic (with confidence) |

> **Memory Hook (1 mark):** CPM = Certain; PERT = Probabilistic.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per the KTU 2024 Scheme, every Part B question has an **internal choice between two sub-questions A and B** of equal difficulty and marks. Both options are provided below for parallel practice.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **hierarchical structure of production planning** in a modern shipyard, listing the planning levels with their time horizons and one shipyard-specific example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shipyard takes **1,20,000 man-hours** to fabricate the first block in a series. Assuming an **80% learning curve**, calculate the man-hours required for the 8th block and the total cumulative man-hours for the first 8 blocks. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

##### Part (a) — Hierarchical Structure (7 marks)

> [!TIP]
> A well-formatted table earns **3 marks**; explanations earn the remaining **4 marks**.

| Level | Time Horizon | Decisions | Shipyard Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Strategic / Long-range** | 3 – 5 years | Yard expansion, new infrastructure | Building a 500 m wet berth for VLCCs |
| **Tactical / Medium-range** | 6 – 18 months | Aggregate output plan, fleet of ships, dock occupancy | Allocating 8 ships among 4 berths in 2025 |
| **Operational / Short-range** | 1 day – 3 months | Shop-floor sequencing, daily dispatch | Sequencing welding of 40 panels in Plate Shop |

**Explanations (key bullets — 4 marks):**

1. **Strategic level** decisions are taken by top management; they are capital-intensive and hard to reverse. Shipyard examples include investment in robotic welding, new gantry cranes, and digital twin systems.
2. **Tactical level** planning decides *which ship occupies which berth* and *when*. Aggregate production planning balances in-house capacity against subcontracting of outfitting units.
3. **Operational level** is executed daily by production engineers using Gantt charts, dispatch lists, and LOB tracking. It is the most **detail-rich** layer, with 24-hour task assignments to specific welders, fitters, and machines.

> **Closing Sentence (1 mark):** These three levels form a **cascading control hierarchy** — a change in the strategic plan ripples down to tactical and operational adjustments.

##### Part (b) — Learning Curve Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $Y_1 = 1{,}20{,}000$ MH, $r = 0.80$, $n = 8$.

**Step 1 — Compute slope $b$:**

$$b = \frac{\log(0.80)}{\log 2} = \frac{-0.09691}{0.30103} = -0.3219$$

**Step 2 — Man-hours for 8th block:**

$$Y_8 = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 8^{-0.3219}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(8^{-0.3219}) &= -0.3219 \times \log_{10}(8) \\
&= -0.3219 \times 0.90309 = -0.2907 \\
\Rightarrow 8^{-0.3219} &= 10^{-0.2907} = 0.5120
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\therefore Y_8 = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 0.5120 = 61{,}440 \text{ MH}$$

**Step 3 — Compute MH for all 8 blocks:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_1 &= 1{,}20{,}000 \\
Y_2 &= 1{,}20{,}000 \times 2^{-0.3219} = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 0.8000 = 96{,}000 \\
Y_3 &= 1{,}20{,}000 \times 3^{-0.3219} \approx 1{,}20{,}000 \times 0.7021 = 84{,}252 \\
Y_4 &= 1{,}20{,}000 \times 4^{-0.3219} = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 0.6400 = 76{,}800 \\
Y_5 &= 1{,}20{,}000 \times 5^{-0.3219} \approx 1{,}20{,}000 \times 0.5957 = 71{,}484 \\
Y_6 &= 1{,}20{,}000 \times 6^{-0.3219} \approx 1{,}20{,}000 \times 0.5617 = 67{,}404 \\
Y_7 &= 1{,}20{,}000 \times 7^{-0.3219} \approx 1{,}20{,}000 \times 0.5345 = 64{,}140 \\
Y_8 &= 61{,}440
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Cumulative total:**

$$
\sum_{x=1}^{8} Y_x = 1{,}20{,}000 + 96{,}000 + 84{,}252 + 76{,}800 + 71{,}484 + 67{,}404 + 64{,}140 + 61{,}440
$$

$$\boxed{\sum_{x=1}^{8} Y_x = 6{,}41{,}520 \text{ MH}}$$

> **Validation (1 mark):** Without learning, 8 blocks would cost $8 \times 1{,}20{,}000 = 9{,}60{,}000$ MH. The actual is $6{,}41{,}520$ MH, a **saving of ~33%**, which is realistic for an 80% learning curve over 8 repetitions.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Line-of-Balance (LOB)** technique for series ship production. State its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shipyard consumes **D = 8,000 tonnes of steel plates annually**. The ordering cost is **S = ₹40,000 per order**, and the holding cost is **H = ₹500 per tonne per year**. Calculate: (i) the EOQ, (ii) the number of orders per year, (iii) the time between orders, and (iv) the total inventory cost. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

##### Part (a) — LOB Technique (7 marks)

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Line-of-Balance is a graphical production planning and control technique used in **repetitive or series-based shipbuilding** to monitor whether the cumulative output of successive sister ships is on track to meet delivery schedules.

> **Working Principle (2 marks):**
> 1. The **Y-axis** represents the **cumulative number of ships completed**.
> 2. The **X-axis** represents **time** (in weeks or months).
> 3. An **objective line** is drawn from the contract start to the delivery date — a straight line whose slope equals the required production rate.
> 4. The **actual production line** (or curve) is plotted from weekly progress reports.
> 5. If the actual line **falls below** the objective line, the planner triggers **expediting actions** (overtime, additional workforce, parallel work, sub-contracting).

> **Advantages (1.5 marks):**
> - Ideal for **series production** (e.g., 5–10 sister bulk carriers).
> - Provides a **simple visual** for top management.
> - Quickly highlights **slippage** before it becomes critical.
> - Encourages **flow balancing** between successive ships.

> **Limitations (1 mark):**
> - Applicable only to **repetitive product lines**.
> - Not suitable for **one-off custom ships**.
> - Requires **accurate progress data** to be effective.

> **Ships/Yard Application (1 mark):** LOB is widely used at **Cochin Shipyard's series tanker line** and at **Korean and Japanese shipyards** for container ship series.

##### Part (b) — EOQ Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $D = 8{,}000$ t/yr, $S = ₹40{,}000$/order, $H = ₹500$/t/yr.

**(i) EOQ:**

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8{,}000 \times 40{,}000}{500}} = \sqrt{\frac{64 \times 10^{8}}{500}}
$$

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{1.28 \times 10^{7}} \approx 3577.7 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**(ii) Number of orders per year:**

$$
N = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{8000}{3577.7} \approx 2.24 \approx 2 \text{ orders/yr (practically rounded)}
$$

**(iii) Time between orders:**

$$
T_{cycle} = \frac{365}{2.24} \approx 163 \text{ days}
$$

**(iv) Total annual inventory cost:**

$$
TC = \frac{D}{Q} S + \frac{Q}{2} H
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC &= \frac{8000}{3577.7} \times 40{,}000 + \frac{3577.7}{2} \times 500 \\
&= 2.24 \times 40{,}000 + 1788.85 \times 500 \\
&= 89{,}600 + 8{,}94{,}425 \\
&= 9{,}84{,}025 \text{ ₹/yr (approximately 9.84 lakhs)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Insight (1 mark):** At EOQ, ordering cost = holding cost = ₹89,600 each — the classical EOQ minimum-cost condition. Any deviation from $Q^{*}$ will increase the total cost.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU 2024 students lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Aggregate Planning" with "Master Production Schedule"** — Aggregate deals with *output rate, inventory, subcontracting*; MPS is the *specific product-quantity-time* list. Mixing them loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units in Learning Curve answers** — Always write **"man-hours"** or **"tonnes"** after the numerical value. Unit-less answers trigger an instant 0.5 mark deduction.
> 3. **Skipping the "why" in CPM/PERT** — Examiners expect at least **one sentence** explaining *why* a particular technique was chosen. Stating formulas without context caps marks at ~60% of allotted.
> 4. **Not drawing the WBS or LOB diagram** — A 14-mark answer without a diagram is auto-flagged. Always include at least **one figure** (WBS, Gantt, LOB, or flowchart).
> 5. **Ignoring the "continuous feedback loop"** — Modern shipyard planning is **dynamic**, not static. Students who describe planning as a one-time activity lose marks on the *Control* step.
> 6. **Wrong assumption on the most-likely estimate $m$** — In PERT, $m$ is NOT the average of $a$ and $b$. It is the **single most-likely value** based on engineering judgment. The weighted formula $t_e = (a+4m+b)/6$ uses 4-to-1 weighting.
> 7. **Forgetting to state time horizons** — Every planning level (strategic/tactical/operational) MUST be tagged with its time horizon (3–5 yr / 6–18 mo / 1 day–3 mo).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 3: Production Planning**

- **Definition:** Production planning converts a ship contract into a time-bound, resource-optimized manufacturing schedule.
- **3 Planning Tiers:** Strategic (3–5 yr), Tactical (6–18 mo), Operational (1 day–3 mo).
- **9 Core Steps:** Forecasting → Capacity → Aggregate → MPS → MRP → Routing/WBS → Scheduling → Dispatching → Expediting.
- **WBS:** Always decompose into Ship → Block → Panel → Stiffener.
- **MPS:** Decides *which ship, how many, and when* — feeds MRP.
- **MRP:** Explodes the BOM into purchase orders for steel, pipes, valves, etc.
- **Gantt Chart:** Bar chart showing tasks vs. time — most visual planning tool.
- **CPM:** Deterministic critical path; identifies bottlenecks; uses single time estimates.
- **PERT:** Probabilistic; uses $(a, 4m, b)$ weighting; gives expected time + variance.
- **LOB (Line-of-Balance):** Cumulative output vs. time graph; **actual must not fall below objective**; ideal for series production.
- **EOQ Formula:** $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}$ — used for steel plate and consumable ordering.
- **Learning Curve:** $Y_x = Y_1 \cdot x^{b}$, $b = \log r / \log 2$ — explains why repeated units get cheaper.
- **Takt Time:** $T_{takt} = T_{available} / D$ — synchronizes pace with demand.
- **Float / Slack:** $F = LS - ES$ — flexibility in scheduling non-critical tasks.
- **Crash Cost:** Used to evaluate *cost of compressing* a project schedule.
- **Key Shipyard KPIs:** MH/CGT, block erection rate (blocks/wk), dock occupancy %, on-time delivery %.
- **Modern Tools:** ERP, MES, Digital Twin, Lean, JIT, TQM — KTU 2024 expects at least one tool name in a 14-mark answer.
- **Feedback Loops:** Always mention that production planning is a *closed-loop* PDCA system, not a one-shot plan.
- **Critical Pitfall:** In shipbuilding, **the critical path often runs through the block erection → launching → fitting-out → trials chain.** Any delay here propagates to delivery.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Production Planning.**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST633 - SHIP PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Scheduling ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Ship Production Management**, **Scheduling** is defined as the systematic process of determining *when* specific activities, operations, and tasks within a ship construction project must be performed, in *what sequence*, and with *what resources*, such that the overall vessel delivery deadline is met with optimal utilization of yard facilities, labor, and material. Mathematically, scheduling can be represented as the optimization of an objective function subject to temporal and resource constraints: $$\text{Minimize } C_{\max} = \max_{j \in J} (C_j)$$ subject to precedence constraints, resource availability, and the master production plan. Where: - $C_{\max}$ = **Makespan** (total project completion time) - $C_j$ = Completion time of job $j$ - $J$ = Set of all jobs/activities in the ship construction project > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Scheduling in shipbuilding is uniquely complex because ships are **one-of-a-kind, large-scale, engineer-to-order products** built in a *fixed-position layout* (the hull stays put, resources come to it). This makes scheduling fundamentally different from mass-production scheduling. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are **organizing a massive wedding banquet** for 500 guests. You must coordinate: - The caterer (arrives 6 hours before guests) - The decorator (needs 4 hours) - The DJ (1 hour setup) - The priest (30 minutes before guests arrive) You cannot start decorating before the hall is cleaned. The DJ cannot play before the band arrives. The caterer cannot serve before the kitchen is ready. This **time-ordered coordination of dependent activities** is exactly what scheduling does — but for a ship with **thousands of activities** across **steel fabrication, hull erection, outfitting, painting, and sea trials**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology Standards Used in KTU 2024 Scheme:** > - **Lead Time (LT)**: Total time from order to delivery. Standard metric: **18–36 months** for commercial vessels. > - **Cycle Time (CT)**: Time to complete one unit of an activity. > - **Takt Time**: Rate of production matching customer demand. > - **Buffer**: Scheduled slack to absorb variability (typical: **10–15%** of activity duration). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Shipyard Scheduling Ship production scheduling operates at **four hierarchical levels**, each with decreasing detail and increasing horizon: | Level | Horizon | Responsible Authority | Output | |:---:|:---:|:---|:---| | **Master Schedule** | Years | Yard Management | Vessel delivery dates | | **Project Schedule** | Months | Production Planning Dept. | Milestone chart | | **Stage Schedule** | Weeks | Section/Block Managers | Gantt charts | | **Operation Schedule** | Days/Hours | Foremen | Work orders | ### 2.2 The Five Pillars of Shipyard Scheduling Techniques #### Pillar 1: Gantt Charts (Bar Charts) - **Henry L. Gantt (1917)** developed this for shipbuilding during WWI. - Visualizes activities as horizontal bars against a time axis. - Best for: Daily/weekly operational scheduling in panel lines, block erection bays. #### Pillar 2: Critical Path Method (CPM) - Deterministic scheduling assuming known activity durations. - Identifies the **critical path** — the longest sequence determining project duration. - Core KTU tool for shipbuilding project control. #### Pillar 3: Program Evaluation and Review Technique (PERT) - Probabilistic scheduling with three-time estimates. - Used in new design, R&D, or first-of-class ships where duration uncertainty is high. #### Pillar 4: Line of Balance (LOB) - **Specifically developed for shipbuilding** by the U.S. Navy in the 1950s. - Plots cumulative output vs. time for repetitive production units (blocks, sections). - Excellent for **block fabrication scheduling** in modern shipyards. #### Pillar 5: Just-In-Time (JIT) / Lean Scheduling - Minimizes inventory of steel plates, pipes, outfitting components. - Reduces Work-In-Progress (WIP) between stages. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — these are the most frequently tested formulas in KTU ESE for Module 3.** | # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Application | |:---:|:---|:---:|:---|:---| | 1 | **Expected Time (PERT)** | $t_e = \dfrac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$ | $t_o$ = optimistic, $t_m$ = most likely, $t_p$ = pessimistic (days) | First-of-class ship scheduling | | 2 | **Variance (PERT)** | $\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2$ | Variance of single activity | Risk analysis | | 3 | **Project Std. Deviation** | $\sigma_p = \sqrt{\sum \sigma_i^2}$ | Sum over critical path activities | Probability of on-time delivery | | 4 | **Z-Value** | $Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_p}$ | $T_s$ = scheduled date, $T_e$ = expected completion | PERT probability calculations | | 5 | **Earliest Start (ES)** | $ES_j = \max_i (EF_i)$ | $i$ = predecessor of $j$ | Forward pass | | 6 | **Earliest Finish (EF)** | $EF_j = ES_j + D_j$ | $D_j$ = duration of activity $j$ | Forward pass | | 7 | **Latest Finish (LF)** | $LF_j = \min_k (LS_k)$ | $k$ = successor of $j$ | Backward pass | | 8 | **Latest Start (LS)** | $LS_j = LF_j - D_j$ | — | Backward pass | | 9 | **Total Float (TF)** | $TF_j = LS_j - ES_j$ | days | Slack available | | 10 | **Free Float (FF)** | $FF_j = \min(ES_k) - EF_j$ | $k$ = successors | Without delaying successors | | 11 | **LOB Production Rate** | $R = \dfrac{N}{T_d}$ | $N$ = blocks, $T_d$ = due date span | Block line balancing | | 12 | **Manpower Smoothing** | $M_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum D_i \cdot m_i}{T_{proj}}$ | $m_i$ = men on activity $i$ | Resource leveling | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Korean and Japanese mega-yards** (Hyundai HI, Samsung HI, Mitsubishi) use **integrated LOB + ERP systems** to deliver VLCCs every 4–5 days by scheduling 400+ blocks with sequenced outfitting. - The **U.S. Navy's Smart Shipyard** program applies CPM-PERT hybrid scheduling for carrier and submarine construction. - **Cochin Shipyard Limited (CSL)** uses Gantt-based scheduling integrated with SAP for its aircraft carrier (IAC) project and LNG carrier construction. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive CPM Worked Example (KTU Board Exam Style) **Problem:** A shipyard plans to construct a hatch cover. The activities and their dependencies are given below. Compute the **Critical Path, Project Duration, Total Float, and Free Float** for each activity. | Activity | Predecessor | Duration (days) | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | A | — | 6 | | B | — | 4 | | C | A | 5 | | D | A | 7 | | E | B, C | 4 | | F | D | 6 | | G | E | 3 | | H | F, G | 5 | #### Step 1: Construct the Network (Activity-on-Node) The dependency mapping is: - A → C, A → D - B → E - C → E - D → F - E → G - F → H, G → H #### Step 2: Forward Pass — Compute ES and EF We use the rule: $ES_j = \max_{i \in P(j)} EF_i$, where $P(j)$ = set of predecessors of activity $j$. **For Activity A (no predecessor):** $$ES_A = 0 \text{ days}, \quad EF_A = ES_A + D_A = 0 + 6 = 6 \text{ days}$$ **For Activity B (no predecessor):** $$ES_B = 0 \text{ days}, \quad EF_B = 0 + 4 = 4 \text{ days}$$ **For Activity C (predecessor = A):** $$ES_C = EF_A = 6 \text{ days}, \quad EF_C = 6 + 5 = 11 \text{ days}$$ **For Activity D (predecessor = A):** $$ES_D = EF_A = 6 \text{ days}, \quad EF_D = 6 + 7 = 13 \text{ days}$$ **For Activity E (predecessors = B, C):** $$ES_E = \max(EF_B, EF_C) = \max(4, 11) = 11 \text{ days}$$ $$EF_E = 11 + 4 = 15 \text{ days}$$ **For Activity F (predecessor = D):** $$ES_F = EF_D = 13 \text{ days}, \quad EF_F = 13 + 6 = 19 \text{ days}$$ **For Activity G (predecessor = E):** $$ES_G = EF_E = 15 \text{ days}, \quad EF_G = 15 + 3 = 18 \text{ days}$$ **For Activity H (predecessors = F, G):** $$ES_H = \max(EF_F, EF_G) = \max(19, 18) = 19 \text{ days}$$ $$EF_H = 19 + 5 = 24 \text{ days}$$ > **Project Duration = EF_H = 24 days** #### Step 3: Backward Pass — Compute LS and LF We use the rule: $LF_j = \min_{k \in S(j)} LS_k$, where $S(j)$ = set of successors of activity $j$. **For Activity H (no successor):** $$LF_H = EF_H = 24 \text{ days}, \quad LS_H = LF_H - D_H = 24 - 5 = 19 \text{ days}$$ **For Activity G (successor = H):** $$LF_G = LS_H = 19 \text{ days}, \quad LS_G = 19 - 3 = 16 \text{ days}$$ **For Activity F (successor = H):** $$LF_F = LS_H = 19 \text{ days}, \quad LS_F = 19 - 6 = 13 \text{ days}$$ **For Activity E (successor = G):** $$LF_E = LS_G = 16 \text{ days}, \quad LS_E = 16 - 4 = 12 \text{ days}$$ **For Activity D (successor = F):** $$LF_D = LS_F = 13 \text{ days}, \quad LS_D = 13 - 7 = 6 \text{ days}$$ **For Activity C (successor = E):** $$LF_C = LS_E = 12 \text{ days}, \quad LS_C = 12 - 5 = 7 \text{ days}$$ **For Activity B (successor = E):** $$LF_B = LS_E = 12 \text{ days}, \quad LS_B = 12 - 4 = 8 \text{ days}$$ **For Activity A (successors = C, D):** $$LF_A = \min(LS_C, LS_D) = \min(7, 6) = 6 \text{ days}$$ $$LS_A = 6 - 6 = 0 \text{ days}$$ #### Step 4: Compute Total Float and Free Float Using $TF_j = LS_j - ES_j$ and $FF_j = \min(ES_k) - EF_j$: | Activity | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | FF | Critical? | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | A | 0 | 6 | 0 | 6 | **0** | 0 | ✓ | | B | 0 | 4 | 8 | 12 | 8 | 7 | ✗ | | C | 6 | 11 | 7 | 12 | 1 | 0 | ✗ | | D | 6 | 13 | 6 | 13 | **0** | 0 | ✓ | | E | 11 | 15 | 12 | 16 | 1 | 0 | ✗ | | F | 13 | 19 | 13 | 19 | **0** | 0 | ✓ | | G | 15 | 18 | 16 | 19 | 1 | 1 | ✗ | | H | 19 | 24 | 19 | 24 | **0** | 0 | ✓ | #### Step 5: Identify the Critical Path The critical path consists of activities with $TF = 0$: $$\boxed{\text{Critical Path: A} \rightarrow \text{D} \rightarrow \text{F} \rightarrow \text{H}, \quad \text{Duration} = 24 \text{ days}}$$ ### 3.2 PERT Three-Time Estimate Derivation For activity $X$ with estimates $t_o$, $t_m$, $t_p$, the **Beta distribution** expected time is derived from: $$t_e = \int_0^\infty t \cdot f(t) \, dt$$ For a Beta distribution with mode at $t_m$ and bounds $[t_o, t_p]$, the mean evaluates to the standard PERT formula: $$t_e = \dfrac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$$ The variance of the Beta distribution reduces to: $$\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2$$ This $\frac{1}{6}$ factor emerges because the standard deviation of a Beta distribution fitting these constraints is approximately $\frac{t_p - t_o}{6}$. ### 3.3 Line of Balance (LOB) Analytical Setup Consider a ship built from **N = 50 blocks**. Each block passes through 4 stages: Steel cutting, Welding, Painting, Outfitting. Stages $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$ have cycle times $c_i$ (days). The desired delivery rate is **1 block per 2 days**. The required production rate at stage $i$ must equal or exceed: $$R_i = \dfrac{N}{T_{due} - \sum_{k=1}^{i-1} c_k}$$ The **cycle time constraint** to avoid bottlenecks: $$c_i \leq \dfrac{1}{R_i}$$ If $c_i$ exceeds this, that stage is the **bottleneck** and must be parallelized or capacity-added. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Hierarchical Scheduling Framework in Shipyards ```mermaid graph TD nodeA[Master Schedule
Vessel Delivery] --> nodeB[Project Schedule
Milestones] nodeB --> nodeC[Stage Schedule
Block Erection] nodeB --> nodeD[Stage Schedule
Outfitting] nodeB --> nodeE[Stage Schedule
Painting] nodeC --> nodeF[Operation Schedule
Daily Work Orders] nodeD --> nodeF nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG[Resource Allocation
Labor and Equipment] nodeF --> nodeH[Progress Monitoring
Earned Value Analysis] nodeH --> nodeI[Feedback Loop
Schedule Updates] nodeI --> nodeB ``` ### 4.2 CPM Forward-Backward Pass Flowchart ```mermaid graph LR nodeA[Input Network
Activities and Dependencies] --> nodeB[Forward Pass
Compute ES and EF] nodeB --> nodeC{All Activities
Processed?} nodeC -->|No| nodeB nodeC -->|Yes| nodeD[Project Duration
equals max EF] nodeD --> nodeE[Backward Pass
Compute LS and LF] nodeE --> nodeF{All Activities
Processed?} nodeF -->|No| nodeE nodeF -->|Yes| nodeG[Compute Total Float
TF equals LS minus ES] nodeG --> nodeH{Any TF equals 0?} nodeH -->|Yes| nodeI[Mark as
Critical Path] nodeH -->|No| nodeJ[Non-Critical
Activity] nodeI --> nodeK[Output
Critical Path] nodeJ --> nodeK ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (LOB) ```mermaid graph TD nodeA[Steel Cutting
Stage 1] --> nodeB[Block Welding
Stage 2] nodeB --> nodeC[Surface Treatment
Stage 3] nodeC --> nodeD[Unit Outfitting
Stage 4] nodeD --> nodeE[Erection on Slipway
Stage 5] nodeA -.-> nodeF[Cycle Time Check] nodeB -.-> nodeF nodeC -.-> nodeF nodeD -.-> nodeF nodeE -.-> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG{Bottleneck
Detected?} nodeG -->|Yes| nodeH[Add Parallel
Capacity] nodeG -->|No| nodeI[Schedule Locked] nodeH --> nodeF ``` ### 4.4 Scheduling Technique Selection Matrix (Schematic) ```mermaid graph TD nodeA[Ship Type] --> nodeB{Repetitive
Production?} nodeB -->|Yes| nodeC[Line of Balance LOB] nodeB -->|No| nodeD{Duration
Uncertain?} nodeD -->|Yes| nodeE[PERT with
Three Estimates] nodeD -->|No| nodeF[CPM with
Single Time Estimate] nodeA --> nodeG{Daily
Operations?} nodeG -->|Yes| nodeH[Gantt Chart] nodeG -.-> nodeB ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define scheduling. List any four scheduling techniques used in ship production management.* **Model Answer:** Scheduling is the determination of the timing and sequence of operations required to complete a ship construction project within the planned lead time while optimizing resource use. The four key techniques are: (1) Gantt Charts, (2) Critical Path Method (CPM), (3) Program Evaluation and Review Technique (PERT), and (4) Line of Balance (LOB). *\[3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for listing with brief relevance\]* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Distinguish between Total Float and Free Float in CPM with formulas.* **Model Answer:** - **Total Float (TF)** is the time an activity can be delayed without delaying the **project completion date**: $TF = LS - ES$. - **Free Float (FF)** is the time an activity can be delayed without delaying the **early start of any successor activity**: $FF = \min(ES \text{ of successors}) - EF$. - Key difference: TF considers project end; FF considers only immediate successors. *\[3 marks\]* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** A ship construction project involves the following activities: | Activity | Predecessors | Duration (weeks) | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | A | None | 3 | | B | None | 2 | | C | A | 4 | | D | A | 6 | | E | B, C | 5 | | F | D | 4 | | G | E | 3 | | H | F, G | 2 | **(a)** Draw the network diagram and determine the **critical path** and **project duration**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compute the **Total Float** and **Free Float** for all activities. Identify which activities are critical. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Network and Critical Path:** Forward Pass: - $ES_A = 0$, $EF_A = 3$ - $ES_B = 0$, $EF_B = 2$ - $ES_C = 3$, $EF_C = 7$ - $ES_D = 3$, $EF_D = 9$ - $ES_E = \max(2, 7) = 7$, $EF_E = 12$ - $ES_F = 9$, $EF_F = 13$ - $ES_G = 12$, $EF_G = 15$ - $ES_H = \max(13, 15) = 15$, $EF_H = 17$ **Project Duration = 17 weeks.** Backward Pass: - $LF_H = 17$, $LS_H = 15$ - $LF_G = 15$, $LS_G = 12$ - $LF_F = 15$, $LS_F = 11$ - $LF_E = 12$, $LS_E = 7$ - $LF_D = 11$, $LS_D = 5$ - $LF_C = 7$, $LS_C = 3$ - $LF_B = 7$, $LS_B = 5$ - $LF_A = \min(3, 5) = 3$, $LS_A = 0$ **Critical Path: A → D → F → H (17 weeks)** **(b) Float Calculations:** | Activity | ES | EF | LS | LF | TF | FF | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | A | 0 | 3 | 0 | 3 | **0** | 0 | | B | 0 | 2 | 5 | 7 | 5 | 5 | | C | 3 | 7 | 3 | 7 | **0** | 0 | | D | 3 | 9 | 5 | 11 | 2 | 0 | | E | 7 | 12 | 7 | 12 | **0** | 0 | | F | 9 | 13 | 11 | 15 | 2 | 2 | | G | 12 | 15 | 12 | 15 | **0** | 0 | | H | 15 | 17 | 15 | 17 | **0** | 0 | **Critical Activities:** A, C, E, G, H --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]** **(a)** Explain the **Line of Balance (LOB)** technique as applied to ship block production. Discuss its advantages over Gantt charts for repetitive shipbuilding work. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A shipyard must produce **40 identical blocks** in **20 weeks**. The blocks pass through 4 stages with cycle times (in days): Stage 1 = 2, Stage 2 = 3, Stage 3 = 2, Stage 4 = 4. The yard works 5 days/week. Compute the **production rate**, identify any **bottleneck stage**, and suggest a remedy. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Line of Balance Technique:** LOB is a scheduling technique developed for repetitive production systems. In shipbuilding, it plots the **cumulative number of completed units vs. time** for each production stage (e.g., cutting, welding, painting, outfitting). The technique: - Establishes a **delivery curve** based on customer demand. - For each stage, plots the **completion curve** showing cumulative output. - If the stage's completion curve lies **above** the delivery curve, that stage has surplus capacity; if **below**, it is a bottleneck. **Advantages over Gantt charts:** 1. Clearly visualizes bottlenecks in multi-stage production lines. 2. Effective for **flow-shop scheduling** of identical blocks. 3. Enables **smooth manpower and resource loading**. 4. Supports **Just-In-Time** delivery synchronization. 5. Detects deviations early through slope comparison of curves. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** N = 40 blocks, Total time = 20 weeks = 100 working days. **Required production rate:** $$R = \frac{N}{T_{due}} = \frac{40 \text{ blocks}}{20 \text{ weeks}} = 2 \text{ blocks/week} = 0.4 \text{ blocks/day}$$ **Time available per block at each stage** (must be ≤ cycle time): $$\text{Required stage cycle} = \frac{T_{due}}{N} = \frac{100}{40} = 2.5 \text{ days/block}$$ **Comparison with actual cycle times:** | Stage | Cycle Time (days) | Required (days) | Status | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | 2 | 2.5 | OK (surplus 0.5 d) | | 2 | 3 | 2.5 | **BOTTLENECK** | | 3 | 2 | 2.5 | OK (surplus 0.5 d) | | 4 | 4 | 2.5 | **BOTTLENECK (severe)** | **Bottleneck stages:** Stage 2 (welding) and Stage 4 (outfitting) — both exceed the required cycle time. **Remedies:** 1. **Parallelize** the bottleneck stage by adding a second welding bay. 2. **Shift work** or **overtime** to extend effective working hours. 3. **Pre-outfitting** at the block level before erection to reduce post-erection workload. 4. **Worker cross-training** to enable dynamic resource shifting. 5. Re-balance the line by adjusting batch sizes and station assignments. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE:** > 1. **Forgetting to take MAX in forward pass for multi-predecessor activities** — this is the #1 error. Always write: $ES_j = \max(\text{all predecessor } EF)$. > 2. **Confusing LS and ES** during float calculation. TF = LS − ES, not LF − EF (which gives the same value but students often mix up the formula reference). > 3. **Not drawing the network diagram** in CPM questions — KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks if only a table is shown. > 4. **Forgetting units** (days, weeks) in the final answer. > 5. **In PERT**, using $t_m$ alone without the $\frac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$ weighting. > 6. **In LOB**, failing to convert between weeks and working days (5 days/week convention is standard in KTU papers). > 7. **Writing definitions without shipbuilding context** — KTU penalizes generic management answers; always tie to shipyard examples. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Scheduling** - ✅ Scheduling determines *when* and *in what sequence* ship construction activities occur. - ✅ Four hierarchical levels: **Master → Project → Stage → Operation**. - ✅ **Gantt charts** for daily/operational use; **CPM** for deterministic project control; **PERT** for uncertain durations; **LOB** for repetitive block production. - ✅ Critical Path = longest path = **zero total float** activities. - ✅ $t_e = \frac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$ — memorize the PERT expected time formula. - ✅ $Z = \frac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_p}$ for PERT probability calculations. - ✅ Forward pass uses **MAX** of predecessor EFs; Backward pass uses **MIN** of successor LSs. - ✅ **Free Float ≤ Total Float** always. - ✅ LOB bottlenecks occur when stage cycle time exceeds the required rate. - ✅ Ship scheduling is dominated by **fixed-position layout** and **engineer-to-order** nature. - ✅ Modern yards integrate CPM + LOB + ERP for real-time schedule control. - ✅ Always state **units** (days/weeks) and **assume 5 working days/week** unless told otherwise. - ✅ **Critical path activities cannot be delayed** without delaying the project. - ✅ **Resource leveling** smooths manpower histograms to avoid peaks and troughs. - ✅ **Buffer (10–15%)** is standard for absorbing variability in shipyard schedules. ---

topics.title: Network Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST633 - SHIP PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Network Models # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Introduction to Network Models in Ship Production Management > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > A **Network Model** is a graphical and mathematical representation of a project's activities, events, and their logical interdependencies, used to plan, schedule, monitor, and control complex engineering projects such as shipbuilding. It transforms a project into a logical network of interconnected tasks with defined durations and precedence relationships. In the context of **ship production management**, a network model serves as the *digital blueprint* of the entire shipbuilding workflow — from initial hull steel cutting to final outfitting, commissioning, and sea trials. Every keel-laying, block fabrication, engine installation, and pipe-fitting activity becomes a node or arrow in a master schedule. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Shipyard as a Train Network" > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** > Imagine a massive railway network connecting 500 cities across India. Each city is a **station (event)**, each railway track is a **route (activity)**, and the time a train takes between stations is the **duration**. Now, your job is to deliver passengers from *Station A* (Keel Laying) to *Station B* (Sea Trials) in the shortest possible time. Some routes run parallel, some depend on others (you can't start Painting before Welding is done), and some routes are "critical" — if they get delayed, the entire project gets delayed. > > A **Network Model in shipbuilding** does exactly this — it models the *Keel → Hull → Outfit → Launch → Trials* journey as a graph of stations and routes, identifying the **critical path** (the longest chain that determines the project's minimum duration). ### 1.3 Why Network Models are Essential in Ship Production > [!TIP] > **Key Reasons (KTU Board Exam Favorite):** > 1. **Visualization** of complex, interdependent shipbuilding tasks > 2. **Critical Path Identification** — the bottleneck sequence determining project duration > 3. **Float/Slack Calculation** — identifying tasks that can be delayed without affecting the project > 4. **Resource Leveling** — smoothing manpower, dock space, and crane usage > 5. **Crashing Decisions** — cost-time trade-off analysis for early delivery > 6. **Progress Monitoring** — Earned Value Analysis using network baselines > 7. **Coordination** among design, production, procurement, and outfitting departments ### 1.4 Classification of Network Models Network Models are broadly classified into two families based on how activities are represented: | Model Type | Full Form | Representation Style | Common Use | |------------|-----------|---------------------|------------| | **AOA** | Activity on Arrow | Activities on arrows, events on nodes | Traditional PERT/CPM | | **AON** | Activity on Node | Activities on nodes, arrows show precedence | Modern project software (Primavera, MS Project) | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Ship production management predominantly uses **AOA (Activity on Arrow)** networks because of the historical legacy of PERT/CPM standards followed by most shipyards (Cochin Shipyard, Mazagon Dock, GRSE). ### 1.5 Core Terminology (Must Memorize for KTU Exams) | Term | Definition | |------|------------| | **Activity** | A task that consumes time and resources (e.g., welding a hull plate) | | **Event** | A milestone marking the start or completion of an activity (e.g., Keel laid) | | **Node** | A point representing an event | | **Dummy Activity** | A logical connector with zero duration, used only to maintain precedence in AOA | | **Critical Path** | The longest path through the network; determines minimum project duration | | **Float (Slack)** | The amount of time an activity can be delayed without delaying the project | | **Earliest Start (ES)** | The earliest time an activity can begin | | **Earliest Finish (EF)** | ES + Duration | | **Latest Start (LS)** | The latest time an activity can begin without delaying the project | | **Latest Finish (LF)** | LS + Duration | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 PERT vs CPM — Two Pillars of Network Modeling > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Insight:** Although PERT and CPM are often taught together, they serve different purposes in ship production. Most KTU questions are comparison-based (5-mark or 7-mark questions). ### 2.1.1 PERT (Program Evaluation and Review Technique) - Developed in **1958** by the U.S. Navy for the **Polaris Missile Project** - Uses **three time estimates** for each activity (probabilistic approach) - Suitable for **research, development, and first-of-its-kind projects** (e.g., building the first aircraft carrier) - Focuses on **time** rather than cost ### 2.1.2 CPM (Critical Path Method) - Developed in **1957** by DuPont and Remington Rand - Uses a **single deterministic time estimate** for each activity - Suitable for **repetitive construction projects** with well-defined activities - Focuses on **time-cost trade-off** | Parameter | **PERT** | **CPM** | |-----------|----------|---------| | Time Estimates | Three (Optimistic, Most Likely, Pessimistic) | One (Deterministic) | | Nature | Probabilistic / Stochastic | Deterministic | | Application | R&D, Non-repetitive | Construction, Repetitive | | Focus | Time | Time and Cost | | Crashing | Not directly considered | Core feature | | Best for Ship Production | First-of-kind warships | Series production (merchant vessels) | ## 2.2 The Three Time Estimates in PERT For each activity, three durations are estimated: $$t_o = \text{Optimistic time (best-case scenario)}$$ $$t_m = \text{Most likely time (normal working conditions)}$$ $$t_p = \text{Pessimistic time (worst-case scenario)}$$ ### 2.2.1 Expected Time (Beta Distribution Approximation) $$t_e = \frac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$$ ### 2.2.2 Variance of Each Activity $$\sigma^2 = \left(\frac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2$$ > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Explanation:** The Beta distribution approximates most real-world activity durations. The weightage of *4* on $t_m$ reflects that "most likely" outcomes occur more frequently than extreme best/worst cases. ## 2.3 Network Construction Logic ### 2.3.1 Rules for AOA Networks > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake (KTU Examiner Note):** > Forgetting to add **dummy activities** results in wrong precedence relationships and incorrect critical paths. Always check the logical flow after drawing. 1. Every activity must have a **unique start and end event number** 2. Event numbers must increase from **left to right** (no cycles) 3. **No two activities** can have the same start and end events (use dummies) 4. There must be a **single start event** and **single end event** 5. **Loops are forbidden** (no cyclic dependencies) ## 2.4 Forward Pass and Backward Pass Calculations ### 2.4.1 Forward Pass (Calculates Earliest Times) For each event $i$: $$E_i = \max_{j \in \text{predecessors}} \{E_j + d_{ji}\}$$ Where $d_{ji}$ is the duration of activity from event $j$ to event $i$. For each activity $i-j$: $$ES_{ij} = E_i, \quad EF_{ij} = E_i + d_{ij}$$ ### 2.4.2 Backward Pass (Calculates Latest Times) For the last event: $$L_n = E_n \text{ (project completion time)}$$ For each event $j$: $$L_j = \min_{k \in \text{successors}} \{L_k - d_{jk}\}$$ For each activity $i-j$: $$LF_{ij} = L_j, \quad LS_{ij} = L_j - d_{ij}$$ ## 2.5 Float Calculations (High-Yield for KTU) ### 2.5.1 Total Float ($TF$) $$TF_{ij} = L_j - E_i - d_{ij} = LS_{ij} - ES_{ij}$$ ### 2.5.2 Free Float ($FF$) $$FF_{ij} = E_j - E_i - d_{ij}$$ ### 2.5.3 Independent Float ($IF$) $$IF_{ij} = E_j - L_i - d_{ij}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Critical Activity Condition:** If $TF = 0$ (or $TF = $ minimum across all activities), the activity is **CRITICAL** and lies on the critical path. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $t_e = \dfrac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$ | PERT Expected Time | days/weeks | | 2 | $\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2$ | PERT Variance | days² | | 3 | $\sigma_{\text{project}} = \sqrt{\sum \sigma^2_{\text{critical}}}$ | Project Std. Deviation | days | | 4 | $Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{\text{project}}}$ | Probability of completion by date $T_s$ | dimensionless | | 5 | $E_i = \max(E_j + d_{ji})$ | Forward pass — Earliest Event Time | days | | 6 | $L_j = \min(L_k - d_{jk})$ | Backward pass — Latest Event Time | days | | 7 | $TF_{ij} = L_j - E_i - d_{ij}$ | Total Float | days | | 8 | $FF_{ij} = E_j - E_i - d_{ij}$ | Free Float | days | | 9 | $IF_{ij} = E_j - L_i - d_{ij}$ | Independent Float | days | | 10 | $\Delta C = C_n - C_c$ | Crash Cost (Normal − Crash) | ₹/₹/$ | | 11 | $\text{Cost Slope} = \dfrac{C_c - C_n}{t_n - t_c}$ | Cost per day of crashing | ₹/day | | 12 | $Z_{\min} = 1.645$ (90%) | Standard normal value for probability | dimensionless | ## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Ship Production > [!TIP] > **Engineering Application:** > - **Block Construction Scheduling:** A modern ship is built in ~300–600 blocks; network models coordinate the welding sequence > - **Dry Dock Occupancy:** Critical path determines minimum stay in dock > - **Outfitting Pre-planning:** Pipe, electrical, and HVAC activities are parallelized using AON > - **Sea Trials Window:** Ensures weather window is not missed due to upstream delays # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Shipbuilding Network Problem > [!NOTE] > **KTU Standard Problem Type:** This is the most frequently asked question in Module 3. Practice this fully. ### Problem Statement A shipyard has identified the following activities for constructing a small cargo vessel. Draw the network and find the **critical path and project duration**. | Activity | Predecessor(s) | Duration (weeks) | |----------|---------------|------------------| | A (Hull Steel Cutting) | — | 4 | | B (Hull Plate Rolling) | A | 3 | | C (Hull Assembly) | B | 6 | | D (Machinery Foundation) | A | 4 | | E (Shaft Alignment) | C, D | 3 | | F (Engine Installation) | D | 5 | | G (Pipe Fitting) | F | 4 | | H (Electrical Cabling) | F | 3 | | I (Painting) | C | 2 | | J (Sea Trials) | E, G, H, I | 4 | ### Step 1: Network Construction (AOA) The activities are drawn as arrows between numbered events 1 through 8: ``` 4 3 6 3 1 ──A──→ 2 ──B──→ 3 ──C──→ 5 ──E──→ 7 ──J──→ 8 │ │ │↘ ▲ ▲ ↑ │ │ │ 2 │ │ │ │ 4│ │ ──I──→ 6─────────┘ │ │ D──┘ │ │ │ │ │↘ │ │ │ │ │ 5 │ │ │ └────────┴──F──→ 4 ──────────→ │ │ │ │ └─G,H─────────────┘ ``` Let me re-render this more clearly with proper event numbering (1→8): - Event 1 (Start) → A → Event 2 - Event 2 → B → Event 3 - Event 2 → D → Event 4 - Event 3 → C → Event 5 - Event 4 → F → Event 6 - Event 5 → I → Event 7 (with dummy from 5 to 6 to maintain E's precedence on F) - Event 6 → G → Event 7 - Event 6 → H → Event 7 - Event 5 → E → Event 8 (where E waits for C and D; using dummy from 4→5) - Event 7 → J → Event 8 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Dummy Activities Used:** Two dummies are required — one from Event 4 to Event 5 (so E waits for D), and one from Event 5 to Event 6 (so G/H wait for I via dummy). ### Step 2: Forward Pass — Calculate Earliest Event Times ($E_i$) Starting from $E_1 = 0$: $$E_1 = 0$$ $$E_2 = E_1 + d_A = 0 + 4 = 4$$ $$E_3 = E_2 + d_B = 4 + 3 = 7$$ $$E_4 = E_2 + d_D = 4 + 4 = 8$$ $$E_5 = \max(E_3 + d_C, \; E_4 + d_{\text{dummy}}) = \max(7 + 6, \; 8 + 0) = \max(13, 8) = 13$$ $$E_6 = \max(E_4 + d_F, \; E_5 + d_{\text{dummy}}) = \max(8 + 5, \; 13 + 0) = \max(13, 13) = 13$$ $$E_7 = \max(E_5 + d_I, \; E_6 + d_G, \; E_6 + d_H) = \max(13 + 2, \; 13 + 4, \; 13 + 3) = \max(15, 17, 16) = 17$$ $$E_8 = \max(E_5 + d_E, \; E_7 + d_J) = \max(13 + 3, \; 17 + 4) = \max(16, 21) = 21$$ **Project Duration = $E_8 = 21$ weeks** ### Step 3: Backward Pass — Calculate Latest Event Times ($L_i$) Starting from $L_8 = E_8 = 21$: $$L_8 = 21$$ $$L_7 = L_8 - d_J = 21 - 4 = 17$$ $$L_5 = \min(L_8 - d_E, \; L_7 - d_I, \; L_6 - d_{\text{dummy}}) = \min(21 - 3, \; 17 - 2, \; ...) = \min(18, 15) = 15$$ $$L_6 = \min(L_7 - d_G, \; L_7 - d_H, \; L_5 - d_{\text{dummy}}) = \min(17 - 4, \; 17 - 3, \; 15 - 0) = \min(13, 14, 15) = 13$$ $$L_4 = \min(L_6 - d_F, \; L_5 - d_{\text{dummy}}) = \min(13 - 5, \; 15 - 0) = \min(8, 15) = 8$$ $$L_3 = L_5 - d_C = 15 - 6 = 9$$ $$L_2 = \min(L_3 - d_B, \; L_4 - d_D) = \min(9 - 3, \; 8 - 4) = \min(6, 4) = 4$$ $$L_1 = L_2 - d_A = 4 - 4 = 0 \;\checkmark$$ ### Step 4: Float Calculations For each activity $i \to j$: | Activity | $i \to j$ | $d_{ij}$ | $E_i$ | $E_j$ | $L_i$ | $L_j$ | $TF = L_j - E_i - d_{ij}$ | |----------|-----------|----------|-------|-------|-------|-------|--------------------------| | A | 1→2 | 4 | 0 | 4 | 0 | 4 | $4 - 0 - 4 = 0$ ✓ | | B | 2→3 | 3 | 4 | 7 | 4 | 9 | $9 - 4 - 3 = 2$ | | C | 3→5 | 6 | 7 | 13 | 9 | 15 | $15 - 7 - 6 = 2$ | | D | 2→4 | 4 | 4 | 8 | 4 | 8 | $8 - 4 - 4 = 0$ ✓ | | Dummy₁ | 4→5 | 0 | 8 | 13 | 8 | 15 | $15 - 8 - 0 = 7$ | | E | 5→8 | 3 | 13 | 21 | 15 | 21 | $21 - 13 - 3 = 5$ | | Dummy₂ | 5→6 | 0 | 13 | 13 | 15 | 13 | $13 - 13 - 0 = 0$ ✓ | | F | 4→6 | 5 | 8 | 13 | 8 | 13 | $13 - 8 - 5 = 0$ ✓ | | G | 6→7 | 4 | 13 | 17 | 13 | 17 | $17 - 13 - 4 = 0$ ✓ | | H | 6→7 | 3 | 13 | 17 | 13 | 17 | $17 - 13 - 3 = 1$ | | I | 5→7 | 2 | 13 | 17 | 15 | 17 | $17 - 13 - 2 = 2$ | | J | 7→8 | 4 | 17 | 21 | 17 | 21 | $21 - 17 - 4 = 0$ ✓ | ### Step 5: Identify the Critical Path Activities with $TF = 0$ are critical. The critical path sequence is: $$\boxed{1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 6 \to 7 \to 8}$$ In activity notation: $$\boxed{A \to D \to F \to G \to J}$$ **Critical Path Duration =** $4 + 4 + 5 + 4 + 4 = 21$ weeks ✓ > [!TIP] > **Interpretation:** The ship cannot be launched in less than 21 weeks. The critical chain is Hull Steel Cutting → Machinery Foundation → Engine Installation → Pipe Fitting → Sea Trials. Any delay here delays the entire project. ## 3.2 PERT Three-Time-Estimate Example > [!NOTE] > **Second Standard KTU Question Type:** PERT probabilistic time analysis. For the **critical path A → D → F → G → J**, the three time estimates are: | Activity | $t_o$ | $t_m$ | $t_p$ | $t_e = \frac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$ | $\sigma^2 = \left(\frac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2$ | |----------|-------|-------|-------|-------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------| | A | 2 | 4 | 6 | $\frac{2 + 16 + 6}{6} = 4$ | $\left(\frac{4}{6}\right)^2 = 0.444$ | | D | 3 | 4 | 7 | $\frac{3 + 16 + 7}{6} = 4.33$ | $\left(\frac{4}{6}\right)^2 = 0.444$ | | F | 4 | 5 | 8 | $\frac{4 + 20 + 8}{6} = 5.33$ | $\left(\frac{4}{6}\right)^2 = 0.444$ | | G | 3 | 4 | 7 | $\frac{3 + 16 + 7}{6} = 4.33$ | $\left(\frac{4}{6}\right)^2 = 0.444$ | | J | 3 | 4 | 6 | $\frac{3 + 16 + 6}{6} = 4.17$ | $\left(\frac{3}{6}\right)^2 = 0.25$ | ### Expected Project Duration $$T_e = 4 + 4.33 + 5.33 + 4.33 + 4.17 = 22.16 \text{ weeks}$$ ### Project Variance $$\sigma_{\text{project}}^2 = 0.444 + 0.444 + 0.444 + 0.444 + 0.25 = 2.026 \text{ weeks}^2$$ $$\sigma_{\text{project}} = \sqrt{2.026} = 1.423 \text{ weeks}$$ ### Probability Calculation If the shipyard management promises delivery in **24 weeks**, what is the probability of meeting the deadline? $$Z = \frac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{\text{project}}} = \frac{24 - 22.16}{1.423} = \frac{1.84}{1.423} = 1.293$$ From standard normal tables: $P(Z \leq 1.29) \approx 0.9015$ **Probability of completion within 24 weeks ≈ 90.15%** > [!TIP] > **Engineering Interpretation:** Management can quote a 24-week delivery commitment with ~90% confidence. To increase this to 95%, they would need to either crash the network or quote a later date. ## 3.3 Cost-Time Trade-Off (Crashing) — Analytical Framework > [!NOTE] > **KTU Advanced Question:** Often asked as a 7-mark sub-part. For each critical activity: | Activity | Normal Time $t_n$ | Crash Time $t_c$ | Normal Cost $C_n$ | Crash Cost $C_c$ | Cost Slope $S = \frac{C_c - C_n}{t_n - t_c}$ | |----------|-------------------|------------------|-------------------|------------------|---------------------------------------------| | A | 4 | 2 | ₹40,000 | ₹80,000 | ₹20,000/day | | D | 4 | 3 | ₹50,000 | ₹70,000 | ₹20,000/day | | F | 5 | 3 | ₹80,000 | ₹1,40,000 | ₹30,000/day | | G | 4 | 2 | ₹60,000 | ₹1,20,000 | ₹30,000/day | | J | 4 | 3 | ₹50,000 | ₹90,000 | ₹40,000/day | ### Crashing Strategy To reduce project duration at minimum cost: 1. Identify the **critical path** 2. Calculate the **cost slope** for each critical activity 3. Crash the activity with the **lowest cost slope** first 4. After each crash, **re-evaluate** the critical path (it may shift) **Example:** To reduce project duration from 21 → 20 weeks, crash activity **A** (lowest cost slope = ₹20,000/day). New duration: 20 weeks, additional cost: ₹20,000. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Network Diagram — Shipbuilding AOA Model > [!NOTE] > **AOA Network Block Diagram** showing the ship production workflow with all 12 activities and the critical path highlighted. ```mermaid graph LR node1(["START Event 1"]) node2(["Event 2"]) node3(["Event 3"]) node4(["Event 4"]) node5(["Event 5"]) node6(["Event 6"]) node7(["Event 7"]) node8(["END Event 8"]) node1 -->|"A: Hull Cutting
4w (CRITICAL)"| node2 node2 -->|"B: Plate Rolling
3w"| node3 node2 -->|"D: Machinery Fdn
4w (CRITICAL)"| node4 node3 -->|"C: Hull Assembly
6w"| node5 node4 -->|"F: Engine Install
5w (CRITICAL)"| node6 node5 -.->|"DUMMY 1
0w"| node6 node5 -->|"I: Painting
2w"| node7 node6 -->|"G: Pipe Fitting
4w (CRITICAL)"| node7 node6 -->|"H: Electrical
3w"| node7 node4 -.->|"DUMMY 2
0w"| node5 node5 -->|"E: Shaft Align
3w"| node8 node7 -->|"J: Sea Trials
4w (CRITICAL)"| node8 style node1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px style node8 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px style node2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px style node4 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px style node6 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px style node7 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!TIP] > **Reading the Diagram:** > - **Green Node 1** = Project Start (Keel Laying) > - **Red Node 8** = Project End (Sea Trials) > - **Yellow/Gold Nodes** = Events on the Critical Path (2, 4, 6, 7) > - **Solid arrows** = Real activities with duration > - **Dotted arrows** = Dummy activities (zero duration, logical links only) ## 4.2 PERT vs CPM — Decision Tree Block Diagram ```mermaid graph TD A["START: New Shipbuilding Project"] --> B{"Is the project
repetitive?"} B -->|"YES: Series production"| C{"Is time-cost
trade-off needed?"} B -->|"NO: First-of-kind vessel"| D["USE PERT"] C -->|"YES"| E["USE CPM"] C -->|"NO"| F["USE PERT
with three estimates"] D --> G["Three time estimates:
to, tm, tp"] E --> H["Single deterministic time
+ Cost slope analysis"] G --> I["Compute te and variance"] H --> J["Identify critical path
and crashing opportunities"] I --> K["Probability of completion
by deadline date"] J --> L["Optimal time-cost
trade-off"] K --> M["PROJECT EXECUTION"] L --> M style A fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px style M fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#FFB6C1 style E fill:#FFD700 style F fill:#FFB6C1 ``` ## 4.3 Forward-Backward Pass Computational Flow ```mermaid graph TD A["INPUT: AOA Network
with activities and durations"] --> B["Step 1: Number Events
1 to N from left to right"] B --> C["Step 2: FORWARD PASS
Compute Ei for each event"] C --> D["Ei = max of predecessor
E + duration"] D --> E["Project Duration T = E_last"] E --> F["Step 3: BACKWARD PASS
Compute Li for each event"] F --> G["Li = min of successor
L - duration"] G --> H["Step 4: Compute Floats
TF = Lj - Ei - dij"] H --> I{"Is TF = 0?"} I -->|"YES"| J["CRITICAL ACTIVITY
Add to critical path"] I -->|"NO"| K["Non-critical
has float/slack"] J --> L["Step 5: Trace critical
path from start to end"] K --> L L --> M["OUTPUT: Critical Path
and Minimum Duration"] style A fill:#87CEEB style M fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px style J fill:#FF6347,color:#fff style K fill:#DDA0DD ``` ## 4.4 Crashing Decision Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD A["Project Duration
Must Reduce"] --> B["Identify ALL
Critical Paths"] B --> C["For each path,
list critical activities"] C --> D["Compute Cost Slope
S = dC / dT"] D --> E{"Path has
common activity?"} E -->|"YES"| F["Crash common activity
if slope is lowest"] E -->|"NO"| G["Crash activity with
minimum slope on each path"] F --> H["Update durations
and re-compute critical path"] G --> H H --> I{"Duration
achieved?"} I -->|"NO"| B I -->|"YES"| J["OPTIMAL CRASH
MINIMUM COST"] style A fill:#FFB6C1 style J fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern:** Short-answer conceptual questions testing definitions and basic understanding. ### Question 1: Define a Network Model. Mention its significance in ship production management. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **Network Model** is a graphical representation of a project consisting of **activities** (tasks) and **events** (milestones) connected by logical arrows, where each activity carries a specified duration. **Significance in Ship Production:** 1. Helps visualize the complex interdependencies of hundreds of shipbuilding tasks 2. Identifies the **critical path** — the bottleneck sequence determining minimum project duration 3. Enables efficient **resource allocation** (manpower, dry docks, cranes) 4. Facilitates **progress monitoring** and early detection of delays 5. Supports **time-cost trade-off** analysis for delivery commitments --- ### Question 2: Differentiate between PERT and CPM. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Basis | PERT | CPM | |-------|------|-----| | Time Estimates | Three (Optimistic, Most Likely, Pessimistic) | Single deterministic value | | Nature | Probabilistic (stochastic) | Deterministic | | Application | R&D, non-repetitive projects | Repetitive construction | | Focus | Time only | Time and Cost | | Crashing | Not directly considered | Core feature | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Two questions of 14 marks each with internal choice. Each sub-part carries 7 marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q:** The following table lists activities for a ship repair project. Construct the network and find the critical path and minimum project duration. | Activity | Predecessor | Duration (days) | |----------|-------------|-----------------| | A | — | 6 | | B | A | 8 | | C | A | 4 | | D | B | 5 | | E | C | 7 | | F | D, E | 6 | | G | E | 3 | | H | F, G | 4 | **Model Solution (7 Marks):** **Step 1: Network Construction (AOA)** - 1 →(A)→ 2 - 2 →(B)→ 3 - 2 →(C)→ 4 - 3 →(D)→ 5 - 4 →(E)→ 6 - 5 →(Dummy)→ 6 *(to make F depend on D)* - 6 →(F)→ 7 - 6 →(G)→ 8 - 7 →(Dummy)→ 8 *(to make H depend on F)* - 8 →(H)→ 9 **Step 2: Forward Pass** $$E_1 = 0$$ $$E_2 = 0 + 6 = 6$$ $$E_3 = 6 + 8 = 14$$ $$E_4 = 6 + 4 = 10$$ $$E_5 = 14 + 5 = 19$$ $$E_6 = \max(19 + 0, \; 10 + 7) = \max(19, 17) = 19$$ $$E_7 = 19 + 6 = 25$$ $$E_8 = \max(19 + 3, \; 25 + 0) = 25$$ $$E_9 = 25 + 4 = 29$$ **Project Duration = 29 days** **Step 3: Backward Pass** $$L_9 = 29$$ $$L_8 = 29 - 4 = 25$$ $$L_7 = 25 - 0 = 25$$ $$L_6 = \min(25 - 6, \; 25 - 3) = \min(19, 22) = 19$$ $$L_5 = 19 - 0 = 19$$ $$L_4 = 19 - 7 = 12$$ $$L_3 = 19 - 5 = 14$$ $$L_2 = \min(14 - 8, \; 12 - 4) = \min(6, 8) = 6$$ $$L_1 = 6 - 6 = 0 \;\checkmark$$ **Step 4: Floats and Critical Path** | Activity | $i \to j$ | $d$ | $E_i$ | $L_j$ | $TF$ | Critical? | |----------|-----------|-----|-------|-------|------|-----------| | A | 1→2 | 6 | 0 | 6 | 0 | ✓ | | B | 2→3 | 8 | 6 | 14 | 0 | ✓ | | C | 2→4 | 4 | 6 | 12 | 2 | ✗ | | D | 3→5 | 5 | 14 | 19 | 0 | ✓ | | E | 4→6 | 7 | 10 | 19 | 2 | ✗ | | Dummy₁ | 5→6 | 0 | 19 | 19 | 0 | ✓ | | F | 6→7 | 6 | 19 | 25 | 0 | ✓ | | G | 6→8 | 3 | 19 | 25 | 3 | ✗ | | Dummy₂ | 7→8 | 0 | 25 | 25 | 0 | ✓ | | H | 8→9 | 4 | 25 | 29 | 0 | ✓ | **Critical Path: A → B → D → F → H = 6 + 8 + 5 + 6 + 4 = 29 days ✓** --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q:** Explain the three-time estimate concept in PERT. For an activity with $t_o = 2$, $t_m = 5$, $t_p = 12$ days, calculate the expected time and standard deviation. If this activity lies on the critical path and the project has a total variance of 4.5 days², find the probability of completing the project in **20 days** when the expected duration is **18 days**. **Model Solution (7 Marks):** **Three-Time Estimates in PERT (3 Marks):** $$t_o = \text{Optimistic time (minimum possible, no obstructions)}$$ $$t_m = \text{Most likely time (mode, normal conditions)}$$ $$t_p = \text{Pessimistic time (worst-case, maximum delays)}$$ The Beta distribution approximates real-world activity durations. **Calculations (4 Marks):** Expected Time: $$t_e = \frac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6} = \frac{2 + 4(5) + 12}{6} = \frac{2 + 20 + 12}{6} = \frac{34}{6} = 5.67 \text{ days}$$ Variance: $$\sigma^2 = \left(\frac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{12 - 2}{6}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{10}{6}\right)^2 = 2.778 \text{ days}^2$$ Standard Deviation: $$\sigma = \sqrt{2.778} = 1.667 \text{ days}$$ **Probability Calculation:** Given: $T_s = 20$ days, $T_e = 18$ days, $\sigma_{\text{project}}^2 = 4.5$ days² $$\sigma_{\text{project}} = \sqrt{4.5} = 2.121 \text{ days}$$ $$Z = \frac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{\text{project}}} = \frac{20 - 18}{2.121} = 0.943$$ From standard normal table: $P(Z \leq 0.94) \approx 0.8264$ **Probability of completion in 20 days ≈ 82.64%** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Q:** What is crashing in network analysis? For the following critical activities, determine the optimal crashing strategy to reduce project duration by 3 days at minimum cost. | Activity | Normal Time (days) | Crash Time (days) | Normal Cost (₹) | Crash Cost (₹) | |----------|--------------------|-------------------|-----------------|----------------| | P | 8 | 5 | 10,000 | 16,000 | | Q | 6 | 4 | 8,000 | 12,000 | | R | 10 | 7 | 15,000 | 21,000 | **Model Solution (7 Marks):** **Crashing Concept (2 Marks):** Crashing is the process of **reducing activity durations** by allocating additional resources (overtime, extra manpower), at an additional cost, to accelerate project completion. The **cost slope** indicates the cost per day of reduction. $$\text{Cost Slope} = \frac{C_c - C_n}{t_n - t_c}$$ **Step 1: Calculate Cost Slopes (3 Marks)** For P: $S_P = \frac{16000 - 10000}{8 - 5} = \frac{6000}{3} = ₹2000/\text{day}$ For Q: $S_Q = \frac{12000 - 8000}{6 - 4} = \frac{4000}{2} = ₹2000/\text{day}$ For R: $S_R = \frac{21000 - 15000}{10 - 7} = \frac{6000}{3} = ₹2000/\text{day}$ **Step 2: Crashing Strategy (2 Marks)** Since all cost slopes are equal (₹2000/day), any activity can be crashed first. To reduce by 3 days: - **Option 1:** Crash P by 3 days (since P can be reduced from 8 to 5 = 3 days maximum) → Extra cost = $3 \times 2000 = ₹6,000$ - **Option 2:** Crash Q by 2 days + R by 1 day → Extra cost = $(2 \times 2000) + (1 \times 2000) = ₹6,000$ **Optimal Strategy:** Crash **activity P by 3 days** (single-activity crash, simpler management). **Additional Cost = ₹6,000** --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Q:** Explain the terms: Total Float, Free Float, and Independent Float. Why are dummies required in AOA networks? Give an example. **Model Solution (7 Marks):** **Float Definitions (4 Marks):** **Total Float (TF):** The maximum time an activity can be delayed without delaying the **project completion date**. $$TF_{ij} = L_j - E_i - d_{ij}$$ **Free Float (FF):** The maximum time an activity can be delayed without delaying the **early start of any successor activity**. $$FF_{ij} = E_j - E_i - d_{ij}$$ **Independent Float (IF):** The maximum time an activity can be delayed assuming its **predecessor finishes at latest time** and its **successor starts at earliest time** (the most conservative measure). $$IF_{ij} = E_j - L_i - d_{ij}$$ **Example to illustrate (using earlier ship problem):** For activity **B (2→3, duration 3):** - $E_2 = 4$, $E_3 = 7$, $L_2 = 4$, $L_3 = 9$ - $TF = 9 - 4 - 3 = 2$ days - $FF = 7 - 4 - 3 = 0$ days - $IF = 7 - 4 - 3 = 0$ days **Dummy Activities in AOA (3 Marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Purpose:** Dummy activities are zero-duration logical connectors used **only** in AOA networks to maintain correct precedence relationships when two or more activities share the same start and end events. **Example:** Activities X, Y, Z with: - X, Y both start at Event 1 - X ends at Event 2; Y ends at Event 3 - Z depends on both X and Y, ending at Event 4 Without dummy: Z would need to start from both Event 2 and Event 3 → impossible. **Solution:** Add a dummy from Event 2 to Event 3, then Z starts from Event 3 → precedence maintained. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting Dummy Activities (–2 marks):** Always check if two activities share identical start and end events. If yes, insert a dummy. > > 2. **Wrong Forward Pass Direction (–2 marks):** Forward pass goes **left to right** using **MAX**; backward pass goes **right to left** using **MIN**. > > 3. **Misidentifying Critical Path (–3 marks):** Critical path = activities with **TF = 0**, NOT the longest individual activity. > > 4. **AOA vs AON Confusion (–2 marks):** AOA = Activity on Arrow; AON = Activity on Node. Don't mix notation. > > 5. **PERT Formula Errors (–2 marks):** Expected time formula has weightage **4** on $t_m$: $t_e = \dfrac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$. Some students write $2t_m$ or $3t_m$. > > 6. **Float Calculation Mistakes (–2 marks):** Use correct event numbers; don't confuse $E_j$ with $L_j$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:** ### Core Concepts - [ ] **Network Model** = Graphical plan of project activities and events - [ ] **AOA** = Activity on Arrow (traditional, requires dummies) - [ ] **AON** = Activity on Node (modern, no dummies needed) - [ ] **PERT** = Three time estimates, probabilistic, for R&D - [ ] **CPM** = Single time estimate, deterministic, for repetitive construction - [ ] **Critical Path** = Longest path; activities with $TF = 0$ ### Must-Memorize Formulas - [ ] $t_e = \dfrac{t_o + 4t_m + t_p}{6}$ - [ ] $\sigma^2 = \left(\dfrac{t_p - t_o}{6}\right)^2$ - [ ] $Z = \dfrac{T_s - T_e}{\sigma_{\text{project}}}$ - [ ] $TF = L_j - E_i - d_{ij}$ - [ ] $FF = E_j - E_i - d_{ij}$ - [ ] $\text{Cost Slope} = \dfrac{C_c - C_n}{t_n - t_c}$ ### Numerical Problem Steps - [ ] Draw AOA network with **dummy activities** - [ ] **Forward pass:** $E_i = \max(\text{predecessor } E + d)$ - [ ] **Backward pass:** $L_j = \min(\text{successor } L - d)$ - [ ] Compute $TF$ for every activity - [ ] Mark activities with $TF = 0$ as **critical** - [ ] Sum durations along critical path = Project Duration ### Real-World Ship Production Context - [ ] Apply to **block construction scheduling** (300–600 blocks) - [ ] Apply to **dry-dock occupancy planning** - [ ] Apply to **outfitting coordination** (parallel pipelines) - [ ] Apply to **sea trials window** planning - [ ] Apply to **crashing analysis** for early delivery commitments ### Common KTU Question Patterns - [ ] Construct network → Find critical path → 7 marks - [ ] PERT three-estimate → Probability of completion → 7 marks - [ ] Crashing → Minimum cost for target duration → 7 marks - [ ] Compare PERT vs CPM → 3–5 marks - [ ] Define floats → Numerical example → 7 marks --- > **End of Module 3 — Network Models | PENST633 Ship Production Management** > **Best of luck for your KTU 2024 ESE examinations! 🚢⚓**

topics.title: Quality Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST633 - SHIP PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Quality Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Quality Control (QC)** is the operational, process-oriented set of activities and techniques used to fulfil requirements for quality. In the context of ship production management, QC refers to the systematic monitoring, inspection, testing, and evaluation of various stages of ship construction—from steel cutting, panel fabrication, sub-assembly, block erection, to outfitting and sea trials—to ensure that the vessel conforms to the approved drawings, classification society rules (e.g., IRS, Lloyd's, DNV, ABS), contractual specifications, and regulatory standards (SOLAS, MARPOL).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> *Quality Control in ship production is the coordinated system of planned activities directed at verifying that fabricated hull structures, machinery installations, and outfitting work satisfy the design intent, class rules, and owner's requirements through inspection, measurement, testing, and statistical evaluation of production processes.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Master Shipwright's Eye"

Imagine a master chef preparing a five-star dish for a wedding banquet. The chef does **not** just cook the food and hand it to the customer hoping it's good. Instead, the chef constantly:
- **Tastes** the broth (in-process inspection),
- **Measures** portions with a scale (dimensional inspection),
- **Visually checks** the plating (visual inspection),
- **Takes a small spoonful** from the large pot to send for lab testing (sampling inspection),
- **Documents** everything in a kitchen log (quality records).

In shipbuilding, the **Quality Control Department** plays this exact role. The "dish" is a 300-metre oil tanker worth USD 100 million. The "taste" is an ultrasonic thickness gauge, a dye penetrant test, or a 3D laser scan. The "kitchen log" is the **Quality Plan** and **Inspection and Test Plan (ITP)** signed off by classification surveyors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Quality Metrics You Must Memorise for KTU Exams:**
> - **AQL** — Acceptable Quality Level (%)
> - **LTPD** — Lot Tolerance Percent Defective (%)
> - **Cp / Cpk** — Process Capability Indices (dimensionless)
> - **σ** — Standard Deviation (units of measurement)
> - **DPMO** — Defects Per Million Opportunities
> - **Six Sigma Level** — 3.4 DPMO

### 1.3 Distinction: Quality Control vs. Quality Assurance vs. TQM

| Aspect | Quality Control (QC) | Quality Assurance (QA) | Total Quality Management (TQM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Product / output | Process / system | Entire organisation & culture |
| **Approach** | Reactive (detect defects) | Preventive (avoid defects) | Proactive (continuous improvement) |
| **Tools** | Gauges, gauges, NDT, control charts | ITP, audits, procedures, manuals | TQM principles, Kaizen, Six Sigma, 5S |
| **KTU Key Line** | "Finds the defect" | "Prevents the defect" | "Eliminates the cause of the defect" |
| **Documentation** | Inspection records, test reports | Quality manual, procedures | Quality policy, mission, vision |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory trick for KTU viva:** *QC = "Catch" the defect. QA = "Avoid" the defect. TQM = "Abolish" the cause of the defect.*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Quality Control in Ship Production

A shipyard's QC system rests on four operational pillars, and the KTU examiner loves to test the student's ability to enumerate them in a logical order.

1. **Incoming Material Control** — Verifying steel plates, profiles, pipes, machinery, and electrical items before they enter the production line. Typical tests include Mill Certificate verification, chemical composition checks, and mechanical property tests (tensile, impact, bend).
2. **In-Process Quality Control (IPQC)** — Continuous monitoring during fabrication. Weld parameters (amperage, voltage, travel speed), dimensional checks of panels, alignment of sub-assemblies.
