3. **Final Inspection and Testing** — Pre-delivery trials. Hydrostatic tests, tank testing, mooring trials, sea trials, inclining experiment, and speed-power trials.
4. **Quality Records and Traceability** — Every piece of steel, every weld, every test must be traceable to the welder, the procedure, and the date. This is the famous **"Welder's QR Code"** on modern yards.

### 2.2 Statistical Quality Control (SQC) — The Heart of QC

SQC applies statistical methods to monitor and control a production process. In shipbuilding, it is heavily used in:
- **Weld quality monitoring** (porosity, undercut, crater cracks).
- **Dimensional control** of large panels (tolerance $\pm 2$ mm over 20 m).
- **Paint thickness** on hull (typical 200–400 µm DFT).
- **Pipe bending ovality** (max 5–8% of nominal diameter).

The two main branches of SQC are:
- **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** → uses **control charts**.
- **Acceptance Sampling** → uses **sampling plans** like MIL-STD-1916 (old MIL-STD-105E), ANSI/ASQ Z1.4.

### 2.3 Control Charts — The SPC Workhorse

A control chart has three lines: the **Centre Line (CL)** equal to the process mean, the **Upper Control Limit (UCL)** at $+3\sigma$, and the **Lower Control Limit (LCL)** at $-3\sigma$. Any point outside these limits signals an **out-of-control condition**, demanding immediate investigation.

For the **$\bar{X}$-chart** (sample mean chart), limits are:

$$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \cdot \bar{R}$$

$$\text{CL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}}$$

$$\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \cdot \bar{R}$$

For the **R-chart** (sample range chart), the limits are:

$$\text{UCL}_R = D_4 \cdot \bar{R}$$

$$\text{CL}_R = \bar{R}$$

$$\text{LCL}_R = D_3 \cdot \bar{R}$$

The constants $A_2, D_3, D_4$ depend on the **sample size n** and are tabulated in standard SPC textbooks.

### 2.4 Process Capability Indices

The capability indices compare the **process spread (6σ)** to the **specification width (USL - LSL)**.

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$

$$C_{pk} = \min\left(C_{pu},\, C_{pl}\right) = \min\!\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3\sigma},\, \frac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Benchmarks (memorise these):**
> - $C_p \geq 1.33$ → process is **capable**.
> - $C_p \geq 1.67$ → process is **highly capable (Six Sigma territory)**.
> - $C_{pk} < 1.0$ → process is **incapable**, immediate action needed.

### 2.5 Acceptance Sampling

When 100% inspection is impossible (e.g., destructive testing of a 50 mm steel plate's Charpy impact), **sampling** is used. The key parameters are:

- **AQL (Acceptable Quality Level):** The worst tolerable process average when a continuing series of lots is submitted for acceptance sampling.
- **LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective):** The poorest level of quality that the consumer is willing to accept in an individual lot.
- **Producer's Risk (α):** Probability of rejecting a good lot (typically **0.05**).
- **Consumer's Risk (β):** Probability of accepting a bad lot (typically **0.10**).
- **OC Curve (Operating Characteristic Curve):** The graph showing probability of acceptance $P_a$ versus incoming quality $p$.

The relationship between these risks is captured by the **AOQL (Average Outgoing Quality Limit)** formula and the sampling plan parameters $n$ (sample size) and $c$ (acceptance number).

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this table. It is the single most-asked numerical content from QC in KTU ESE papers.**

| \# | Formula | Variables / Meaning | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2\bar{R}$ | Control limits for $\bar{X}$-chart | Same as measurement |
| 2 | $\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2\bar{R}$ | Lower control limit | Same as measurement |
| 3 | $\text{UCL}_R = D_4\bar{R}$ | Upper limit for R-chart | Same as measurement |
| 4 | $\text{LCL}_R = D_3\bar{R}$ | Lower limit for R-chart | Same as measurement |
| 5 | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL}-\text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Process capability | Dimensionless ($\geq 1.33$) |
| 6 | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL}-\bar{X}}{3\sigma},\,\dfrac{\bar{X}-\text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Adjusted capability (centring) | Dimensionless ($\geq 1.33$) |
| 7 | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{U \times N}$ | Defects per million opportunities | D = defects, U = units, N = opportunities |
| 8 | $\text{Yield (Y)} = e^{-DPMO/10^6}$ | Rolled-throughput yield | $0 \le Y \le 1$ |
| 9 | $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Standard error of mean | Reduces with $\sqrt{n}$ |
| 10 | $\text{OC: } P_a = \sum_{k=0}^{c} \binom{n}{k} p^k (1-p)^{n-k}$ | Binomial OC curve | $p$ = fraction defective |

### 2.7 Real-World Utility in Ship Production

- **Welding Quality:** $C_{pk}$ analysis on weld leg length and throat thickness to certify procedure qualification.
- **Hull Plate Flatness:** SPC on thermal cutting accuracy of steel plates (1–3 mm tolerance).
- **Pipe Spool Fabrication:** Sampling inspection of weld radiographic films.
- **Paint & Corrosion:** DFT (Dry Film Thickness) control charts on blast-cleaned and primed surfaces.
- **Engine Bedplate Levelness:** Acceptance sampling of dimensional measurements across the main engine foundation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Control Chart Limits from First Principles

A control chart is built on the **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)**. The CLT states that for a process in statistical control, the means of random samples of size $n$ drawn from a population with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$ will be approximately normally distributed with:

$$\bar{X} \sim N\!\left(\mu,\, \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)$$

Therefore, the **standard error of the mean** is:

$$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

By the **Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7)** of a normal distribution, **99.73%** of sample means lie within $\pm 3\sigma_{\bar{X}}$ of $\mu$ when the process is in control. Hence, the natural control limits are:

$$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \mu + 3 \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

$$\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \mu - 3 \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

In practice, we use $\bar{\bar{X}}$ to estimate $\mu$ and $\bar{R}/d_2$ to estimate $\sigma$, where $d_2$ is a constant for sample size $n$. Substituting:

$$\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2\sqrt{n}} = A_2 \bar{R}$$

where $A_2 = \dfrac{3}{d_2\sqrt{n}}$. This yields the practical formula:

$$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}, \qquad \text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Process Capability Index $C_p$

Suppose specification limits are $\text{LSL}$ (lower) and $\text{USL}$ (upper). The total specification width is:

$$W_{spec} = \text{USL} - \text{LSL}$$

A process in statistical control exhibits natural variability spanning $\pm 3\sigma$ on either side of the mean, so the total **process spread** is:

$$W_{proc} = 6\sigma$$

The **Process Capability Index $C_p$** is defined as the ratio of allowable spread to actual spread:

$$C_p = \frac{W_{spec}}{W_{proc}} = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$

**Interpretation:**
- If $C_p = 1.0$, the process just barely fits.
- If $C_p = 1.5$, the process has a 50% safety margin.
- If $C_p < 1.0$, the process produces more defects than the spec allows.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — $C_{pk}$ Adjustment for Off-Centring

$C_p$ assumes the process mean is perfectly centred between LSL and USL. In reality, the process drifts. Define the actual mean as $\bar{X}$. The distance from the mean to the nearest specification limit is:

$$d_{min} = \min\!\left(\text{USL} - \bar{X},\; \bar{X} - \text{LSL}\right)$$

The **one-sided** capability is:

$$C_{pu} = \frac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}, \qquad C_{pl} = \frac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}$$

The **adjusted capability** takes the worse (smaller) of the two:

$$C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu},\, C_{pl})$$

When the process is perfectly centred, $C_{pk} = C_p$. The shift between process mean and target is often reported as:

$$\text{Shift} = \bar{X} - \frac{\text{USL} + \text{LSL}}{2}$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — Control Chart Construction

**Problem:** A shipyard measures the weld leg length (in mm) on 5 sample subgroups (each of size n = 5) taken from a panel line. The grand mean and average range are:

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = 10.0\,\text{mm}, \qquad \bar{R} = 1.2\,\text{mm}$$

For $n = 5$, the SPC constant $A_2 = 0.577$. Compute the control limits for the $\bar{X}$-chart and determine if a new sample with mean $\bar{X}_{new} = 11.5$ mm is in control.

**Step 1 — Identify given data.**
$\bar{\bar{X}} = 10.0$, $\bar{R} = 1.2$, $A_2 = 0.577$.

**Step 2 — Apply the $\bar{X}$-chart formulas.**

$$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \cdot \bar{R} = 10.0 + 0.577 \times 1.2$$

$$0.577 \times 1.2 = 0.6924$$

$$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = 10.0 + 0.6924 = 10.6924 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the lower limit.**

$$\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \cdot \bar{R} = 10.0 - 0.6924 = 9.3076 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Compare new sample.**

$$\bar{X}_{new} = 11.5 \text{ mm} > \text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = 10.6924 \text{ mm}$$

**Conclusion:** The new sample mean **exceeds the upper control limit**; the process is **out of control**. The foreman must halt welding, check electrode diameter, amperage setting, and travel speed.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Process Capability in Pipe Ovality

**Problem:** Steel pipes used in a fuel-oil system must have ovality between LSL = 1% and USL = 9% of nominal diameter. A sample of 50 pipes yields a mean ovality $\bar{X} = 4.5\%$ with $\sigma = 1.2\%$. Compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ and comment on process capability.

**Step 1 — Compute $C_p$.**

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} = \frac{9 - 1}{6 \times 1.2} = \frac{8}{7.2} = 1.111$$

**Step 2 — Compute the two one-sided values.**

$$C_{pu} = \frac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3\sigma} = \frac{9 - 4.5}{3 \times 1.2} = \frac{4.5}{3.6} = 1.25$$

$$C_{pl} = \frac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \frac{4.5 - 1}{3.6} = \frac{3.5}{3.6} = 0.972$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_{pk}$.**

$$C_{pk} = \min(1.25,\, 0.972) = 0.972$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.**
- $C_p = 1.111$ → process spread fits within specifications, but margin is thin.
- $C_{pk} = 0.972 < 1.0$ → process is **incapable** because the mean is **shifted toward LSL**. Action: re-centre the pipe-bending machine toward the midpoint 5%.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Tools to Stages

| Production Stage | QC Tool | KTU Key Phrase |
|---|---|---|
| Steel receipt | Mill cert + IGC test | "Source control" |
| Cutting & forming | Dimensional control chart | "In-process SPC" |
| Welding | WPS/PQR + NDT (UT, RT, MPI, DPI) | "Procedure qualification" |
| Block erection | Total station / laser scan alignment | "Dimensional survey" |
| Pipe spooling | Hydrostatic test, isometric check | "Pressure integrity" |
| Outfitting | Visual + functional tests | "Snag-list / punch list" |
| Pre-launch | Inclining experiment, dock trial | "Sea-trial verification" |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PDCA (Deming) Cycle — The Backbone of Continuous Improvement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["PLAN\nDefine quality objectives\nIdentify processes\nCreate ITP"] --> node2["DO\nExecute production\nTrain workforce\nCollect QC data"]
    node2 --> node3["CHECK\nMeasure vs specifications\nPlot control charts\nConduct NDT & audits"]
    node3 --> node4["ACT\nCorrect deviations\nStandardise improvements\nUpdate procedures"]
    node4 --> node1
    node3 --> node5["IF DEVIATION DETECTED\nStop work, root cause analysis\nCAPA implementation"]
    node5 --> node2
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Shipyard Quality Management System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1["GOVERNANCE LAYER"]
        A1["Top Management\nQuality Policy\nManagement Review"]
    end
    subgraph L2["DOCUMENTATION LAYER"]
        B1["Quality Manual"]
        B2["Procedures SOPs"]
        B3["Inspection and Test Plan ITP"]
    end
    subgraph L3["EXECUTION LAYER"]
        C1["Incoming Material Control"]
        C2["In Process Quality Control IPQC"]
        C3["Final Inspection and Testing"]
    end
    subgraph L4["VERIFICATION LAYER"]
        D1["Class Surveyor ABS DNV IRS"]
        D2["Owner Representative"]
        D3["Flag State Inspection"]
    end
    subgraph L5["IMPROVEMENT LAYER"]
        E1["Corrective Action CAPA"]
        E2["Preventive Action"]
        E3["Internal Quality Audits"]
        E4["Kaizen Six Sigma Projects"]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C1 --> D2
    C3 --> D3
    D1 --> E1
    D2 --> E3
    E1 --> E4
    E3 --> E2
    E2 --> A1
```

### 4.3 SPC Decision Flow — When a Point Falls Outside Control Limits

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeF["Collect subgroup sample"] --> nodeG["Compute subgroup mean and range"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Plot on control chart"]
    nodeH --> nodeI{"Point inside\ncontrol limits?"}
    nodeI -->|"Yes"| nodeJ["Continue production\nProcess in control"]
    nodeI -->|"No"| nodeK["STOP THE LINE"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["Identify assignable cause\nusing Ishikawa fishbone"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["Implement corrective action"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["Reverify process capability"]
    nodeN --> nodeJ
```

### 4.4 Quality Inspection Hierarchy in a Shipyard

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["First Party\nShipyard Self Inspection"] --> nodeQ["Second Party\nOwner Representative Surveyor"]
    nodeQ --> nodeR["Third Party\nClassification Society ABS DNV IRS LR BV"]
    nodeR --> nodeS["Fourth Party\nFlag State Maritime Authority"]
    nodeS --> nodeT["Fifth Party\nInternational Maritime Organisation IMO"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**
Define Quality Control. How is it different from Quality Assurance in the context of ship production?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Quality Control is the operational technique and set of activities used to fulfil quality requirements, including inspection, testing, and monitoring of products and processes. In ship production, QC involves measurement, NDT, and inspection of hull, machinery, and outfitting at various stages.

| Aspect (1 mark) | QC | QA |
|---|---|---|
| Focus | Detects defects in the product | Prevents defects in the process |
| Approach | Reactive inspection | Preventive system design |
| Documentation | Inspection and test records | Quality manual, SOPs, ITP |

QC focuses on the **product**; QA focuses on the **system**. (1 mark for the distinction)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**
List any **six** non-destructive testing (NDT) methods commonly used in ship production and state what each detects.

**Model Answer (3 marks — half mark each):**
1. **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** — Detects internal flaws, lamination, cracks in plates and welds.
2. **Radiographic Testing (RT)** — Detects volumetric defects (porosity, slag, lack of fusion) in welds.
3. **Magnetic Particle Inspection (MPI)** — Detects surface and near-surface cracks in ferromagnetic steel.
4. **Dye Penetrant Inspection (DPI)** — Detects surface-breaking cracks on any non-porous material.
5. **Eddy Current Testing (ET)** — Detects surface defects in tubing and heat-exchanger plates.
6. **Visual Testing (VT)** — Detects surface defects, misalignments, dimensional deviations.
7. *(Bonus)* **Acoustic Emission Testing** — Monitors growing cracks under load.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - In derivation questions, **never skip the intermediate step** $\sigma_{\bar{X}} = \sigma/\sqrt{n}$. Examiners allocate **1 mark** specifically for this transition.
> - When asked to "discuss" a concept, students often write textbook definitions only. Examiners want **ship-production-specific examples** (e.g., name a specific weld type, a specific NDT method, a specific class rule).
> - In $C_{pk}$ questions, **forgetting the off-centring** is the single most common mistake. Always state $\bar{X}$ vs target before computing.
> - Do **not** confuse $D_3$ and $D_4$. Their values differ — refer to the SPC table for sample size.
> - Always **conclude with a managerial recommendation** in 14-mark questions. The examiner's rubric reserves 1–2 marks for "process improvement suggestion."

---

#### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
(a) **Explain the concept of Statistical Process Control (SPC) with neat sketches of an $\bar{X}$-chart and an R-chart.** Discuss the procedure for constructing the $\bar{X}$-chart for monitoring the weld leg length in a shipbuilding panel line. **(7 marks)**

(b) **The following data refer to weld leg length (in mm) measured on a panel line. Five samples of size 5 each were taken.**

| Sample | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mean $\bar{X}$ | 9.8 | 10.2 | 10.1 | 10.0 | 9.9 |
| Range R | 1.0 | 1.4 | 0.8 | 1.2 | 1.1 |

For $n = 5$, the SPC constants are $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$.
**(i)** Compute the centre line and control limits for the $\bar{X}$-chart and the R-chart.
**(ii)** A new sample of size 5 gives $\bar{X} = 11.0$ mm, $R = 1.5$ mm. Is the process in control? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Conceptual Part (7 marks):**
SPC is the application of statistical techniques to monitor and control a production process. The main SPC tool is the control chart, introduced by Walter Shewhart in 1924. A control chart distinguishes between **common-cause variation** (inherent randomness) and **special-cause variation** (assignable causes). (2 marks)

**Procedure for constructing an $\bar{X}$-chart** (3 marks):
1. Collect $k$ subgroups, each of size $n$.
2. Compute subgroup mean $\bar{X}_i$ and range $R_i$.
3. Compute grand mean $\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{1}{k}\sum \bar{X}_i$ and average range $\bar{R} = \frac{1}{k}\sum R_i$.
4. Compute $\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$, $\text{CL} = \bar{\bar{X}}$, $\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$.
5. Plot $\bar{X}_i$ against sample number; verify all points lie within limits.

Sketches: $\bar{X}$-chart with horizontal lines for UCL, CL, LCL and plotted sample means; R-chart with UCL, CL, LCL. (2 marks)

**(b) Numerical Part (7 marks):**
**Step 1 — Compute $\bar{\bar{X}}$ and $\bar{R}$.**

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{9.8 + 10.2 + 10.1 + 10.0 + 9.9}{5} = \frac{50.0}{5} = 10.0 \text{ mm}$$

$$\bar{R} = \frac{1.0 + 1.4 + 0.8 + 1.2 + 1.1}{5} = \frac{5.5}{5} = 1.1 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — $\bar{X}$-chart limits.**

$$\text{UCL}_{\bar{X}} = 10.0 + 0.577 \times 1.1 = 10.0 + 0.6347 = 10.635 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{LCL}_{\bar{X}} = 10.0 - 0.6347 = 9.365 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{CL}_{\bar{X}} = 10.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — R-chart limits.**

$$\text{UCL}_R = 2.114 \times 1.1 = 2.325 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{LCL}_R = 0 \times 1.1 = 0 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{CL}_R = 1.1 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Test the new sample.**

New $\bar{X} = 11.0$ mm > UCL$_{\bar{X}} = 10.635$ mm → **out of control** on the $\bar{X}$-chart.
New $R = 1.5$ mm < UCL$_R = 2.325$ mm → **in control** on the R-chart.

**Conclusion:** The process mean has shifted upward, but the within-sample variability is acceptable. The probable cause is a **change in welder setting** (e.g., increased current or travel speed reduction). The foreman should stop the line, verify the welding parameters against the WPS, and recalibrate. (1 mark for managerial recommendation)

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
(a) **What is meant by Process Capability? Derive the expressions for $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ indices. Explain their significance in quality control of ship hull fabrication.** **(7 marks)**

(b) **The diameter of a marine shaft is specified as $\phi 250^{+0.10}_{-0.05}$ mm. A sample of 40 shafts produced by the lathe gives a mean diameter of 250.04 mm and a standard deviation of 0.025 mm. Calculate $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, and state whether the process is capable. If the mean drifts to 250.07 mm, what is the new $C_{pk}$?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Conceptual Part (7 marks):**
Process capability is the ability of a process to produce output within specification limits. It is measured by comparing the natural spread of the process (6σ) to the specification width. (1 mark)

**Derivation of $C_p$:** Let USL and LSL be specification limits. Specification width is $W_{spec} = \text{USL} - \text{LSL}$. The process spread is $W_{proc} = 6\sigma$. Define:

$$C_p = \frac{W_{spec}}{W_{proc}} = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} \quad \text{(2 marks for derivation)}$$

**Derivation of $C_{pk}$:** $C_p$ does not account for off-centring. Define one-sided capabilities:

$$C_{pu} = \frac{\text{USL} - \bar{X}}{3\sigma}, \qquad C_{pl} = \frac{\bar{X} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}$$

The process is limited by the smaller value:

$$C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu},\, C_{pl}) \quad \text{(2 marks for derivation)}$$

**Significance in ship hull fabrication:** $C_p$ tells the yard whether the rolling/forming/cutting process is inherently capable of meeting the IACS tolerance. $C_{pk}$ tells the yard whether the mean is on target. For a $\pm 2$ mm plate tolerance, the shipyard must achieve $C_{pk} \ge 1.33$. (2 marks for discussion)

**(b) Numerical Part (7 marks):**
**Given:** USL = 250.10 mm, LSL = 249.95 mm, $\bar{X} = 250.04$ mm, $\sigma = 0.025$ mm.

**Step 1 — Compute $C_p$.**

$$C_p = \frac{250.10 - 249.95}{6 \times 0.025} = \frac{0.15}{0.15} = 1.00$$

**Step 2 — Compute $C_{pu}$ and $C_{pl}$.**

$$C_{pu} = \frac{250.10 - 250.04}{3 \times 0.025} = \frac{0.06}{0.075} = 0.80$$

$$C_{pl} = \frac{250.04 - 249.95}{0.075} = \frac{0.09}{0.075} = 1.20$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_{pk}$.**

$$C_{pk} = \min(0.80,\, 1.20) = 0.80$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.** $C_p = 1.00$ indicates the process spread is just at the edge; $C_{pk} = 0.80 < 1.0$ means the process is **not capable** because the mean is shifted toward the upper spec. The lathe operator must re-centre the process downward. (1 mark for conclusion)

**Step 5 — Recompute $C_{pk}$ with $\bar{X} = 250.07$ mm.**

$$C_{pu} = \frac{250.10 - 250.07}{0.075} = \frac{0.03}{0.075} = 0.40$$

$$C_{pl} = \frac{250.07 - 249.95}{0.075} = \frac{0.12}{0.075} = 1.60$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(0.40,\, 1.60) = 0.40$$

**Interpretation:** Drift in the mean has worsened $C_{pk}$ from 0.80 to 0.40. The process is now severely incapable. **Recommendation:** immediate machine stoppage, tool replacement, and statistical re-centring. (1 mark for recommendation)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist — Quality Control (Module 3)**

- **Definition Mastery:** QC is product-focused and reactive; QA is process-focused and preventive; TQM is culture-focused and proactive.
- **The "Big 4" Stages of QC in Ship Production:** *Source control → In-process control → Final inspection → Records and traceability.*
- **SPC Constants (must memorise for $n = 5$):** $A_2 = 0.577$, $D_3 = 0$, $D_4 = 2.114$.
- **The 3-Sigma Rule:** UCL = Mean + 3σ, LCL = Mean − 3σ. Any point outside = out-of-control.
- **$C_p$ vs $C_{pk}$:** $C_p$ = spread capability; $C_{pk}$ = spread + centring capability. Always compute both.
- **Capability Benchmarks:** $C_p \ge 1.33$ (capable), $C_{pk} \ge 1.0$ (acceptable minimum in KTU model answers), $C_{pk} \ge 1.67$ (Six Sigma).
- **Acceptance Sampling Risks:** Producer's Risk α = 0.05, Consumer's Risk β = 0.10. These are the default KTU numerical values.
- **Six Sigma Level:** 3.4 DPMO corresponds to 4.5σ from the mean. The famous Motorola benchmark.
- **PDCA Cycle (Deming):** Plan → Do → Check → Act. Always close the loop with CAPA.
- **NDT Memory Trick:** "**U**ltrasound finds internal, **R**ays see volume, **M**agnets catch surface, **D**ye reveals open cracks."
- **Class Surveyors to Remember:** IRS (India), Lloyd's Register (UK), DNV (Norway), ABS (USA), Bureau Veritas (France), ClassNK (Japan).
- **ISO 9001:2015 — Process Approach** with risk-based thinking. Always mention "Plan-Do-Check-Act" cycle in ISO 9001 answers.
- **Key Ship-Production Examples to Use in Answers:** Welding leg length, hull plate flatness, paint DFT, pipe ovality, shaft diameter, engine alignment.
- **Common Pitfall:** Never write "$C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ are the same." They are equal only when the process is perfectly centred.
- **Managerial Closing Line for 14-mark answers:** "The yard should implement CAPA, retrain the workforce on the WPS, and conduct a fresh $C_{pk}$ study to verify recovery."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maintenance Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST633 - SHIP PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Maintenance Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Maintenance Analysis** in the context of Ship Production Management is defined as the systematic, engineering-based evaluation of all activities, procedures, resources, and strategies required to retain a ship, its machinery, systems, and shipyard production equipment in, or restore them to, a state in which they can perform their intended operational functions throughout their designed service life.

It is the decision-making discipline that determines **what** must be maintained, **when** it must be maintained, **how** it must be maintained, and **at what cost**, drawing inputs from reliability engineering, failure statistics, life-cycle economics, classification society rules, and shipyard production scheduling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Maintenance Analysis is the quantitative and qualitative study of equipment/ship system deterioration patterns, failure modes, downtime behaviour, and the corresponding preventive/corrective actions required to ensure a defined level of operational readiness at minimum life-cycle cost.*

The discipline is built on three foundational pillars:

1. **Reliability** – The probability that a system performs its intended function under stated conditions for a stated period of time.
2. **Maintainability** – The probability that a failed system is restored to operational effectiveness within a given time, using prescribed procedures and resources.
3. **Availability** – The fraction of time a system is operationally ready for use, derived from reliability and maintainability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industry Standard:**
> The maritime industry generally targets an operational **Availability of 0.95 to 0.99** for critical propulsion and navigation systems on commercial vessels.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a brand-new **family car** you have just bought. The owner's manual gives you two extreme philosophies of looking after it:

- **Philosophy A (Breakdown Maintenance):** You never service the car. You just drive it until the engine seizes or the brakes fail, and then you repair what has broken. The car is cheap to "look after" day-to-day, but the breakdowns are catastrophic, expensive, dangerous, and leave you stranded.
- **Philosophy B (Preventive Maintenance):** You change engine oil every 5,000 km, replace brake pads before they wear out, and inspect belts regularly. You spend small, predictable amounts routinely to avoid the big disasters.

A ship in a shipyard (or in service at sea) is exactly the same — but on a vastly larger, more expensive, and more safety-critical scale. **Maintenance Analysis** is the engineering science that decides whether we should be a *Philosophy A* or *Philosophy B* owner, and crucially, **how often, how much, and what exactly** to service.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world ship example:** A bulk carrier's main engine bearing is not allowed to "run to failure." Classification societies (IACS members like IRS, DNV, LR, BV) mandate **running hours-based overhauls** of critical machinery. Maintenance Analysis is what determines those running-hour intervals based on reliability data, wear-out curves, and economic optimization.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally used in maritime maintenance analysis and will appear repeatedly in KTU examination questions:

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** Average operational time between two consecutive failures of a repairable system, expressed in **hours**.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** Average time required to restore a failed system to operational status, including diagnosis, repair, and recommissioning, in **hours**.
- **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure):** For non-repairable items (like electronic chips, bearings sealed for life), the average time to first failure, in **hours**.
- **Failure Rate (λ):** Number of failures per unit time, typically **failures per million hours** in ship machinery analysis.
- **Availability (A):** Dimensionless ratio between 0 and 1, often expressed as a percentage.
- **MTBSA / MLDT (Mean Logistic Downtime):** For naval vessels, includes administrative and supply delays.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Maintenance Strategies

Maintenance in a shipyard and onboard a ship is classified into the following five hierarchical strategies, listed in increasing order of engineering sophistication:

1. **Breakdown / Corrective Maintenance (CM)** – Action taken only after a failure has occurred. The system is restored to a working state. The motto is *"if it ain't broke, don't fix it."*
2. **Scheduled / Preventive Maintenance (PM)** – Time-based or usage-based interventions performed regardless of equipment condition (e.g., monthly, every 5,000 running hours, every X tonnes of cargo handled).
3. **Predictive / Condition-Based Maintenance (CBM / PdM)** – Maintenance scheduled based on real-time condition monitoring data (vibration analysis, oil debris, thermography, ultrasonic thickness gauging).
4. **Opportunistic Maintenance** – Maintenance piggy-backed onto a planned shutdown. For example, inspecting a boiler during a scheduled dry-dock period.
5. **Design-Out Maintenance** – Permanent improvement at the design stage to eliminate a recurring failure mode (e.g., replacing a copper-nickel pipe with a corrosion-resistant titanium alloy).

> [!NOTE]
> **Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM):** RCM is not a separate "type" of maintenance but rather a *decision-logic framework* (popularized by Nowlan & Heap in the 1978 United Airlines report for the U.S. Department of Defense) used to *select* the most appropriate maintenance type for each failure mode of a system. Modern shipyards apply RCM rigorously for warship and high-value commercial vessel systems.

### 2.2 The Reliability "Bathtub" Curve in Ship Systems

The classic reliability curve plots failure rate $\lambda(t)$ versus time and has three regions:

- **Region I – Infant Mortality (Burn-in):** High early failures due to manufacturing defects, installation errors, or commissioning mistakes. We "burn-in" the equipment to flush out weak components.
- **Region II – Useful Life (Random Failures):** Low, constant failure rate. This is the operational life period governed by Poisson statistics. **$\lambda(t) = \text{constant} = \lambda$**.
- **Region III – Wear-out:** Failure rate rises sharply due to fatigue, corrosion, wear, and degradation. Replacement or overhaul is mandatory before this region.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reliability (exponential) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless | Valid in Region II only |
| 2 | Unreliability / CDF | $F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless | Cumulative failure probability |
| 3 | Failure Rate (constant) | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | per hour | Region II only |
| 4 | MTTF (non-repairable) | $\text{MTTF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | hours | Exponential distribution |
| 5 | Inherent Availability | $A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | dimensionless | Repairable, single-unit |
| 6 | Achieved Availability | $A_a = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MDT}}$ | dimensionless | MDT = Mean Downtime |
| 7 | Operational Availability | $A_o = \dfrac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime} + \text{Downtime}}$ | dimensionless | Real-world |
| 8 | System Reliability (series) | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | dimensionless | All components must work |
| 9 | System Reliability (parallel) | $R_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)$ | dimensionless | At least one must work |
|10 | Mean Downtime | $\text{MDT} = \text{MTTR} + \text{MLDT}$ | hours | MLDT = logistics delay |
|11 | Spare part reorder (EOQ) | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | units | Wilson's formula |
|12 | Downtime cost rate | $C_d = (C_l + C_p) \cdot T_d$ | currency | $C_l$ = loss, $C_p$ = penalty |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Maintenance Analysis in shipbuilding is used to:

- Plan **dry-dock surveys** at optimal intervals (typically 5 years for Special Survey).
- Decide on **classification society survey scope** (Class entry, annual, intermediate, special, continuous).
- Calculate **life-cycle support cost (LCC)** of a naval frigue or commercial tanker.
- Optimize the **PM vs. CM trade-off** for engine rooms.
- Determine **spares holding levels** in port-side bonded stores.
- Justify **condition-monitoring investments** (cost of vibration sensors vs. savings from avoided failures).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Inherent Availability for a Single Repairable System

A single repairable system alternates between an *Up* state (working) of random duration $X$ and a *Down* state (failed, under repair) of random duration $Y$. Over a long observation window, the **mean Up-time** is $\mathbb{E}[X] = \text{MTBF}$ and the **mean Down-time** is $\mathbb{E}[Y] = \text{MTTR}$.

**Step 1:** Define instantaneous availability as the probability the system is operational at time $t$:

$$A(t) = P(\text{system working at time } t)$$

**Step 2:** For a long-run, steady-state (ergodic) repairable system, the time-average availability equals the ensemble-average availability:

$$\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T \mathbf{1}_{\{\text{up}\}}(t) \, dt = \frac{\text{Total Up-time}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}}$$

**Step 3:** In each complete cycle, the total time is $\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}$. Therefore:

$$A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

**Step 4:** Alternative form by dividing numerator and denominator by MTBF:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_i &= \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF}}} \\[6pt]
    &= \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{1}{\lambda \cdot \text{MTTR}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows that availability depends only on the dimensionless product $\lambda \cdot \text{MTTR}$, which is sometimes called the **serviceability ratio** or its inverse the **maintenance ratio**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Reliability Function from the Failure Rate Definition

The failure rate $\lambda(t)$ is defined as the conditional probability of failure in the next infinitesimal interval, given survival to time $t$:

$$\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t \le T < t + \Delta t \mid T \ge t)}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 1:** Express this using the unreliability function $F(t)$ and reliability $R(t)$:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dF(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$, so $\dfrac{dF}{dt} = -\dfrac{dR}{dt}$:

$$\lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** Separate variables:

$$- \lambda(t) \, dt = \frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

**Step 4:** Integrate from $0$ to $t$, using the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\int_0^t \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau &= \int_1^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} \\[4pt]
-\int_0^t \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau &= \ln R(t) \\[4pt]
R(t) &= \exp\!\left(-\int_0^t \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Specialize to the **constant-failure-rate (Region II)** case where $\lambda(\tau) = \lambda$:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

This is the famous **exponential reliability law**, valid for complex ship machinery in its useful life (e.g., diesel engine main block, pumps, switchboards).

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Series System Reliability (Ship as a Chain)

A ship can be idealized as a *series* of $n$ subsystems. The vessel is mission-capable **only if every subsystem works**.

**Step 1:** Define event $A_i$ = "subsystem $i$ is working at time $t$". The vessel works if all $A_i$ occur.

**Step 2:** If subsystems fail **independently** (a common but important assumption in KTU problems):

$$R_{\text{ship}}(t) = P(A_1 \cap A_2 \cap \cdots \cap A_n)$$

**Step 3:** Apply the multiplication rule for independent events:

$$R_{\text{ship}}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(A_i) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$$

**Step 4:** For identical exponential subsystems with failure rate $\lambda_i = \lambda$:

$$R_{\text{ship}}(t) = e^{-n \lambda t} = e^{-\lambda_{\text{system}} t}, \quad \text{where } \lambda_{\text{system}} = n\lambda$$

This shows that adding more components in series **decreases** system reliability — a key insight when designing redundant (parallel) ship systems.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Maintenance Type

| Failure Mode Characteristic | Most Suitable Maintenance Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Hidden failure, safety-critical (e.g., fire pump) | **Scheduled PM + Functional Test** | Failure is not self-revealing; PM ensures periodic verification. |
| Rapid wear-out, economic consequence high (e.g., cylinder liners) | **Scheduled Overhaul** | Time-based replacement is cheaper than monitoring. |
| Slowly developing fault, costly to inspect visually (e.g., crankshaft bearings) | **Predictive / CBM (vibration, wear-debris analysis)** | Condition-based intervention is more economical. |
| Random failure, low cost, non-critical (e.g., cabin light bulb) | **Corrective / Breakdown Maintenance** | No point in PM; replacement is cheap. |
| Multiple failure modes, complex system (e.g., main engine, steering gear) | **RCM Analysis** | A formal decision tree selects the optimal mix. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** In a 14-mark question on Maintenance Analysis, examiners frequently give a block diagram of a ship system and ask candidates to (a) draw a reliability block diagram, (b) compute system reliability or availability, and (c) recommend a maintenance strategy. Master the formulas in the cheat sheet above to score full marks.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Model Solution)

**Question:** A ship's steering gear system has MTBF = 1,200 hours and MTTR = 8 hours. Calculate: (i) Failure rate, (ii) Inherent availability, (iii) Probability of failure-free operation for a 100-hour voyage leg.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) Failure rate } \lambda &= \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}} = \frac{1}{1200} \text{ hr}^{-1} = 8.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ hr}^{-1} \\[6pt]
\text{(ii) Inherent availability } A_i &= \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{1200}{1200 + 8} = \frac{1200}{1208} = 0.9934 \\[6pt]
\text{(iii) Reliability for 100 hr } R(100) &= e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(1/1200) \times 100} = e^{-0.08333} = 0.9200
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, the steering gear is **99.34% available** in the long run, and has a **92.0% chance** of completing a 100-hour voyage without failure.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Maintenance Analysis Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship System Under Study] --> B{Failure Mode Identified?}
    B -- No --> C[Design-Out Maintenance at Next Refit]
    B -- Yes --> D[Severity Classification]
    D --> D1[Hidden Safety Critical]
    D --> D2[Evident Safety Critical]
    D --> D3[Operational Economic]
    D --> D4[Non-Critical]
    D1 --> E1[Scheduled PM plus Functional Test]
    D2 --> E2[Scheduled PM plus Redundancy]
    D3 --> E3[Predictive CBM with Threshold Limits]
    D4 --> E4[Corrective Run to Failure]
    E1 --> F[Collect Reliability Data]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Compute MTBF MTTR and Availability]
    G --> H[Update PMS Database]
    H --> I[Continuous Improvement Loop]
    I --> A
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Reliability Block Diagram of a Ship's Vital Auxiliary System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Main Power Source Bus] --> P1[Pump 1]
    M --> P2[Pump 2]
    P1 --> C[Common Discharge Header]
    P2 --> C
    C --> V[Valve Block]
    V --> L[Sea or Deck Load]
    note1["Pump 1 and Pump 2 are in parallel redundancy.
    Total system reliability is much higher
    than each individual pump."]
    P1 -. commentary .-> note1
    P2 -. commentary .-> note1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** The pumps $P_1$ and $P_2$ are in *parallel* because the load is served as long as **at least one** pump is working. The other elements (bus, header, valve block, load) are in *series* with the parallel combination. This hybrid reliability-block-diagram (RBD) is the standard structure for ship fire pumps, lubricating-oil pumps, and steering gear power units.

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Maintenance Cost vs. Interval

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Maintenance Interval Axis] --> Y[Total Cost per Unit Time]
    Y --> Y1[Very Short Interval = Excessive PM Cost]
    Y --> Y2[Very Long Interval = High Breakdown Cost]
    Y --> Y3[Optimum Interval = Minimum Total Cost]
    C1[PM Cost] --> C2
    C2[Breakdown Loss Cost] --> C3
    C3[Total Cost equals PM plus Breakdown] --> Y
```

> [!TIP]
> The **optimum PM interval** in ship machinery is found by setting $\dfrac{d}{dT}(C_{PM}(T) + C_{CM}(T)) = 0$, where $T$ is the maintenance interval. This is a classic KTU derivation question worth 7 marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
Define the term *Mean Time Between Failures* (MTBF) as applied to ship machinery. How is it related to the failure rate $\lambda$ in the constant-failure-rate region of the bathtub curve?

**Model Answer:**
MTBF is the arithmetic mean of the operational time intervals between consecutive failures of a repairable ship system or equipment, measured over a sufficiently long observation period. In the constant-failure-rate (Region II – useful life) region of the bathtub curve, the exponential law holds, and the relationship is:

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

Hence, a system with a failure rate of $10^{-4}$ per hour has an MTBF of $10{,}000$ hours.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
Distinguish between *Preventive Maintenance* and *Predictive (Condition-Based) Maintenance* in the context of a ship's diesel engine.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Preventive Maintenance (PM) | Predictive Maintenance (CBM) |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Calendar time or usage hours (e.g., every 4,000 h) | Real-time condition indicators (vibration, oil analysis, temperature) |
| Philosophy | "Replace on schedule" | "Replace when condition deteriorates to threshold" |
| Cost profile | Predictable, fixed | Variable, often lower over life |
| Data requirement | None on actual health | Continuous sensor data and trending |
| Best suited to | Wear-out-dominant items | Slowly developing faults |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** A ship's main propulsion diesel engine is modelled as a series reliability block diagram consisting of four sub-assemblies with the following constant failure rates: $\lambda_1 = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ /h (turbocharger), $\lambda_2 = 3 \times 10^{-5}$ /h (fuel injection system), $\lambda_3 = 4 \times 10^{-5}$ /h (lub-oil pump set), $\lambda_4 = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ /h (sea-water cooling). For a 500-hour mission, determine: **(7 marks)**
   1. The system failure rate and system MTBF.
   2. The reliability of the engine for the 500-hour mission.
   3. The unreliability (probability of at least one failure) over the same mission.

**(b)** Explain with suitable sketch the **Bathtub Curve** of failure rate and discuss the maintenance strategy most appropriate for each region. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — System failure rate** (series, additivity of constant rates):

$$\lambda_{\text{sys}} = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3 + \lambda_4 = (5+3+4+2)\times 10^{-5} = 14 \times 10^{-5} \text{ hr}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — System MTBF:**

$$\text{MTBF}_{\text{sys}} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{\text{sys}}} = \frac{1}{14 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 7142.86 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3 — Reliability for 500 hours** (exponential law):

$$R(500) = e^{-\lambda_{\text{sys}} \times 500} = e^{-14 \times 10^{-5} \times 500} = e^{-0.07} \approx 0.9324$$

**Step 4 — Unreliability:**

$$F(500) = 1 - R(500) = 1 - 0.9324 = 0.0676 \;(\text{or } 6.76\%)$$

---

**Model Solution (b):**

The **Bathtub Curve** plots hazard (failure) rate $\lambda(t)$ on the y-axis and time $t$ on the x-axis. It has three distinct regions:

- **Region I – Infant Mortality / Burn-in:** Failure rate is high but decreasing. Caused by manufacturing defects, sub-standard materials, and commissioning errors. **Strategy:** Aggressive **burn-in** testing at the shipyard, equipment debugging, and early-life replacement of defective batches.

- **Region II – Useful Life / Random Failures:** Failure rate is low and constant. Failures are random, Poisson-distributed, and unpredictable. **Strategy:** **Corrective (run-to-failure) maintenance** for non-critical items combined with **redundancy** for safety-critical items, and a scheduled **condition-monitoring** programme to detect incipient failures.

- **Region III – Wear-out:** Failure rate rises sharply due to fatigue, corrosion, wear, and aging. **Strategy:** **Scheduled preventive maintenance / overhaul** before entering this region. For example, diesel engine cylinder liners are replaced at fixed running-hour intervals, and pumps are overhauled before the wear-out knee.

A simple schematic using Mermaid is given below:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Region I Infant Mortality] --> R2[Region II Useful Life Constant Lambda]
    R2 --> R3[Region III Wear Out]
    S1[Strategy Burn In Debug] -->|applies to| R1
    S2[Strategy Corrective plus CBM plus Redundancy] -->|applies to| R2
    S3[Strategy Scheduled PM plus Overhaul] -->|applies to| R3
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Define *Inherent Availability* and *Operational Availability* of a shipboard system. A generator set has MTBF = 2,000 h and MTTR = 12 h. If the mean logistics and administrative delay (MLDT) is 8 h per failure, calculate: **(7 marks)**
   1. Inherent availability.
   2. Achieved availability.
   3. Operational availability if the system is scheduled to operate 200 h per month.

**(b)** What is **Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)**? List and briefly explain the seven classical questions of the RCM decision logic. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Inherent Availability** (only corrective downtime considered):

$$A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{2000}{2000 + 12} = \frac{2000}{2012} \approx 0.9940 \;(99.40\%)$$

**Step 2 — Achieved Availability** (corrective + logistics downtime):

$$A_a = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MDT}} = \frac{2000}{2000 + (12+8)} = \frac{2000}{2020} \approx 0.9901 \;(99.01\%)$$

**Step 3 — Operational Availability** (uses scheduled operating time):

$$A_o = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Uptime} + \text{Downtime}} = \frac{200}{200 + 20} = 0.9091 \;(90.91\%)$$

where monthly downtime = 20 h (assumed as 1 month of operation data; the exact fraction can also be derived from $1 - A_o$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistake in Availability Questions:** Students often compute **Inherent Availability** and label it as **Operational Availability**. They are *not* the same. $A_i$ excludes logistics and preventive downtime; $A_o$ includes *all* downtime including supply delays and administrative non-availability. Always read the question carefully and use the correct formula.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)** is a systematic, logic-tree-based engineering methodology used to determine the most cost-effective and technically appropriate maintenance requirement for each physical asset in a system, by preserving its function rather than preserving the hardware itself.

The seven classical RCM questions (per SAE JA1011 / Nowlan & Heap) are:

1. **What are the functions and associated desired performance standards of the asset in its present operating context?**
2. **In what ways can it fail to fulfil its functions?** (functional failures)
3. **What is the cause of each functional failure?** (failure modes)
4. **What happens when each failure occurs?** (failure effects)
5. **In what way does each failure matter?** (failure consequences: hidden, safety, environmental, operational, economic)
6. **What can be done to prevent or predict each failure?**
7. **What should be done if a suitable preventive task cannot be found?** (default action: redesign or run-to-failure)

RCM is widely used for naval platforms (e.g., Indian Navy frigates, U.S. Navy CVN carriers) and high-value commercial systems like offshore rigs and LNG carriers.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Maintenance Analysis questions:**
> 1. **Confusing MTBF with MTTF** – MTBF is for *repairable* systems; MTTF is for *non-repairable* items. The ship is a repairable system, so use MTBF.
> 2. **Using the exponential law outside Region II** – $R(t)=e^{-\lambda t}$ is **invalid** during infant mortality and wear-out. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if you apply it uncritically.
> 3. **Misidentifying parallel vs. series** – In a ship system, pumps and generators are often in *parallel redundancy*, but piping, valves, and switchboards are in *series*. Wrong identification gives wrong reliability.
> 4. **Forgetting units in failure rate** – Always state *per hour* or *per million hours*; failure rate is a frequency.
> 5. **Not interpreting the question on "which maintenance type"** – Examiners expect a *justified recommendation* linked to the failure mode, not just a definition.
> 6. **In a 14-mark question, skipping the diagram** – A reliability block diagram, bathtub curve, or decision tree is worth 2–3 marks by itself.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Maintenance Analysis** integrates reliability, maintainability, and availability engineering with shipyard economics and classification rules.
- **Five maintenance types:** Breakdown (CM), Preventive (PM), Predictive (CBM), Opportunistic, Design-Out.
- **Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)** is a *decision framework*, not a maintenance type.
- **Master the four core formulas:**
  - $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$
  - $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$
  - $A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR}}$
  - $R_s = \prod R_i$ (series), $R_p = 1 - \prod(1-R_i)$ (parallel)
- **Three availabilities to distinguish:** $A_i$ (inherent), $A_a$ (achieved), $A_o$ (operational).
- **The Bathtub Curve** has Infant Mortality, Useful Life, and Wear-out regions, each with its own maintenance strategy.
- **Series systems degrade** as you add components; **parallel systems improve** reliability dramatically — fundamental to ship system design.
- **MTBF vs MTTF:** Repairable vs non-repairable; ships are almost always repairable.
- **PM interval optimization** is found by minimizing total cost per unit time: $C_T(T) = C_{PM}(T) + C_{CM}(T)$.
- **Classification society surveys** (IACS members) enforce PM: Annual, Intermediate, Special, Continuous.
- **Dry-docking** is a classic *opportunistic maintenance* event — co-ordinate hull, machinery, electrical, and navigation work into a single yard stay to minimize downtime.
- **Spares optimization** uses Wilson's Economic Order Quantity: $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$.
- **Always draw a diagram** in 14-mark answers — RBD, Bathtub, or decision tree.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production Standards in Several Parts of the Ship Production Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST633 - SHIP PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Production Standards in Several Parts of the Ship Production Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Production Standard** is a scientifically established quantitative benchmark that prescribes the *allowable time, effort, materials, and quality criteria* required to perform a specific unit of work under defined operating conditions in ship construction. It forms the *baseline reference* against which actual performance is measured, productivity is calculated, and planning/cost estimates are formulated.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a **Production Standard** in shipbuilding is essentially a *time-and-effort norm* (often expressed as **Standard Man-Hours per ton of steel**, or **SMH/ton**) that governs every discrete activity — from plate nesting and oxy-fuel cutting to block erection, welding, pipe-fitting, blasting, and painting.

### 1.2 The Four Pillars of a Ship Production Standard

| Pillar | What it Prescribes | Unit of Measure |
|---|---|---|
| **Time Standard** | Allowable duration for a task | Minutes / Man-Hours (MH) |
| **Quality Standard** | Acceptance criteria (weld grade, surface roughness) | Class-society rules, ISO codes |
| **Material Standard** | Consumption allowance + wastage norm | kg/ton, m²/m², % waste |
| **Method Standard** | The prescribed sequence & tooling | Work Instruction Sheet |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Recipe Card for a Ship"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a production standard as a "cookbook recipe" for shipbuilding.**
> A chef's recipe says: "Use 200 g of flour, knead for 8 minutes, bake at 180 °C for 25 minutes." If the chef deviates, the dish fails. Similarly, a production standard says: "For a 12 mm plate, cut at 700 mm/min, bevel at 35°, weld using 1.2 mm wire at 24 V, deposit 1.8 kg/hour." Deviation leads to schedule slippage, cost overrun, or non-conformance to the *Classification Society* (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, IRS).

**Where standards come from:**
- **Work Measurement** (time study, synthesis from standard data, PMTS — Predetermined Motion Time Systems like MTM, MOST).
- **Historical records** of previous ships of similar class.
- **Manufacturer's data** for machinery and equipment output rates.
- **Classification society** and **ISO/ASME codes** for quality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A standard is *not* a maximum nor a minimum — it represents the time a **qualified worker**, working at a **normal pace**, following the **prescribed method**, on a **typical ship**, should take. This is the **100% performance rating** concept.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Ship Production Standards Are Unique

Unlike batch production (cars, electronics), shipbuilding is a **one-of-a-kind, large-project, heavy-engineering** activity. Therefore:

1. **Standards are parametric** — they are expressed *per ton of steel (DWT)*, *per metre of weld*, or *per square metre of surface*, not per piece.
2. **They evolve with yard maturity** — Japanese and Korean yards have SMH/ton as low as **15–25 hours**, while many yards target **30–50 hours**.
3. **They are yard-specific but benchmarked** — yards compare their *Total Standard Man-Hours (TSMH)* with international benchmarks (e.g., OECD WP6 reports).

## 2.2 The Standard Man-Hour (SMH) Framework

The single most important metric in the entire ship production process is the **Standard Man-Hour per Compensated Gross Tonnage (CGT) or per ton of steel**.

$$
\text{Specific Man-Hours} = \frac{\text{Total Productive Man-Hours (TPMH)}}{\text{Steel Weight Outfitted (tons)}}
$$

> **Units:** *hours/ton* (often written **h/t** or **SMH/t**)

### 2.3 Breakdown of SMH by Production Phase

| Phase of Ship Production | Typical SMH/t Share | KTU Exam Focus |
|---|---|---|
| Steel preparation (cutting, blasting, priming) | **8 – 12 %** | Plate nesting, cutting standards |
| Sub-assembly & block fabrication | **25 – 35 %** | Tonnage per block, fit-up allowances |
| Welding (all processes combined) | **30 – 40 %** | Deposition rate, arc-on time, bevel standards |
| Surface treatment & painting | **5 – 8 %** | m²/hr coverage rate |
| Outfitting (machinery, pipe, electrical) | **20 – 30 %** | Piping man-hours per metre |
| Erection, launching, dock trials | **5 – 10 %** | Erected tonnage per day, alignment tolerances |

## 2.4 Work-Measurement Techniques Used to Build the Standard

> [!NOTE]
> A standard is only as good as the method used to derive it. KTU 2024 expects students to know **four** methods:

1. **Direct Time Study (Stopwatch Method)** — observes an element, rates the worker (Performance Rating × Normal Time), and adds allowances.

   $$
   \text{Standard Time} = \text{Observed Time} \times \text{Rating Factor} \times (1 + \text{Allowance Fraction})
   $$

2. **Standard Data (SYNTHESIS)** — tabulates standard times for basic *elements* (e.g., "cut 1 m of 12 mm plate"). New jobs are estimated by *summing* the element data.

3. **Predetermined Motion Time Systems (PMTS)** — uses published motion-time tables:
   - **MTM** (Methods-Time Measurement)
   - **MOST** (Maynard Operation Sequence Technique)
   - **WTDS / WOFAC**

4. **Historical / Standard Data Banks** — averaged from completed ships; useful in early estimating.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Unit | Purpose in Ship Production |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Standard Time} = OT \times R \times (1 + A)$ | OT = observed time; R = rating (decimal); A = allowance (decimal) | Establish time standard from stopwatch study |
| 2 | $\text{Normal Time} = OT \times R$ | minutes | Worker-rated time |
| 3 | $\text{Specific Man-Hours} = \dfrac{\text{TPMH}}{\text{Steel (t)}}$ | h/t | Yard productivity KPI |
| 4 | $\text{Welding Deposition Rate} = \dfrac{W \times 60}{T}$ | kg/hr; W = wire mass used (kg), T = arc time (min) | Setting welding standard |
| 5 | $\text{Arc Time} = \dfrac{\text{Weld Volume}}{\text{Cross-Section} \times \text{Travel Speed}}$ | minutes | Converting joint geometry into time |
| 6 | $\text{Cutting Length} = \dfrac{\text{Plate Area}}{\text{Plate Length}}$ or via nesting CAD | metres | Plate-utilisation standard |
| 7 | $\text{Painting Coverage} = \dfrac{\text{Area}}{\text{DFT} \times \text{Solid \%}} \times 10$ | m²/L | Paint consumption standard |
| 8 | $\text{Block Erection Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Block Tonnage}}{\text{Crane Days}}$ | t/day | Berth / crane utilisation |
| 9 | $\text{Piping Man-Hour} = k \times D^{1.5} \times L$ | MH; D = dia (in), L = length (m) | Outfitting standard (empirical) |
| 10 | $\text{Allowance} = \text{Relief} + \text{Personal} + \text{Contingency}$ | % of normal time | Standard must include non-productive time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rounding convention for KTU papers:** ratings are always given in **decimals** (e.g., 1.10 means 110 % — a fast worker). Allowances are typically **15 – 25 %** in shipbuilding (heavy, hot, confined work).

## 2.6 Why Standards Matter in Real Shipyards

- **Bid estimation:** Yards quote price by multiplying SMH × labour rate + material cost.
- **Capacity planning:** Total man-hours required ÷ workforce size = project duration.
- **Performance control:** Earned hours vs. actual hours → efficiency %.
- **Learning curve forecasting:** As the 2nd, 3rd, 4th ship of a class is built, the *standard* is itself **revised downward** using the **learning-curve equation** (Wright's model, 80–90 % slope).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Standard Time from a Time Study

**Problem context (KTU typical):**
A stopwatch study on a welder performing a *fillet weld on 8 mm plate* gave the following observed elemental times (in minutes) and performance ratings. The allowance is 20 %. Compute the **standard time per metre of weld**.

| Element | Observed Time (min) | Rating |
|---|---|---|
| Pick up & position plate | 0.45 | 1.00 |
| Strike arc, tack | 0.30 | 1.10 |
| Weld 1 m of fillet (6 mm leg) | 4.20 | 1.05 |
| Deslag, inspect | 0.60 | 1.00 |
| Set down, mark complete | 0.25 | 1.00 |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute Normal Time (NT) for each element:**

$$
NT_i = OT_i \times R_i
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
NT_1 &= 0.45 \times 1.00 = 0.450 \ \text{min} \\
NT_2 &= 0.30 \times 1.10 = 0.330 \ \text{min} \\
NT_3 &= 4.20 \times 1.05 = 4.410 \ \text{min} \\
NT_4 &= 0.60 \times 1.00 = 0.600 \ \text{min} \\
NT_5 &= 0.25 \times 1.00 = 0.250 \ \text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum the Normal Times:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma NT &= 0.450 + 0.330 + 4.410 + 0.600 + 0.250 \\
&= 6.040 \ \text{min/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Allowance (A = 20 % = 0.20):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Standard Time (ST)} &= \Sigma NT \times (1 + A) \\
&= 6.040 \times (1 + 0.20) \\
&= 6.040 \times 1.20 \\
&= 7.248 \ \text{min/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to a worker-friendly unit (sec/m) for shop display:**

$$
ST = 7.248 \times 60 = 434.88 \ \text{s/m} \approx 435 \ \text{s/m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A qualified welder should complete **1 metre of 6 mm-leg fillet weld in 7.25 minutes** (rated) under the yard's allowance policy. If actual time is 6.0 min, the worker is **120.8 % efficient** (i.e., producing 20.8 % above standard).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Welding Deposition Standard

**Context:** A shipyard uses **FCAW (Flux-Cored Arc Welding)** with **1.2 mm wire** for block welding. The standard joint is a *single-V butt weld*, 10 mm plate, 60° included angle, root gap 2 mm.

### Step A — Compute the cross-sectional area of the weld metal

The V-groove area (per metre length) is:

$$
A = \frac{1}{2} \times t \times \tan\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) + g
$$

where $t$ = plate thickness (10 mm), $\theta$ = included angle (60°), $g$ = root gap (2 mm).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(30°) &= 0.5774 \\
A &= 0.5 \times 10 \times 0.5774 + 2 \\
&= 2.887 + 2 \\
&= 4.887 \ \text{mm}^2 \text{ per mm of weld}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Per metre of weld length:**

$$
A_{1m} = 4.887 \times 1000 = 4887 \ \text{mm}^2/\text{m} = 4.887 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^2/\text{m}
$$

### Step B — Compute the volume of weld metal per metre

$$
V = A_{1m} \times 1 \ \text{m} = 4.887 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^3/\text{m}
$$

### Step C — Convert volume to mass of weld metal

Using steel density $\rho = 7850 \ \text{kg/m}^3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= V \times \rho \\
&= 4.887 \times 10^{-3} \times 7850 \\
&= 38.36 \ \text{kg/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step D — Apply a deposition efficiency of 85 % (typical for FCAW)

$$
\text{Wire Consumed} = \frac{m}{\eta} = \frac{38.36}{0.85} = 45.13 \ \text{kg/m}
$$

### Step E — Compute the deposition time

If the welder's standard deposition rate is **3.5 kg/hr** (rated, with arc-on):

$$
T = \frac{38.36}{3.5} = 10.96 \ \text{hr/m} = 657.6 \ \text{min/m}
$$

Add 25 % for set-up, tacking, deslag, interpass cleaning:

$$
ST_{\text{weld}} = 657.6 \times 1.25 = 822 \ \text{min/m} \approx 13.7 \ \text{hr/m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **one of the most useful standard calculations in shipbuilding**, because every kilo of weld metal translates directly into both *labour cost* and *consumable cost* on the BOQ (Bill of Quantities).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Specific Man-Hour (SMH) for a Complete Vessel

**Data for a Bulk Carrier of 35,000 DWT, steel weight 6,800 t:**

| Department | Productive MH (h) |
|---|---|
| Steel preparation | 68,000 |
| Welding & fabrication | 220,000 |
| Block assembly | 95,000 |
| Painting & blasting | 38,000 |
| Outfitting (mach + pipe + elec) | 150,000 |
| Erection & trials | 32,000 |
| **Total** | **603,000** |

### Step-by-Step

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Specific MH} &= \frac{603{,}000}{6{,}800} \\
&= 88.68 \ \text{h/t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Benchmarking:**

| Yard Type | Typical SMH/t |
|---|---|
| World-class (Japan/Korea) | **15 – 25** |
| European advanced | **25 – 40** |
| Indian mid-tier | **60 – 100** |
| First-time / learning yard | **120 – 200** |

> Hence, the 88.7 h/t yard is at the *upper edge of an Indian mid-tier yard* — there is **scope to reduce to 50 h/t by adopting learning-curve gains** (e.g., 90 % slope over 5 sister ships).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Learning-Curve Adjustment of the Standard

Wright's learning-curve equation (cumulative average basis):

$$
Y_n = Y_1 \times n^{b}
$$

where $Y_n$ = standard for the $n$-th ship, $Y_1$ = first-ship standard, $b = \log_2(\text{slope}) = \log_2(0.90) = -0.152$.

For the 4th ship of a class with $Y_1 = 88.7$ h/t and 90 % slope:

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \frac{\ln(0.90)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{-0.1054}{0.6931} = -0.1520 \\
Y_4 &= 88.7 \times 4^{-0.152} \\
&= 88.7 \times \frac{1}{4^{0.152}} \\
&= 88.7 \times \frac{1}{1.2365} \\
&= 71.73 \ \text{h/t}
\end{aligned}
$$

> So the **revised production standard** for the 4th ship is **~71.7 h/t** — a 19 % reduction achieved through workforce learning.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — Standard-Setting Workflow in a Shipyard

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Specification and GA Drawings] --> B[Process Breakdown Structure PBS]
    B --> C[Element Identification per Activity]
    C --> D{Method of Work Measurement}
    D --> D1[Direct Time Study Stopwatch]
    D --> D2[Standard Data Synthesis]
    D --> D3[PMTS MOST or MTM]
    D --> D4[Historical Data Banks]
    D1 --> E[Observed Time OT and Rating R]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Compute Normal Time NT equals OT times R]
    F --> G[Add Allowances Personal Relief Contingency]
    G --> H[Publish Standard Time ST per Element]
    H --> I[Aggregate into Activity Standards]
    I --> J[Roll Up to Department SMH per ton]
    J --> K[Set Specific Man Hour SMH per ton for the Vessel]
    K --> L[Issue to Planning Costing and Shop Floor]
    L --> M[Monitor Earned vs Actual Hours]
    M --> N{Performance within plus minus 10 percent}
    N -->|No| O[Review and Revise Standard]
    N -->|Yes| P[Standard Validated and Locked]
    O --> C
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Production-Standard Allocation Across the Ship-Building Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Steel Stage 8 to 12 percent SMH]
        A1[Plate Nesting]
        A2[Oxy Fuel or Plasma Cutting]
        A3[Shot Blasting and Priming]
    end
    subgraph B[Fabrication Stage 25 to 35 percent SMH]
        B1[Plate Bending and Rolling]
        B2[Sub Assembly of Panels]
        B3[Block Assembly in Shop]
    end
    subgraph C[Welding Stage 30 to 40 percent SMH]
        C1[SMAW for Thick Plate]
        C2[FCAW for Panel Welds]
        C3[SAW for Long Seams]
    end
    subgraph D[Outfitting Stage 20 to 30 percent SMH]
        D1[Machinery Installation]
        D2[Piping Fabrication and Laying]
        D3[Electrical Cable Tray and Termination]
    end
    subgraph E[Erection Stage 5 to 10 percent SMH]
        E1[Block Lift by Crane]
        E2[Dock or Slip Alignment]
        E3[Launching and Sea Trials]
    end
    A --> B --> C --> D --> E
    A --> E
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Feedback Loop: Standard vs Actual Performance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Published Standard SMH per ton] --> P[Planning allocates Earned Hours]
    P --> W[Worker executes and clocks Actual Hours]
    W --> V{Efficiency equals Earned by Actual times 100}
    V -->|< 90 percent| X1[Investigate root cause]
    V -->|90 to 110 percent| X2[Normal accept]
    V -->|> 110 percent| X3[Review standard for looseness]
    X1 --> R[Revise method and retrain]
    R --> S
    X3 --> S
    X2 --> S
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams matter for KTU 2024:** The 14-mark questions frequently ask students to *"draw and explain the work-measurement process"* or *"describe the SMH distribution in a modern shipyard"*. These three Mermaid blocks cover the **three most-asked topics** in this Module.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define "Production Standard" in the context of ship production management. Mention any two methods used to set it. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
A *Production Standard* in shipbuilding is a pre-determined time-and-effort norm (usually expressed as **Standard Man-Hours per ton of steel**) that a qualified worker should consume to perform a defined task under a prescribed method and working conditions.

*Two methods of setting:*

1. **Direct Time Study** — stopwatch observation of an experienced worker, with **Performance Rating** and **Allowances** applied.
2. **Predetermined Motion Time Systems (PMTS)** such as **MTM** or **MOST**, where the basic human motions are assigned fixed time values from published tables.

> [!NOTE]
> *Other acceptable methods: Standard-Data Synthesis, Historical Data Analysis, Work-Sampling.*

### Q2. Distinguish between "Normal Time" and "Standard Time". Why is an allowance added? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Normal Time (NT) | Standard Time (ST) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Observed time corrected for worker's pace rating | Normal time plus allowances for non-productive needs |
| Formula | $NT = OT \times R$ | $ST = NT \times (1 + A)$ |
| Excludes fatigue/breaks | Yes | No |
| Used for | Engineering analysis | Planning, costing, performance evaluation |

*Why allowance is added:* No human can work at rated pace 100 % of the shift. An **allowance** (typically 15–25 % in shipyards) compensates for *personal needs, rest, contingency* (waiting for crane, gas check, supervisor's inspection) and unavoidable *relief fatigue* (working overhead, in heat, in confined spaces).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

> **(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the **step-by-step procedure** of conducting a *stopwatch time study* to establish a welding standard in a shipyard. List the formulae used. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A shipyard measured the following elements for a *pipe-flange bolting operation*. The time-study observer's rating for each element is given. Allowance = 22 %. Calculate the **Standard Time** per joint. **(7 marks)**

| Element | OT (min) | Rating |
|---|---|---|
| Pick up gasket & align | 0.80 | 0.95 |
| Place gasket | 0.45 | 1.00 |
| Insert 8 bolts by hand | 1.20 | 1.05 |
| Tighten with impact wrench | 2.40 | 1.10 |
| Visual check & mark | 0.50 | 0.90 |

#### Model Solution

**(a) Stopwatch time-study procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Define the task** — e.g., "weld 1 m of 6 mm fillet on 8 mm plate".
2. **Select the worker** — qualified, experienced, average representative.
3. **Brief the worker** — explain purpose; the standard being set is *not* for punishment.
4. **Record the method** — write the prescribed procedure (WPS) on the study sheet.
5. **Observe with stopwatch** — record *elemental* times (continous method, snap-back, or cumulative).
6. **Rate the worker's pace** — apply Performance Rating $R$ against the "normal" 100 %.
7. **Compute Normal Time** for each element: $NT_i = OT_i \times R_i$.
8. **Sum** to get total normal time.
9. **Apply allowance factor** $A$: $ST = \Sigma NT \times (1 + A)$.
10. **Convert, display, and publish** as a standard on the shop floor (often in seconds per metre).
11. **Review periodically** — re-validate after 6–12 months or on new vessel class.

**Formulae used:**

$$
NT = OT \times R
$$

$$
ST = \Sigma NT \times (1 + A)
$$

**(b) Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Normal Time per element:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NT_1 &= 0.80 \times 0.95 = 0.760 \ \text{min} \\
NT_2 &= 0.45 \times 1.00 = 0.450 \ \text{min} \\
NT_3 &= 1.20 \times 1.05 = 1.260 \ \text{min} \\
NT_4 &= 2.40 \times 1.10 = 2.640 \ \text{min} \\
NT_5 &= 0.50 \times 0.90 = 0.450 \ \text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Normal Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma NT &= 0.760 + 0.450 + 1.260 + 2.640 + 0.450 \\
&= 5.560 \ \text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Standard Time with 22 % allowance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ST &= 5.560 \times (1 + 0.22) \\
&= 5.560 \times 1.22 \\
&= 6.7832 \ \text{min per joint} \\
&\approx 6.78 \ \text{min/joint}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Standard time ≈ **6.78 minutes per pipe-flange joint** (or 407 seconds/joint).

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

> **(a)** Explain the **Specific Man-Hour (SMH) concept**. How is it distributed across the major stages of ship construction? Give typical percentage shares and the yard-benchmark ranges. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A Panamax bulk carrier has **8,200 t** of outfitting-weight steel. The yard's standard man-hour data is as follows. Calculate the **Specific Man-Hour per ton** and **total productive hours** required. Comment on the yard's productivity position. **(7 marks)**

| Department | Standard MH (planned) |
|---|---|
| Steel prep & cutting | 95,000 |
| Welding (all types) | 245,000 |
| Block & sub-assembly | 140,000 |
| Painting & blasting | 52,000 |
| Outfitting (mach, pipe, elec) | 188,000 |
| Erection, launching, trials | 44,000 |
| **Total** | **764,000** |

#### Model Solution

**(a) SMH concept & distribution (7 marks):**

The **Specific Man-Hour (SMH)** is the *total productive man-hours consumed per ton of steel (outfitted weight)*. It is the most widely used yard-productivity KPI.

$$
\text{SMH} = \frac{\Sigma \text{Productive MH (all depts)}}{\text{Steel Weight Outfitted (t)}}
$$

*Distribution (typical, KTU-referenced ranges):*

| Stage | SMH Share | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Steel preparation | 8 – 12 % | Cutting, blasting, priming |
| Sub-assembly & block fabrication | 25 – 35 % | Panel → unit → block |
| Welding (combined) | 30 – 40 % | SMAW + FCAW + SAW |
| Surface treatment & paint | 5 – 8 % | Blasting, shop & dock |
| Outfitting | 20 – 30 % | Pipe, machinery, electric |
| Erection, launching, trials | 5 – 10 % | Block-lift, alignment, sea trial |

*Yard benchmark SMH per ton of steel:*

- World-class (S. Korea, Japan): **15 – 25 h/t**
- European advanced: **25 – 40 h/t**
- Indian mid-tier: **60 – 100 h/t**
- New / learning yard: **120 – 200 h/t**

**(b) Numerical computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Total Productive MH (given):**

$$
\Sigma MH = 764{,}000 \ \text{h}
$$

**Step 2 — Specific Man-Hour per ton:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SMH &= \frac{764{,}000}{8{,}200} \\
&= 93.17 \ \text{h/t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Department-wise SMH share:**

| Department | SMH share (h/t) | % of total |
|---|---|---|
| Steel prep | 11.59 | 12.4 % |
| Welding | 29.88 | 32.1 % |
| Block assembly | 17.07 | 18.3 % |
| Painting | 6.34 | 6.8 % |
| Outfitting | 22.93 | 24.6 % |
| Erection | 5.37 | 5.8 % |
| **Total** | **93.17** | **100 %** |

**Step 4 — Comment on productivity:**

- The yard's **93.2 h/t** places it at the **upper edge of the Indian mid-tier band** (60–100 h/t).
- *Welding* (32 %) is on the **high side** of the typical 30–40 % band — scope exists for mechanised SAW and multi-arc systems.
- *Outfitting* (24.6 %) is **healthy** — indicates a reasonable pre-outfitting strategy.
- To reach the **world-class 30 h/t** mark, the yard must invest in *block-joining automation*, *welding robotics*, and apply the **learning-curve model (90 % slope)** to sister ships.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall / Valuation Warning:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse *Compensated Gross Tonnage (CGT)* with *Deadweight (DWT)* or *Light Ship Weight*. SMH is *always* per ton of **outfitted steel weight**, not per DWT.
> - Forgetting the **allowance factor** is the single most common 2-mark deduction. Always convert OT → NT → ST in that order.
> - Rating factor must be a **decimal** (1.10), not a percentage — writing "110" without context loses 0.5 mark.
> - In welding problems, students often forget the **deposition efficiency** (η ≈ 0.85 for FCAW, 0.95 for SAW). Examiner specifically tests this.
> - For learning-curve questions, write the **slope-formula** $b = \log_2(s)$ *and* the cumulative-form equation $Y_n = Y_1 n^b$. Skipping one loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read twice before exam):**

- **Production Standard** = scientifically set benchmark (time / quality / material) for one unit of work.
- The most common metric: **Specific Man-Hour per ton of outfitted steel (SMH/t)**.
- **Standard Time formula:** $ST = OT \times R \times (1 + A)$ — remember the order: *observed → normal → standard*.
- **Allowance A** in shipbuilding = **15 – 25 %** (higher than in light engineering) due to heat, height, confined spaces.
- **Methods to set standards:** (i) Stopwatch Time Study, (ii) Standard-Data Synthesis, (iii) PMTS (MTM / MOST), (iv) Historical / Yard Data Banks.
- **Welding deposition rate** $ = \dfrac{\text{wire mass}}{\text{arc time}}$; *FCAW efficiency ≈ 85 %, SAW efficiency ≈ 95 %*.
- **Painting standard** is expressed in **m² per litre** at a given **DFT** (Dry Film Thickness).
- **Block erection rate** is expressed in **tonnes per crane-day**.
- **Piping man-hour empirical** ≈ $k \cdot D^{1.5} \cdot L$ where D is diameter in inches and L is length in metres.
- **SMH distribution** (must memorise): Steel prep ≈ 10 %, Welding ≈ 35 %, Assembly ≈ 25 %, Outfitting ≈ 20 %, Painting ≈ 5 %, Erection ≈ 5 %.
- **World-class benchmark:** **15 – 25 h/t** (Japan, Korea); Indian mid-tier: **60 – 100 h/t**.
- **Learning curve (Wright's model):** $Y_n = Y_1 \cdot n^b$, where $b = \log_2(\text{slope})$. A **90 % slope** means every doubling of cumulative output reduces the unit standard by 10 %.
- **Two-yard standards are yard-specific** — never use Korean standards directly in an Indian yard context without a *conversion / benchmarking study*.
- **Quality standards** in welding refer to **AWS / ASME / ISO 3834 / Class Society** weld-class levels (e.g., Class 1 for high-stress hatch corners).
- **Always** state the *unit* with the answer (h/t, kg/m, m²/L, min/joint).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods of Man-hour Determination
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST633 - SHIP PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Methods of Man-hour Determination

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
