**Man-hour determination** is the systematic engineering methodology used in ship production to quantify the human effort (in hours) required to perform a defined task, operation, or complete an entire shipbuilding/ repair project under standard working conditions. It is the foundational input for production planning, scheduling, costing, capacity planning, and labour control in shipyards.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, man-hour determination is defined as:

> *The process of establishing the standard time required by a qualified worker, working at a normal pace, using the prescribed method, with due allowance for personal needs, rest, and contingencies, to complete a specific unit of work.*

The unit of measurement is the **Man-Hour (MH)**, where:

$$1 \text{ Man-Hour} = 1 \text{ Worker} \times 1 \text{ Hour of effective work}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Man-hour determination bridges **Engineering Design** and **Production Reality**. Without accurate man-hour estimates, neither budgets, schedules, nor productivity improvements can be reliably executed in a shipyard.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are planning a **wedding feast** for 200 guests. Before deciding the menu, venue, or budget, the first thing you estimate is: *"How many total hours of cooking effort do I need?"* You may consider:

- A chef can chop 5 kg of vegetables per hour
- A helper can arrange 20 plates per hour
- The head chef needs 3 hours for the biryani alone

You **add up all these individual times** to get the **total effort in man-hours**. Based on that, you decide whether you need 2 cooks, 5 cooks, or a full catering team. If the wedding is in 3 days, you check if you have *enough hours in those 3 days* to complete the work.

**Man-hour determination in ship production is exactly this**, but applied to:
- Welding a hull plate
- Erecting a bulkhead
- Piping a 100-metre fuel line
- Painting a superstructure

Each task is broken down into smaller measurable elements, time is allocated, and the **total man-hours are summed** to plan the entire ship construction.

## 1.3 Why Man-Hour Determination is Critical in Ship Production

- **Bidding & Cost Estimation:** Shipbuilding contracts are priced based on man-hour estimates.
- **Production Planning:** Determines number of workers, gang sizes, and shift schedules.
- **Scheduling:** Critical Path Method (CPM) requires man-hour input.
- **Productivity Measurement:** Actual MH vs Standard MH indicates efficiency.
- **Cost Control:** Labour cost = Man-hours × Hourly rate.
- **Performance Benchmarking:** Yard-to-yard comparison.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used in KTU Curriculum:**
> - **Shipbuilding productivity** is measured in **Man-Hours per Compensated Gross Tonnage (CGT)**.
> - Modern global benchmark (2024) for new ship construction: **30–60 MH/CGT** (varies by ship type and yard).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Concept: The Building Blocks of Man-Hour Estimation

Every man-hour estimate, regardless of the method used, is built on three fundamental quantities:

### 2.1.1 Basic Time (BT)
The time required by a **qualified worker, working at normal pace**, to perform a task **without any allowance** for rest, personal needs, or contingencies.

### 2.1.2 Normal Time (NT)
The basic time **adjusted by the performance rating** of the observed worker (since not all workers work at "normal" pace during time studies).

### 2.1.3 Standard Time (ST)
The total time that an average qualified worker needs to complete the task, **including all necessary allowances** for fatigue, personal needs, and unavoidable delays.

## 2.2 The Master Equation of Man-Hour Determination

The universally accepted relationship used across all KTU problems is:

$$NT = OT \times PR$$

$$ST = NT \times (1 + A)$$

where:
- $OT$ = **Observed Time** (from time study)
- $PR$ = **Performance Rating** factor (typically **100 = Normal, 120 = Fast, 80 = Slow**)
- $A$ = **Allowance factor** (decimal, e.g., 0.20 for 20%)

Total Man-Hours for a project:

$$MH_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (ST_i \times Q_i)$$

where:
- $ST_i$ = Standard time for operation $i$ (hours per unit)
- $Q_i$ = Quantity of units for operation $i$

## 2.3 Classification of Methods of Man-Hour Determination

In KTU 2024 scheme, the methods are broadly classified into:

| S.No | Category | Methods | Accuracy |
|------|----------|---------|----------|
| 1 | **Direct Measurement** | Stopwatch Time Study, Activity Sampling, Group Timing | High |
| 2 | **Predetermined Standards** | MTM, Work Factor, Basic Motion Timestudy | Very High |
| 3 | **Analytical / Synthetic** | Synthesis from elements, Standard Data | High |
| 4 | **Estimation by Comparison** | Historical Records, Analogy, Regression | Moderate |
| 5 | **Statistical / Mathematical** | Statistical Methods, Work Sampling | Moderate-High |

## 2.4 Detailed Examination of Each Method

### 2.4.1 Direct Time Study (Stopwatch Method)
- A trained industrial engineer observes a worker performing a task with a stopwatch.
- The job is broken into **elements** (smallest measurable sub-operations).
- Time for each element is recorded over multiple cycles.
- **Westinghouse Rating System** is used to adjust observed time to normal time.
- Most accurate for **repetitive, manual operations** (e.g., plate cutting, pipe bending).

### 2.4.2 Standard Data System
- A database of standard times for **basic work elements** is built from past time studies.
- New jobs are analysed by **synthesizing** standard data for each element.
- Best suited for **batch production** environments.
- Significantly reduces time study effort for recurring tasks.

### 2.4.3 Predetermined Motion Time Systems (PMTS)
These are **synthesized** standard time systems where **every basic human motion** (reach, grasp, move, position, release) has a pre-assigned time value.

**(a) Methods Time Measurement (MTM):**
- Developed by H.C. Maynard in 1948.
- Each motion (e.g., Reach 30 cm = 10.5 TMU) has a defined TMU value.
- 1 TMU = 0.00001 hour = 0.0006 minute.

**(b) Work Factor System:**
- Developed by J.H. Quick, W.J. Shea, and R.A. Koehler.
- Considers 8 basic motions: *Transport, Grasp, Pre-position, Assemble, Use, Disassemble, Release, Search.*

**(c) Basic Motion Timestudy (BMT):**
- Developed by Australian Productivity Council.
- Less detailed than MTM, easier to apply in shipyards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** PMTS methods are independent of worker skill, hence **bias-free**, but require extensive training of analysts.

### 2.4.4 Analytical Estimation (Using Standard Time Tables)
- Used when no historical data exists.
- Based on published standard times for shipbuilding operations.
- Examples: Cutting steel plate = X MH/tonne, Welding = Y MH/metre.

### 2.4.5 Comparison / Analogy Method
- Compares the new task with a **similar previously executed task**.
- Man-hours are estimated by proportional scaling.
- Fastest method but **lowest accuracy** — used only in early bid stages.

### 2.4.6 Activity Sampling (Work Sampling)
- Statistical method based on **random observations** of workers.
- Worker activities are classified into categories (e.g., *productive, idle, delayed*).
- Number of observations required:

$$N = \frac{4 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{E^2}$$

where:
- $p$ = proportion of time spent in activity (decimal)
- $E$ = acceptable error (e.g., 0.05 for 5%)
- $N$ = required number of observations

### 2.4.7 Statistical / Regression Methods
- Multiple regression models correlate MH with influencing parameters (e.g., plate thickness, weld length, ship size).
- Equation form:

$$MH = a + b_1 X_1 + b_2 X_2 + \ldots + b_n X_n$$

where $X_i$ are influencing variables.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is a ready-reckoner for solving any KTU 2024 ESE problem on this topic.**

| **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Units** | **Use Case** |
|------------|-------------|-----------|--------------|
| $NT = OT \times \dfrac{PR}{100}$ | Observed time adjusted for skill/effort | minutes | Time study problems |
| $ST = NT \times (1 + A)$ | Includes rest, personal, contingency allowances | minutes | Standard time setting |
| $BT = NT$ | Time at normal pace without allowance | minutes | Costing |
| $MH = N_{workers} \times \text{Hours}$ | Effort measure | MH | Resource planning |
| $MH_{total} = \sum ST_i \times Q_i$ | Project total effort | MH | Project estimation |
| $N = \dfrac{4 p (1-p)}{E^2}$ | Sample size for work sampling | observations | Work sampling problems |
| $C = \dfrac{\text{Standard MH}}{\text{Actual MH}} \times 100$ | Performance efficiency index | % | Productivity analysis |
| $\text{Productivity} = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{MH Input}}$ | Output per man-hour | varies | Yard comparison |
| $MH/CGT$ | Shipbuilding productivity index | MH/CGT | International benchmarking |
| $\text{SAF} = 1 + A$ | Standard Allowance Factor | dimensionless | Multi-step problem simplification |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Shipyard Bidding:** Companies like Hyundai Heavy Industries, Cochin Shipyard, and Mazagon Dock use man-hour estimates as a **commercial weapon** during tender submission.
- **Lean Manufacturing:** Standard times enable identification of **non-value-added activities** for elimination.
- **Wage Incentive Schemes:** Piece-rate and bonus systems are based on standard times.
- **Capacity Planning:** Determines if the yard can deliver within the contract period.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Observed Time to Standard Time (The Master Derivation)

This derivation is the **backbone** of every man-hour determination numerical in KTU exams.

### Step 1: Start with Observed Time
A time study engineer observes a worker on a plate cutting operation. The stopwatch records:

$$OT = 2.40 \text{ minutes (per plate)}$$

### Step 2: Apply Performance Rating
The engineer, using the **Westinghouse Rating System**, assesses the worker's skill and effort. Suppose:

$$PR = 110 \text{ (10% above normal)}$$

Normal Time is then:

$$NT = OT \times \frac{PR}{100}$$

$$NT = 2.40 \times \frac{110}{100}$$

$$NT = 2.64 \text{ minutes}$$

> *Logic:* The worker was 10% faster than normal. To estimate the "normal" worker's time, we adjust downward.

### Step 3: Add Allowances
The company policy provides the following allowances:

| Allowance Type | Percentage |
|----------------|------------|
| Personal Needs | 5% |
| Rest/Fatigue | 10% |
| Contingency | 5% |
| **Total Allowance (A)** | **20%** = 0.20 |

Standard Time:

$$ST = NT \times (1 + A)$$

$$ST = 2.64 \times (1 + 0.20)$$

$$ST = 2.64 \times 1.20$$

$$ST = 3.168 \text{ minutes per plate}$$

### Step 4: Compute Man-Hours for a Job
If 500 plates need to be cut:

$$MH_{total} = ST \times Q$$

$$MH_{total} = 3.168 \times 500$$

$$MH_{total} = 1584 \text{ minutes}$$

Convert to hours:

$$MH_{total} = \frac{1584}{60} = 26.4 \text{ Man-Hours}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Work Sampling — Required Number of Observations

A shipyard wants to estimate the proportion of time workers spend in *productive* work. Preliminary observations suggest 70% of time is productive. The manager wants 95% confidence with ±5% accuracy.

### Step 1: Identify Parameters
- $p = 0.70$
- $1 - p = 0.30$
- $E = 0.05$

### Step 2: Apply the Formula

$$N = \frac{4 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{E^2}$$

$$N = \frac{4 \times 0.70 \times 0.30}{(0.05)^2}$$

$$N = \frac{0.84}{0.0025}$$

$$N = 336 \text{ random observations}$$

### Step 3: Interpretation
The analyst must take **336 random observations** (spread across shifts and days) to reliably estimate the proportion of productive time.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Synthesis from Standard Data

Consider a pipe spool fabrication job with three operations:

| Operation | Standard Time (min/unit) | Quantity |
|-----------|--------------------------|----------|
| Cut pipe | 1.5 | 100 |
| Weld joint | 4.0 | 100 |
| Hydrotest | 6.0 | 100 |

### Step 1: Compute Man-Hours Per Operation

$$MH_1 = 1.5 \times 100 = 150 \text{ min}$$

$$MH_2 = 4.0 \times 100 = 400 \text{ min}$$

$$MH_3 = 6.0 \times 100 = 600 \text{ min}$$

### Step 2: Total Man-Hours

$$MH_{total} = 150 + 400 + 600 = 1150 \text{ min}$$

$$MH_{total} = \frac{1150}{60} = 19.17 \text{ Man-Hours}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is a high-yield KTU table** — examiners often ask students to compare methods.

| **Criterion** | **Time Study** | **Standard Data** | **PMTS (MTM)** | **Work Sampling** | **Analogy** |
|---------------|---------------|-------------------|----------------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Accuracy** | High | High | Very High | Moderate | Low |
| **Cost** | High | Medium | Medium-High | Low | Very Low |
| **Time Required** | High | Medium | High | Medium | Very Low |
| **Worker Bias** | Yes | No (clean data) | None | None | Inherited |
| **Best Use** | Repetitive tasks | Batch production | Short cycles | Multi-task studies | Early estimates |
| **Skill Needed** | Trained engineer | Engineer + records | Specialist | Statistician | Estimator |
| **Statistical Validity** | Yes (if many cycles) | Yes | Deterministic | Yes | No |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following diagram maps the **logical flow** of man-hour determination — from data collection to final project estimate.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Shipyard receives contract or work order] --> B[Identify operations and tasks]
    B --> C{Select method}
    C --> D[Time Study with stopwatch]
    C --> E[Standard Data lookup]
    C --> F[PMTS / MTM analysis]
    C --> G[Work Sampling]
    C --> H[Analogy / Historical data]
    D --> I[Record Observed Time OT]
    E --> J[Retrieve standard element time]
    F --> K[Sum motion time values]
    G --> L[Random observations N]
    H --> M[Scale from prior project]
    I --> N[Apply Performance Rating]
    J --> O[Sum to get Normal Time]
    K --> O
    L --> P[Compute proportion p]
    M --> Q[Adjust by analogy factor]
    N --> R[Compute Normal Time NT]
    O --> R
    P --> R
    Q --> R
    R --> S[Add Allowances A]
    S --> T[Compute Standard Time ST]
    T --> U[Multiply by quantity Q]
    U --> V[Sum across all operations]
    V --> W[Total Man Hours for project]
    W --> X[Generate cost estimate and schedule]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Process Map — Conversion Logic

This second diagram clarifies the **master equation chain** — the most-tested concept in KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Observed Time OT] -->|multiply by PR/100| B[Normal Time NT]
    B -->|multiply by 1 + A| C[Standard Time ST per unit]
    C -->|multiply by Quantity Q| D[Total Man Hours]
    D -->|divide by 8| E[Man Days]
    E -->|divide by 22| F[Man Months]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Tree — Choosing the Right Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need man-hour estimate] --> B{Is the task repetitive?}
    B -->|Yes| C{Is it a short cycle under 2 minutes?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Use PMTS / MTM]
    C -->|No| E[Use Standard Data System]
    B -->|No| F{Is there historical data?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Use Standard Data or Analogy]
    F -->|No| H[Use Work Sampling and Synthesis]
    H --> I[Apply time study for new elements]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Allowance Breakdown Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Standard Time] --> B[Basic / Normal Time]
    A --> C[Allowance Add ons]
    C --> D[Personal Needs 5 percent]
    C --> E[Rest and Fatigue 10 percent]
    C --> F[Contingency 5 percent]
    C --> G[Process Delay 5 percent]
    C --> H[Special Allowance]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Standard Time and list its main components. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

**Standard Time** is the time required by a qualified worker, working at a normal pace, using the prescribed method, including all necessary allowances, to complete a specific unit of work.

**Main Components:**
1. **Observed Time (OT):** Actual time recorded during time study.
2. **Performance Rating (PR):** Adjustment factor for worker's skill and effort.
3. **Normal Time (NT):** OT × (PR/100)
4. **Allowances (A):** Percentage additions for personal needs, fatigue, and contingencies.
5. **Standard Time (ST):** NT × (1 + A)

**Formula:**

$$ST = OT \times \frac{PR}{100} \times (1 + A)$$

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Time Study and Work Sampling. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Time Study** | **Work Sampling** |
|------------|----------------|--------------------|
| **Method** | Continuous observation with stopwatch | Random instant observations |
| **Data type** | Detailed elemental times | Activity proportions (percentages) |
| **Duration** | Long (entire task cycle) | Short, spread over days |
| **Analyst workload** | High | Low |
| **Worker awareness** | Yes (may alter behaviour) | No (Hawthorne effect minimal) |
| **Statistical base** | Few long observations | Many short observations |
| **Best suited for** | Repetitive, short-cycle tasks | Multi-activity, long-cycle studies |
| **Sample size formula** | Not applicable | $N = \dfrac{4 p (1-p)}{E^2}$ |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) — Comprehensive Time Study Problem

**(a)** A shipyard conducts a time study on a **plate edge preparation (beveling) operation**. The observed time per plate is **4.8 minutes**. The worker's performance is rated at **115%** using the Westinghouse system. The company policy provides the following allowances: Personal 6%, Rest 12%, Contingency 4%. Determine the **Standard Time per plate**. If **800 plates** are to be beveled, calculate the **total man-hours required** and the **number of workers** needed to complete the job in **5 working days** (8 hours/day). **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **Westinghouse Rating System** in detail. Describe its four sub-factors and how the overall performance rating is determined. Why is rating considered a **subjective** but **indispensable** step in man-hour determination? **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given:**
- Observed Time, $OT = 4.8$ minutes
- Performance Rating, $PR = 115\%$
- Allowances: Personal 6% + Rest 12% + Contingency 4% = 22%

**Step 1: Compute Normal Time**

$$NT = OT \times \frac{PR}{100}$$

$$NT = 4.8 \times \frac{115}{100}$$

$$NT = 4.8 \times 1.15$$

$$NT = 5.52 \text{ minutes per plate}$$

**Step 2: Compute Standard Time**

$$A = 0.06 + 0.12 + 0.04 = 0.22$$

$$ST = NT \times (1 + A)$$

$$ST = 5.52 \times 1.22$$

$$ST = 6.7344 \text{ minutes per plate}$$

**Step 3: Total Man-Minutes for 800 Plates**

$$MH_{total} = ST \times Q$$

$$MH_{total} = 6.7344 \times 800$$

$$MH_{total} = 5387.52 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 4: Convert to Man-Hours**

$$MH_{total} = \frac{5387.52}{60}$$

$$MH_{total} = 89.79 \text{ Man-Hours}$$

**Step 5: Number of Workers for 5 Days**

Available time per worker in 5 days:

$$T_{available} = 5 \times 8 = 40 \text{ hours}$$

Number of workers required:

$$N = \frac{MH_{total}}{T_{available}}$$

$$N = \frac{89.79}{40}$$

$$N = 2.245 \approx 3 \text{ workers (rounded up)}$$

**Final Answer:** Standard Time = **6.7344 min/plate**, Total Man-Hours = **89.79 MH**, Workers required = **3**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Westinghouse Rating System** — a structured method developed at the Westinghouse Electric Company to **adjust observed time** to normal time by evaluating the worker's performance across four dimensions:

**1. Skill (Maximum 6 grades, A1 to C2):**
Evaluates the worker's proficiency, coordination, and consistency.

| Grade | Description | Rating |
|-------|-------------|--------|
| A1 | Superskill | +0.15 |
| A2 | | +0.13 |
| B1 | Excellent | +0.11 |
| B2 | | +0.08 |
| C1 | Good | +0.06 |
| C2 | | +0.03 |

**2. Effort (Maximum 6 grades, A1 to D):**
Measures the will to work — speed, application, and drive.

**3. Conditions (Maximum 6 grades, A to D):**
Assesses environmental factors — temperature, light, noise, ventilation.

**4. Consistency (Maximum 3 grades, A to C):**
Measures the variability of elemental times — repeated cycles should yield similar times.

**Overall Performance Rating Calculation:**

$$PR = 1 + (\text{Skill factor} + \text{Effort factor} + \text{Condition factor} + \text{Consistency factor})$$

**Why Subjective but Indispensable:**
- **Subjective:** Each engineer's judgment may vary; rating depends on the observer's experience and bias.
- **Indispensable:** No two workers perform identically. Without rating, the observed time cannot be generalised to the "average worker," making all man-hour standards inconsistent.
- Statistical reliability improves with **multiple observers** and **rating committees** — hence modern yards use **multiple raters** and average their ratings.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — Predetermined Motion Time System & Work Sampling

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **Methods Time Measurement (MTM)** system of man-hour determination. List **at least 8 basic motions** used in MTM with their TMU values for a typical reach of **30 cm**. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** In a shipbuilding workshop, a work sampling study was conducted. **400 random observations** were taken. The analyst found that in **280 cases**, workers were engaged in productive work. Determine: **(i)** The proportion of productive time, **(ii)** The standard error of the proportion, **(iii)** Whether the sample size is adequate at **95% confidence** (E = 0.05), and **(iv)** If 100,000 man-hours are available annually, how many man-hours of productive work does this represent? **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Methods Time Measurement (MTM)** is a **predetermined motion time system** developed by **H.C. Maynard, G.J. Stegemerten, and J.L. Schwab** in 1948.

**Principle:** Every manual task can be decomposed into a sequence of **basic human motions**. Each motion has a pre-determined time value in **Time Measurement Units (TMU)**. Standard time = Sum of TMU values × number of occurrences.

**Conversion:**

$$1 \text{ TMU} = 0.00001 \text{ hour} = 0.0006 \text{ minute} = 0.036 \text{ second}$$

**Basic MTM Motions and TMU Values:**

| S.No | Motion | Symbol | TMU for 30 cm reach |
|------|--------|--------|----------------------|
| 1 | Reach | R | 10.5 TMU |
| 2 | Move | M | 12.0 TMU |
| 3 | Grasp | G | 6.8 TMU |
| 4 | Position | P | 16.4 TMU |
| 5 | Release | RL | 2.0 TMU |
| 6 | Disengage | D | 4.0 TMU |
| 7 | Turn | T | 9.1 TMU |
| 8 | Apply Pressure | AP | 10.6 TMU |
| 9 | Crank | C | 17.0 TMU |
| 10 | Eye Travel | ET | 7.3 TMU |

**Sketch — MTM Analysis Chart:**

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  WORKER       OBJECT      TOOL                 │
│    [W]   <--→  [O]    <--→  [T]                │
│     │                                                  
│     │  Reach (R) → Grasp (G) → Move (M) →    
│     │  Position (P) → Release (RL) → Disengage(D)│
│     ▼                                                  
│  [FINISHED POSITION]                              │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Advantages of MTM:**
- Worker-bias free
- Consistent standards across plants
- Useful for method improvement
- Ideal for short, repetitive cycles

**Limitations:**
- Requires trained analysts
- Tedious for long, non-repetitive tasks
- Costly to implement

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Total observations, $N = 400$
- Productive observations, $x = 280$

**Step (i): Proportion of Productive Time**

$$p = \frac{x}{N}$$

$$p = \frac{280}{400} = 0.70 \text{ or } 70\%$$

**Step (ii): Standard Error of the Proportion**

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{N}}$$

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{0.70 \times 0.30}{400}}$$

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{0.21}{400}}$$

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{0.000525}$$

$$\sigma_p = 0.0229 \text{ or } 2.29\%$$

**Step (iii): Adequacy Check at 95% Confidence**

At 95% confidence, the acceptable error at 5% is:

$$E = 1.96 \times \sigma_p$$

$$E = 1.96 \times 0.0229 = 0.0449 \text{ or } 4.49\%$$

Since computed error (4.49%) is **less than** allowable error (5%), the sample size is **adequate**.

**Step (iv): Productive Man-Hours in 100,000 MH**

$$MH_{productive} = p \times MH_{total}$$

$$MH_{productive} = 0.70 \times 100{,}000$$

$$MH_{productive} = 70{,}000 \text{ Man-Hours}$$

**Final Answer:**
- Productive proportion = **70%**
- Standard error = **0.0229 (2.29%)**
- Sample size is **adequate** at 95% confidence
- Productive man-hours = **70,000 MH**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this topic — Read Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Confusing Normal Time and Standard Time:** Students often forget to add allowances. Normal Time ≠ Standard Time. Always add $(1 + A)$ at the end.
> 2. **Unit conversion errors:** Observed time may be given in minutes but final answer expected in hours — or vice versa. 1 hour = 60 minutes = 100 TMU = 3600 seconds.
> 3. **Performance Rating as percentage vs. decimal:** $PR = 110$ means divide by 100, not multiply by 1.10 directly without first dividing. Use the formula $NT = OT \times PR / 100$.
> 4. **Allowance arithmetic:** Personal 5% + Rest 10% + Contingency 5% = **20%, not 25%** (don't forget the first one is already a percentage of Normal Time, not of each other).
> 5. **Work Sampling formula:** Use the simplified formula $N = \frac{4 p(1-p)}{E^2}$ only for 95% confidence. For 99% confidence, use $N = \frac{6.6 p(1-p)}{E^2}$ — this is a frequent trick question.
> 6. **Confusing TMU:** 1 TMU = 0.0006 min, NOT 0.006 min. Multiplication by 1000 errors are common.
> 7. **Rounding up workers:** Always round **UP** the number of workers required (e.g., 2.2 → 3 workers), never down.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — for last-hour KTU preparation**

- **Man-Hour (MH):** Unit of human effort. 1 MH = 1 worker × 1 hour.
- **Master Equation:** $ST = OT \times \frac{PR}{100} \times (1 + A)$.
- **Observed Time (OT):** Raw stopwatch reading.
- **Normal Time (NT):** $NT = OT \times PR / 100$.
- **Standard Time (ST):** $NT \times (1 + A)$ — includes ALL allowances.
- **Performance Rating (PR):** Standard = 100; Fast worker > 100; Slow worker < 100.
- **Allowances:** Personal (5–6%), Rest/Fatigue (10–12%), Contingency (5%), Process Delay (5%).
- **Methods of Man-Hour Determination:**
   - **Time Study** — direct, stopwatch, high accuracy.
   - **Standard Data** — from historical records, synthesis.
   - **PMTS** — MTM, Work Factor, BMT (motion-based, bias-free).
   - **Work Sampling** — random observations, statistical, $N = \frac{4 p(1-p)}{E^2}$.
   - **Analogy / Historical** — fast but rough.
   - **Statistical / Regression** — multi-variable models.
- **MTM:** 1 TMU = 0.00001 hour; basic motions = Reach, Move, Grasp, Position, Release, etc.
- **Productivity Index:** MH/CGT — lower is better.
- **Cost Calculation:** Labour Cost = Man-Hours × Hourly Wage Rate.
- **Standard Data advantage:** Reduces future time-study effort for similar tasks.
- **PMTS advantage:** Worker-bias free; works at design stage (before actual production).
- **Work Sampling advantage:** Does not disrupt work; minimal Hawthorne effect.
- **Choose PMTS** for short, repetitive, manual cycles.
- **Choose Time Study** for medium-cycle, established tasks.
- **Choose Work Sampling** for long-cycle, multi-activity jobs.
- **Choose Analogy** at the **bidding/quotation stage** for speed.
- **Always include allowances** — never quote Normal Time as Standard Time in scheduling.
- **Total project MH** = Sum of (Standard Time per operation × Quantity of that operation).
- **Number of workers** = Total MH / (Available days × Hours per day).
- **Rounding rule for workers:** Always round **UP**.
- **Statistical confidence:** 95% → factor 4; 99% → factor 6.6; 90% → factor 2.7.
- **Shipyard benchmark:** Modern productivity ≈ 30–60 MH/CGT depending on ship type.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Information Exchange During Ship Production - Communication Between Departments and Other Stakeholders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST633 - SHIP PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Information Exchange During Ship Production - Communication Between Departments and Other Stakeholders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Information Exchange in Ship Production** is the structured, bidirectional, and often multi-modal flow of technical, administrative, and managerial data between functionally distinct entities (internal departments, external vendors, regulatory bodies, and clients) involved in the design, fabrication, assembly, outfitting, and delivery of a marine vessel. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme, this concept is treated as a critical subsystem of **Ship Production Management**, governing how a shipyard's Plan of Work translates into executable action through systematic documentation, digital exchange, and feedback loops.

The discipline is grounded in **Product Lifecycle Management (PLM)**, **Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP)**, and **Computer-Aided Design/Manufacturing (CAD/CAM)** frameworks, regulated by classification societies (e.g., **IRS, LR, DNV, ABS, BV**) and statutory authorities (e.g., **IMO, Flag State, SOLAS**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** Information exchange is not merely data transfer — it is the **controlled dissemination of verified, version-controlled production intelligence** that synchronizes the geometric model, the material schedule, the work breakdown structure (WBS), and the regulatory approvals across all stakeholders.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Hospital Surgery Team**
>
> Imagine a major heart surgery in a hospital. The cardiologist, anesthetist, surgical nurses, blood bank, and the patient's family all operate in separate rooms, yet the surgery succeeds because a **central chart (the patient's file)** flows between them. Each person updates a specific section, signs and timestamps it, and passes it on.
>
> Now replace the hospital with a **shipyard**, the patient with a **ship hull**, and the medical team with the **design office, plate shop, assembly hall, outfitting berth, and classification surveyor**. The "patient's chart" is the **production information packet** — drawings, BOMs, work instructions, approval certificates.
>
> Without a synchronized chart, the welder may weld a plate the designer never released, the purchaser may order a pipe the outfitting planner does not need, and the class surveyor may reject a tank already coated. **Information exchange is the nervous system of ship production.**

## 1.3 Primary Stakeholder Categories

| **Category** | **Examples** | **Nature of Information** |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal Departments** | Design Office, Production Planning, Procurement, Fabrication, Outfitting, QA/QC, Erection, Commissioning | Drawings, BOMs, work orders, NCRs, progress reports |
| **Shipowner / Client** | Owner's representative, technical superintendent | Specification updates, change orders, acceptance protocols |
| **Classification Society** | IRS/LR/DNV/BV/ABS surveyors | Approval drawings, material certificates, weld procedures, test records |
| **Statutory Authorities** | Flag State (e.g., MCA, RINA), IMO, Coastal Regulator | SOLAS/MARPOL compliance, load line, tonnage, stability documents |
| **OEM & Suppliers** | Engine makers (MAN, Wärtsilä), equipment vendors | GA drawings, interface dimensions, delivery schedules, type approvals |
| **Subcontractors** | Steel stockists, painting contractors, electrical yards | Scope of work, free-issue material lists, ITP (Inspection Test Plan) |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics in Information Exchange

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) Mandated by KTU 2024 Module 4:**
> - **Information Lead Time (ILT)** = Date of issue − Date of requirement at shop floor
> - **Rework Due to Information Error (RDIE)** = % of rework caused by wrong/missing data
> - **Document Revision Cycle (DRC)** = Average number of revisions per drawing
> - **First-Time-Right Index (FTRI)** = % of tasks executed correctly on first attempt due to correct information
> - **Inter-Departmental Query Resolution Time (IDQRT)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Information Flow Architecture in a Modern Shipyard

Ship production information moves through **four primary layers**, each with distinct velocity, format, and authorization protocols.

### Layer 1 — Engineering Definition Layer
- **Owner:** Design Office
- **Content:** 3D CAD model, lines plan, GA, structural drawings, piping isometric, electrical schematics, HVAC, accommodation layout
- **Format:** Native CAD (e.g., **Tribon, AVEVA Marine, FORAN, CATIA, NAPA**) → neutral exchange (**STEP AP242, IGES, JT**)
- **Velocity:** Slow (weeks to months) but high in volume

### Layer 2 — Production Planning Layer
- **Owner:** Production Planning / Technology Department
- **Content:** Block division plan, assembly tree, erection sequence, work breakdown structure (WBS), resource loading, **PMS (Planned Manufacturing Sequence)**
- **Format:** MS Project, Primavera P6, in-house ERP
- **Velocity:** Medium (weekly cycles)

### Layer 3 — Shop Floor Execution Layer
- **Owner:** Foremen, Supervisors, Welders, Fitters
- **Content:** Work orders, **Nesting + Cutting plans**, Welding procedure specifications (WPS), PQR, ITP checkpoints
- **Format:** Paper printouts, tablets, MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems) terminals
- **Velocity:** Real-time (hourly/shift basis)

### Layer 4 — Verification & Compliance Layer
- **Owner:** QA/QC, Class Surveyor, Owner Rep
- **Content:** Inspection records, NDT reports, material certificates (MTC), Class approval letters, test reports
- **Format:** Signed PDFs, database entries
- **Velocity:** Real-time feedback into Layer 3

## 2.2 The Five Pillars of Information Exchange

### Pillar A — Drawings & 3D Model Exchange
- **Critical Rule:** Only drawings with a valid **revision code (Rev 0, Rev 1, Rev A, Rev B…)** and a class/owner **approval stamp** are released to production.
- A **Master Drawing Register (MDR)** maintains the single source of truth.
- Any change triggers an **Engineering Change Note (ECN)** or **Design Change Request (DCR)**.

### Pillar B — Material & Procurement Information
- Linked via the **Bill of Materials (BOM)** and **Bill of Quantities (BOQ)**.
- Material grades must align with **Class Society Rules** (e.g., IRS Pt.3, LR Pt.2, DNV Pt.2).
- Long-lead items (e.g., **main engine, shaft, propellers, AC plants**) require **early procurement** to protect the **milestone schedule**.

### Pillar C — Production & Schedule Information
- Communicated through **Weekly Progress Meetings (WPM)**, **Look-ahead Plans (LAP)**, and **Daily Constraint Logs**.
- Use of **Gantt charts**, **Line of Balance (LOB)**, and **S-curve** progress.

### Pillar D — Quality & Compliance Information
- **Welding:** WPS/PQR/WQT traceability.
- **NDT:** UT, RT, MPI, DPI reports.
- **Class:** Survey status — *Held, Approved, Released for Coating, etc.*

### Pillar E — Outfitting & System Information
- Piping, HVAC, electrical cabling, fire-fighting, navigation.
- **Interface control documents (ICDs)** between hull and machinery vendors.

## 2.3 Information Flow Patterns

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern 1: Serial (Linear) Flow**
> Design → Planning → Procurement → Fabrication → Assembly → Outfitting → Trials
> - Slow, high error compounding, low rework efficiency.
> - Common in small traditional yards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern 2: Parallel (Concurrent) Flow**
> Design, procurement, and pre-fabrication begin simultaneously using **zonal outfitting** and **block philosophy**.
> - High information density at interfaces.
> - Mandated by modern KTU 2024 yard management approach.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern 3: Feedback / Closed-Loop Flow**
> Shop floor → NCR → Design Review → Revised Drawing → Re-issue.
> - Quality-driven, continuously self-correcting.
> - Uses **P-D-C-A (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Metric** | **Formula** | **Unit** | **Engineering Utility** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Information Lead Time (ILT) | $ILT = T_{issue} - T_{requirement}$ | **days** | Measures design responsiveness |
| First-Time-Right Index | $FTRI = \dfrac{N_{correct}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | **%** | Quality of information clarity |
| Rework Cost Ratio | $RCR = \dfrac{C_{rework}}{C_{total\,project}} \times 100$ | **%** | Quantifies information failure cost |
| Document Revision Density | $DRD = \dfrac{N_{revisions}}{N_{drawings}}$ | dimensionless | Index of design maturity |
| Information Density per Block | $IDB = \dfrac{\Sigma I_i}{N_{blocks}}$ | **MB/block** | Data engineering load (in digital yards) |
| Communication Overhead Ratio | $COR = \dfrac{T_{communication}}{T_{production}} \times 100$ | **%** | Efficiency indicator |
| Vendor Response Time | $VRT = T_{query\,closure} - T_{query\,raised}$ | **hours/days** | External coordination health |
| ECN Cycle Time | $ECT = T_{approval} - T_{ECN\,raised}$ | **days** | Engineering responsiveness to field issues |

> [!WARNING]
> **Mark-Loss Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly write absolute values in tables using the `|` character. In KTU answer scripts, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ notation when copying these tables into your answer.

## 2.5 Regulatory Framework Anchors

- **SOLAS (Safety of Life at Sea):** Mandates specific drawings/approvals for fire safety, life-saving, navigation.
- **MARPOL:** Outfitting drawings for pollution prevention equipment.
- **MLC 2006:** Accommodation layout, crew safety information.
- **Class Rules:** Every drawing must bear a class stamp; **As-Built** drawing set is mandatory at delivery.
- **ISO 9001:2015:** Information control, document control, traceability.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Derivation Framework

## 3.1 Worked Analytical Problem: Calculating Project Information Efficiency

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU Module 4 Style):**
> A shipyard is building a 30,000 DWT bulk carrier. The design office issues **420 drawings** over the project life. Of these, **58 drawings** had to be revised at least once. During fabrication, **120 rework incidents** occurred; **35 of these** were traceable to wrong or missing information. The project cost is **₹180 Crores**, and the rework cost was **₹4.2 Crores**.
>
> Compute:
> (i) Document Revision Density (DRD)
> (ii) Information-Induced Rework Ratio (IIRR)
> (iii) Rework Cost Ratio (RCR)
> (iv) First-Time-Right Index (FTRI), given total fabrications = 4,200
> (v) Comment on the information health of the yard.

### Step 1 — Compute Document Revision Density (DRD)

$$
\begin{aligned}
DRD &= \frac{N_{revisions}}{N_{drawings}} \\
&= \frac{58}{420} \\
&= 0.1381 \approx 0.14
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Approximately **14 revisions per 100 drawings**. For a modern yard, benchmark is **< 0.10**. The yard is performing **moderately poorly** in design maturity.

### Step 2 — Compute Information-Induced Rework Ratio (IIRR)

$$
\begin{aligned}
IIRR &= \frac{N_{rework\,from\,info\,error}}{N_{total\,rework}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{35}{120} \times 100 \\
&= 29.17\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Almost **30% of all rework** stems from information failure — a serious red flag warranting a **Document Control Audit (DCA)**.

### Step 3 — Compute Rework Cost Ratio (RCR)

$$
\begin{aligned}
RCR &= \frac{C_{rework}}{C_{total\,project}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{4.2}{180} \times 100 \\
&= 2.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Industry benchmark (KTU textbook, Andrews, Lamb) is **2–5%**. The yard is at the lower end — cost-bearable but unsustainable if it scales to 5+ vessels/year.

### Step 4 — Compute First-Time-Right Index (FTRI)

$$
\begin{aligned}
FTRI &= \frac{N_{correct}}{N_{total}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{4{,}200 - 120}{4{,}200} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{4{,}080}{4{,}200} \times 100 \\
&= 97.14\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A **97.14% FTRI** is excellent globally. World-class yards (Hyundai HI, Samsung HI) target **> 98%**.

### Step 5 — Engineering Recommendation

The yard has a **strong execution culture** (high FTRI) but a **weak design maturity process** (DRD = 0.14) and **high information-induced rework** (29.17% of all rework). Recommendation:

- Implement **3D model-based single source of truth (SSOT)** with AVEVA/Tribon.
- Enforce **frozen design dates** per block release.
- Introduce a **Back-Check (BC)** protocol: Design verifies with Production before issue.
- Adopt a **Class & Owner concurrent approval workflow** (parallel signing).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Inter-Department Information Matrix

This matrix is a high-yield KTU 2024 exam diagram. The **row** sends information; the **column** receives it. Cells contain the **dominant information type**.

| **From ↓ / To →** | **Design** | **Planning** | **Procurement** | **Fabrication** | **Outfitting** | **QA/QC** | **Class/Owner** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Design** | Rev notes | Block plan, GA | BOM/BOQ | Released dwgs | Piping iso | Class-approved dwgs | For approval |
| **Planning** | Schedule input | Sequence | Material lead time | WBS, routing | Outfit zones | ITP schedule | Survey plan |
| **Procurement** | Vendor data | Delivery dates | PO closure | Free-issue list | OEM GA | MTC | Type approval |
| **Fabrication** | As-built feed | Progress % | Reject reports | NC reports | Hull ready date | NDT/Weld logs | Survey calls |
| **Outfitting** | Interface NCRs | Outfit progress | Vendor delays | Equipment fit-up | System I/O | Loop test results | Sea trial prep |
| **QA/QC** | NCR closure | Quality KPIs | Vendor rating | Hold/Release | Test packs | Audit findings | Survey status |
| **Class/Owner** | Comments/conds | Witness dates | Sub-vendor list | Inspection calls | System approval | Non-conformities | Final sign-off |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** The **Inter-Departmental Information Matrix** is one of the most frequently asked 14-mark questions in Ship Production Management. Drawing this matrix accurately and explaining any **one diagonal/row** in detail fetches full marks.

## 3.3 Communication Channels Hierarchy

1. **Formal Written (Letters, ECN, MOM):** Legal binding, archived.
2. **Digital PLM/ERP (Workflow with digital signature):** Speed + audit trail.
3. **Meetings (Weekly, Kick-off, Review):** For complex decisions.
4. **Verbal + Email:** Day-to-day coordination, but must be **minuted**.
5. **Shop Floor Display (MES / Andon):** Real-time status.

> **Communication Rule (KTU):** *No verbal instruction is valid until reduced to writing within 24 hours.*

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Information Exchange Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Shipowner and Class Society] -->|Approval and Comments| B[Design Office]
    B -->|Released Drawings and BOM| C[Production Planning]
    C -->|Work Orders and Schedule| D[Procurement]
    C -->|Nesting and WPS| E[Fabrication Shop]
    D -->|Material and OEM Parts| E
    D -->|Equipment and Piping| F[Outfitting Berth]
    E -->|Blocks and Sub Assemblies| G[Erection Berth]
    F -->|Piping and Systems| G
    G -->|Completed Hull| H[Commissioning and Trials]
    H -->|Class Trials| A
    E -.->|NDT and MTC| Q[QA QC Department]
    Q -.->|NCR and Feedback| B
    Q -.->|Survey Calls| A
    F -.->|NCR and Feedback| B
    E -.->|NCR and Feedback| B
```

> **Reading Guide:** Solid arrows represent **forward information flow**; dotted arrows represent **feedback/closure flow**. Notice the **central role of QA/QC** as a verification node and the **closed loop back to the owner/class** for trials.

## 4.2 Inter-Departmental Information Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D[Design Office] -- Drawing Release --> P[Planning]
    P -- Routing --> S[Shop Floor]
    P -- Long Lead List --> M[Material Management]
    M -- MTC --> Q[Quality Assurance]
    S -- NC Report --> Q
    Q -- NCR --> D
    Q -- Survey Status --> C[Class Surveyor]
    S -- As Built Data --> P
    M -- Vendor Delay --> P
    C -- Comments --> D
    D -- Revision Issued --> S
```

## 4.3 Engineering Change Note (ECN) Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ECN_Raised: Field discovers discrepancy
    ECN_Raised --> Design_Review: Triage within 48 hrs
    Design_Review --> Approved: Valid change
    Design_Review --> Rejected: Not valid
    Rejected --> [*]
    Approved --> Drawing_Update: New revision issued
    Drawing_Update --> Class_Approval: Concurrent loop
    Class_Approval --> Shop_Floor_Release: New rev distributed
    Shop_Floor_Release --> Implementation: Foreman acknowledges
    Implementation --> Closure: As built confirmed
    Closure --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Stakeholder Communication Sphere Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Core_Yard["CORE YARD INTERNAL DOMAIN"]
        D1[Design Office]
        P1[Production Planning]
        F1[Fabrication]
        O1[Outfitting]
        Q1[Quality and Class]
    end
    subgraph External_Stakeholders["EXTERNAL STAKEHOLDER DOMAIN"]
        S1[Shipowner]
        S2[Class Society]
        S3[Statutory Authority]
        S4[OEM Vendors]
        S5[Subcontractors]
    end
    subgraph Shared_Protocols["SHARED COMMUNICATION LAYER"]
        N1[PLM System]
        N2[ERP System]
        N3[Document Control]
        N4[EDMS]
    end
    D1 --- N1
    P1 --- N2
    Q1 --- N3
    F1 --- N4
    O1 --- N4
    S1 --- N1
    S2 --- N3
    S3 --- N3
    S4 --- N2
    S5 --- N2
```

> **Reading Note:** The **Shared Communication Layer** (PLM, ERP, EDMS, Document Control) is the **technological backbone** that mediates information between the internal yard and the external stakeholder sphere. This is the architecture implemented in **Shipyard 4.0** frameworks.

---
