3.3 Analytical Matrix — DBMS Selection Criteria for Shipyards | Criterion | RDBMS (e.g., PostgreSQL) | NoSQL Document (e.g., MongoDB) | Graph DB (e.g., Neo4j) | |-----------|--------------------------|--------------------------------|------------------------| | **Structured BOM data** | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | | **Hull 3D model metadata** | ★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ | | **Cable/pipe network queries** | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★★ | | **Vendor / procurement data** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ | | **IoT sensor streams** | ★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★ | | **ACID compliance** | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | | **Scalability to fleet level** | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | | **KTU 2024 Recommended Use** | Master data | IoT & docs | Network routing | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Trend Note:** Modern shipyards increasingly adopt **polyglot persistence** — combining multiple DB types within a single data fabric, accessed via unified APIs. # 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Three-Tier DBMS Architecture for a Shipyard ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph EXT["EXTERNAL TIER - User Views"] V1["Design Office View
Hull Lines CAD Data"] V2["Production View
Work Orders and Schedules"] V3["QA View
Weld Certificates and NDT"] V4["Owner View
Progress Reports"] end subgraph APP["APPLICATION TIER - Business Logic"] A1["CAD-CAM Integration
FORAN NAPA AVEVA"] A2["MES Module
Block Erection Tracking"] A3["QA Engine
WPS PQR Compliance"] A4["Reporting Engine
Dashboards KPIs"] end subgraph CONC["CONCEPTUAL TIER - Logical Schema"] C1["Ship Entity
Hull No Class Owner"] C2["Block Entity
Zone Panel Stage"] C3["Material Entity
Plate Pipe Fitting"] C4["Weld Entity
WPS NDT Welder"] end subgraph INT["INTERNAL TIER - Physical Storage"] I1["Hull Tables
SQL Server Cluster"] I2["Document Store
MongoDB As-Built PDFs"] I3["Time Series DB
InfluxDB IoT Sensors"] I4["Backup and DR
RAID 6 Off-site"] end V1 --> A1 V2 --> A2 V3 --> A3 V4 --> A4 A1 --> C1 A1 --> C3 A2 --> C1 A2 --> C2 A3 --> C4 A4 --> C1 C1 --> I1 C2 --> I1 C3 --> I1 C4 --> I1 C1 --> I2 C2 --> I2 C4 --> I3 I1 --> I4 I2 --> I4 I3 --> I4 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — ER Diagram of a Shipyard Production Database ```mermaid erDiagram SHIP ||--o{ BLOCK : "is divided into" SHIP ||--|| CLASS_SOCIETY : "is registered with" SHIP ||--o{ WORK_ORDER : "has" SHIP ||--o{ CERTIFICATE : "holds" BLOCK ||--o{ PANEL : "contains" BLOCK ||--o{ WELD : "joined by" BLOCK ||--o{ WORK_ORDER : "scheduled in" WELD }o--|| WPS : "uses procedure" WELD }o--|| WELDER : "executed by" WELD ||--o{ NDT_REPORT : "verified by" MATERIAL ||--o{ STOCK : "stored as" WAREHOUSE ||--o{ STOCK : "houses" SUPPLIER ||--o{ MATERIAL : "provides" SHIP { string Hull_No PK string Class_Symbol string Owner_Name date Keel_Lay_Date date Delivery_Date } BLOCK { string Block_ID PK string Hull_No FK string Zone_Code double Weight_Tonnes string Stage } WELD { string Weld_ID PK string Block_ID FK string WPS_ID FK string Welder_ID FK string Joint_Type } MATERIAL { string Mat_ID PK string Mat_Name string Supplier_ID FK string Grade } SUPPLIER { string Supplier_ID PK string Supplier_Name string Country } WAREHOUSE { string Warehouse_ID PK string Location } STOCK { string Mat_ID FK string Warehouse_ID FK int Quantity } WPS { string WPS_ID PK string Process string Material_Group } WELDER { string Welder_ID PK string Name string Qualification_Level } NDT_REPORT { string Report_ID PK string Weld_ID FK string Method string Result date Inspection_Date } CERTIFICATE { string Cert_ID PK string Hull_No FK string Cert_Type date Issue_Date } CLASS_SOCIETY { string Society_ID PK string Society_Name } WORK_ORDER { string WO_ID PK string Hull_No FK string Block_ID FK date Start_Date date End_Date string Status } PANEL { string Panel_ID PK string Block_ID FK string Material_ID FK double Thickness_mm } ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Data Flow in a Shipyard DBMS ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT["DATA INPUT SOURCES"] I1["Hull Design
CAD System"] I2["Procurement
ERP Module"] I3["Shop Floor
Barcode Scans"] I4["QA Lab
NDT Reports"] I5["Class Surveyor
External Portal"] end subgraph VAL["VALIDATION AND PROCESSING"] V1["Schema Check"] V2["Business Rules
Class UR Compliance"] V3["ACID Transaction
Logging"] end subgraph STORE["CENTRAL DATABASE"] S1["Master Tables
Hull Material Weld"] S2["Audit Trails"] S3["Document Store
Certificates Drawings"] end subgraph OUTPUT["DATA OUTPUT CONSUMERS"] O1["Production Planner
Gantt Charts"] O2["Project Manager
KPI Dashboards"] O3["Owner
Progress Reports"] O4["Digital Twin
Lifecycle Support"] end I1 --> V1 I2 --> V1 I3 --> V1 I4 --> V1 I5 --> V1 V1 --> V2 V2 --> V3 V3 --> S1 V3 --> S2 V3 --> S3 S1 --> O1 S1 --> O2 S3 --> O3 S1 --> O4 S2 --> O2 ``` # 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define Database Management System (DBMS). List any four advantages of using a DBMS in a ship production environment.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** A Database Management System (DBMS) is a software system that enables the definition, creation, maintenance, and controlled access to a centralized database. In ship production, it serves as the **digital backbone** for managing design, material, production, quality, and lifecycle data across all shipyard departments. **Four Advantages (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):** 1. **Data Integrity:** Ensures unique, validated identifiers for every plate, pipe, weld, and component — eliminating conflicting data. 2. **Reduced Redundancy:** Through normalization, the same hull data is stored once and referenced by all departments. 3. **Concurrent Access:** Multiple planners, fitters, and inspectors can query and update the database simultaneously. 4. **Enhanced Security:** Role-based access (designer vs. surveyor vs. worker) protects sensitive design and commercial data. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Explain the concept of ACID properties in a DBMS. Why are they critical for ship production data?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** ACID stands for: - **A — Atomicity (1 mark):** A transaction (e.g., recording a weld inspection) is either fully completed or not executed at all. There is no "half-done" state. Critical for ensuring that an NDT report is not saved without linking it to the specific weld ID. - **C — Consistency (0.5 marks):** The database moves from one valid state to another, preserving all defined rules (e.g., class society constraints). - **I — Isolation (0.5 marks):** Concurrent transactions do not interfere with each other — two welders updating adjacent blocks will not corrupt each other's data. - **D — Durability (0.5 marks):** Once a transaction is committed, it survives system failures — essential for audit trails required by IACS. **Criticality for ship production (bonus):** Non-ACID databases risk losing traceability of welds, which can lead to **class society non-conformities**, ship detention, or even catastrophic structural failure at sea. --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` **Part (a)** — *Discuss the architecture of a 3-tier DBMS with a neat block diagram. Explain how this architecture is implemented in a modern shipyard. (7 marks)* **Model Answer:** **Definition (1 mark):** The 3-tier architecture separates the user interface, business logic, and data storage into three independent layers, providing modularity, scalability, and security. **Three Tiers Explained (4 marks — 1.33 each):** 1. **Presentation Tier (External Level):** The user interface layer — web portals, CAD workstations, shop-floor tablets, and dashboards. In a shipyard, this includes the design office's CAD interface, the production planner's Gantt charts, and the QA inspector's mobile forms. 2. **Application Tier (Logical Level):** Contains the business logic — validation rules, calculation engines, and integration services. In a shipyard, this tier hosts the production scheduling algorithms, nesting optimizers, and compliance checkers (e.g., verifying weld qualifications against IACS UR W17). 3. **Data Tier (Internal Level):** The centralized database server with all operational data — hull lines, materials, welds, certificates. Uses RDBMS engines (Oracle/PostgreSQL) and document stores. **Implementation in a Modern Shipyard (2 marks):** - **Design office** uses 3-tier systems like FORAN or AVEVA Marine to push hull data into the central DB. - **Production shop floor** uses barcode/RFID scanners (clients) that write to the application server, which validates against class rules before committing to the DB. - **Owner and class surveyor** access the same database through secure external portals (e.g., Bureau Veritas' VeriStar). **Block Diagram (must be drawn — 1 mark allocated):** Refer to the **3-Tier Architecture diagram in Section 4.1** of these notes. --- **Part (b)** — *With a suitable example from ship production, explain the concept of normalization up to 3NF. (7 marks)* **Model Answer:** **Definition (1 mark):** Normalization is the process of organizing database tables to minimize redundancy and eliminate anomalies (insert, update, delete). **The Three Normal Forms with Ship Production Examples (5 marks):** | Form | Rule | Ship Production Example | |------|------|-------------------------| | **1NF** | All attributes must be atomic | A "Block_Components" cell should not contain a comma-separated list of parts. | | **2NF** | 1NF + no partial dependency | If a `BLOCK_MATERIAL` table has composite key (Block_ID, Mat_ID), then `Block_Weight` (depending only on Block_ID) must be moved to a separate `BLOCK` table. | | **3NF** | 2NF + no transitive dependency | `Mat_ID → Supplier_ID → Supplier_Country` — supplier country is transitively dependent and must be moved to a `SUPPLIER` table. | **Step-by-Step Worked Example (1 mark):** Refer to the **Worked Example 1 in Section 3.1** of these notes — it walks through the normalization of a shipyard material table into 3NF, ending with four linked tables: `MATERIAL`, `SUPPLIER`, `WAREHOUSE`, and `STOCK`. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]` **Part (a)** — *Explain the Entity-Relationship (ER) model. Draw an ER diagram for a ship production database showing entities: SHIP, BLOCK, WELD, WELDER, MATERIAL, and relationships between them. (7 marks)* **Model Answer:** **ER Model Definition (1 mark):** The ER model is a conceptual data model that represents the database as a collection of **entities** (real-world objects), **attributes** (properties), and **relationships** (associations). It is widely used in shipyard data design before implementation. **Entities & Attributes (3 marks — 0.5 each):** - **SHIP** — Hull_No (PK), Class_Symbol, Owner_Name, Keel_Lay_Date - **BLOCK** — Block_ID (PK), Hull_No (FK), Zone_Code, Weight_Tonnes - **WELD** — Weld_ID (PK), Block_ID (FK), WPS_ID (FK), Welder_ID (FK) - **WELDER** — Welder_ID (PK), Name, Qualification_Level - **MATERIAL** — Mat_ID (PK), Mat_Name, Grade **Relationships & Cardinality (2 marks):** - SHIP *is divided into* BLOCK — **1 : Many** - BLOCK *joined by* WELD — **1 : Many** - WELD *executed by* WELDER — **Many : 1** (one welder performs many welds) - WELD *uses procedure* WPS — **Many : 1** - BLOCK *uses* MATERIAL — **Many : Many** (via a junction table) **ER Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the **ER Diagram in Section 4.2** of these notes. The Mermaid `erDiagram` block is accepted as a valid schematic representation. --- **Part (b)** — *Discuss the role of DBMS in modern ship production. Highlight its applications in (i) Material Management, (ii) Quality Assurance, and (iii) Production Planning. (7 marks)* **Model Answer:** **Role of DBMS — Introduction (1 mark):** A modern shipyard is a complex, data-intensive enterprise. The DBMS acts as the **central nervous system**, integrating data from design through to delivery and lifecycle support. It underpins the **Industry 4.0 / Smart Shipyard** paradigm. **(i) Material Management (2 marks):** - Stores the **Bill of Materials (BOM)** for the entire vessel, linked to the hull structure. - Tracks **vendor master data**, lead times, certifications (e.g., 3.2 certificates per EN 10204). - Implements **Just-In-Time (JIT)** procurement by linking consumption data from the shop floor to the purchase order system. - Records **traceability** — every plate can be traced back to its heat number, supplier, and certificate (mandatory under IACS UR W11). **(ii) Quality Assurance (2 marks):** - Manages **Welding Procedure Specifications (WPS)**, **Procedure Qualification Records (PQR)**, and **Welder Qualifications** in a queryable format. - Stores **NDT reports** (UT, RT, MPI, DPI) and links them to specific welds. - Issues automatic alerts when a weld is due for re-inspection or when a welder's qualification is about to expire. - Generates **class society submission packages** digitally. **(iii) Production Planning (2 marks):** - Stores the **Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)** of the ship — Hull No → Block → Panel → Plate. - Maintains **work orders** with planned vs. actual dates, linked to **Gantt charts** and **critical path** analysis. - Integrates with **MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems)** to track real-time progress on the shop floor via barcode/RFID. - Supports **what-if scenarios** — e.g., "What if we delay Block 12 by 3 days?" — by re-running schedules against the database. > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do NOT confuse "DBMS" with "Database."** The database is the data; the DBMS is the software that manages it. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this mistake. > - **Always state the normal form being explained** (1NF / 2NF / 3NF) before listing its rule. Students who list the rule without naming the form lose **0.5 marks**. > - **In ER diagrams, cardinality (1:M, M:N) MUST be shown explicitly.** A diagram with entities and no relationships marked gets only **partial credit**. > - **In normalization questions, always end with the lossless join verification** ($R_1 \cap R_2 \rightarrow R_1$ or $R_2$). Skipping this loses **0.5 marks**. > - **Do not write generic DBMS applications** (e.g., "used in banks"). Every answer must be **mapped to ship production** — examiners want shipyard-specific examples. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — DBMS in Ship Production** - ✅ **DBMS** = software to define, store, retrieve, and manage data centrally. - ✅ **Three-schema architecture**: Internal (physical) → Conceptual (logical) → External (user views). - ✅ **3-tier architecture** is the standard for modern shipyards. - ✅ **ACID properties** (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) are non-negotiable for ship production data. - ✅ **Data Models**: Hierarchical, Network, Relational, Object-Oriented, NoSQL — each suited to different shipyard data types. - ✅ **Normalization**: - 1NF → atomic values - 2NF → no partial dependencies - 3NF → no transitive dependencies - ✅ **ER Model** uses entities (SHIP, BLOCK, WELD…), attributes, and relationships (1:1, 1:M, M:N). - ✅ **Ship Production Applications**: - **Material Management** → BOM, vendor data, traceability (IACS UR W11). - **QA** → WPS, PQR, NDT, welder qualifications. - **Production Planning** → WBS, work orders, scheduling. - **Lifecycle** → As-built digital twin, predictive maintenance. - ✅ **Key Standard:** ISO 9001:2015 + IACS Unified Requirements mandate auditable, traceable data. - ✅ **Future Trend:** Digital twin, Industry 4.0, polyglot persistence, IoT integration — all DBMS-driven. - ✅ **KTU Hot-Question Words:** "Normalize to 3NF," "Draw ER diagram," "Explain 3-tier architecture," "ACID properties" — practice these four question types exhaustively.

topics.title: The Inventory Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST633 - SHIP PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** The Inventory Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Inventory Problem** in Ship Production Management is the systematic decision-making framework used to determine the **optimal quantity** of materials, components, and parts to order (or produce internally) and the **optimal timing** of such orders, so that the total cost of inventory (ordering + holding + shortage) is minimized while ensuring uninterrupted shipyard operations.

In a shipbuilding context, inventory refers to all **stockpiled resources** held by the yard — from heavy steel plates, profiles, and pipes, to propulsion equipment, outfitting components, consumables, and spare parts — that are waiting to be consumed in the construction, outfitting, or maintenance of vessels.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** Inventory is an idle resource of any kind — physical goods, intangible assets, or human resource effort — held in storage awaiting use, sale, or further processing. The *inventory problem* is the Operations Research (OR) formulation that asks: *"How much to order?"* and *"When to order?"*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Shipyard Pantry

Imagine a shipyard canteen manager who stocks tea, sugar, and biscuits for 2,000 workers. If she orders too much, the biscuits go stale (cost of holding/obsolescence) and tie up the canteen's limited locker space (working capital locked in). If she orders too little, the workers go hungry during shift change, morale drops, and emergency trips to the market cost a premium (stockout cost). 

The **Inventory Problem** is precisely this balancing act — but scaled up to millions of rupees worth of steel plates, main engines, and navigation electronics for a ship. The same three forces compete:

- **Ordering too frequently** → high procurement, transport, and inspection costs.
- **Ordering too much** → high holding, insurance, capital, and deterioration costs.
- **Stocking too little** → project delays, production stoppages, and penalty costs (very expensive in shipbuilding, where a missing $500 part can delay a $50 million vessel).

## 1.3 Categories of Shipyard Inventory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Major Classes of Inventory in a Shipyard:**
> 1. **Raw Materials (RM):** Steel plates, sections, pipes, copper, aluminium — typically **45–60%** of inventory value.
> 2. **Work-In-Progress (WIP):** Partially fabricated blocks, sub-assemblies, painted panels.
> 3. **Finished Goods (FG):** Completed vessels awaiting delivery (rare in custom shipbuilding).
> 4. **MRO (Maintenance, Repair, Operations):** Spare parts, consumables, lubricants, tools.

## 1.4 Why the Inventory Problem Matters in Shipbuilding

Ship production is uniquely inventory-sensitive because:

- **Long manufacturing lead times** (often 12–24 months per vessel).
- **Bulky, heavy items** (a single steel plate may weigh 8–15 tonnes).
- **Specialized, low-vendor-base items** (main engines, shaft lines, navigation gear often come from a single approved supplier worldwide).
- **High capital lock-up** — steel alone can represent **20–25%** of contract value.
- **Schedule penalties** — daily liquidated damages (LD) for late delivery can run into **₹10 lakh – ₹1 crore per day** on commercial vessels.

## 1.5 Independent vs. Dependent Demand

> [!NOTE]
> **Independent Demand:** Demand for finished ship systems (e.g., navigation consoles, lifeboats) is forecasted independently.
>
> **Dependent Demand:** Demand for raw materials (plates, profiles) is *derived* from the ship's Bill of Materials (BoM). This distinction drives whether we use **EOQ models** (independent) or **MRP logic** (dependent).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of Total Inventory Cost (TIC)

The objective of any inventory model is to minimize:

$$\text{TIC} = C_o + C_h + C_s + C_p$$

where:
- $C_o$ = Total Ordering Cost (per order × number of orders)
- $C_h$ = Total Holding (Carrying) Cost (per unit per year × average inventory)
- $C_s$ = Total Shortage (Stockout) Cost
- $C_p$ = Total Purchase Cost (sometimes excluded as it is a constant if demand is fixed)

### 2.1.1 Detailed Cost Elements

| Cost Component | What it Includes (Shipyard Examples) | Typical % of Item Value per Year |
|----------------|--------------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Ordering Cost ($C_o$)** | Purchase requisition processing, vendor selection, RFQ issuance, PO preparation, transport, insurance, inspection & QC, customs clearance | Independent of order quantity |
| **Holding Cost ($C_h$)** | Capital cost (interest on locked working capital), warehousing rent, depreciation, insurance, taxes, pilferage, obsolescence, deterioration | **15% – 30%** of unit cost |
| **Shortage Cost ($C_s$)** | Lost production hours, idle labour, schedule slippage, penalty/LD, emergency freight, premium procurement | Hard to quantify but **very high** |
| **Purchase Cost ($C_p$)** | Unit price × annual demand | Often 60–75% of TIC if model is naive |

## 2.2 Classification of Inventory Models

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Major Families of Inventory Models (KTU 2024 Module 4 Emphasis):**
>
> **A. Deterministic Models** — demand and lead time are known with certainty.
> 1. **EOQ (Economic Order Quantity)** — instantaneous replenishment, constant demand.
> 2. **EPQ (Economic Production Quantity / Lot-Sizing Model)** — finite replenishment rate.
> 3. **Quantity Discount Models.**
> 4. **Reorder Point & Safety Stock Models.**
>
> **B. Probabilistic Models** — demand and/or lead time are random variables.
> 1. **Continuous Review (Q, R) model** with safety stock.
> 2. **Periodic Review (P, s) model.**
> 3. **Service level models (Type 1 & Type 2).**

## 2.3 Assumptions of the Classical EOQ Model (Wilson, 1934)

1. Demand rate ($D$) is constant and known.
2. Lead time ($L$) is constant and known.
3. Replenishment is instantaneous (infinite production rate).
4. No quantity discounts.
5. Shortages are not permitted (backorders = 0).
6. Order cost ($S$) and holding cost ($H$) are constant.
7. Only one item is considered at a time.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table contains every formula a student must memorize for the KTU 2024 ESE on this topic.** Units are in parens.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Notes / Units |
|---|--------------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | **EOQ (Wilson) Optimal Order Quantity** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | $D$=annual demand (units/yr), $S$=order cost (₹/order), $H$=holding cost (₹/unit·yr) |
| 2 | **EOQ in terms of unit cost ($C$)** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{iC}}$ | $i$=carrying cost rate (decimal), $C$=unit purchase cost (₹) |
| 3 | **Optimal Number of Orders per year** | $N^* = \dfrac{D}{Q^*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{DH}{2S}}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | **Optimal Time Between Orders (Cycle Time)** | $T^* = \dfrac{Q^*}{D} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2S}{DH}}$ | years (convert to days/months) |
| 5 | **Average Inventory** | $\bar{I} = \dfrac{Q^*}{2}$ | units |
| 6 | **Minimum Total Variable Cost** | $\text{TVC}^* = \sqrt{2DHS}$ | ₹/yr |
| 7 | **Reorder Point (Deterministic)** | $R = D \cdot L$ | units; $L$=lead time (yr) |
| 8 | **Safety Stock (variable lead time)** | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_L \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | $z$=service-level z-score, $\sigma_L$=std dev of demand |
| 9 | **EOQ with Backorders Allowed** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{(H+p)}{p}}$ | $p$=backorder penalty (₹/unit·yr) |
| 10 | **EOQ with Quantity Discount — All-Units** | $\text{Total Cost} = DC + \dfrac{D}{Q}S + \dfrac{Q}{2} \cdot iC$ | Find $Q$ in each price break that minimizes TC |
| 11 | **Economic Production Quantity (EPQ)** | $Q_p^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{p-d}}$ | $p$=production rate (units/day), $d$=demand rate |
| 12 | **Maximum Inventory (EPQ)** | $I_{\max} = Q_p^* \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right)$ | units |
| 13 | **Production Run Time** | $t_p = \dfrac{Q_p^*}{p}$ | time units |
| 14 | **Total Inventory Cost (EPQ)** | $\text{TC}^* = \sqrt{2DHS} \cdot \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{d}{p}}$ | ₹/yr |
| 15 | **Average Inventory (EPQ)** | $\bar{I} = \dfrac{I_{\max}}{2}$ | units |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The EOQ/EPQ framework is the **backbone of every ERP module** (SAP MM, Oracle SCM) used by modern shipyards like Hyundai Heavy Industries, Daewoo, Mazagon Dock, Cochin Shipyard, and L&T Shipbuilding. It directly governs:

- Procurement of bulk steel plates from SAIL/JSW.
- Sizing of block fabrication bays (because high WIP inventory is a yard-space problem).
- Just-in-Time (JIT) feasibility studies for outfitting stages.
- Spare parts provisioning for ship-defence contracts.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Wilson EOQ Formula

We consider one inventory cycle. Let:
- $D$ = annual demand (units/year)
- $Q$ = order quantity per cycle (units)
- $S$ = ordering cost per order (₹/order)
- $H$ = holding cost per unit per year (₹/unit·year)
- $C$ = unit purchase cost (₹/unit)

### Step 1 — Number of Orders per Year
Since each order brings in $Q$ units and annual demand is $D$:

$$N = \dfrac{D}{Q} \quad \text{orders per year}$$

### Step 2 — Annual Ordering Cost
Multiply number of orders by cost per order:

$$C_o = S \cdot N = \dfrac{DS}{Q}$$

### Step 3 — Average Inventory
Inventory drops linearly from $Q$ to 0 between orders. The average inventory over a cycle is the area under the triangle divided by cycle time:

$$\bar{I} = \dfrac{Q + 0}{2} = \dfrac{Q}{2}$$

### Step 4 — Annual Holding Cost
Multiply average inventory by per-unit holding cost:

$$C_h = H \cdot \bar{I} = \dfrac{HQ}{2}$$

### Step 5 — Total Variable Cost (excluding constant purchase cost $DC$)

$$\text{TVC}(Q) = C_o + C_h = \dfrac{DS}{Q} + \dfrac{HQ}{2}$$

### Step 6 — Minimize TVC by Differentiating with Respect to $Q$
Take first derivative and set to zero:

$$\dfrac{d(\text{TVC})}{dQ} = -\dfrac{DS}{Q^2} + \dfrac{H}{2} = 0$$

### Step 7 — Solve for $Q^*$

$$\dfrac{H}{2} = \dfrac{DS}{Q^{*2}} \implies Q^{*2} = \dfrac{2DS}{H} \implies Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$$

### Step 8 — Second-Derivative Check (Confirm Minimum)
$$\dfrac{d^2(\text{TVC})}{dQ^2} = \dfrac{2DS}{Q^3} > 0 \quad \forall \, Q>0$$

Since the second derivative is positive, the critical point is a **minimum**. This confirms $Q^*$ is the optimal economic order quantity.

### Step 9 — Substitute $Q^*$ Back to Get Minimum TVC

$$\text{TVC}^* = \dfrac{DS}{\sqrt{2DS/H}} + \dfrac{H}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{2DHS}$$

$$\boxed{\text{TVC}_{\min} = \sqrt{2DHS}}$$

This is the elegant closed-form result that shows EOQ cost scales as the **geometric mean** of demand and the two cost parameters.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Economic Production Quantity (EPQ)

In shipbuilding, many items are produced internally (e.g., fabricated panels in the panel line) rather than purchased from outside. Replenishment is therefore **not instantaneous** — production proceeds at rate $p$ while demand continues at rate $d$.

### Step 1 — Inventory Build-Up During Production
While the lot is being produced (duration $t_p = Q/p$), inventory rises at rate $(p - d)$:

$$I_{\max} = (p - d) \cdot t_p = Q \left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Average Inventory
Inventory peaks at $I_{\max}$, then depletes at rate $d$ until it reaches zero:

$$\bar{I}_{EPQ} = \dfrac{I_{\max}}{2} = \dfrac{Q}{2}\left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Annual Holding Cost
$$C_h = H \cdot \bar{I}_{EPQ} = \dfrac{HQ}{2}\left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Total Variable Cost
$$\text{TVC}(Q) = \dfrac{DS}{Q} + \dfrac{HQ}{2}\left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Differentiate and Solve

$$\dfrac{d(\text{TVC})}{dQ} = -\dfrac{DS}{Q^2} + \dfrac{H}{2}\left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right) = 0$$

$$Q^{*2} = \dfrac{2DS}{H\left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right)}$$

$$\boxed{Q_p^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{p-d}}}$$

Note that as $p \to \infty$, the second factor $\to 1$, and EPQ reduces to plain EOQ — a beautiful consistency check.

### Step 6 — Minimum TVC for EPQ
$$\text{TVC}^*_{EPQ} = \sqrt{2DHS} \cdot \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{d}{p}}$$

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — A Shipyard Problem

> **Problem (KTU-style):** Mazagon Dock Shipbuilders requires **10,000 stainless-steel pipes per year** for its submarine programme. Each pipe costs **₹8,000**. Ordering cost is **₹25,000 per order**. Holding cost is **20% per year** of the item value. Lead time is **6 days**. The yard operates 300 days per year.
>
> **Find:** (i) EOQ, (ii) Optimal number of orders, (iii) Cycle time, (iv) Reorder point, (v) Total minimum cost.

**Given:**
- $D = 10{,}000$ pipes/yr
- $C = ₹8{,}000$/pipe
- $S = ₹25{,}000$/order
- $i = 0.20$ → $H = iC = 0.20 \times 8000 = ₹1{,}600$/pipe·yr
- $L = 6$ days; working days = 300
- Daily demand $= D/300 = 10{,}000/300 = 33.33$ pipes/day

### Solution

**Step (i) — EOQ:**
$$Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 10000 \times 25000}{1600}}$$

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{5 \times 10^{8}}{1600}} = \sqrt{3.125 \times 10^{5}} \approx 559 \text{ pipes}$$

**Step (ii) — Number of Orders per Year:**
$$N^* = \dfrac{D}{Q^*} = \dfrac{10000}{559} \approx 17.9 \approx 18 \text{ orders/yr}$$

**Step (iii) — Cycle Time:**
$$T^* = \dfrac{Q^*}{D} = \dfrac{559}{10000} \approx 0.0559 \text{ yr} = 20.4 \text{ days}$$

**Step (iv) — Reorder Point:**
$$R = d \cdot L = 33.33 \times 6 = 200 \text{ pipes}$$

**Step (v) — Minimum Total Variable Cost:**
$$\text{TVC}^* = \sqrt{2DHS} = \sqrt{2 \times 10000 \times 1600 \times 25000}$$

$$\text{TVC}^* = \sqrt{8 \times 10^{11}} = ₹894{,}427 \text{ per year}$$

**Verification of Order + Holding Split at EOQ:**
- Annual Order Cost $= DS/Q^* = 25000 \times 17.9 = ₹447{,}214$
- Annual Holding Cost $= HQ^*/2 = 1600 \times 559/2 = ₹447{,}214$
- ✅ Both equal, confirming EOQ is the minimum (the "equal-cost rule").

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of an Inventory Decision System in a Shipyard

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Contract Awarded] --> B[Bills of Materials BoM Exploded]
    B --> C{Is Item Standard Catalogue or Project Specific}
    C -- Standard Independent --> D[Apply EOQ / EPQ Model]
    C -- Project Specific Dependent --> E[Apply MRP Logic]
    D --> F[Compute Q star and R]
    E --> G[Compute Planned Order Releases]
    F --> H[Inventory Status File ISF]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Stock Available greater than R}
    I -- Yes --> J[Defer Order Reorder Later]
    I -- No --> K[Trigger Purchase Requisition PR]
    K --> L[Vendor RFQ and PO]
    L --> M[Goods Receipt and QC]
    M --> N[Bin Stock Update ISF]
    N --> H
    J --> H
```

**Reading the diagram:** The system continuously compares on-hand stock against the reorder point $R$. When stock falls below $R$, a purchase requisition is auto-generated; on receipt, the ISF is updated and the cycle restarts. This is a classic **continuous-review (Q, R) policy**.

## 4.2 Inventory Level vs. Time — EOQ Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Stock at Q max arrives] --> P1[Depletes linearly to 0 over T star days]
    P1 --> P2[Order placed at Reorder Point R]
    P2 --> P3[Stock arrives instantly back to Q max]
    P3 --> P1
```

The shape is a classic **sawtooth waveform**: each cycle, inventory drops linearly from $Q^*$ to 0 (since demand is constant), at which instant a replenishment order arrives instantaneously, returning stock to $Q^*$.

## 4.3 Cost Trade-off Visualization (Why EOQ is Optimal)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Costs
        OC[Ordering Cost DS over Q decreases with larger Q]
        HC[Holding Cost HQ over 2 increases with larger Q]
    end
    OC --> SUM[Total Cost TVC]
    HC --> SUM
    SUM --> MIN[Minimum at Q star where OC equals HC]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** At the optimal EOQ, the annual ordering cost **exactly equals** the annual holding cost. Any deviation from $Q^*$ in either direction strictly increases TVC. This is the **equal-cost rule** that examiners love to test.

## 4.4 ABC Classification Flow (Shipyard Spare Parts)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[All Inventory Items in Yard] --> B[Rank by Annual Consumption Value]
    B --> C{Cumulative percent}
    C -- Top 10 to 15 percent --> D[Class A Tight Control Frequent Review EOQ with Safety Stock]
    C -- Next 20 to 30 percent --> E[Class B Moderate Control Periodic Review]
    C -- Bottom 55 to 70 percent --> F[Class C Loose Control Bulk Orders Annual Review]
```

In a typical shipyard:
- **Class A:** Main engine spares, navigation electronics, bow thrusters.
- **Class B:** Pumps, valves, electrical panels.
- **Class C:** Fasteners, gaskets, lubricants, paints.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Inventory" in the context of Ship Production Management. List any four types of inventory held in a shipyard.**

**Model Answer:**
Inventory refers to the stock of raw materials, semi-finished goods, finished goods, consumables, and spares held by an organization to support future production, sale, or operational needs.

Four types of inventory in a shipyard:
1. **Raw Materials** — steel plates, profiles, pipes, copper cables.
2. **Work-In-Progress (WIP)** — partially fabricated blocks, panels, units under outfitting.
3. **Finished Goods (FG)** — completed vessels awaiting delivery/trials.
4. **MRO (Maintenance, Repair, Operations) Items** — lubricants, spares, tools, consumables.

*(For full 3 marks: definition + 4 types = 1 + 2 marks.)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**State the equal-cost rule of the EOQ model. Why is it significant for shipyard procurement managers?**

**Model Answer:**
The equal-cost rule states that at the optimum Economic Order Quantity ($Q^*$), the **annual ordering cost equals the annual holding cost**, i.e.:

$$\dfrac{DS}{Q^*} = \dfrac{HQ^*}{2}$$

**Significance for shipyard procurement managers:**
- Provides a quick **sanity check**: if ordering cost ≠ holding cost, current order size is sub-optimal and must be adjusted.
- Indicates the **trade-off equilibrium** between the two opposing cost forces.
- Helps managers intuitively halve or double an order quantity to see which way TVC moves.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided into sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students answer either **Question A** *or* **Question B**.

---

### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 — Expected Pattern]

**(a)** *Derive the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) formula from first principles. State clearly all assumptions of the classical EOQ model.* **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define Variables:** Let $D$ = annual demand (units/yr), $Q$ = order quantity per order (units), $S$ = ordering cost per order (₹/order), $H$ = holding cost per unit per year (₹/unit·yr).

**Step 2 — Number of Orders per Year:** Since each order fulfills $Q$ units of demand and total annual demand is $D$:

$$N = \dfrac{D}{Q}$$

**Step 3 — Annual Ordering Cost:** Multiply $N$ by $S$:

$$C_o = N \cdot S = \dfrac{DS}{Q}$$

**Step 4 — Average Inventory (Linear depletion from $Q$ to 0):**

$$\bar{I} = \dfrac{Q + 0}{2} = \dfrac{Q}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Annual Holding Cost:**

$$C_h = H \cdot \bar{I} = \dfrac{HQ}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Total Variable Cost:**

$$\text{TVC}(Q) = \dfrac{DS}{Q} + \dfrac{HQ}{2}$$

**Step 7 — Differentiate with respect to $Q$ and equate to zero:**

$$\dfrac{d(\text{TVC})}{dQ} = -\dfrac{DS}{Q^2} + \dfrac{H}{2} = 0$$

**Step 8 — Solve for $Q^*$:**

$$Q^{*2} = \dfrac{2DS}{H} \implies \boxed{Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}}$$

**Step 9 — Second-derivative check:**

$$\dfrac{d^2(\text{TVC})}{dQ^2} = \dfrac{2DS}{Q^3} > 0$$

This confirms a **minimum**.

**Assumptions of Classical EOQ:**
1. Demand rate is **constant** and deterministic.
2. Lead time is **constant** and deterministic.
3. Replenishment is **instantaneous** (infinite production rate).
4. Order cost $S$ and holding cost $H$ are **fixed and known**.
5. **No shortages** are permitted.
6. **No quantity discounts** are available.
7. Only **one item** is considered in isolation.

---

**(b)** *A shipyard uses 50,000 litres of marine-grade paint per year for hull coating. The paint costs ₹400 per litre. Ordering cost is ₹18,000 per order. Holding cost is 25% per year of the item value. Lead time is 12 days. The yard works 300 days per year.* 
*Calculate:*
*(i) Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)* 
*(ii) Optimal number of orders per year* 
*(iii) Minimum total variable inventory cost per year* 
*(iv) Reorder point in litres* **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $D = 50{,}000$ L/yr; $C = ₹400$/L; $S = ₹18{,}000$/order; $i = 0.25$; $H = 0.25 \times 400 = ₹100$/L·yr; $L = 12$ days; working days = 300.

**(i) EOQ:**

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 50000 \times 18000}{100}}$$

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.8 \times 10^9}{100}} = \sqrt{1.8 \times 10^7} \approx 4{,}243 \text{ litres}$$

**(ii) Optimal Number of Orders:**

$$N^* = \dfrac{D}{Q^*} = \dfrac{50000}{4243} \approx 11.78 \approx 12 \text{ orders/yr}$$

**(iii) Minimum Total Variable Cost:**

$$\text{TVC}^* = \sqrt{2DHS} = \sqrt{2 \times 50000 \times 100 \times 18000}$$

$$\text{TVC}^* = \sqrt{1.8 \times 10^{11}} = ₹424{,}264 \text{ per year}$$

**Verification:** At EOQ, Ordering Cost = Holding Cost = $₹2{,}12{,}132$. ✅

**(iv) Reorder Point:**

Daily demand $= 50{,}000 / 300 = 166.67$ L/day.

$$R = d \cdot L = 166.67 \times 12 = 2{,}000 \text{ litres}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall #1:**
> Students often confuse **carrying cost rate $i$** with the **holding cost $H$**. The model answer must show $H = iC$ as an explicit intermediate step, otherwise the examiner deducts 1 mark under KTU valuation norms.

---

### Question B [14 Marks] [Alternative Choice — KTU Pattern]

**(a)** *Explain the Economic Production Quantity (EPQ) model. Derive the EPQ formula. How does it differ from the basic EOQ model?* **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The **EPQ model** (also called the *lot-sizing model* or *production lot-size model*) applies when **replenishment is gradual** rather than instantaneous — that is, items are produced or fabricated **internally** at a finite rate $p$, while demand continues to be drawn at rate $d$.

**Step 1 — Production Cycle Duration:**
If lot size is $Q$ and production rate is $p$:

$$t_p = \dfrac{Q}{p}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Inventory at End of Production Run:**
Inventory builds up at the **net rate** $(p - d)$ during $t_p$:

$$I_{\max} = (p - d) \cdot t_p = Q\left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Average Inventory:**

$$\bar{I} = \dfrac{I_{\max}}{2} = \dfrac{Q}{2}\left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Annual Holding Cost:**

$$C_h = H \cdot \bar{I} = \dfrac{HQ}{2}\left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Total Variable Cost:**

$$\text{TVC}(Q) = \dfrac{DS}{Q} + \dfrac{HQ}{2}\left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Differentiate and solve:**

$$-\dfrac{DS}{Q^2} + \dfrac{H}{2}\left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right) = 0$$

$$\boxed{Q_p^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{p-d}}}$$

**Differences from Basic EOQ:**

| Feature | EOQ | EPQ |
|---------|-----|-----|
| Replenishment | Instantaneous | Gradual at rate $p$ |
| Inventory profile | Sawtooth from $Q$ to 0 | Climbs to $I_{\max}$, then drops |
| Average inventory | $Q/2$ | $Q/2 \cdot (1 - d/p)$ |
| Optimal $Q$ | $\sqrt{2DS/H}$ | $\sqrt{2DS/H} \cdot \sqrt{p/(p-d)}$ |
| Use case | Purchased items | Internally produced items (e.g., fabricated panels) |

---

**(b)** *A shipyard produces propeller shafts in its own machine shop. Annual demand is 400 shafts/year. Production rate is 800 shafts/year. Setup cost per production run is ₹40,000. Holding cost is ₹500 per shaft per year.* 
*Determine:*
*(i) Optimal production lot size (EPQ)* 
*(ii) Maximum inventory level* 
*(iii) Annual minimum total cost* 
*(iv) Length of each production run* **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $D = 400$ shafts/yr; $p = 800$ shafts/yr; $S = ₹40{,}000$/run; $H = ₹500$/shaft·yr; $d = 400$/yr.

**(i) EPQ:**

$$Q_p^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 400 \times 40000}{500}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{800}{800-400}}$$

$$Q_p^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{3.2 \times 10^7}{500}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{800}{400}} = \sqrt{64000} \cdot \sqrt{2}$$

$$Q_p^* = 252.98 \times 1.414 \approx 358 \text{ shafts}$$

**(ii) Maximum Inventory:**

$$I_{\max} = Q_p^* \left(1 - \dfrac{d}{p}\right) = 358 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{400}{800}\right) = 358 \times 0.5 = 179 \text{ shafts}$$

**(iii) Minimum Annual Total Cost:**

$$\text{TC}^* = \sqrt{2 \times 400 \times 500 \times 40000} \cdot \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{400}{800}}$$

$$\text{TC}^* = \sqrt{1.6 \times 10^{10}} \cdot \sqrt{0.5} = 126{,}491 \times 0.7071 \approx ₹89{,}443 \text{ per year}$$

**(iv) Length of Production Run:**

$$t_p = \dfrac{Q_p^*}{p} = \dfrac{358}{800} \approx 0.448 \text{ yr} \approx 163.4 \text{ days}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall #2:**
> A common mistake is forgetting the factor $\sqrt{p/(p-d)}$ in EPQ and computing a plain EOQ. This will lose 2 marks. Always re-read the question to see if it specifies "internally produced" or "finite production rate".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: The Inventory Problem**

- **Inventory** = idle resource held for future use. In shipyards it includes RM, WIP, FG, and MRO.
- **The Inventory Problem** answers two questions: *How much to order?* and *When to order?*
- **Total Cost** = Order + Holding + Shortage + Purchase.
- **EOQ (Wilson)** = $\sqrt{2DS/H}$ — for purchased items, instantaneous replenishment.
- **At EOQ**, ordering cost = holding cost (the *equal-cost rule*).
- **EPQ** = $\sqrt{2DS/H} \cdot \sqrt{p/(p-d)}$ — for items produced internally with finite production rate.
- **Reorder Point** $R = d \times L$ (deterministic). With variability, add safety stock $SS = z\sigma$.
- **Holding cost rate $i$** typically ranges from **15%–30%** of unit cost per year.
- **Lead time** in shipyards is critical because of long, often **12–24 month** vessel production cycles.
- **ABC Analysis** classifies inventory into A (tight control), B (moderate), C (loose).
- **Independent demand** → use EOQ/EPQ; **Dependent demand** → use MRP.
- **Numerical answer** must always carry **units** (litres, shafts, ₹/yr) to earn full KTU marks.
- **Always verify** EOQ/EPQ cost using the equal-cost rule as a sanity check.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functions of Inventory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST633 - SHIP PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Functions of Inventory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inventory** in the context of ship production management refers to the stock of raw materials (steel plates, profiles, pipes), purchased components (engines, generators, pumps, valves), work-in-progress (partially fabricated hull blocks, partially outfitted modules), and finished goods (commissioned vessels awaiting delivery) held by a shipyard at any given point in time to support the continuous flow of shipbuilding and repair operations.

The **Functions of Inventory** represent the strategic and operational roles that this stocked material plays in enabling a shipyard to deliver vessels on time, within budget, and to the required quality specifications. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Ship Production Management*, inventory serves multiple functions that directly influence lead time, throughput, working capital, and risk exposure in shipbuilding enterprises.

## 1.2 Inventory in the Shipyard Context — An Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Shipyard as a Hospital Pharmacy:**
> Imagine a multi-specialty hospital. Doctors prescribe medicines, surgeries need scalpels, and ICU beds require oxygen. If the pharmacy runs out of even one critical drug, a life may be lost. The hospital pharmacy therefore maintains a *carefully calibrated stock* — not too much (because medicines expire and tie up money) and not too little (because emergencies don't wait). This stock serves the function of **buffering, decoupling, and anticipation**.
> A shipyard works exactly the same way. A steel-cutting line cannot wait for a delayed shipment of plates from South Korea. An engine room cannot function without the right turbochargers. Therefore, the yard maintains *inventory* to bridge the gap between **demand** (vessel completion schedule) and **supply** (vendor delivery schedule).

## 1.3 Why Functions of Inventory Matter in Ship Production

In shipbuilding, the **bill of materials (BOM)** for a single vessel can run into **millions of line items** (over **2 million** parts for a large crude carrier, ~**8 million** parts for an aircraft carrier). With such complexity:

- **Lead times** for propulsion machinery may exceed **18 months**
- **Hull steel** is typically ordered in **production lot sizes** of 200–500 tonnes
- **Outfitting components** arrive from hundreds of suppliers across continents

The functions of inventory provide the structural mechanism by which shipyards manage this staggering complexity.

## 1.4 Standard Inventory Metrics Used in Shipyards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Key inventory metrics used throughout ship production management:
> - **Inventory Turnover Ratio (ITR)** = Cost of Goods Sold / Average Inventory
> - **Days of Inventory on Hand (DOH)** = 365 / ITR
> - **Service Level** = Probability of not stocking out (typically targeted at **95%–99%** for critical ship parts)
> - **Reorder Point (ROP)** = (Average Daily Demand × Lead Time) + Safety Stock

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Core Functions of Inventory

Every stock of material held in a shipyard — whether it is a stack of **grade-A shipbuilding steel plates**, a crate of **bronze propellers**, or a partially welded **double-bottom block** — performs one or more of the following seven fundamental functions:

### Function 1 — Meeting Anticipated Demand
Inventory is held to satisfy **forecasted customer demand**. In shipbuilding, demand is usually project-based: a defence contract requires 6 frigates over 5 years; a commercial order book has 3 bulk carriers scheduled for delivery every 8 months.

> [!NOTE]
> **Shipyard Example:** A shipyard building 12 product tankers per year holds pre-stocked **diesel generator sets** so that engine-room outfitting is never delayed by a 10-month vendor lead time.

### Function 2 — Decoupling Operations
Inventory acts as a **buffer** between dependent stages of production so that each stage can operate independently. In a modern shipyard, the **panel line**, **block assembly shop**, **hull erection bay**, and **outfitting berth** are decoupled by strategic inventory points.

> Without decoupling, a 2-day breakdown in the blasting-and-painting shop would halt the entire hull erection line, costing the yard **₹15–25 lakhs per day** in idle labour and berth occupancy.

### Function 3 — Buffering Against Supply Uncertainty (Safety Stock)
Real-world supply is unreliable. Steel mills face rolling-mill breakdowns, foundries have capacity constraints, and global logistics suffer port congestion. Inventory provides **safety stock** — a hedge against this variability.

### Function 4 — Economies of Scale (Lot Sizing)
Vendors offer **quantity discounts** and freight rate breaks. A shipyard ordering **500 tonnes of plates** in one shipment enjoys lower per-tonne cost than ordering 50 tonnes ten times. Inventory enables the yard to **exploit these economies**.

### Function 5 — Smoothing Production (Level Production)
Shipyards prefer a **steady workforce** rather than hiring-and-firing based on order book fluctuations. Inventory allows production to remain at a constant rate while demand varies — this is the **level production strategy**.

### Function 6 — Hedging Against Price Increases
Steel prices, copper prices, and bunker fuel prices are notoriously volatile. By **stockpiling** materials when prices are low, the shipyard insulates itself against future price spikes.

### Function 7 — Pipeline / In-Transit Inventory
Goods moving between the supplier's warehouse and the shipyard's receiving bay are also inventory. For a shipyard procuring a **main engine from Korea**, the 45-day ocean voyage represents **in-transit inventory** that must be accounted for.

## 2.2 Types of Inventory Held in a Shipyard

| Inventory Type | Description | Shipyard Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Raw Materials** | Basic inputs awaiting processing | Steel plates, profiles, pipes |
| **Work-In-Progress (WIP)** | Partially completed units | Welded blocks, painted panels |
| **Finished Goods** | Completed products awaiting delivery | Commissioned vessels in handover |
| **MRO (Maintenance, Repair, Operations)** | Consumables for plant upkeep | Welding electrodes, gases, lubricants |
| **Safety Stock** | Buffer against demand/supply variability | Extra spares of critical equipment |
| **Anticipation Stock** | Built up before known demand surge | Pre-stocked steel before monsoon shutdowns |
| **In-Transit Inventory** | Goods en route from vendor | Engine block on a ship from Europe |
| **Cycle Stock** | Stock that depletes and replenishes cyclically | Routine consumables, standard fittings |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are recurring KTU examination favourites.**

| Formula | Symbol Description | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | D = Annual Demand, S = Ordering Cost/order, H = Holding Cost/unit/year | Units of EOQ = same as demand units |
| $TC = \dfrac{D}{Q}S + \dfrac{Q}{2}H$ | Total annual inventory cost (excluding purchase cost) | ₹ / year |
| $ROP = \bar{d} \cdot L + SS$ | Reorder Point = Avg daily demand × Lead time + Safety stock | Units |
| $SS = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | Safety Stock using service level z and std deviation $\sigma_d$ | Units |
| $ITR = \dfrac{COGS}{\text{Avg Inventory}}$ | Inventory Turnover Ratio | Dimensionless |
| $DOH = \dfrac{365}{ITR}$ | Days of Inventory on Hand | Days |
| $N = \dfrac{D}{EOQ}$ | Number of orders per year | Orders / year |
| $T = \dfrac{EOQ}{D} \cdot 365$ | Time between orders (cycle time) | Days |
| $\bar{d} = \dfrac{D}{\text{working days}}$ | Average daily demand | Units / day |
| $VC = c \cdot D + \dfrac{D}{Q}S + \dfrac{Q}{2}H$ | Total Variable Cost including purchase cost $c$ | ₹ / year |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The functions of inventory, when correctly implemented, allow shipyards to:

- Reduce **block erection cycle time** by 15–25%
- Achieve **on-time delivery (OTD)** of vessels, a critical KPI in commercial shipping
- Negotiate better **bulk procurement contracts** with steel mills and equipment OEMs
- Maintain a **level workforce** of welders, fitters, and electricians across the order book cycle
- Comply with **classification society** requirements for traceability of certified materials (every plate must have a Mill Test Certificate — therefore inventory traceability is non-negotiable)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) Model

The EOQ model mathematically determines the **optimal order quantity** that minimizes the total annual inventory cost. It is derived by balancing two opposing costs: **ordering cost** and **holding cost**.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumptions of the classical EOQ model:**
> 1. Demand rate is **constant and deterministic** (D units/year).
> 2. Lead time is **constant and known**.
> 3. Order quantity arrives **instantaneously** (no gradual replenishment).
> 4. No **quantity discounts**.
> 5. Ordering cost S and holding cost H are **fixed and known**.
> 6. No **stockouts** are permitted.

### 3.1.2 Defining the Cost Components

Let $Q$ = order quantity in units.

**Step 1 — Annual Ordering Cost:**
Since the number of orders placed per year is $\dfrac{D}{Q}$, and each order costs $S$:

$$\text{Ordering Cost} = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S$$

**Step 2 — Annual Holding (Carrying) Cost:**
With instantaneous replenishment and constant demand, average inventory level is $\dfrac{Q}{2}$. With holding cost $H$ per unit per year:

$$\text{Holding Cost} = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H$$

**Step 3 — Total Annual Inventory Cost (excluding purchase cost):**

$$\begin{aligned}
TC &= \text{Ordering Cost} + \text{Holding Cost} \\
TC &= \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S + \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.1.3 Minimizing Total Cost

To find the optimal $Q$, differentiate $TC$ with respect to $Q$ and set the derivative to zero:

$$\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$

Solving for $Q$:

$$\frac{DS}{Q^{2}} = \frac{H}{2}$$

$$Q^{2} = \frac{2DS}{H}$$

$$\boxed{EOQ = Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}}$$

### 3.1.4 Verification Using Second-Derivative Test

The second derivative confirms this is a minimum:

$$\frac{d^{2}(TC)}{dQ^{2}} = \frac{2DS}{Q^{3}} > 0 \quad \text{(for positive Q)}$$

Since the second derivative is positive, the total cost function is **convex** at $Q^{*}$, confirming a minimum.

### 3.1.5 Substitution: The Optimal Total Cost

Substituting $Q^{*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ back into the total cost equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
TC^{*} &= \frac{D}{\sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}} \cdot S + \frac{\sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}}{2} \cdot H \\
TC^{*} &= \sqrt{\frac{DS \cdot H}{2}} + \sqrt{\frac{DS \cdot H}{2}} \\
TC^{*} &= 2 \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}} \\
TC^{*} &= \sqrt{2DSH}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.1.6 Key Insight — Ordering Cost = Holding Cost at EOQ

At the optimum, the two components of cost are **exactly equal**:

$$\text{Ordering Cost at EOQ} = \text{Holding Cost at EOQ} = \sqrt{\frac{DSH}{2}}$$

This is a useful **sanity check** during numerical problem solving.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A shipyard consumes **10,000 tonnes** of Grade-A shipbuilding steel plates per year. The cost of placing one order is **₹25,000** and the annual holding cost is **₹200 per tonne per year**. Determine:
> (i) Economic Order Quantity
> (ii) Optimal number of orders per year
> (iii) Time between successive orders (assume 300 working days/year)
> (iv) Total annual inventory cost at EOQ

### Solution

**Given:**
- $D = 10{,}000$ tonnes/year
- $S = 25{,}000$ ₹/order
- $H = 200$ ₹/tonne/year

**(i) EOQ Calculation:**

$$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 25{,}000}{200}}$$

$$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{500{,}000{,}000}{200}} = \sqrt{2{,}500{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{EOQ = 1581.14 \text{ tonnes per order}}$$

**(ii) Optimal Number of Orders:**

$$N = \frac{D}{EOQ} = \frac{10{,}000}{1581.14} = 6.32 \approx 7 \text{ orders per year}$$

**(iii) Time Between Orders:**

$$T = \frac{EOQ}{D} \times 300 = \frac{1581.14}{10{,}000} \times 300 = 47.43 \text{ days}$$

**(iv) Total Annual Inventory Cost:**

$$\begin{aligned}
TC^{*} &= \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 25{,}000 \times 200} \\
TC^{*} &= \sqrt{100{,}000{,}000{,}000} \\
TC^{*} &= ₹3{,}16{,}227.77 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Verification Trick:** Confirm by computing both cost components separately — they should each equal ₹1,58,113.88. This adds up to ₹3,16,227.77, matching the formula.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Functions of Inventory Mapped to Inventory Type

| Function of Inventory | Primary Inventory Type Used | Shipyard KPI Impacted |
|---|---|---|
| Meeting anticipated demand | Cycle stock + Anticipation stock | On-time delivery (OTD) |
| Decoupling operations | Work-In-Progress buffers | Throughput time, Berth occupancy |
| Buffering supply uncertainty | Safety stock | Stockout frequency, Schedule slip |
| Economies of scale | Lot-size stock | Procurement cost / tonne |
| Smoothing production | Anticipation stock + WIP | Workforce stability, Labour productivity |
| Hedging price increases | Speculative stock | Material cost variance |
| Pipeline inventory | In-transit stock | Lead time, Working capital tied up |

## 3.4 Advanced — Safety Stock Derivation

When demand during lead time is **stochastic** (variable) and follows a normal distribution:

$$\text{Safety Stock } (SS) = z \cdot \sigma_{LT}$$

Where:
- $z$ = service-level z-score (e.g., **1.65 for 95%**, **2.33 for 99%**)
- $\sigma_{LT}$ = standard deviation of demand during lead time

If demand is variable per period with standard deviation $\sigma_d$ and lead time is $L$ periods:

$$SS = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$$

The **Reorder Point** then becomes:

$$ROP = (\bar{d} \cdot L) + SS$$

Where $\bar{d}$ is the average demand per period.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Seven Functions of Inventory in a Shipyard

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Shipyard Inventory Management System"] --> nodeB["Function 1: Meeting Anticipated Demand"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Function 2: Decoupling Operations"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Function 3: Safety Stock Buffering"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["Function 4: Economies of Scale Lot Sizing"]
    nodeA --> nodeF["Function 5: Smoothing Production Levels"]
    nodeA --> nodeG["Function 6: Hedging Against Price Increase"]
    nodeA --> nodeH["Function 7: Pipeline In Transit Inventory"]

    nodeB --> nodeB1["Steel plates for forecast hull orders"]
    nodeC --> nodeC1["WIP buffers between panel line and block shop"]
    nodeD --> nodeD1["Spare turbochargers held against vendor delay"]
    nodeE --> nodeE1["Bulk procurement of 500 tonne steel lots"]
    nodeF --> nodeF1["Level welder workforce across 12 month build"]
    nodeG --> nodeG1["Copper stock purchased before LME price spike"]
    nodeH --> nodeH1["Main engines on 45 day ocean voyage from Korea"]

    nodeB1 --> nodeOut["Outcome: On Time Delivery OTD 95 percent target"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeOut
    nodeD1 --> nodeOut
    nodeE1 --> nodeOut
    nodeF1 --> nodeOut
    nodeG1 --> nodeOut
    nodeH1 --> nodeOut
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Total Cost vs Order Quantity (EOQ Trade-Off Curve)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeAxis["Order Quantity Q in tonnes"] --> nodePlot["Total Cost Curve"]
    nodePlot --> nodeTOC["Total Cost TC"]
    nodePlot --> nodeOC["Ordering Cost DS divided by Q"]
    nodePlot --> nodeHC["Holding Cost Q divided by 2 times H"]
    nodeTOC --> nodeMin["Minimum at Q equals EOQ"]
    nodeOC --> nodeMin
    nodeHC --> nodeMin
    nodeMin --> nodeMark["At EOQ Ordering Cost equals Holding Cost"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The trade-off curve illustrates the fundamental inventory principle: **as order quantity increases, ordering cost decreases but holding cost increases**. The EOQ is the exact point where the two intersect, minimizing total cost.

## 4.3 Flow Diagram — Inventory Cycle in a Shipyard

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart["Vendor Supplier"] --> nodeShip["In Transit Inventory 45 days"]
    nodeShip --> nodeReceive["Receiving Inspection and QA"]
    nodeReceive --> nodeRaw["Raw Material Store Steel Plates Profiles"]
    nodeRaw --> nodeFab["Fabrication Shop Cutting and Welding"]
    nodeFab --> nodeWIP["Work In Progress Block Assembly Bay"]
    nodeWIP --> nodeErect["Hull Erection Berth"]
    nodeErect --> nodeOutfit["Outfitting Engine Room and Accommodation"]
    nodeOutfit --> nodePaint["Painting and Commissioning"]
    nodePaint --> nodeDeliver["Vessel Delivery to Owner"]
    nodeDeliver --> nodeEnd["Inventory Cycle Complete"]

    nodeWIP -.-> nodeDecouple["WIP buffers enable DECOUPLING"]
    nodeRaw -.-> nodeSS["Safety Stock held for BUFFERING"]
    nodeShip -.-> nodePipe["In transit stock is PIPELINE function"]
```

## 4.4 Inventory Position Diagram (Saw-Tooth Model)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeR1["Reorder Point ROP reached"] --> nodeOrder["Place order Q units"]
    nodeOrder --> nodeWait["Wait for lead time L"]
    nodeWait --> nodeReceive["Receive Q units instantly"]
    nodeReceive --> nodeDeplete["Inventory depletes at rate d units per day"]
    nodeDeplete --> nodeR1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The classic **saw-tooth inventory pattern** shows inventory decreasing linearly between replenishments and jumping back up to the order quantity upon arrival. The Reorder Point must be set at the level where inventory equals expected demand during lead time plus safety stock.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define inventory. List any four functions of inventory in ship production management.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Inventory refers to the stock of raw materials, components, work-in-progress, and finished goods held by a shipyard to support uninterrupted shipbuilding operations.

**Four Functions (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
1. **Meeting anticipated demand** — holding pre-stocked materials for forecasted vessel orders.
2. **Decoupling operations** — WIP buffers between fabrication, block assembly, and erection stages.
3. **Buffering against supply uncertainty** — safety stock for critical items like marine engines.
4. **Hedging against price increases** — stockpiling steel/copper during low-price market windows.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between safety stock and anticipation stock with a shipyard example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Basis | Safety Stock | Anticipation Stock |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** (1 mark) | Buffer against unpredictable demand/supply variability | Built up before known demand surge |
| **Trigger** | Statistically computed (z × σ) | Managerial judgement, seasonal plan |
| **Shipyard Example** (1 mark) | Extra turbochargers held in case vendor delays delivery by 3 months | Steel plates stocked before monsoon when rolling mills shut down for maintenance |
| **Formula** | $SS = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$ | Determined by production planning schedule |

---

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> - **Do NOT confuse "functions of inventory" with "types of inventory"** — these are different topics. Functions describe *why* we hold stock; types describe *what kind* of stock we hold.
> - In EOQ numericals, **always state the units explicitly** — KTU examiners deduct marks when units are missing.
> - When listing functions, provide a **shipyard-specific example** for each — generic textbook answers lose marks.
> - In derivations, **show every step** of the differentiation; examiners award step-marks even if the final answer is incorrect.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **seven functions of inventory** with one shipyard example for each function. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A shipyard uses **8,000 units** of marine-grade pipes annually. The ordering cost is **₹15,000 per order** and the holding cost is **₹25 per unit per year**.
   (i) Calculate the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ).
   (ii) Calculate the optimal number of orders per year.
   (iii) Calculate the time between two consecutive orders (assume 250 working days per year).
   (iv) Calculate the total annual inventory cost at EOQ. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Answer for Question A

**Part (a) — Seven Functions of Inventory with Shipyard Examples (7 marks):**

**1. Meeting Anticipated Demand (1 mark):**
Inventory is held to satisfy forecasted demand for vessel components. *Shipyard Example:* A shipyard with confirmed orders for 4 offshore patrol vessels pre-stocks **marine main engines** 12 months in advance to meet scheduled keel-laying dates.

**2. Decoupling Operations (1 mark):**
Inventory acts as a buffer between dependent production stages. *Shipyard Example:* Partially welded **double-bottom blocks** are held as WIP buffers between the block assembly shop and the hull erection berth, allowing each stage to operate at its own pace.

**3. Buffering Against Supply Uncertainty (1 mark):**
Safety stock hedges against vendor delays. *Shipyard Example:* Shipyards typically hold **3 months' safety stock** of critical imported items such as azimuth thrusters, which have vendor lead times of 9–14 months.

**4. Economies of Scale / Lot Sizing (1 mark):**
Bulk procurement reduces per-unit cost. *Shipyard Example:* Ordering **500 tonnes of AH36-grade steel** in a single shipment from a Korean mill attracts a 12% volume discount and lower ocean freight per tonne.

**5. Smoothing Production / Level Production (1 mark):**
Steady inventory levels enable a constant workforce. *Shipyard Example:* During a 2-month lull between contracts, the yard continues **cutting and nesting plates** for the next vessel to retain its 800-welder workforce without retrenchment.

**6. Hedging Against Price Increases (1 mark):**
Stockpiling during low-price periods. *Shipyard Example:* Buying **200 tonnes of copper cathodes** when LME copper is at ₹600/kg, anticipating it will rise to ₹750/kg in 6 months.

**7. Pipeline / In-Transit Inventory (1 mark):**
Goods in transit are still inventory. *Shipyard Example:* A **diesel generator set** shipped from a German OEM takes 45 days to reach the Indian shipyard — this entire transit period is accounted for as in-transit inventory.

---

**Part (b) — EOQ Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $D = 8{,}000$ units/year
- $S = 15{,}000$ ₹/order
- $H = 25$ ₹/unit/year

**(i) EOQ Calculation (2 marks):**

$$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8{,}000 \times 15{,}000}{25}}$$

$$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{240{,}000{,}000}{25}} = \sqrt{9{,}600{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{EOQ = 3098.39 \approx 3099 \text{ units per order}}$$

**(ii) Optimal Number of Orders (1.5 marks):**

$$N = \frac{D}{EOQ} = \frac{8{,}000}{3098.39} = 2.58 \approx 3 \text{ orders per year}$$

**(iii) Time Between Orders (1.5 marks):**

$$T = \frac{EOQ}{D} \times 250 = \frac{3098.39}{8{,}000} \times 250$$

$$\boxed{T = 96.82 \text{ days between successive orders}}$$

**(iv) Total Annual Inventory Cost at EOQ (2 marks):**

$$TC^{*} = \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 8{,}000 \times 15{,}000 \times 25}$$

$$TC^{*} = \sqrt{6{,}000{,}000{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{TC^{*} = ₹77{,}459.67 \text{ per year}}$$

**Verification (½ mark, optional):**
Ordering cost at EOQ = $\dfrac{8000}{3098.39} \times 15000$ = ₹38,729.83
Holding cost at EOQ = $\dfrac{3098.39}{2} \times 25$ = ₹38,729.83 ✓
Total = ₹77,459.67 ✓

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **different types of inventory costs** in ship production management with a neat diagram. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A shipbuilding company uses **12,000 tonnes** of steel plates annually. The cost of placing one order is **₹30,000** and the annual carrying cost is **₹150 per tonne per year**.
   (i) Determine the Economic Order Quantity.
   (ii) Determine the number of orders to be placed per year.
   (iii) Determine the total variable cost of inventory per year at the EOQ level if the unit purchase cost of steel plate is **₹45,000 per tonne**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Answer for Question B

**Part (a) — Types of Inventory Costs (7 marks):**

The four principal types of inventory costs are:

**1. Holding / Carrying Cost (2 marks):**
Cost of keeping inventory in storage. Includes:
- **Capital cost** (opportunity cost of locked-up working capital) — typically 12–18% per year
- **Storage cost** (warehousing, racks, climate control)
- **Insurance and taxes**
- **Obsolescence and deterioration** (critical for perishable items like paint, electrodes)
- **Shrinkage and pilferage**

*Shipyard example:* Storing 500 tonnes of Grade-A steel plates in a covered yard for one year costs approximately ₹750 per tonne in insurance, capital, and handling.

**2. Ordering Cost (Setup Cost) (2 marks):**
Cost incurred each time an order is placed.
- Purchase requisition preparation
- Vendor selection and negotiation
- Transportation and receiving inspection
- Quality assurance and Mill Test Certificate verification

*Shipyard example:* Importing a marine engine from Germany involves ~₹2.5 lakhs in vendor communication, ocean freight booking, customs clearance, and stage inspection.

**3. Shortage / Stockout Cost (1.5 marks):**
Cost of running out of inventory:
- Lost production / delayed vessel delivery
- Penalty clauses in shipbuilding contracts (often 0.1%–0.5% of contract value per day of delay)
- Expedited shipping costs for emergency replenishment

**4. Purchase / Item Cost (1.5 marks):**
The actual price paid for the material itself. Often the largest cost component but is **not minimized by EOQ** — EOQ minimizes only the ordering + holding combination.

**Diagram (included below):**

```
                  Total Cost
                      │
       ┌──────────────┼──────────────┐
       │              │\             │
       │   Holding    │ \  Total     │
       │     /        │  \  Cost     │
       │    /         │   \   ___    │
       │   /          │    \ /   \   │
       │  /           │    / \   /   │
       │ / Ordering   │   /   \ /    │
       │/_____________│__/_____\/____│___ Quantity Q
       0            EOQ        2Q
```

The total cost curve is **U-shaped**, reaching its minimum at EOQ where ordering cost equals holding cost.

---

**Part (b) — EOQ with Purchase Cost Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $D = 12{,}000$ tonnes/year
- $S = 30{,}000$ ₹/order
- $H = 150$ ₹/tonne/year
- $c = 45{,}000$ ₹/tonne (unit purchase cost)

**(i) EOQ (2 marks):**

$$EOQ = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 30{,}000}{150}} = \sqrt{\frac{720{,}000{,}000}{150}}$$

$$EOQ = \sqrt{4{,}800{,}000} = \boxed{2190.89 \text{ tonnes per order}}$$

**(ii) Number of Orders Per Year (1.5 marks):**

$$N = \frac{12{,}000}{2190.89} = \boxed{5.48 \approx 6 \text{ orders per year}}$$

**(iii) Total Variable Cost at EOQ (3.5 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
VC &= c \cdot D + \frac{D}{EOQ} \cdot S + \frac{EOQ}{2} \cdot H \\
VC &= (45{,}000)(12{,}000) + \sqrt{2DSH} \\
VC &= 54{,}00{,}00{,}000 + \sqrt{2 \times 12{,}000 \times 30{,}000 \times 150} \\
VC &= 54{,}00{,}00{,}000 + \sqrt{1{,}08{,}00{,}00{,}00{,}000} \\
VC &= 54{,}00{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}28{,}633 \\
VC &= ₹54{,}03{,}28{,}633 \text{ per year}
\end{aligned}$$

The **inventory-related cost** (excluding purchase cost) is only **₹3,28,633** — a tiny fraction of total cost, justifying the EOQ investment.

---

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Functions of Inventory**

- ✅ The **7 functions** of inventory are: (1) Meeting anticipated demand, (2) Decoupling operations, (3) Buffering supply uncertainty, (4) Economies of scale, (5) Smoothing production, (6) Hedging prices, (7) Pipeline inventory.
- ✅ In shipyards, inventory is critical because lead times for marine equipment can exceed **18 months** and BOMs run into **millions of items**.
- ✅ The **classical EOQ formula** is $EOQ = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ — applied when demand is constant and lead time is known.
- ✅ At EOQ, **Ordering Cost = Holding Cost** = $\sqrt{\dfrac{DSH}{2}}$.
- ✅ **Total Variable Cost** includes purchase cost + ordering cost + holding cost: $VC = cD + \sqrt{2DSH}$.
- ✅ **Safety Stock** formula: $SS = z \cdot \sigma_d \cdot \sqrt{L}$; **Reorder Point**: $ROP = \bar{d} \cdot L + SS$.
- ✅ **Inventory Turnover Ratio** is a key yard KPI — higher is better (typical shipyards target **4–6 turns/year** for raw steel).
- ✅ Always **state units** in EOQ numericals; examiners deduct marks for missing units.
- ✅ Distinguish between **functions of inventory** (why we hold stock) and **types of inventory** (what kind of stock we hold) — this is a common exam pitfall.
- ✅ For 14-mark answers, **always include a shipyard-specific example** for each theoretical point to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inventory Costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST633 - SHIP PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inventory Costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Inventory Cost** in the context of **Ship Production Management** refers to the aggregate financial expenditure incurred by a shipyard to acquire, store, maintain, and utilize the vast array of raw materials, semi-finished components, parts, and consumables required during the lifecycle of ship construction. These costs form a critical pillar of **Production Planning & Control (PPC)** and **Materials Management** in shipbuilding, directly influencing the yard's working capital, throughput time, and ultimately the **vessel's cost of construction**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Inventory costs are the **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** associated with materials held in the shipyard — comprising **Ordering (Setup) Costs, Holding (Carrying) Costs, Shortage (Stockout) Costs, and Purchase (Acquisition) Costs**. The objective of the materials manager is to minimize the *Total Relevant Cost* while maintaining production continuity.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Steel Plate Warehouse" Intuition

Imagine you run a **shipyard cafeteria** that supplies meals to ship welders and fitters. You have to decide how much rice (analogous to *steel plates* in shipbuilding) to keep in storage.

- If you order **too often** (small batches), you pay huge **delivery truck charges** every week — this is the **Ordering Cost**.
- If you order **too much** (huge piles of rice in the warehouse), the rice gets spoiled, mice eat it, and the warehouse rent goes up — this is the **Holding Cost**.
- If you run **out of rice** mid-shift, workers stop welding, the ship delivery is delayed, and the ship-owner sues you — this is the **Shortage Cost** (catastrophic in shipbuilding!).

**The shipyard manager's dilemma:** *How much to order, and how often, so that the SUM of all these costs is minimum?* This is the essence of inventory cost analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Ship Production:**
> In shipbuilding, materials constitute **60–70% of the total contract value**. Even a **1% reduction** in inventory carrying cost can save **crores of rupees** for a large vessel build. Hence, mastering inventory cost mathematics is non-negotiable for a KTU Marine/Naval Architecture student.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Units

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit (Shipyard Context) |
|---|---|---|
| Annual Demand | D | Tonnes/year (for steel) or Nos./year (for valves) |
| Order Quantity | Q | Tonnes or units per order |
| Unit Purchase Price | P | ₹/Tonne or ₹/Unit |
| Ordering Cost per Order | S | ₹/order |
| Holding Cost per unit per year | H | ₹/unit/year |
| Shortage Cost per unit | Sₛ | ₹/unit short |
| Lead Time | L | Days or Weeks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Conversion:** Annual Holding Cost is typically expressed as a **percentage (i) of the unit price (P)**, i.e., **H = i·P**, where **i ≈ 15% – 25% per annum** for general shipyard stores and **20% – 30%** for long-lead imported items (e.g., main engines, propellers).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Inventory Cost

Inventory cost in ship production is decomposed into four functional cost components. Each component behaves differently with respect to the order quantity **Q** — understanding this is the *core* of the KTU module.

### Pillar 1 — Purchase Cost (Acquisition Cost)
- **Definition:** The direct cost paid to the vendor for the material itself.
- **Formula:** $$C_P = D \cdot P$$
- **Behavior:** *Independent of Q* (constant in classical EOQ models). In *quantity discount* models, it varies in steps.
- **Shipyard Example:** Cost of 500 tonnes of AH36 grade steel plate at ₹85,000/tonne.

### Pillar 2 — Ordering Cost (Setup / Reorder Cost)
- **Definition:** All administrative, transportation, inspection, and documentation costs incurred *once per purchase order*.
- **Components Include:**
  - Purchase requisition preparation
  - Vendor quotation evaluation
  - Purchase Order (PO) processing
  - Inland/sea freight
  - Inward inspection & quality certification
  - Invoice reconciliation
- **Formula:** $$C_O = \frac{D}{Q} \cdot S$$
- **Behavior:** **Decreases as Q increases** (fewer orders per year).
- **Why it matters in shipbuilding:** Steel plates, pipes, and outfit items are sourced in bulk from mills like SAIL, JSPL, or imported from Korea/Japan. Each order involves heavy logistics, customs (for imports), and classification society certification — making **S very high**.

### Pillar 3 — Holding Cost (Carrying Cost)
- **Definition:** The cost of *keeping* inventory in the yard's storage for a period of time.
- **Components Include:**
  - **Capital cost** (opportunity cost of money tied up) — usually the largest component (60–80% of H).
  - **Storage/warehousing cost** (rent, lighting, security).
  - **Insurance & taxes** on stored materials.
  - **Obsolescence, deterioration, corrosion** (critical for steel plates, electronics).
  - **Shrinkage & pilferage**.
- **Formula:** $$C_H = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot H = \frac{Q}{2} \cdot i \cdot P$$
- **Behavior:** **Increases linearly with Q** (more stock = more cost).
- **Why it matters in shipbuilding:** Open-yard storage of steel plates leads to **rust & corrosion**; covered warehouses for electronics, pumps, and switchboards are expensive. **Capital is locked up** for the entire build duration (12–24 months).

### Pillar 4 — Shortage Cost (Stockout Cost)
- **Definition:** The penalty cost when demanded material is **not available** in inventory.
- **Components Include:**
  - **Production downtime** (idle labour, idle cranes, idle welding stations).
  - **Expediting costs** (air-freighting, overtime manufacturing).
  - **Penalty for ship delivery delay** (Liquidated Damages — LD).
  - **Loss of customer goodwill / future contracts**.
  - **Backorder handling cost**.
- **Formula:** $$C_S = \frac{Q_S^2}{2 \cdot D} \cdot S_s$$ (for backorder model)
- **Behavior:** **Decreases as Q increases** (more buffer = fewer shortages).
- **Why it matters in shipbuilding:** **EXTREMELY HIGH.** A single missing circuit breaker or main engine part can halt an entire assembly line for weeks. LD clauses in shipbuilding contracts often charge **$10,000 – $50,000 per day** of delay.

## 2.2 Total Inventory Cost (TIC) Model

The fundamental equation a KTU student *must memorize*:

$$TIC(Q) = \underbrace{D \cdot P}_{\text{Purchase}} + \underbrace{\frac{D}{Q} \cdot S}_{\text{Ordering}} + \underbrace{\frac{Q}{2} \cdot H}_{\text{Holding}} + \underbrace{\text{Shortage Cost}}_{\text{If applicable}}$$

For the basic EOQ model (no shortage, no discount):
