$$TIC(Q) = D \cdot P + \frac{D \cdot S}{Q} + \frac{H \cdot Q}{2}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these equations appear in 90% of KTU ESE Part B questions on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Expression | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | Optimal order size | units |
| 2 | Optimal Number of Orders/year | $N^* = \dfrac{D}{Q^*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{DS}{2H}}$ | Orders placed annually | orders/yr |
| 3 | Optimal Time Between Orders | $T^* = \dfrac{Q^*}{D} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2S}{DH}}$ | Cycle time | years |
| 4 | Minimum Total Variable Cost | $TVC^* = \sqrt{2DSH}$ | Excludes purchase cost | ₹/year |
| 5 | Reorder Point | $ROP = d \cdot L$ | Trigger for new order | units |
| 6 | Holding Cost in terms of P | $H = i \cdot P$ | Capital + storage % | ₹/unit/yr |
| 7 | Total Annual Cost (basic) | $TC = \dfrac{DS}{Q} + \dfrac{HQ}{2}$ | Variable cost only | ₹/year |
| 8 | Average Inventory | $\bar{I} = \dfrac{Q}{2}$ | Mean stock | units |
| 9 | Inventory Turnover Ratio | $ITR = \dfrac{D}{\bar{I}} = \dfrac{2D}{Q}$ | Stock efficiency | ratio |
| 10 | Shortage Cost (backorder) | $C_S = \dfrac{S_s \cdot Q_s^2}{2(D - d)}$ | Per cycle | ₹/cycle |

## 2.4 Cost-Quantity Trade-off — The "Bathtub" Curve

A unique insight: If you plot **Ordering Cost** and **Holding Cost** against order quantity **Q** on the same graph, they intersect at a unique point — the **EOQ**. To the *left* of EOQ, ordering dominates; to the *right*, holding dominates. This is graphically shown in Section 4.

$$C_O(Q) = \frac{DS}{Q} \quad \text{and} \quad C_H(Q) = \frac{HQ}{2}$$

At the **minimum**, $\dfrac{d(TC)}{dQ} = 0$, giving the **optimality condition**:

$$\boxed{\frac{DS}{Q^2} = \frac{H}{2} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{Ordering Cost} = \text{Holding Cost at EOQ}}$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility in Shipyards

| Shipyard Function | Inventory Cost Application |
|---|---|
| **Procurement Department** | Decides lot size for steel plate, piping, valves, cables. |
| **Finance Department** | Calculates working capital requirement & cost of capital. |
| **Production Planning** | Schedules material arrival to match block erection sequence. |
| **Quality Assurance** | Determines inspection frequency vs. rejection rates. |
| **Project Management** | Estimates LD exposure due to material delays. |
| **Warehouse Management** | Allocates covered/open yard space (a fixed cost). |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the EOQ Formula (Wilson's Formula)

The **Economic Order Quantity** derivation is a *guaranteed* KTU Part B question. Below is the complete, line-by-line derivation that earns full marks.

### Given:
- Annual demand = $D$ (units/year)
- Ordering cost per order = $S$ (₹/order)
- Holding cost per unit per year = $H$ (₹/unit/year)
- Order quantity = $Q$ (units/order)
- Purchase cost per unit = $P$ (₹/unit) — *independent of Q*

### Step 1: Define the Total Variable Cost Function
The annual variable cost has two parts — ordering and holding:

$$TVC(Q) = \underbrace{\frac{D}{Q} \cdot S}_{\text{Annual Ordering Cost}} + \underbrace{\frac{Q}{2} \cdot H}_{\text{Annual Holding Cost}}$$

**Logic:** Number of orders per year = (Annual demand) ÷ (Order size) = $D/Q$. Each costs $S$. Inventory rises linearly from 0 to $Q$ during a cycle, so *average* inventory is $Q/2$. Each unit held for a year costs $H$.

### Step 2: Differentiate TVC with respect to Q
Take the first derivative using calculus (power rule for $Q^{-1}$ and $Q^1$):

$$\frac{d(TVC)}{dQ} = \frac{d}{dQ}\left(\frac{DS}{Q}\right) + \frac{d}{dQ}\left(\frac{HQ}{2}\right)$$

$$= DS \cdot \frac{d}{dQ}(Q^{-1}) + \frac{H}{2} \cdot \frac{d}{dQ}(Q)$$

$$= DS \cdot (-Q^{-2}) + \frac{H}{2} \cdot (1)$$

$$\boxed{\frac{d(TVC)}{dQ} = -\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2}}$$

### Step 3: Apply the Optimality Condition
For a minimum, set the first derivative to zero:

$$-\frac{DS}{Q^2} + \frac{H}{2} = 0$$

### Step 4: Solve Algebraically for Q
Rearrange:

$$\frac{H}{2} = \frac{DS}{Q^2}$$

Multiply both sides by $Q^2$:

$$\frac{H \cdot Q^2}{2} = DS$$

Multiply both sides by 2:

$$H \cdot Q^2 = 2DS$$

Divide both sides by H:

$$Q^2 = \frac{2DS}{H}$$

Take the positive square root (Q must be positive):

$$\boxed{Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}}}$$

**This is the classical Wilson EOQ formula.**

### Step 5: Second-Order Verification (Confirm Minimum)
Take the second derivative:

$$\frac{d^2(TVC)}{dQ^2} = \frac{d}{dQ}\left(-\frac{DS}{Q^2}\right) = \frac{2DS}{Q^3}$$

Since $D, S, Q > 0$, we have $\dfrac{d^2(TVC)}{dQ^2} > 0$, confirming that the critical point is a **minimum**, not a maximum.

### Step 6: Minimum Total Variable Cost
Substitute $Q^*$ back into TVC:

$$TVC^* = \frac{DS}{Q^*} + \frac{HQ^*}{2}$$

At optimum, ordering cost equals holding cost (each = $TVC^*/2$):

$$TVC^* = 2 \cdot \frac{HQ^*}{2} = H \cdot Q^*$$

$$= H \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{H^2 \cdot \frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{2DSH}$$

$$\boxed{TVC^* = \sqrt{2DSH}}$$

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Shipbuilding Context)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A shipyard requires **5,000 tonnes/year** of shipbuilding steel plates. The ordering cost is **₹25,000 per order**, and the holding cost is **20% of the unit price**. The unit price of steel is **₹80,000 per tonne**. Determine: (a) EOQ, (b) Optimal number of orders, (c) Time between orders, (d) Total annual inventory cost, (e) Reorder point if lead time is 7 days.

### Given Data:
$D = 5{,}000$ tonnes/year; $S = ₹25{,}000$/order; $P = ₹80{,}000$/tonne; $i = 20\% = 0.20$; Lead time $L = 7$ days.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Compute Holding Cost per unit per year**
$$H = i \cdot P = 0.20 \times 80{,}000 = ₹16{,}000 \text{ per tonne per year}$$

**Step 2: Compute EOQ**
$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot D \cdot S}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 5{,}000 \times 25{,}000}{16{,}000}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{250{,}000{,}000}{16{,}000}} = \sqrt{15{,}625}$$

$$\boxed{Q^* = 125 \text{ tonnes per order}}$$

**Step 3: Optimal Number of Orders per year**
$$N^* = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{5{,}000}{125} = 40 \text{ orders/year}$$

**Step 4: Time Between Orders (Cycle Time)**
$$T^* = \frac{Q^*}{D} \times 365 = \frac{125}{5{,}000} \times 365$$

$$\boxed{T^* = 9.125 \text{ days} \approx 9 \text{ days between orders}}$$

**Step 5: Total Annual Inventory Cost (Variable only)**
$$TVC^* = \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 5{,}000 \times 25{,}000 \times 16{,}000}$$

$$= \sqrt{4 \times 10^{12}} = ₹2{,}00{,}000 \text{ per year}$$

**Verification by individual components:**
- Ordering cost = $(D/Q^*) \cdot S = 40 \times 25{,}000 = ₹10{,}00{,}000$

Wait — let me recheck: $\sqrt{2 \times 5000 \times 25000 \times 16000} = \sqrt{4{,}000{,}000{,}000{,}000} = \sqrt{4 \times 10^{12}} = 2 \times 10^6 = ₹20,00,000$

Let me recompute carefully:
$2 \times 5000 = 10{,}000$
$10{,}000 \times 25{,}000 = 2.5 \times 10^8$
$2.5 \times 10^8 \times 16{,}000 = 4 \times 10^{12}$
$\sqrt{4 \times 10^{12}} = 2 \times 10^6 = ₹20{,}00{,}000$

- Ordering cost: $(5000/125) \times 25{,}000 = 40 \times 25{,}000 = ₹10{,}00{,}000$
- Holding cost: $(125/2) \times 16{,}000 = 62.5 \times 16{,}000 = ₹10{,}00{,}000$
- **Sum = ₹20,00,000** ✓ (matches TVC*)

**Step 6: Reorder Point (ROP)**
Daily demand: $d = D/365 = 5{,}000/365 \approx 13.70$ tonnes/day
Lead time $L = 7$ days

$$ROP = d \cdot L = 13.70 \times 7 \approx 95.89 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Step 7: Total Cost including Purchase**
$$TC^* = D \cdot P + TVC^* = 5{,}000 \times 80{,}000 + 20{,}00{,}000$$

$$= ₹400{,}00{,}00{,}000 + ₹20{,}00{,}000 = ₹420 \text{ Crore approx.}$$

## 3.3 Sensitivity Analysis Matrix

A KTU favourite — *What if parameters change?*

| Scenario | Parameter Changed | New EOQ | Effect on Total Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Demand doubles (D → 2D) | Demand ↑ | $Q^* \times \sqrt{2}$ | $TC^* \times \sqrt{2}$ |
| Ordering cost quadruples (S → 4S) | S ↑ | $Q^* \times 2$ | $TC^* \times 2$ |
| Holding cost drops by 75% (H → H/4) | H ↓ | $Q^* \times 2$ | $TC^* \times 1/\sqrt{2}$ |
| Unit price increases (P ↑) | P ↑ | $Q^*$ unchanged | Holding cost ↑ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** EOQ is *insensitive* to large parameter changes. A 100% error in cost estimation causes only a ~30% error in TC. This is the famous **EOQ robustness property**, often asked as a 3-mark question.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
**Define the term "Inventory Carrying Cost" and list any four components of it in a shipyard context.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Inventory Carrying Cost (or Holding Cost)** is the cost of maintaining inventory in storage over a period of time. It includes both the **opportunity cost of capital locked up** in materials and the **physical cost of storage**.

Four components in a shipyard:
1. **Capital cost** — Interest on money tied up in steel plates, pipes, and equipment (largest component, typically 60–80% of H).
2. **Storage/warehousing cost** — Rent or depreciation of covered warehouses, open yards, and crane usage.
3. **Insurance and taxes** — Annual premium for marine cargo policy and local municipal taxes.
4. **Deterioration and obsolescence** — Corrosion of steel plates, expiry of paints/chemicals, obsolescence of electronic components.

*(1 mark for definition + ½ mark × 4 = 2 marks for components = 3 marks total)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**Distinguish between "Ordering Cost" and "Shortage Cost" with one shipyard example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Ordering Cost | Shortage Cost |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Cost incurred each time a purchase order is placed. | Cost incurred when demanded material is unavailable. |
| **Nature** | Planned, controllable, deterministic. | Unplanned, probabilistic, often punitive. |
| **Relation to Q** | Decreases as Q ↑. | Decreases as Q ↑. |
| **Shipyard Example** | ₹25,000 spent on PO processing, inland transport, and inspection of 100 tonnes of steel plates. | Delay of main engine delivery by 30 days causing LD of $15,000/day = $450,000 penalty. |

*(1 mark definition each + 1 mark example each = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Question Paper, Module 4)]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **various components of inventory costs** relevant to a ship production yard, with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A shipyard uses **8,000 units/year** of a particular valve. The cost of placing one order is **₹1,200** and the holding cost per unit per year is **₹6**. Determine:
- (i) Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)
- (ii) Optimal number of orders per year
- (iii) Time between two consecutive orders
- (iv) Total annual inventory cost (variable part)
- (v) Reorder point if lead time is 10 days. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Components of Inventory Cost (7 marks)

Inventory cost in a ship production yard comprises four major components:

**1. Purchase Cost (Acquisition Cost)** (1 mark)
This is the direct price paid to the supplier. For example, ₹80,000 per tonne of AH36 steel plate. Often constant, but reduces with bulk discounts.

**2. Ordering Cost (Setup Cost)** (2 marks)
Incurred **per order**, regardless of order size. Includes:
- Purchase requisition & PO preparation
- Vendor selection & negotiation
- Inland/sea freight & insurance in transit
- Customs duty & clearance (for imports like main engines, propellers)
- Inward inspection by QA/Classification Society
- Invoice processing & payment

Example: For a shipyard ordering 100 valves, the procurement, transport, and inspection overhead may amount to ₹1,200 per order.

**3. Holding (Carrying) Cost** (2 marks)
Cost of **keeping** stock in the yard. Includes:
- Capital cost (i·P) — typically **15–25% of unit price per annum**
- Covered warehouse / open yard rent
- Insurance, fire & theft protection
- Deterioration (corrosion of steel, expiry of paints)
- Pilferage & handling

Example: Storing imported navigation equipment worth ₹50 lakh for 6 months incurs significant capital cost.

**4. Shortage (Stockout) Cost** (2 marks)
Penalty when material is **not available**. Includes:
- Production line stoppage
- Idle labour and crane costs
- Expediting via air-freight
- Liquidated damages (LD) for ship delivery delay
- Loss of reputation & future contracts

Example: Delay in delivering a critical radar system may cause 15 days of ship delay, attracting $20,000/day LD.

**Conclusion:** The materials manager balances these four costs by selecting the **EOQ**, minimizing the total relevant cost.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** $D = 8{,}000$ units/year, $S = ₹1{,}200$/order, $H = ₹6$/unit/year, $L = 10$ days.

**(i) EOQ:**
$$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 8{,}000 \times 1{,}200}{6}} = \sqrt{\frac{19{,}200{,}000}{6}} = \sqrt{3{,}200{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{Q^* = 1{,}788.85 \approx 1{,}789 \text{ units per order}}$$ *(1 mark)*

**(ii) Optimal Number of Orders/year:**
$$N^* = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{8{,}000}{1{,}789} \approx 4.47 \text{ orders/year}$$ *(1 mark)*

**(iii) Time Between Orders:**
$$T^* = \frac{Q^*}{D} \times 365 = \frac{1{,}789}{8{,}000} \times 365 \approx 81.6 \text{ days}$$ *(1 mark)*

**(iv) Total Annual Variable Cost:**
$$TVC^* = \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 8{,}000 \times 1{,}200 \times 6}$$
$$= \sqrt{1{,}15{,}200{,}000} = ₹10{,}732.5 \text{ per year}$$ *(2 marks)*

**Verification:**
- Ordering cost = $N^* \cdot S = 4.47 \times 1{,}200 = ₹5{,}367.5$
- Holding cost = $(Q^*/2) \cdot H = (1{,}789/2) \times 6 = ₹5{,}367.0$
- Sum ≈ ₹10{,}734 ✓ *(0.5 mark verification)*

**(v) Reorder Point (ROP):**
$$d = \frac{D}{365} = \frac{8{,}000}{365} = 21.92 \text{ units/day}$$
$$ROP = d \times L = 21.92 \times 10 = 219.2 \approx 220 \text{ units}$$ *(1 mark)*

**Order is placed when stock falls to 220 units.**

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative)
**(a)** Derive the **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** formula from the basic total cost model. State the assumptions clearly. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A shipyard procures **12,000 kg/year** of welding electrodes. The supplier offers:
- Price slab 1: ₹200/kg for order size < 1,000 kg
- Price slab 2: ₹180/kg for order size 1,000–2,999 kg
- Price slab 3: ₹160/kg for order size ≥ 3,000 kg

Ordering cost = ₹1,500/order. Holding cost = 25% of unit price. Determine the **most economical order quantity**. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution (Concise Outline)

**Part (a):** Follow the derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes — start with $TVC = DS/Q + HQ/2$, differentiate, set to zero, solve for $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$. State **7 standard assumptions**: deterministic demand, constant lead time, instantaneous replenishment, no stockouts, single product, independent orders, constant ordering & holding costs. *(7 marks — 4 for derivation, 2 for assumptions, 1 for EOQ box)*

**Part (b) — Quantity Discount Model:**

| Slab | Price P | H = 0.25P | EOQ* (unconstrained) | Feasible? | TC at adjusted Q |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (< 1000) | ₹200 | ₹50 | $\sqrt{2 \times 12000 \times 1500 / 50} = 849$ kg | Yes (< 1000) | Compute |
| 2 (1000–2999) | ₹180 | ₹45 | $\sqrt{2 \times 12000 \times 1500 / 45} = 894$ kg | No, adjust to 1000 | Compute |
| 3 (≥ 3000) | ₹160 | ₹40 | $\sqrt{2 \times 12000 \times 1500 / 40} = 949$ kg | No, adjust to 3000 | Compute |

**Calculate TC = D·P + DS/Q + HQ/2** for each feasible/adjusted Q and select minimum. *(7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Cost students 2–4 marks each in KTU ESE):**
> 1. **Forgetting units in the final answer.** Always state "tonnes/year", "₹/year", "days", etc. A naked number is treated as incomplete.
> 2. **Using purchase cost inside TVC*** unnecessarily. TC* = TVC* + D·P is the full form. EOQ minimization applies to the variable part only.
> 3. **Confusing Reorder Point (ROP) with EOQ.** ROP = demand during lead time; EOQ = economic lot size. They serve different purposes.
> 4. **Not verifying that ordering cost = holding cost at EOQ.** Examiners award 1 bonus mark for this cross-check.
> 5. **Misapplying the discount model.** In quantity discount problems, if the unconstrained EOQ falls in a lower price slab, you *must* evaluate the candidate at the price-break quantity of the higher slab.
> 6. **In ship-building context questions, failing to mention** corrosion, classification society delays, or LD clauses — these are domain-specific terms examiners expect.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Pin this on your wall before the exam:**

- ☐ Inventory Cost = Purchase + Ordering + Holding + Shortage.
- ☐ EOQ formula: $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ — Wilson's Formula, 1913.
- ☐ At EOQ, **Ordering Cost = Holding Cost** (key identity).
- ☐ Minimum TVC* = $\sqrt{2DSH}$.
- ☐ Holding cost is usually expressed as $H = iP$ (i ≈ 15–25%).
- ☐ Reorder Point = daily demand × lead time (basic model).
- ☐ Cycle time $T^* = Q^*/D$ in years; convert to days × 365.
- ☐ EOQ is **robust** — a 100% parameter error gives only ~30% TC error.
- ☐ In shipyards: materials = 60–70% of contract value; corrosion, LD, and import delays are key cost drivers.
- ☐ Purchase cost is **constant** in basic EOQ, **stepwise** in discount models.
- ☐ Shortage cost in shipbuilding is **catastrophic** — never compromise safety stock.
- ☐ Inventory Turnover Ratio = D / (Q/2) — higher is better for shipyard stores.
- ☐ Always convert units consistently (tonnes, ₹/tonne, days) before substituting.
- ☐ In discount problems: test **all price-break quantities** plus unconstrained EOQ, then pick minimum TC.
